Enterprise Resource Planning System Subscription Increase — Approval

resource planning system for years five (5) through ten (10) of the existing agreement in the amount of $242,000,
for a new total amount of $4,513,883; establish a contingency reserve of $30,000; and authorize the Finance
Director to execute the subscription growth acknowledgement in substantially the form presented and issue
change orders within the contingency amount.
CEQA: This Council action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there is no
possibility that the approval of an increase in yearly subscriptions for the City’s ERP system may have a significant
effect on the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)

costs.

compliant procurement program is not a project subject to the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA),

Resources Code Section 21065.)

10.
Wastewater Treatment Plant On-Call Consulting Services — Amendment
Approve Amendment No. 4 to the Professional Consultant Services Agreement with Carollo Engineers, Inc. for
Wastewater Treatment Plant on-call consulting services in the amount of $250,000, for a new not-to-exceed
amount of $1,675,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the
form presented.
CEQA: The proposed action is exempt from environmental review under the California Environmental Quality Act
(CEQA), since entering on-call professional services agreements is an organizational or administrative activity that
will not result in direct or indirect significant physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section
15378(b)(5).) In addition, on June 6, 2016, the City Council certified a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact
Report (Final PEIR) (SCH No. 2015032006), which serves as the primary environmental compliance document for
implementation of the Clean Water Program. On July 16, 2018, the City approved Addendum 1 to the Final PEIR,
and on April 15, 2019, approved Addendum 2. Any resultant capital improvement or operations and maintenance-
related projects resulting from the on-call professional consulting services shall be reviewed on an individual basis
or evaluated for consistency with the PEIR and Addenda.

City Council action on the following public hearing(s) represents a final decision concluding all administrative proceedings. Judicial

17.
Accessory Dwelling Unit Ordinance - Zoning Code Amendments — Ordinance Introduction
Introduce an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 27.19, “Accessory Dwelling Unit and Junior
Accessory Dwelling Unit – Residential Zones” to address comments from the California Department of Housing
and Community Development (HCD) to align with current state law, and to address Housing Element programs,
and find it exempt from CEQA pursuant to Section 21080.17 of the Public Resources Code and Section 15282(h)
of the CEQA Guidelines.
CEQA: The proposed ordinance is statutorily exempt from the requirements of the California Environmental
Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to Section 21080.17 of the Public Resources Code and Section 15282(h) of the CEQA
Guidelines because it is an accessory dwelling unit ordinance that is implementing the provisions of the
Government Code related to ADUs and JADUs.

PUBLIC COMMENTS/REQUEST TO SPEAK
Prior to the Meeting 
Send comments to: clerk@cityofsanmateo.org until 4 p.m. the day of the meeting.
During the meeting
By Zoom: Click the link at the top of the agenda and you’ll be added to the meeting. All attendees are muted by default. When the item of interest
is open for consideration, select the “Raise Your Hand” icon and you will be called on at the appropriate time.
By telephone: Call (408) 638-0968 and enter the conference ID found at the top of the meeting agenda. When the item of interest is open for
consideration, select *9 to raise your hand. When called upon, press *6 to unmute, state your name and provide your comments.
In Person: At the meeting complete a “Request to Speak” form. Organized Group and Presentations: Up to five (5) minutes for a group presentation,
with as many speakers as can be accommodated within that time allowance. To qualify for a group presentation, all individuals associated with the
presentation must be clearly identified and confirmed as in attendance at the meeting. Presentation materials must be submitted to
clerk@cityofsanmateo.org one (1) business day in advance of the meeting.
ACCESSIBILITY: In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act and to ensure meaningful access for all participants, including those with
limited English proficiency, individuals requiring special accommodations or language assistance to participate in this meeting may contact the City
Clerk’s Office at (650) 522-7040 or clerk@cityofsanmateo.org . Notification 48 hours prior to the meeting will enable the City to make reasonable

PUBLIC COMMENT
Taso Zografos expressed concerns regarding the Humboldt Street bike lanes and the related public outreach 
process. Ryan commented on skate park conditions and requested improved engagement with the 
skateboarding community.
OLD BUSINESS
5.
Wet Weather Preparations – Update
Matt Fabry, Director of Public Works, gave the presentation.
Public Comment: Rich Hedges thanked the Public Works team for their wet weather preparation efforts.
Council asked questions and provided comments. Council thanked staff for progress to date and discussed 
potential outcomes of the Storm Drain Master Plan, including the use of updated modeling to identify 
infrastructure and maintenance needs. Council discussed next steps for lagoon dredging, including strategies to 
increase bid competitiveness, anticipated timelines, community impacts, and options for reuse or disposal of 
dredged material. Council inquired about remaining homes in FEMA flood zones. Staff noted that a potential 
North Central sandbag station remains under consideration and that efforts will continue to identify a feasible 
location.
6.
Economic Development Work Plan 2026-2029 — Adoption
Jennifer Chen, Economic Development Manager, gave the presentation.
Public Comment: Erica Wood spoke in support of the economic development work plan and offered the 
Chamber’s support for its implementation. Vince Siminitus encouraged recognition and continuation of the 
City’s age-friendly business certification program.
Council asked questions and made comments. Council emphasized aligning City branding with partners such as 
the Chamber, amending the workplan to include policy supporting local small businesses, and ensuring 
economic development plans include fostering a clean and safe business environment. Council also discussed 
increasing opportunities for public-private partnerships, lessons learned from past experiences, and strategies to 
strengthen downtown and other neighborhoods. Additional discussion included the need for clear performance 
metrics (such as sales tax, unemployment, and vacancy rates), continued outreach with business districts 
(including 25th Avenue), and potential initiatives that may require future Council consideration and direction.
Motion to adopt Economic Development Work Plan 2026-2029. Motion passed 5-0.
Moved:       
Newsom, Seconded: Cwirko-Godycki
Ayes:
Loraine, Newsom, Fernandez, Cwirko-Godycki, Diaz Nash
Noes: 
None 
STUDY SESSION

Public Comment: Trina Pierce expressed concerns regarding the item, citing potential community impacts and 
requesting Council consideration of alternatives and/or additional analysis prior to implementation
Council asked questions and made comments. Council discussed timing for potential community budgeting 
efforts, commended staff for emergency preparedness training, and discussed economic development

SUBJECT:
Ceremonial Street Naming Application Honoring Former Mayor Jerry Hill — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the application from Deputy Mayor Fernandez and Council Member Diaz Nash for the ceremonial street naming 
of O’Farrell Street to honor former Mayor Jerry Hill, consistent with the City’s Ceremonial Street Naming Policy adopted by 
Resolution No. 109 (2025).
BACKGROUND:
On November 17, 2025, the City Council adopted Resolution No. 109 (2025) (Attachment 1), establishing a policy 
framework through which the City Council may recognize individuals for outstanding contributions to the community 
through the designation of a ceremonial street name.
On January 6, 2026, the City Clerk’s Office received an application submitted by Deputy Mayor Nicole Fernandez and 
Council Member Lisa Diaz Nash, requesting ceremonial recognition of former Mayor Jerry Hill. The application was 
reviewed by the City Clerk’s Office and found to be substantially compliant with the eligibility and procedural requirements 
outlined in Resolution No. 109 (2025).
Consistent with the policy, the application included a written statement addressing the eligibility criteria, which require at 
least ten (10) years of community involvement, an extraordinary and sustained voluntary commitment to the community, 
or significant contributions to the City of San Mateo. The submitted statement summarized the proposed honoree’s public 
service, including service on the San Mateo City Council (including one term as Mayor), the San Mateo County Board of 
Supervisors, the California State Assembly, and the California State Senate.
The application proposes O’Farrell Street as the location for the ceremonial street designation. The policy specifies that 
ceremonial street names do not replace or modify the legal street name and are implemented through the placement of a 
ceremonial street sign topper. The policy further provides that ceremonial street names should generally be located on 
streets of civic or community significance. O’Farrell Street, immediately adjoining 20th Avenue, has served as a primary 
access corridor to City Hall since 1962.
If approved by the City Council, City staff will coordinate the fabrication and installation of the ceremonial street sign 
topper in accordance with the standards and placement requirements established in Resolution No. 109 (2025). In 
addition, staff will provide written notification to all residential and commercial properties located on the affected street 
block(s) in advance of installation.
The application requests that the ceremonial designation be installed for an indefinite duration, consistent with the policy 
framework. 
11 of 1110

--- page 12 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
BUDGET IMPACT:
The estimated cost for fabrication and installation of the ceremonial street sign topper is approximately $1,150. Sufficient 
funds are available within the City Council budget to cover these costs, as well as ancillary expenses associated with 
required written notifications and the coordination of a ceremonial unveiling event.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This ceremonial street naming application is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative 
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Resolution No. 109 (2025)

Martin McTaggart, City Clerk
mmctaggart@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7042

--- page 13 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. 109 ( 2025) 
APPROVING A POLICY FOR THE CEREMONIAL NAMING OF STREETS
WHEREAS, the San Mateo City Council desires to recognize individuals that are of particular importance
to the City of San Mateo or have made significant contributions to improving the quality of life in the City by
ceremonially naming sections of public streets in their honor; and
WHEREAS, currently, there is no formal policy addressing guidelines for the content or placement of
ceremonial street signs; and
WHEREAS, a policy is needed to ensure consistency, proper neighborhood outreach, traffic safety, and
appropriate sign content; and
WHEREAS, this Resolution authorizes the City Council to confer ceremonial names on public streets and
allow for the installation of appropriate signage, which shall be distinct from, and not change or replace, existing
legal street names and signs. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY FINDS and
RESOLVES that: 
1. 
This Resolution is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “ existing facility,” because it consists of the
operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public
or private structures, facilities, mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving
negligible or no expansion of use. ( CEQA Guidelines Section 15301.) 
2. 
Candidates for ceremonial street names must exhibit a minimum of 10 years of community
involvement or demonstrated an extraordinary, consistent and voluntary commitment to the
community, or have had contributed significantly to the City of San Mateo. 
Ceremonial street names shall be in the form of a “ Sign Topper,” which is a sheet metal sign that is
placed on top of the standard City street sign identification and shall adhere to the following
specifications: 
a. 
Ceremonial street names will not change or replace existing legal street names and signs.  
b. Ceremonial street names should generally be placed on streets of civic and community
significance.  
c. 
Only entire blocks of public streets may be granted ceremonial street names.  
d. Staff will identify streets with a limited number of blocks, and each street sign on each
block will receive a Street Topper.  
e. 
Ceremonial street names must be the name of an individual. 
f. 
A ceremonial street naming application may only be initiated by two current City
Councilmembers. 
g. 
A ceremonial street Sign Topper must include the word “ Honorary”, and be a brown sign
with white lettering.  
h. Only one ceremonial Sign Topper may be placed on a single street block. 
i. 
Only one Sign Topper may be placed on any pole. 
Docusign Envelope ID: 82C1B684- AE2E-4E26-A59C- 15DF844B126B

Ceremonial Street Naming Policy — Adoption
j. 
Sign Toppers shall not be attached to traffic signal mast arms or traffic signal poles. 
k. 
Ceremonial names shall not duplicate any currently existing official street names. 
l. 
No individual may receive City Council approval for a Sign Topper recognition on more
than one street or intersection. 
m. Only one Ceremonial Street Sign Topper may be approved by City Council per fiscal year, 
however exceptions in some years can be made as circumstances warrant based on City
Council direction.  
n. Staff will send out notices to all residential and commercial addresses on the street or
block in which the Ceremonial Street Sign Topper is added.  
o. 
The ceremonial street name will expire in ten years, but the Council may choose to keep
the topper for a longer or indefinite period.

SUBJECT:
2033 La Salle Drive - Zoning Reclassification, Zoning Code Amendment, and Planning Application for an Early Childhood 
Education Center — Ordinance Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt an Ordinance to approve a Zoning Reclassification from R1-B to R1-B/Q8, and a Zoning Code Amendment to provide 
development standards for an educational facility and find it exempt from CEQA pursuant to Section 15061(b)(3) 
(Common Sense Exemption). 
BACKGROUND:
On January 12, 2026, the City Council introduced an Ordinance (Attachment 1) to approve a Zoning Reclassification from 
R1-B to R1-B/Q8 and a Zoning Code Amendment to establish development standards for an educational facility, and to find 
these actions exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to Section 15061(b)(3) (Common 
Sense Exemption). Approval of the Ordinance ensures that the City’s Zoning Code provides clear and appropriate 
development standards for early childhood education facilities, supports the orderly development of community-serving 
uses, and allows future projects of this type to be reviewed and regulated in a consistent and transparent manner. 
If adopted, the Ordinance will become effective 30 days after adoption.
BUDGET IMPACT:
No budget impact is anticipated as a result of this Ordinance adoption.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The Zoning Reclassification and Zoning Map Amendment are exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
pursuant to the Common Sense Exemption under CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3), as the action is administrative in 
nature and does not authorize or result in any physical change to the environment.
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
DRAFT ORDINANCE NO. ____ (2026)
AMENDING TITLE 27 OF THE SAN MATEO MUNICIPAL CODE, RELATED TO QUALIFIED OVERLAY 8 (Q8) 
DISTRICTS TO ESTABLISH DEVELOPMENT STANDARDS FOR THE PROPOSED EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION 
CENTER IN THE Q8 OVERLAY AND AMENDING THE ZONING MAP OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO TO REZONE THE 
SITE AT 2033 LA SALLE DRIVE FROM R1-B (ONE FAMILY DWELLINGS) TO R1-B/Q8 (ONE FAMILY DWELLINGS, 
QUALIFIED OVERLAY DISTRICT 8)
WHEREAS, on June 17, 2019, the City Council established the Qualified Overlay District 8 (Q8) designation 
to provide for the development of land pursuant to standards and regulations that reflect the unique 
characteristics of educational facilities within residential districts; and
WHEREAS, the Carey School (hereinafter referred to as the “Applicant”) submitted Planning Application 
No. PA-2025-012 to the Planning Division for a Zoning Reclassification, Zoning Map Amendment, Site Plan and 
Architectural Review, Special Use Permit, and Site Development Planning Application to rezone the property 
located at 2033 La Salle Drive from R1-B to R1-B/Q8, and to establish and construct an early childhood education 
center at the same location (collectively referred to as the “Project”) in the City of San Mateo (“City”); and 
WHEREAS, the City has previously applied Qualified Overlay designations to private educational facilities 
to address site-specific operational needs; and 
WHEREAS, the General Plan 2040 Public Services Facilities Element has a goal to “foster the healthy 
development and education of children of all abilities, incomes, and backgrounds,” (Goal PSF-6) and a specific 
policy that encourages childcare centers in residential neighborhoods where they meet the City standards (Policy 
PSF 6.5); and 
WHEREAS, educational facilities are permitted in the R1-B zoning district with a Special Use Permit; 
however, the single-family development standards do not address the site-specific operational needs of such 
facilities; and 
WHEREAS, the Q8 Overlay addresses the unique characteristics of the educational facility, while limiting 
the uses on the site and protecting the surrounding uses by requiring a Special Use Permit for all uses within this 
overlay; and 
WHEREAS, the zoning reclassification from R1-B to R1-B/Q8 and zoning code amendment would 
implement the General Plan by establishing site specific development standards for an educational facility that 
provides childcare services while protecting the surrounding users; and 
WHEREAS, on December 9, 2025, the Planning Commission held a duly noticed public hearing, at which 
time the Commission received and considered written and oral evidence, and by a vote of unanimously (4-0), with 
one commissioner absent, to recommend that the City Council approve the Zoning Reclassification, Zoning Map 
Amendment, Site Plan and Architectural Review, Special Use Permit, and Site Development Planning Application, 
subject to the Findings for Approval and Conditions of Approval for Planning Application No. PA-2025-012; and
16 of 1110

--- page 17 ---
WHEREAS, the City Council has determined that the proposed rezoning and zoning code amendments are 
consistent with the City’s General Plan because the Q8 overlay zone authorizes deviations from the standards of 
the underlying R1-B zoning district, the Project includes a Special Use Permit that limits uses on the site and 
protects the surrounding neighborhood, and the Project otherwise conforms with the Residential Very Low land 
use designation of the site. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO ORDAINS AS FOLLOWS:
Section 1. Subsection (h) of Section 27.60.180 of the San Mateo Municipal Code is amended as follows:
(h) Q8 (Qualified Overlay District 8) - Limitations and Conditions. The following limitations and conditions 
shall pertain to the use of the property zoned Q8:
(1) Uses. All uses in this district shall require a special use permit, as set forth in Chapter 27.74 of 
the Zoning Code.
(2) Development.  Future development in this district shall comply with the site plans and 
conditions of approval in all applicable Planning Applications. Development standards include allowance 
of awning up to nine (9) feet in height within the rear yard setback.
(3) Floor Area Ratio.  This district shall have a maximum floor area ratio of 0.39.
(4) Parking.  Off-site parking facilities shall not be located in excess of 1,000 feet from the project 
site. Uncovered parking may be allowed within the front and side setback areas.
(5)  Signage. Signage on the site shall be allowed in accordance with Title 25 of the San Mateo 
Municipal Code.
Section 2. Environmental Determination. In accordance with California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
Guidelines, the proposed zoning reclassification and the zoning map amendment are exempt from further 
environmental review pursuant to the “Common Sense” exemption (14 CCR Section 15061(b)(3)). The Common 
Sense exemption applies where it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility the activity may have a 
significant effect on the environment. The proposed zoning reclassification and zoning overlay do not authorize 
physical development or ground disturbance on the site. Rather, the amendment provides additional allowances 
for potential future use of the site as an educational facility; it does not approve a specific development proposal 
or permit construction activities.
Pursuant to Municipal Code Section 27.60.180(h), all uses within the Q8 Overlay require approval of a Special Use 
Permit (SUP), and any development of the site requires Site Plan and Architectural Review (SPAR). Therefore, the 
proposed zoning reclassification and code amendment only change the site’s zoning designation and do not, in 
themselves, authorize any construction, demolition, or land disturbance that could result in physical 
environmental impacts. 
Section 3. Legislative findings. At a duly noticed public hearing on January 12, 2026, the City Council of the City 
of San Mateo reviewed and considered the associated staff report and attachments thereto, the Categorical 
Exemption, together with comments received during the public review process, and hereby finds and determines 
as follows:   
17 of 1110

--- page 18 ---
1.
CONFORMANCE WITH GENERAL PLAN. The City Council finds and determines that the 
proposed zoning code amendment and zoning reclassification from One Family Dwellings (R1-
B) to One Family Dwellings with Qualified District Overlay 8 (R1-B/Q8) are in conformance 
with the Residential Very Low land use designation of the General Plan of the City of San 
Mateo. The amendment establishes development standards for an educational facility 
providing childcare services within a residential district, consistent with General Plan Land 
Use Policy 3.5 and Public Services Facilities Policy 6.5, which encourage childcare centers in 
residential neighborhoods where they meet City standards. While educational facilities are 
currently permitted in the R1-B zoning district with a Special Use Permit, no specific 
development standards exist to address the unique characteristics of such uses within 
residential areas. The Qualified Overlay designations have previously been applied to private 
educational facilities to address site-specific operational needs. Extending the Q8 Overlay and 
amending the Zoning Code would implement the General Plan policies by establishing tailored 
development standards that facilitate the development of educational facilities in appropriate 
residential settings.  
2.
INTENT OF ZONING CODE. The overlay district and rezoning conform to the purpose of 
Municipal Code Section 27.60.180 in that the rezoning provides standards for the use of land 
with unique site characteristics as a private educational facility. 
Section 4. Zoning Map. The Zoning Map of the City of San Mateo is hereby amended to indicate as follows:  the 
property generally known as APN: 039-040-080 as depicted in Exhibit A, attached hereto, shall be reclassified from 
the R1-B (One Family Dwellings ) Zoning District to the R1-B/Q8 (One Family Dwellings , Qualified Overlay District 
8) Zoning District and the Zoning Map shall be amended to reflect this reclassification.
Section 5. Severability. In the event any section, clause or provision of this ordinance shall be determined invalid 
or unconstitutional, such section, clause or provision shall be deemed severable and all other sections or portions 
hereof shall remain in full force and effect. The City Council hereby declares that it would have passed this 
ordinance and each section, subsection, sentence, clause or phrase thereof, irrespective of the fact that any one 
or more section, subsection, sentence, clause or phrase be declared unconstitutional. 
Section 6. Publication. This Ordinance shall be published in summary in a newspaper of general circulation, posted 
in the City Clerk's Office, and posted on the City's website, all in accord with Section 2.15 of the City Charter.
Section 7. Legislative History and Effective Date. This ordinance was introduced on Clerk to complete., and 
adopted on Clerk to complete., and shall be effective 30 days after its adoption.

SUBJECT:
Enterprise Resource Planning System Subscription Increase — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a subscription growth and expansion increase to the City’s agreement with Workday for an enterprise resource 
planning system for years five (5) through ten (10) of the existing agreement in the amount of $242,000, for a new total 
amount of $4,513,883; establish a contingency reserve of $30,000; and authorize the Finance Director to execute the 
subscription growth acknowledgement in substantially the form presented and issue change orders within the contingency 
amount.
BACKGROUND:
In February 2022, the City of San Mateo entered into an agreement with Workday for software subscription, training, and 
delivery assurance costs related to the City’s enterprise resource planning (ERP) application. The Workday ERP supports 
the City’s core administrative functions of Human Capital Management (HCM), Payroll, and Core Financials and is used by 
all City of San Mateo and San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department employees. The City is now entering the 5th year of the 
10-year software subscription agreement with Workday.
 
As part of the original Master Services Agreement with Workday, the City must report annually on various Workday usage 
metrics from the prior year to evaluate whether a higher annual subscription fee is needed. For the first years of the 
agreement, Workday accepted a baseline estimate of all the City’s usage metrics provided in January 2022 using 2021 
data, because the Payroll and Human Resources modules had not been implemented yet. 
Since the City went live with the Human Resources and Payroll modules in Workday in March 2025, City staff needed to 
provide Workday its first official headcount in addition to other usage metrics as part of the annual reporting due in 
January 2026, covering usage metrics from 12/26/24 – 12/25/25. The updated headcount, the first in four years, showed 
an increase in the City’s overall system users due to several factors including some increases in staffing, lower employee 
vacancy rates, and an updated method for counting part-time employees in the Workday system. As a result, the total 
reported headcount for the City of San Mateo and San Mateo Consolidated Fire Department was 10.4% higher in 2025 
than in 2021. 
In accordance with the Master Services Agreement, the subscription for each year may be adjusted and calculated using a 
predefined growth and expansion table. Given the updated usage metrics provided for 2025, Year 5 of the agreement with 
Workday (2/24/2026 through 2/23/2027) will cost $40,382 more than originally estimated due to the updated headcount. 
In addition, City staff does not anticipate a change in the overall headcount in the remaining years of our agreement with 
Workday and expect the new baseline annual subscription cost to remain. To support this increase in subscription costs for 
Years 5 through 10, City staff recommends approving the Subscription Growth Acknowledgement (Attachment 1), 
increasing the agreement amount by $242,000 for the remaining 6 years of the agreement with Workday, for a total 
20 of 1110

--- page 21 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
agreement amount of $4,513,883, and a $30,000 contingency to cover any unanticipated changes in subscription costs due 
to other usage metric increases. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The current subscription fee for Year 5 of the Workday contract is $382,336 and is included in the Information Technology 
Department's operating budget. The additional subscription fee for FY 25-26 is $40,382. Given some budget savings in 
other areas, the Department can absorb the additional cost this year and will budget for the additional subscription fees in 
the next budget year.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This Council action is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that 
the approval of an increase in yearly subscriptions for the City’s ERP system may have a significant effect on the 
environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Revisions and Retitle of Crime and Intelligence Analyst Position — Approval
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to revise the Crime and Intelligence Analyst job specification and retitle as the Real Time Information 
Center (RTIC) Coordinator position, located in the Police Department.
BACKGROUND:
In October 2024, the Police Department launched the Real Time Information Center (RTIC) to serve as a centralized hub for 
utilizing live camera feeds, data platforms, and advanced technologies in support of field operations. Since its inception, 
the RTIC has quickly demonstrated its value, relying on a combination of overtime staffing and light-duty officers to ensure 
coverage. The RTIC has now reached a stage where clear oversight, consistent management, and strategic direction are 
necessary. As a result, there is an operational need for an RTIC Coordinator to ensure efficiency and long-term success of 
this centralized center.  
In August 2025, the Crime and Intelligence Analyst resigned, creating a vacancy. There is only one position in this 
classification. Since then, newly acquired technologies have significantly mitigated the operational gaps traditionally filled 
by an analyst, shifting the Department’s needs from crime analysis to overall coordination of the RTIC. 
After a thorough review of the classification and operational needs, the Police Department and Human Resources 
Department recommend the Crime and Intelligence Analyst classification be revised to reflect the duties and 
responsibilities of a RTIC Coordinator. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no fiscal impact as RTIC Coordinator would assume the same salary range and exempt status currently allocated to 
the vacant Crime and Intelligence Analyst classification.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This reclassification is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that it will not cause a physical 
change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution

WHEREAS, pursuant to the San Mateo Municipal Code Section 2.57.030(d), approval of new 
classifications and related job specifications, salary schedules, compensation changes, elimination or 
reinstatement of positions, reallocation of positions, reclassifications, and revisions of job specifications 
must be approved by City Council Resolution; and

to convert a vacant Crime and Intelligence Analyst position to a Real Time Information Center (RTIC) 
Coordinator position within the Police Department; and

attached as Exhibit A.

--- page 27 ---
REAL TIME INFORMATION CENTER COORDINATOR 
 
 
 
DEFINITION 
 
Under general direction, oversees the operational and administrative functions of the Real Time 
Information Center (RTIC), ensuring the seamless integration of technology, personnel, and 
processes, responsible for strategic planning, decision making, comprehensive reports, and 
fostering a collaborative environment, playing a pivotal role in enhancing public safety through 
effective surveillance, data analysis, and coordinated response efforts. 
 
DISTINGUISHING CHARACTERISTICS 
 
The RTIC Coordinator is a non-sworn classification responsible for overseeing all RTIC 
operations. This role manages resources, develops and implements training requirements, and 
ensures the RTIC is functioning effectively.  This position provides technical and functional 
oversight of RTIC to ensure the delivery of timely, accurate real-time intelligence to support field 
operations and departmental decision-making. The RTIC also participates directly in RTIC 
operations during peak periods, coordinates scheduling and staffing, and represents the City in 
related initiatives. Work requires a high level of independent judgment, technical expertise, and the 
ability to collaborate effectively with sworn personnel, specialized units, and external partners. 
 
SUPERVISION RECEIVED AND EXERCISED 
 
Receives directions from the Police Technical Services Administrator.  Does not exercise direct 
supervision over professional staff. 
 
EXAMPLES OF ESSENTIAL DUTIES - Duties may include, but are not limited to, the following: 
 
Efficiently manages resources, including personnel, budget, and technology, to enhance the overall 
effectiveness of the center. 
 
Develops training programs and ensures the continuous professional development of personnel 
assigned to the RTIC. 
 
Produces complex reports and bulletins for the Police Department to develop strategies and tactics 
to effectively prevent and control crime. 
 
Assists the Police Department with real-time information sharing; utilizes video feeds, intelligence 
databases, and other systems as needed. 
 
Assists operations and management personnel in planning the deployment of resources for the 
27 of 1110

--- page 28 ---
City of San Mateo 
Real Time Information Center Coordinator 
Page 2 of 4 
 
prevention and suppression of criminal activity. 
 
Creates and maintains informational databases; uses complex databases and software applications 
utilized by the RTIC. 
 
Participates in operations with specialized units to provide real time intelligence through radio 
communication and other authorized communication platforms.  
Coordinates the scheduling of the RTIC, ensuring coverage when applicable. 
 
Acts as an operator for the RTIC during peak operation times.  
 
Monitors the performance of operators assigned to the RTIC for compliance with policies, 
procedures, and relevant laws.  
 
Develops and implements work related procedures to satisfy requirements of local, state, and 
federal laws. 
 
Represents the City in interdepartmental, interagency task forces, community, and professional 
meetings as required; makes presentations as necessary. 
 
Testifies in court. 
Performs other related duties as assigned. 
 
 
QUALIFICATIONS 
 
Knowledge of: 
 
• Methods of identifying essential information. 
 
 
• Investigative and identification methods and techniques. 
• The methods and procedures of operating radio transmitting and receiving equipment. 
• Specialized computer software including Computer Aided Dispatch, video systems, and 
intelligence systems. 
• Federal, state, and local laws, as they relate to the essential functions of this position. 
• Standard office practices, procedures, methods, and equipment. 
• Organizing, maintaining, and retrieving data from electronic files. 
 
 
Ability to: 
• Identify complex problems and review related information to develop and evaluate options 
and implement solutions. 
• Use independent reasoning to solve complex problems. 
• Prioritize and make fast and accurate decisions. 
28 of 1110

--- page 29 ---
City of San Mateo 
Real Time Information Center Coordinator 
Page 3 of 4 
 
• Effectively communicate orally, verbally and in writing with coworkers, law enforcement 
personnel, supervisors, and to the public. 
• Work with restricted law enforcement documents and highly confidential information. 
• Establish and maintain cooperative working relationships with those contacted in the course 
of work. 
• Attend work and perform duties on a regular and consistent basis.  
 
 
EXPERIENCE AND TRAINING GUIDELINES 
 
Any combination equivalent to experience and training that would likely provide the required  
knowledge and abilities are qualifying. A typical way to obtain knowledge and abilities would be: 
 
Experience: 
 
Three (3) years of experience with a law enforcement agency that includes the operation of 
transmitting and receiving radio equipment. Two (2) of those years must include experience with 
crime intelligence, investigations, and/or emergency dispatch.  
 
Training: 
 
Equivalent to a bachelor’s degree from an accredited college or university is highly desirable. 
 
License or Certificates: 
 
Possession of Part 107 certificate (Remote Pilot Certificate) to fly drones with the Police 
Department’s drone team within the first year of employment. 
 
Required Special Qualification: 
 
Must successfully complete a thorough law enforcement background investigation. 
 
 
ADA SPECIAL REQUIREMENT: 
 
Essential duties require the following physical abilities and work environment: 
Ability to work in a standard office environment.

CRIME AND INTELLIGENCE ANALYST 
REAL TIME INFORMATION CENTER COORDINATOR 
 
DEFINITION 
To perform analysis of the underlying reasons and causes of crime; examine criminal activities to 
identify crime series, patterns, and trends; identify investigative leads by determining related 
cases and potential suspects; forecast occurrences of crime; disseminate analytical findings to 
departmental personnel and others; works in special projects outside the area of specialization 
with considerable independence; and to do related work as required. 
Under general direction, oversees the operational and administrative functions of the Real Time 
Information Center (RTIC), ensuring the seamless integration of technology, personnel, and 
processes, responsible for strategic planning, decision making, comprehensive reports, and 
fostering a collaborative environment, playing a pivotal role in enhancing public safety through 
effective surveillance, data analysis, and coordinated response efforts. 
 
DISTINGUISHING CHARACTERISTICS 
The Crime and Intelligence Analyst is a non-sworn classification primarily responsible for 
conducting complex crime analyses.  
The RTIC Coordinator is a non-sworn classification responsible for overseeing all RTIC 
operations. This role manages resources, develops and implements training requirements, and 
ensures the RTIC is functioning effectively.  This position provides technical and functional 
oversight of RTIC to ensure the delivery of timely, accurate real-time intelligence to support 
field operations and departmental decision-making. The RTIC also participates directly in RTIC 
operations during peak periods, coordinates scheduling and staffing, and represents the City in 
related initiatives. Work requires a high level of independent judgment, technical expertise, and 
the ability to collaborate effectively with sworn personnel, specialized units, and external 
partners. 
 
SUPERVISION RECEIVED AND EXERCISED 
Receives direction from higher level departmental sworn or administrative management 
personnel. the Police Technical Services Administrator.  Does not exercise direct supervision over 
professional staff. 
 
 
EXAMPLES OF DUTIES - Duties may include, but are not limited to, the following:

Analyze and determine trends in criminal activity, identify criminal offenders, trends, and 
patterns of crime; convey this information in various formats to appropriate department 
personnel. 
 
Compile, analyze, correlate, and evaluate data from crime, intelligence and arrest reports, law 
enforcement databases and other sources, looking for patterns and trends. 
 
Study offender and victim characteristics and methods of operation. 
 
Analyze dates, times, geography, and environment of crimes to forecast date, time, and location 
of next crime in a series. 
 
Summarize and analyze qualitative and quantitative data using calculations such as frequencies, 
percent changes, cross-tabulations, variation, and correlation. 
 
Produce complex reports and bulletins for the police department to develop strategies and tactics 
to effectively prevent and control crime. 
 
Manage date available to the public via the department’s data transparency webpage including 
evaluating, recommending, and implementing content and design improvements to increase 
effectiveness and efficiency; perform routine updates of data provided on the site. 
 
Assists operations and management personnel in planning the deployment of resources for the 
prevention and suppression of criminal activity. 
 
Establish criminal profiles that include prior crimes and criminal relationships as well as criminal 
organizations.   
 
Develop crime-mapping analysis of geographic data from police reports, calls for service, and 
other sources.

SUBJECT:
County of San Mateo Compost Broker Program — Memorandum of Understanding
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a Memorandum of Understanding with San Mateo County to participate in the Senate Bill 1383 Compost Broker 
Program; authorize the City Manager to execute the Memorandum of Understanding in substantially the form presented; 
authorize the City Manager to execute future renewals of the MOU to allow continued participation in the Compost Broker 
Program, subject to City Council approval of associated costs; and authorize payment to San Mateo County in an amount 
not to exceed $300,000 for calendar year 2026 program costs.
BACKGROUND: 
Senate Bill (SB) 1383 is a short-term climate pollutants reduction act requiring, in part, a statewide 75 percent 
reduction in the disposal of organic waste compared to 2014 levels by 2025. To meet this goal, the City is required to 
procure an amount of recovered organics waste product (e.g., compost, mulch) proportional to the population and 
amount of organic waste generated in the City. The City’s annual recovered organic waste product (ROWP) 
procurement target is 8,244 tons.
 
Currently, the City participates in the San Mateo Resource Conservation District’s (SMRCD) Compost Broker Program 
(Program) which is a collaboration between the SMRCD, every city and town within San Mateo County, and the County 
of San Mateo to help jurisdictions meet the SB 1383 ROWP procurement target. Through the Program, the SMRCD 
functions as the City’s direct service provider procuring and distributing compost to farm and rangeland to assist in 
reaching the City’s ROWP procurement target.
The Program has been recognized by CalRecycle, the California Resource Recovery Association, and the U.S. Composting 
Council as a first-of-its-kind public-private partnership that both advances SB 1383 procurement goals and improves the 
health and resiliency of California’s working lands.
While an important part of meeting the City’s annual target, the current Program secures only 13% of the requirement. 
The City meets the remainder of its annual ROWP target through additional agreements between the City and multiple 
other resource conservation districts (RCDs) in the state, including RCDs in Monterey, Santa Cruz, Placer, and Tuolumne 
Counties. The revised Program implemented by this Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) is intended to allow the 
City to meet a higher percentage of the City‘s procurement target at a much lower cost.
Using administrative efficiencies, partnerships with other regional Resource Conservation Districts, and economies of 
scale, the County proposes to, over time, lower the compost procurement costs from what the City currently pays for 
compost from $75-$78/ton to perhaps as low as $42-$46/ton. 
37 of 1110

--- page 38 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
The current Program will conclude in 2026 unless a new agreement is adopted. To continue, the County proposes a new 
MOU structure. Under this agreement, the City will provide an annual contribution and receive procurement credit in 
proportion to its participation. Starting in 2026, the County will administer the Program, including overseeing the 
agreement with the SMRCD, contract management, billing, record keeping, and reporting. This arrangement reduces 
administrative burden for the City, lowers costs significantly, and strengthens regional collaboration while supporting the 
City‘s SB 1383 obligations.
The City’s contribution for calendar year 2026 is $300,000. Of the City’s annual contribution, 96-98% of the funding will 
support activities that help meet the City’s annual procurement target and 2-4% will cover the County’s administrative 
costs, though administrative costs may be increased above 4% after the first year of implementation based on the total 
staff time required to administer the Program. Once the MOU is executed, the City may annually renew the MOU under 
the same terms and conditions, which staff recommends for the City Manager to be given authority to execute. 
Contribution amounts for each subsequent Program year will be subject to City Council approval.  The MOU may be ended 
by either party with 30 days written notice.  
 
While the County administers the program, the City remains responsible for meeting the total procurement target. In 
September of each year, the County will provide to the City the anticipated tons of ROWP procurement credit to be 
received in the calendar year following, which will allow the City to plan for how much ROWP procurement credit will need 
to be met beyond the Program’s support. Participation offers significant advantages by reducing duplicative effort, 
ensuring consistent reporting, and leveraging economies of scale to lower costs.
Participation in the current Program has been very successful in assisting the City in meeting the SB 1383 requirements. 
Staff has participated in the development of the Program and advocated for the County to take on the role outlined in the 
MOU. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The City can self-determine its annual funding contribution to the Program and will receive a ROWP procurement credit 
proportional to its financial contribution. To fund calendar year 2026, the County will invoice the City for an initial 
contribution of $300,000. Of the total invoiced amount, the County will retain between 2% and 4% of the total amount to 
offset staff time costs required to administer the Program. The percentage of funding retained for County administrative 
costs will be adjusted in future years based on the total staff time required to administer this Program. There are sufficient 
funds in the Solid Waste Program’s annual FY 2025-26 budget for this initial contribution.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
Approval of the MOU between the City of San Mateo and the County of San Mateo to establish a SB 1383 compliant 
procurement program is not a project subject to the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), because it can be seen 
with certainty that it will not cause a physical change in the environment. (Public Resources Code Section 21065.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Jack Johnson, Waste Management Supervisor
jjohnson@cityofsanmateo.org

MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING 
BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO AND THE COUNTY OF SAN MATEO FOR 
THE IMPLEMENTATION OF A SENATE BILL (SB) 1383 COMPLIANT PROCUREMENT 
PROGRAM
THIS MEMORANDUM OF UNDERSTANDING (MOU), entered into on this 31st day of 
January 2026, by and between the County of San Mateo, hereinafter called "the County" and the City of 
San Mateo, hereinafter called "the Jurisdiction";
W I T N E S S E T H:
WHEREAS, the State of California enacted Senate Bill (SB) 1383 (Chapter 395, Statutes of 
2016), which required the California Department of Resources Recycling and Recovery (CalRecycle) to 
adopt regulations to reduce organic waste disposal by 75 percent by 2025; and
WHEREAS, the SB 1383 regulations (Title 14, Division 7, Chapter 12, Article 12 of the 
California Code of Regulations) require jurisdictions to procure recovered organic waste products 
(ROWPs) to meet annual targets set by CalRecycle; and
WHEREAS, in 2021, the Jurisdiction, the County, and the San Mateo Resource Conservation 
District (RCD) launched a program to comply with SB 1383 requirements (Program), which between 
2021 and 2024 procured and applied 21,000 tons of cost-reduced compost for local farms and 
rangelands, contributing to countywide procurement targets; and
WHEREAS, the Program will conclude in 2026 unless a new agreement is established; and 
WHEREAS, all parties have recognized the value of continuing the Program to support SB 1383 
compliance, leverage prior CalRecycle SB 1383 Local Assistance Grant investments and advance 
progress toward procurement targets; and
WHEREAS, the Jurisdiction shall determine its annual funding contribution to the Program and 
shall receive procurement credit in proportion to its contribution; and 
WHEREAS, the County has agreed to administer the Program on the Jurisdiction’s behalf to 
reduce collective staff time, duplicative administrative burdens and costs in exchange for an 
administrative fee; and
WHEREAS, the Jurisdiction will remain ultimately responsible for meeting its annual ROWP 
procurement targets.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS HEREBY AGREED BY THE PARTIES HERETO AS FOLLOWS:
1. Services to be Performed by the County
A. Program Administration
a.
The County shall serve as Program administrator, overseeing the agreement with the 
RCD to ensure coordination and successful implementation of the Program, in 
39 of 1110

--- page 40 ---
accordance with SB 1383 requirements. In this role, the County shall provide contract 
management, fiscal oversight, and coordination with participating jurisdictions. 
b. The County shall serve as the direct service provider and allocate procurement credits in 
proportion to the Jurisdiction’s contribution to the Program.
c.
The County shall execute an intergovernmental agreement with the RCD by February 28, 
2026, to implement the Program and shall maintain the agreement for the duration of this 
MOU.
B. Program Funding
a.
The County shall invoice the Jurisdiction for an initial contribution, set by the 
Jurisdiction, for an amount not to exceed $300,000, by February 28, 2026. The initial 
contribution will fund the Program for calendar year 2026. Invoices may be issued after 
this date or divided into multiple billings if necessary to align with the Jurisdiction’s 
fiscal schedule. Of the total invoiced amount, the County will retain between 2% and 4% 
of the total amount to offset staff time costs required to administer the Program. The 
County will track all administrative costs associated with the Program and will furnish 
data to the Jurisdiction justifying the final administrative rate that will be retained. The 
rate will not exceed 4% in the first year of implementation. The percentage of funding 
retained for County administrative costs will be adjusted in future years based on the total 
staff time required to administer this Program. 
b. The County shall provide a Program Authorization Letter Template to the Jurisdiction, by 
September 1, 2026, and annually thereafter, for use in authorizing its continued 
participation in the Program for the next calendar year. In this letter, the Jurisdiction shall 
specify its total contribution, the anticipated tons of ROWP credit to be received, and the 
percent of its contribution that the County will retain to offset Program administration 
costs. The letter shall be signed by an employee of the Jurisdiction who is authorized to 
commit and disburse funding to the Program. 
c.
The County shall invoice the Jurisdiction for its contribution to the Program as defined in 
the Authorization Letter no later than January 30, 2027, and annually thereafter. If no 
authorization letter is provided for a given calendar year, the County shall not invoice the 
Jurisdiction.
d. The County shall carry forward any unspent Program contribution for the Jurisdiction’s 
future use in the Program.
e.
Upon termination of this MOU, the County shall return any unspent contributions to the 
Jurisdiction within sixty (60) days.
C. Recordkeeping & Reporting
a.
The County shall provide the Jurisdiction with access to an online database containing all 
information related to Program activities, in compliance with SB 1383 recordkeeping and 
40 of 1110

--- page 41 ---
reporting requirements. The database shall clearly identify the ROWP procurement completed by 
the County on the Jurisdiction’s behalf under this MOU. 
b. The County shall provide the Jurisdiction with the information necessary to report 
procurement activities in its Electronic Annual Report (EAR) to CalRecycle. This 
information shall be provided no later than June 1 of each year.
c.
The County shall provide the Jurisdiction with written status reports twice per year on all 
Program activities.
D. Additional Work
a.
With written approval, the County may invoice the Jurisdiction for supplemental 
contributions to the Program, to increase the Jurisdiction’s procurement credit. Such 
approval shall specify the total funding amount and the portion allocated to Program 
activities and County administrative costs. Jurisdictions are not obliged to support 
additional work. A Jurisdiction choosing not to support additional work shall not be 
grounds for terminating this MOU. The Jurisdiction is not guaranteed any benefit from 
additional work for which it does not contribute.
b. With written approval, the County may invoice the Jurisdiction for contributions to 
additional projects or agreements undertaken on the Jurisdiction’s behalf to increase 
procurement credit or reduce the Jurisdiction’s procurement target set by CalRecycle. 
Such approval shall specify the total funding amount and the portion allocated to 
Program activities and County administrative costs.
2. Tasks to be Completed by the Jurisdiction
A. The Jurisdiction shall pay in full all invoices submitted by the County within forty-five (45) days 
of receipt.  
B. The Jurisdiction shall provide the Authorization Letter to the County by November 1, 2026, and 
annually thereafter, to authorize its continued participation in the Program for the upcoming 
calendar year. The letter shall specify the total contribution, the anticipated tons of ROWP credit 
to be received, and the percentage of its contribution that the County will retain to offset 
Program administration costs.
C. The Jurisdiction may authorize participation in the Program for one (1) year or for multiple 
consecutive calendar years, with mutual agreement from the County. A multi-year authorization 
shall specify the total annual contribution amount for each year covered and shall remain subject 
to annual adjustment of the County’s administrative cost percentage. The Jurisdiction may revise 
or rescind its authorization for any future year by providing written notice to the County no later 
than November 1 preceding the year for which the change is requested.
D. The Jurisdiction shall be responsible for completing and submitting its EAR to CalRecycle, 
reporting the information provided by the County.
41 of 1110

--- page 42 ---
E. The Jurisdiction shall pay any additional invoices it has approved in writing, within forty-five 
(45) days of receipt, for supplemental contributions to the Program or for additional projects and 
agreements undertaken by the County on the Jurisdiction’s behalf. 
3. Consideration
This MOU enables the continuation of the collaborative Program, jointly developed by the Jurisdiction, 
County, and RCD since 2021. By participating in this MOU, the Jurisdiction avoids the administrative 
and financial burden of creating and managing a separate contract or Program and allocating staff time, 
allowing the County on behalf of all the cities in San Mateo County to leverage economies of scale to 
reduce countywide SB 1383 procurement compliance costs.
In return, the Jurisdiction contributes funding to the Program and receives procurement credit in 
proportion to its contribution. This Program advances shared countywide goals by strengthening local 
food system resiliency, improving soil health, reducing climate impacts, and supporting long-term 
economic and environmental benefits across San Mateo County.
4. Relationship of Parties
It is expressly understood that this is an agreement between independent entities, the County and the 
Jurisdiction, and that no individual agency, employee, partnership, joint venture, or other relationship is 
established by this MOU. The intent of both the County and the Jurisdiction is to create an independent 
collaborative relationship.
5. Hold Harmless 
A. It is agreed that the County shall defend, hold harmless, and indemnify Jurisdiction and its officers, 
employees, agents, and servants from any and all claims, suits, or actions of every name, kind, and 
description brought by a third party which arise out of the terms and conditions of this MOU and 
which result from the acts or omissions of the County and/or its officers, employees, agents, and 
servants.
B. It is agreed that Jurisdiction shall defend, save, hold harmless, and indemnify the County and its 
officers, employees, agents, and servants from any and all claims, suits, or actions of every name, 
kind, and description brought by a third party which arise out of the terms and conditions of this 
MOU and which result from the acts or omissions of Jurisdiction and/or their respective officers, 
employees, agents and servants.
C. County shall defend, hold harmless, and indemnify Jurisdiction from and against any and all claims 
for wages, salaries, benefits, taxes, and all other withholdings and charges payable to, or in respect 
to, County’s representatives for services provided under this MOU.
D. Jurisdiction shall defend, hold harmless, and indemnify County from and against any and all claims 
for wages, salaries, benefits, taxes, and all other withholdings and charges payable to, or in respect 
to, Jurisdiction’s representatives for services provided under this MOU.
E. The duty of each party to defend, hold harmless, and indemnify the other as set forth herein shall 
include the duty to defend as set forth in Section 2778 of the California Civil Code.

F. In the event of concurrent negligence (or intentional/reckless acts) of Jurisdiction and/or their

damage to persons and/or property which arise out of the terms and conditions of this MOU shall be 
apportioned according to the California theory of comparative fault.

the provisions of this body of the MOU shall prevail. Any prior MOU, promises, negotiations, or 
representations between the parties not expressly stated in this document are not binding. Either party 
may request a modification to this MOU at any time. All subsequent modifications shall be in writing 
and will become effective when signed by all parties.

Jurisdiction of any assignments made in advance.

SUBJECT:
Underground Flow Equalization System Odor Control System Temporary Rental — Change Order 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an increase to the rental agreement with PureAir Filtration for the Underground Flow Equalization System (UFES) 
temporary odor control system rental in the amount of $22,000, for a total amount not to exceed $120,000; and authorize 
the Public Works Director to execute the change order in substantially the form presented. 
BACKGROUND:
The Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) oversees the operation of UFES, which is a critical component in San Mateo’s 
storm water and sewer system management plan to prevent sanitary sewer overflows along the Delaware corridor. To 
meet air quality emissions permit requirements during operation, the UFES facility is required to process air and gases 
through a foul air (FA) fume scrubber at all times. 
Due to damage to the existing FA system, the UFES facility could not be operated as constructed. A rental FA system was 
procured and installed in early November. The existing UFES FA system is manufactured by PureAir Filtration LLC, which is 
also the provider of the portable rented FA system, which is the same design as the approved, installed system. 
Staff recommends approving an increase to the rental agreement and authorizing the Public Works Director to execute the 
associated change order (Attachment 1) to keep the FA system in place until the installed system can be repaired and is 
functional, which is anticipated to be in late March 2026. The original rental was authorized on October 24, 2025, for an 
amount not to exceed $98,000. The additional funds in the amount of $22,000, for a revised budget of $120,000, will allow 
for the costs of the rental FA system through March 2026, and to allow for any needs beyond the quoted amount to cover 
any unforeseen costs such as additional work hours, mobilizations, repairs, or materials. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the WWTP operating budget in the Sewer Fund to cover the cost of this increase. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This change order is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Transportation Staffing Restructuring – Adoption
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to add an additional Engineering Manager position and reallocate an Engineering Technician II position 
to an Assistant/Associate Transportation Planner position. 
BACKGROUND:
Staff is proposing to restructure the Transportation Division to add capacity and flexibility in the Traffic Engineering, 
Transportation Planning, Land Development, Utility, and Right-of-Way Sections of the Public Works Department. This will 
allow staff to better deliver on two of Council’s priority initiatives in the recently adopted Strategic Plan, namely the 
Transportation and Housing initiatives.  In general, the restructuring would split the existing Transportation Division in two, 
creating a Transportation Division and a Development Services Division, with the former generally focused on 
transportation issues and the latter generally focused on development issues. Council is asked to approve a new 
Engineering Manager position and reallocate a vacant Engineering Technician II position to a new Assistant/Associate 
Transportation Planner to support the restructuring. 
Basis for Recommendation
Transportation planning and development review are central to the City’s ability to implement adopted policies, deliver 
capital projects, manage growth, and maintain day-to-day mobility and parking operations. Considering the scope and 
complexity of the City’s transportation responsibilities, the increasing pace of development and implementation driven by 
voter-approved and state-mandated requirements, and the staffing considerations necessary to support timely, 
coordinated, and effective service delivery, a restructuring is necessary to better align staffing resources with adopted 
priorities and evolving service demands.
Across the Active Transportation, Safety, Vehicle Congestion Management, and Parking Management categories of the 
2040 General Plan Circulation Element, the City has adopted more than 26 transportation-related policies. These policies, 
together with the City Strategic Plan’s Transportation priority initiative and multiple City Council FY 25-26 priorities 
focused on transportation, reflect a commitment to advancing a safe, efficient, and well-functioning transportation 
system. The City has also adopted multiple transportation plans that define an ambitious vision for safety and mobility, 
and in recent years has shifted its focus from planning to implementation, including projects such as the Delaware Safe 
Routes to School Project and the 3rd and Norfolk Bicycle and Pedestrian Improvements Project.  
The Transportation Planning section also plays a critical role in delivering and managing the City’s parking programs. Their 
efforts include customer service, data collection and analysis, consultant and contract oversight, and management of 
residential, downtown, and citywide parking systems. This work is essential to the City’s day-to-day operations, supporting 
effective management of parking in neighborhoods and businesses, maintaining orderly use of the public right-of-way, and 
49 of 1110

--- page 50 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
ensuring continued parking revenue collection, tracking, and forecasting. 
Land development review plays a critical role in ensuring that new projects are implemented safely, efficiently, and in 
alignment with adopted City policies and standards. Recent housing accountability and streamlining requirements 
constrain local discretionary ability and increase review demands for private development. Measure T has resulted in a 
significant increase in development applications. AB 130 and SB 330 are accelerating housing project review timelines and  
requiring staff to process applications, prepare reports, and coordinate reviews (including evaluating transportation 
impacts) more quickly to meet state-mandated timeframes. 
The City’s ability to deliver on these adopted priorities and timelines is increasingly constrained by limited staff capacity. 
The proposed restructuring will help align staff resources with existing commitments, strengthen implementation of 
transportation projects and land development review, and directly support City Council direction and community 
priorities. 
Proposed Restructuring
The Transportation Division currently consists of 16.5 full-time equivalent (FTE) staff, including the Engineering Manager 
(1.0 FTE) that oversees the division and the Traffic Engineering (5.0 FTE), Transportation Planning (2.5 FTE, all currently 
vacant), and Land Development, Utility, and Right-of-Way (8.0 FTE) Sections. The first two of these sections are primarily 
focused on the City’s management of the roadway system, including planning and policy development, multimodal 
network planning, traffic operations and signal coordination, speed management and traffic calming, transportation 
impact mitigation for development projects, construction traffic management, parking management, public outreach, safe 
routes to school program implementation, and interagency coordination. The latter section is primarily focused on the 
interface between private development and third parties and the public right-of-way and includes the Public Works-
focused aspects of reviewing private development applications, oversight and permitting of third-party utilities (including 
small wireless facilities) within the public right-of-way, and issuance of encroachment permits for events or work within 
the right-of-way. 
Following the proposed restructure, the Transportation Division would split into two divisions – a Transportation Division 
which includes Traffic Engineering and Transportation Planning, and a Development Services Division which includes Land 
Development, Utility, and Right-of-Way. This restructuring requires approval of a new Engineering Manager position that 
would oversee the Development Services Division, with the existing Engineering Manager position overseeing the 
Transportation Division.  In addition, a vacant Engineering Technician role within the Land Development, Utility, and Right-
of-Way section would be reallocated to create a second Assistant/Associate Transportation Planner position within the 
Transportation Planning section. With Council’s January 12, 2026, approval to revise the Principal Transportation Planner 
classification to a Senior/Principal Transportation Planner, this restructuring would revise the Transportation Planning 
section to consist of two Assistant/Associate Transportation Planners and a Senior/Principal Transportation Planner.  
A dedicated Transportation Engineering Manager and a revised Transportation Planning section will allow staff to better 
deliver on Council’s transportation priorities discussed above.  Replacing the current structure (Engineering Technician and 
part-time per-diem Safe Routes to School Coordinator) with a second Assistant/Associate Transportation Planner position 
consolidates responsibilities into full-time roles capable of independently managing programs and project delivery. This 
structure improves overall capacity and responsiveness while providing flexibility for workload balancing, mentoring, and 
long-term program continuity. Listed below are the primary efforts advanced by the Transportation Planning section.
Planning Efforts
•
Complete Streets Plan
•
Parking and Transportation Planning Policy Development and Evaluation (Parking, Transportation Planning, 
Transportation Demand Management, Vehicle Miles Traveled)
•
City Transportation Planning Representation (SamTrans, San Mateo County Safe Routes to School Program, C/CAG)
•
Transportation Grant Identification and Development
•
Micromobility and Shared Mobility Policy and Program Development
•
Transportation planning items for the Development Review Board, Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission, 
50 of 1110

--- page 51 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
and City Council
•
Multimodal transportation system planning and network development
•
Update of transportation-related impact fees
Safe Routes to School 
•
Strategic Plan implementation
•
Capacity-building and City support of school district initiatives
•
Support for identifying quick build improvements near schools 
•
Bike Rodeo event support
•
Distribution of safety equipment, including bike locks and helmets and lighted stop paddles, through school 
partners
Parking Programs 
•
Customer service for parking programs (Downtown, Residential, and Citywide) 
•
Oversight of parking consultants and contracts
•
Parking counts/study/analysis
•
Residential Parking Permit Program (RPPP) program management and permitting 
•
Downtown parking analysis and data collection 
•
Maintain Downtown parking occupancy systems and ensure data is accurate and available to the public 
•
Curb marking requests 
•
Assist with parking policy evaluation and development 
•
Curb management program, including evaluation of loading zones and trash management practices
•
Track and apply for grants related to parking programs 
•
Bicycle parking infrastructure
•
Parking Revenue Allocation/Forecast
Capital Project Delivery
These are primarily delivered by the Traffic section, but are supported, especially when it comes to planning, outreach, and 
engagement, by the Transportation Planning section. 
•
19th Avenue/Fashion Island Multimodal Improvement Project 
•
Peninsula Avenue Complete Streets Multimodal Project
•
Citywide Traffic Calming Program
•
28th Avenue Gap Closure
•
Hillsdale/Alameda De Las Pulgas Signal
•
Bike San Mateo Program
•
Quick Build Mobility Program
•
Hillsdale High School/31st Avenue Pedestrian Safety Improvement Project
A dedicated Development Services Engineering Manager offers the following benefits to the City’s current level of service:
•
Maintain timely and predictable reviews despite the growing volume of permit applications.
•
Provide consistent oversight and quality control to ensure adherence to standards as permitting processes and 
regulatory requirements continue to evolve.
•
Foster proactive compliance among staff and external stakeholders.
•
Strengthen coordination across departments, developers, utility providers, contractors, and other permit 
applicants, to support efficient project delivery. 
•
Expand capacity to provide clear, responsive communication to developers, contractors, and other interested 
stakeholders. 
•
Expand ability to support regional coordination by engaging with local and regional partners to stay informed 
about evolving requirements and promote alignment and collaboration across municipalities. 
•
Support the development of a Comprehensive Construction Management Plan for Measure T, which would 
establish a consistent Citywide framework for managing cumulative impacts from simultaneous construction 
51 of 1110

--- page 52 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
impacts associated with increased development activity, and encouraging coordination amongst developers. 
Based on the above, staff recommends Council adopt the attached Resolution to add an additional Engineering Manager 
position and reallocate an Engineering Technician II position to establish an additional Assistant/Associate Transportation 
Planner position.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
The expected impact of this change on the General Fund is $81,000 annually. Any budget necessary to fill positions in the 
current fiscal year will be absorbed within the department’s operating budget and savings from departmental vacancies. 
Budget to fund these positions in future fiscal years will be requested as part of the annual budget development process.
This budget impact assumes no chargebacks to capital or private development projects. It is forecasted that a minimum of 
50% of this amount will be charged annually to capital or private development projects. If the City pursues greater cost 
recovery for its parking programs in the future, the net impact to the General Fund could decrease further, as one of the 
Assistant/Associate Transportation’s primary roles is support of the City’s parking programs. 
The following assumptions were made to estimate the budget impact: 
•
The new Engineering Manager (1.0 FTE) and Assistant/Associate Transportation Planner (1.0 FTE) positions are 
conservatively set at top step (step 5). The Principal Transportation Planner will be underfilled by a Senior 
Transportation Planner. As a result, the actual costs for these positions may be less than budgeted.
•
The Engineering Technician II (1.0 FTE) position is being reallocated to establish the second Assistant/Associate 
Transportation Planner position. Salary savings from eliminating the per-diem Safe Routes to School Coordinator 
position will help to offset the increased costs of the reallocation.  
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The creation or reallocation of positions is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative 
activity that will not result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
