SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant On-Call Consulting Services — Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 4 to the Professional Consultant Services Agreement with Carollo Engineers, Inc. for Wastewater 
Treatment Plant on-call consulting services in the amount of $250,000, for a new not-to-exceed amount of $1,675,000; 
and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendment in substantially the form presented. 
BACKGROUND:
The City is currently under a Regional Water Quality Control Board Cease-and-Desist Order requiring improvements to its 
sewer collection system and Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) to eliminate sanitary sewer overflows and 
unauthorized discharges of untreated or partially treated wastewater to San Francisco Bay. The Clean Water Program 
(CWP) includes several capital improvements that are currently under construction and intended to comply with the 
Cease-and-Desist Order. 
As part of the CWP, the City has identified capital improvements needed to maintain and/or improve the WWTP reliability, 
performance, and safety (Projects). The agreements for on-call professional services provide support for the Projects and 
maintenance and improvement projects implemented through the WWTP’s Operations and Maintenance groups. The 
required on-call professional services include, but are not limited to, alternatives analyses and engineering studies, design 
services, bid period services, engineering services during construction, and operational support. 
On August 15, 2022, City Council approved agreements with two consulting firms; Carollo Engineers, Inc. (Carollo) and 
Brown and Caldwell, to provide professional consulting services in support of the City’s planned rehabilitation of the 
existing WWTP. Each agreement authorized a total of up to $400,000 for services performed on an on-call basis. The 
agreement terms are three years, with the option for two additional one-year extensions and an additional budget of up to 
$100,000 for each year extended. A summary of Carollo’s Task Order history and current status is provided below: 
1.
Carollo was awarded Task 1 – 3 Water Pumping Improvements and Task 1A – Ferric Chloride to Combined Sludge 
under the agreement.
2.
On August 5, 2024, City Council approved Amendment No. 1 to the agreement, increasing the amount by $450,000 
for a revised total agreement amount of $850,000. Carollo was awarded Task 1B - Engineering Services for Spent 
Backwash Pumps and Ferric Chloride Day Tank and Transfer Pump, Task 1C – Additional Potholing, and Task 2 - 
WAS Thickening Alternatives Analysis Project. 
3.
On June 25, 2025, City Council approved Amendment No. 2 to increase the agreement amount by $475,000 for a 
revised total agreement amount of $1,325,000. Carollo was awarded Task Order 3 - Dewatering System 
Centrifuges 1 and 2 Replacement Project. On August 31, 2025, the first one-year extension option was exercised 
through Amendment No. 3 to the agreement, increasing compensation by $100,000, for a new revised agreement 
54 of 1110

--- page 55 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
total of $1,425,000.
In December 2025, the 3-Water Pumping and Ferric Chloride to Combined Sludge Line Project was awarded for 
construction. Carollo will provide engineering services during construction for this phase. For Carollo to provide 
engineering services for these tasks, an increase of $250,000 to the on-call agreement is recommended to complete this 
work and address any potential unforeseen issues in the project. This would result in the revised total agreement amount 
of $1,675,000. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds available in the CWP WWTP Annual Major Components Project (46t090) budget. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The proposed action is exempt from environmental review under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), since 
entering on-call professional services agreements is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct 
or indirect significant physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).) In addition, on June 6, 
2016, the City Council certified a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) (SCH No. 2015032006), 
which serves as the primary environmental compliance document for implementation of the Clean Water Program. On July 
16, 2018, the City approved Addendum 1 to the Final PEIR, and on April 15, 2019, approved Addendum 2. Any resultant 
capital improvement or operations and maintenance-related projects resulting from the on-call professional consulting 
services shall be reviewed on an individual basis or evaluated for consistency with the PEIR and Addenda. 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

SUBJECT:
Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action Projects Package III — Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Award a professional services agreement to HDR Engineering, Inc. to provide Engineering Services During Construction 
(Phase 3) for Immediate Action Projects Package-III in an amount not to exceed $332,916; establish a contingency reserve 
of $50,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreement in the substantially the form presented and 
approve any necessary change orders within the approved contingency amount.
BACKGROUND:
The City is currently under a Regional Water Quality Control Board Cease and Desist Order requiring improvements to its 
sewer collection system and Wastewater Treatment Plant (WWTP) to eliminate sanitary sewer overflows and 
unauthorized discharges of untreated or partially treated wastewater to San Francisco Bay. The Clean Water Program 
(CWP) includes several capital improvements that are currently under construction and intended to comply with the 
Cease-and-Desist Order. 
The Wastewater Treatment Plant Immediate Action Projects Package III (IAP-III) Project is a component of the CWP and 
includes improvements to the Wastewater Treatment Plant’s (WWTP) effluent pump station, site waste pump station, and 
plant-wide mechanical and electrical equipment, as well as replacement of effluent flowmeters and sludge heating and 
recirculation piping. The project is part of the CWP’s efforts to modernize the existing WWTP and maintain safe and 
reliable operations. 
IAP-III engineering services were planned to be executed in three phases: Phase 1 - Project Analyses, Phase 2 - Detailed 
Design and Bid Phase Services, and Phase 3 - Engineering Services during Construction. 
On September 16, 2019, City Council awarded a professional services agreement to HDR Engineering, Inc. (HDR) to provide 
design and engineering services for the IAP-III project.  However, due to challenges with the project, an oversight was 
made where the agreement with HDR lapsed. Therefore, a new agreement with HDR needs to be executed to complete 
the IAP-III project. Below is a detailed timeline of the agreement and amendments with HDR and its current status:
1.
On September 16, 2019, Council awarded the professional services agreement to HDR to proceed with Phase 1 - 
Project Analyses for an amount of $217,932. 
2.
On October 19, 2020, City Council approved Amendment No. 1 for HDR to complete Phase 2 – Detailed Design for 
an amount of $801,563. The total contract amount was $1,019,495. During Phase 2, the City issued two more 
amendments to include scope for additional improvements and to optimize coordination with the larger WWTP 
Upgrade & Expansion project, which is already under construction. Both Amendments No. 2 and 3 were no-cost-
58 of 1110

--- page 59 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
increase amendments as HDR estimated they could complete the additional scope within the existing contract amount. 
3.
In April 2022, the IAP-III Project was advertised for competitive bids, with only a single bid submitted that was 
significantly higher than HDR's engineering estimate. On June 20, 2022, City Council adopted a resolution to reject 
the bid and authorize staff to negotiate with the bidder. A contingency-funded Amendment No. 4 was executed 
for HDR to provide support during negotiations, update the design as needed, and support the bid phase for a 
second time for an amount of $80,000. The total contract amount was $1,099,495. In February 2023, the revised 
contract documents were advertised and on May 15, 2023, the construction contract was awarded to the 
responsive low bidder.
4.
On 28 June 2023, City Council approved Amendment No. 5 for HDR to complete Phase 3 - Engineering Services 
during Construction for services for an amount of $422,000. The project term was scheduled to be completed by 
April 30, 2025. At this time, the cumulative agreement total was $1,521,495 and contingency reserve was $42,000.
 
Unforeseen site conditions and contractor setbacks has caused the project to be delayed. HDR’s agreement was not 
renewed in response to the delay in the required time frame, and the agreement expired by its terms. Due to this, the 
remaining project funds and contingency have expired. 
For HDR to provide engineering services during construction for the remainder of the construction period (estimated to be 
completed in July 2026), a new agreement with an expiration of December 31, 2026, for an amount of $332,916 and 
contingency reserve of $50,000 is recommended to complete the project and cover any additional unforeseen issues as 
work progresses. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds available to fund this agreement in the WWTP IAP Package III Project (46t010). 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The proposed action is exempt from environmental review under the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), since 
entering on-call professional services agreements is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct 
or indirect significant physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).) In addition, on June 6, 
2016, the City Council certified a Final Programmatic Environmental Impact Report (Final PEIR) (SCH No. 2015032006), 
which serves as the primary environmental compliance document for implementation of the Clean Water Program. On July 
16, 2018, the City approved Addendum 1 to the Final PEIR, and on April 15, 2019, Addendum 2. Any resultant capital 
improvement or operations and maintenance-related projects resulting from the on-call professional consulting services 
shall be reviewed on an individual basis or evaluated for consistency with the PEIR and Addenda. 
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

AGREEMENT WITH HDR ENGINEERING, INC.
FOR PROFESSIONAL CONSULTANT SERVICES
FOR IMMEDIATE ACTION PROJECTS PACKAGE III PROJECT
This agreement, made and entered into this day of__________ by and between the CITY OF SAN 
MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California, hereinafter referred to as 
"CITY", and HDR ENGINEERING, INC., a Nebraska corporation, hereinafter referred to as "CONSULTANT":
RECITALS:
A.
CITY desires certain design professional services hereinafter described.
B.
CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these design professional services by reason of 
its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT has offered to provide the 
required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein.
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows:
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF PROJECT
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is described in Exhibit A, 
Scope of Services, attached and incorporated by reference.
SECTION 2 - RELIANCE UPON PROFESSIONAL SKILL OF CONSULTANT
It is mutually understood and agreed by and between the parties hereto that CONSULTANT is skilled in 
the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this Agreement and that CITY 
relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in the most skillful manner, and CONSULTANT 
agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's work by CITY does not operate as a release 
of CONSULTANT from said obligation.
SECTION 3 - PROJECT COORDINATION/STAFFING
3.1
CITY
MATTHEW FABRY, PUBLIC WORKS DIRECTOR, will be the representative of CITY for all purposes under 
this Agreement. THOMAS RUARK is hereby designated as the PROJECT MANAGER and shall supervise the day-to-
day progress and execution of this Agreement.
3.2
CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT's services are unique and personal.  CONSULTANT shall not assign or transfer its interest 
or obligation under this Agreement without the CITY's written consent.  CONSULTANT shall not subcontract its 
duties under this Agreement without the CITY's written consent.

--- page 62 ---

Rev 7/21
CONSULTANT shall assign a single PROJECT DIRECTOR to have overall responsibility for the progress and 
execution of this Agreement for CONSULTANT. MALIKA RAMANATHAN is hereby designated as the PROJECT 
DIRECTOR for CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT shall assign a PROJECT COORDINATOR to represent CONSULTANT during the day-to-day 
work on the Project. JILLIAN DRESSLER is hereby designated as the PROJECT COORDINATOR for CONSULTANT.
CONSULTANT shall assign additional key personnel as follows:
Electrical Engineer – Gustavo Iniguez Vazquez
Electrical Engineer – Robb McComb

Project Coordinator – Tom Hoffman
In addition, CONSULTANT is specifically authorized to subcontract with the following subconsulting firms 
to assist in providing the services required by this Agreement:
___________________________
___________________________
___________________________
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all sub-consultants necessary for 
performance of the CONSULTANT’s scope of work.  CONSULTANT shall manage, evaluate, and incorporate sub-
consultants work into the project as necessary.
All work on this project shall be accomplished by the above-named CONSULTANT's personnel and 
subconsultants.  CONSULTANT shall not substitute personnel or subconsultants without the CITY's written 
consent.
SECTION 4 - INDEPENDENT CONTRACTOR
It is expressly agreed that in the performance of the professional services required under this 
Agreement, CONSULTANT shall at all times be considered an independent contractor as defined in Labor Code 
Section 3353, under control of the CITY as to the result of the work but not the means by which the result is 
accomplished.  Nothing herein shall be construed to make CONSULTANT an agent or employee of CITY while 
providing services under this Agreement.
SECTION 5 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT
CONSULTANT's services shall be furnished as described below and as more particularly described in 
Exhibit A, Scope of Services.  Any changes in the Scope of Services must be approved in advance, in writing, by 
the Public Works Director.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and coordination of all 
studies, reports, designs, drawings, specifications, and other items furnished by CONSULTANT under this 
62 of 1110

--- page 63 ---
Professional Services – Estero (PW)
Page 3 of 18
Rev 7/21
Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in 
its studies, reports, designs, drawings, specifications, and other items.
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this Agreement.
All documents, drawings and specifications relating the project prepared by CONSULTANT or 
CONSULTANT’s approved sub-consultants shall be approved, signed, and sealed by professional engineers duly 
registered in the State of California for each required discipline.  The Contract Documents prepared by 
CONSULTANT shall provide for a completed project that conforms to all applicable national, state and local 
construction codes and ordinances, building, fire, electrical, plumbing and occupancy codes and standards.
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform the 
services of CONSULTANT.  No subconsultant of CONSULTANT will be recognized by CITY as such; rather, all 
subconsultants are deemed to be the agents of CONSULTANT, and CONSULTANT agrees to be responsible for 
their performance.  CONSULTANT shall give its personal attention to the fulfillment of the provisions of this 
Agreement by all of its employees and subconsultants, if any, and shall keep the work under its control.  If any 
employee or subconsultant of CONSULTANT fails or refuses to carry out the provisions of this Agreement or 
appears to be incompetent or to act in a disorderly or improper manner, he or she shall be discharged 
immediately from the work under this Agreement on demand of CITY.
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in Municipal Code 
Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer Price Index escalator.
CONSULTANT shall furnish CITY with every reasonable opportunity for CITY to ascertain that the services 
of CONSULTANT are being performed in accordance with the requirement and intentions of this Agreement.
SECTION 6 - DUTIES OF CITY
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project.
CITY shall examine all documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.
SECTION 7 - TERM, PROGRESS AND COMPLETION
This Agreement shall commence upon execution of the Agreement.  The Parties agree that the 
Agreement shall be considered effective from August 1, 2025, to December 30, 2026. 
SECTION 8 - PAYMENT
Payment shall be made by the CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full performances 
of the services and any reimbursable expenses set forth in this Agreement, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a 
fee based on verified time and materials not to exceed $332,916.00. Requests for payments shall be itemized 
and correspond to the various items of work described in Exhibit A and shall be based on the rate and cost 
schedule set forth in Exhibit C.

Rev 7/21
Payment for extra work or changes in the work will not be made unless for work authorized in advance 
in writing by the PUBLIC WORKS DIRECTOR.  Prior to commencing such extra work or changes, CONSULTANT and 
CITY shall agree upon an estimated not to exceed cost for such extra work.  In no event shall CONSULTANT be 
paid for additional work that is necessary because of CONSULTANT's errors or oversights.
CONSULTANT shall maintain complete and accurate records as to the number of hours worked by 
persons and the direct costs incurred during each phase under this Agreement.  All such records shall be 
maintained on a generally accepted accounting basis and shall be clearly identified and readily accessible.  
CONSULTANT shall provide free access to such books and records to the CITY at all times, shall permit the CITY 
to examine and audit those books and records, shall permit the CITY to make copies of those books and records, 
and shall permit the CITY to inspect all work data, documents, proceedings, and activities related to this 
Agreement for a period of three (3) years from the date of final payment under this Agreement.  All accounting 
records shall provide an understandable breakdown of costs charged to this Agreement.  All records 
encompassed by this subparagraph, as well as supporting documentation, shall be kept separate from other 
CONSULTANT documents and records and shall be maintained by CONSULTANT for three (3) years after 
CONSULTANT's receipt of final payment under this Agreement.
CONSULTANT shall bill the CITY within thirty (30) days for all compensable activity on the project.
CITY shall pay CONSULTANT within thirty (30) days of receipt of billings.  If CITY disagrees with any 
portion of a billing, the CITY shall promptly notify CONSULTANT of the disagreement, and the CITY and the 
CONSULTANT shall attempt to resolve the disagreement.  CITY's payment of any amounts shall not constitute a 
waiver of any disagreement.
SECTION 9 - CHANGES IN WORK
CITY may order changes in scope or character of work, either decreasing or increasing the amount of 
CONSULTANT's services by amending this Agreement.  In the event that such changes are ordered, CONSULTANT 
shall be entitled to full compensation for all work performed prior to receipt of notice of change.  In no event 
shall CITY be liable for payment unless the amount of such extra compensation shall first have been agreed to in 
writing by CITY.
In the event that changes are ordered pursuant to this section, the schedule for progress and 
completion in Section 7 of this Agreement and compensation in Section 8 of this Agreement shall be adjusted by 
negotiation between CONSULTANT and CITY, subject to approval, if necessary, by the San Mateo City Council.
SECTION 10 - CONFLICT OF INTEREST
CONSULTANT understands that its professional responsibility is solely to CITY.  CONSULTANT covenants 
that it presently has no interest, and will not acquire any interest, direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which 
would conflict in any manner or degree with the performance of the services under this Agreement.  
CONSULTANT shall not knowingly, and shall take reasonable steps to ensure that, it does not employ a person 
having such an interest in the performance of this Agreement.  If after employment of a person, CONSULTANT 
discovers that it has employed a person with a direct or indirect interest that would conflict with its 
performance of this Agreement, CONSULTANT shall promptly sever the employment relationship.

SECTION 11 - TERMINATION
A.
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY may, by ten days 
written notice to CONSULTANT, terminate this Agreement in whole or in part at any time, either for CITY'S 
convenience or because of the failure of CONSULTANT to fulfill its agreement obligations.  Upon receipt and 
within ten days of such notice CONSULTANT shall:
1.
Immediately discontinue all services affected (unless the notice directs otherwise); and
2.
Deliver to CITY all data, documents, reports, estimates, summaries and such other information 
and materials as may have been accumulated by CONSULTANT in performing this Agreement, whether 
completed or in progress.
B.
If the termination is for the convenience of CITY, CONSULTANT shall be compensated for all authorized 
work performed prior to notification of termination but no amount shall be allowed for anticipated profit on 
unperformed services.
C.
If, after any notice of termination for failure to fulfill agreement obligations, it is determined by a court 
or other arbitrator that CONSULTANT had not so failed, the termination shall be deemed to have been effected 
for the convenience of CITY.  In such event, adjustment in the agreement price shall be made as provided in 
paragraph (B) of this section.
D.
CONSULTANT may terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the project upon sixty (60) days 
written notice to CITY, but only in the event of substantial failure of performance by CITY or in the event CITY 
abandons or indefinitely postpones the Project.
E.
Upon termination of this Agreement or suspension of work on the project by either CITY or 
CONSULTANT, all duties of CITY and CONSULTANT as set forth in Sections 5 and 6 herein above shall terminate.
SECTION 12 - STATUS OF REPORTS
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although instruments 
of professional service, are and shall be the property of the CITY, whether the project for which they are made is 
executed or not.  All documents furnished to CONSULTANT by the CITY and all reports and supportive data 
prepared by the CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of this Agreement are the CITY's property 
and shall be delivered to the CITY upon the completion of CONSULTANT's services or at the CITY's written 
request. All reports, information, data, and exhibits prepared or assembled by CONSULTANT in connection with 
the performance of its services pursuant to this Agreement are confidential until released by the CITY to the 
public, and the CONSULTANT shall not make any of these documents or information available to any individual 
or organization not employed by the CONSULTANT or the CITY without the written consent of the CITY before 
any such release.

SECTION 13 - INSURANCE
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit D to this 
Agreement.
SECTION 14 - NONASSIGNABILITY
Both parties hereto recognize that this Agreement is for the personal services of CONSULTANT and 
cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written consent of CITY.
SECTION 15 - INDEMNITY
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY and the Estero Municipal Improvement
District, their elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss,
liability, damage, and expense arising out of CONSULTANT’s negligence, recklessness or willful misconduct in the
performance of its design professional services under this Agreement. To the maximum extent permitted by law
and Civil Code section 2782.8, CONSULTANT agrees to defend CITY and the Estero Municipal Improvement
District, their elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents against any such claims.
SECTION 16 - WAIVERS
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such term, 
covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of any other 
term, condition, ordinance, or law.  The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or other money which 
may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding breach or violation by the 
other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any applicable law or ordinance.
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEYS FEES
Attorney fees in an amount not exceeding $85 per hour per attorney, and in total amount not exceeding 
$5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to 
enforce the provisions of this Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable 
whether in the trial court, appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, 
appeals, or actions.  It is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than 
$5000 for attorney’s fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.
SECTION 18 - NONDISCRIMINATION
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with applicable 
regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its subconsultants shall 
discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national origin, ancestry, physical 
handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a bona fide occupational 
qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act.

SECTION 19 - MEDIATION
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be agreed to by 
the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one name from mediators 
listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the California State Board of Mediation and Conciliation, 
or other agreed upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw.
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation and making 
a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once commenced by a meeting 
with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but not more than 60 days, unless the 
maximum time is extended by the parties.
SECTION 20 - LITIGATION
CONSULTANT shall testify at CITY's request if litigation is brought against CITY in connection with 
CONSULTANT's services under this Agreement.  Unless the action is brought by CONSULTANT, or is based upon 
CONSULTANT's wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate CONSULTANT for preparation for testimony, testimony, and 
travel at CONSULTANT's standard hourly rates at the time of actual testimony.
SECTION 21 - NOTICES
All notices hereunder shall be given in writing and mailed, postage prepaid, addressed as follows:
To CITY:

City of San Mateo

SECTION 22 - AGREEMENT CONTAINS ALL UNDERSTANDINGS; AMENDMENTS
This document represents the entire and integrated agreement between CITY and CONSULTANT and 
supersedes all prior negotiations, representations, and agreements, either written or oral.
This document may be amended only by written instrument, signed by both CITY and CONSULTANT.

SECTION 24 - SEVERABILITY
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise unenforceable by a 
court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full force and 
effect.
SECTION 25 - GOVERNING LAW
This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
SECTION 26 - VENUE
In the event of litigation, venue will be in the County of San Mateo.

EXHIBIT D
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE 
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate 
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or 
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease. 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT.
Other Insurance Provisions
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions:
Additional Insured Status
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).

Primary Coverage
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.
Notice of Cancellation
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City.
Waiver of Subrogation
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.  
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.  
Acceptability of Insurers
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City.
Verification of Coverage
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
January 26, 2026 
SUBJECT:
On-Call Consultant Services for Transportation Analysis Report Preparation — Amendment 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 2 to the agreements with Kittelson and Associates, Inc., AECOM Technical Services, Inc., Steer 
Davies and Gleave, and Fehr and Peers to extend each agreement term through June 30, 2028, including up to one 
optional one-year extension, and to update the payment rates; increase Kittelson and Associates agreement by $200,000 
for a total not-to-exceed amount of $600,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the amendments in 
substantially the form presented and execute any future term extensions or amendments that do not change the not-to-
exceed amount of the agreements. 
BACKGROUND:
The Public Works and Community Development Departments require consultant services to prepare transportation impact 
analyses (TIAs) and review transportation demand management (TDM) plans for private development projects. Given the 
high volume of private development projects, the potential for multiple analyses to occur simultaneously, and the need to 
maintain an efficient entitlement process, staff initiated a competitive Request for Qualifications (RFQ) process in August 
2019 to establish on-call consultant teams.
In January 2020, the City Council approved 2.5-year agreements with four firms—AECOM, Fehr & Peers, Kittelson and 
Associates, and Steer Davies and Gleave—for a not-to-exceed amount of $250,000 per agreement to provide these 
services. Staff have been satisfied with the performance of these on-call consultants. In 2022 and 2023, new agreements 
were executed with each of the four firms to renew the services and extend the term through June 30, 2024.
On June 17, 2024, to ensure uninterrupted transportation analysis services for private development projects, Council 
approved Amendment No. 1, extending the existing agreements for an additional two years and increasing the not-to-
exceed amount per agreement by $150,000 (to a maximum of $400,000).
With the adoption of the 2040 General Plan and the passing of Measure T, staff initially anticipated a revised TIA 
development and review process in which developers would submit TIAs for peer review by the City, thereby reducing 
reliance on on-call consultants for full TIA preparation. However, recent changes in state housing laws have streamlined 
approvals for certain housing projects, imposing tighter decision timelines on local agencies from the date a planning 
application is submitted. These constraints shift additional responsibility and risk to local agencies. Concurrently, the City 
has experienced a significant influx of planning applications, with approximately 5,000 housing units currently in the 
pipeline, including several large projects exceeding 1,000 units.
To ensure that transportation analysis services for private development planning applications continue without 
interruption, Public Works recommends executing Amendment No. 2 to extend the existing agreements with all four 
87 of 1110

--- page 88 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
consultants for an additional two years (through June 30, 2028), updating the payment rates, and increasing the not-to-
exceed amount for the Kittelson and Associates agreement by $200,000, as it is the only firm currently forecasted to 
exhaust its funding before the end of the extended term.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Each consultant will be issued a blanket purchase order in alignment with the total not-to-exceed amount. The task orders 
will be charged to the planning application trust accounts, which are funded by the project developer, or to specific project 
accounts. All consultant costs will be charged to the appropriate account and are fully recovered.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because it can be seen with certainty that there is no possibility that extending 
agreements for professional transportation review services may have a significant effect on the environment. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15061(b)(3).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Amendment No. 2 – Kittelson and Associates
Att 2 – Amendment No. 2 – AECOM 
Att 3 – Amendment No. 2 – Steer Davies 
Att 4 – Amendment No. 2 – Fehr and Peers

AMENDMENT NO. 2 TO THE AGREEMENT 
BETWEEN THE CITY OF SAN MATEO AND 
KITTELSON AND ASSOCIATES FOR
ON-CALL TRANSPORTATION ANALYSIS REPORT PREPARATION
($250,000 [Original Amount] $150,000 [Amendment No. 1]
$200,000 [Amendment No. 2])
This Amendment is made and entered into this day of _______ 2026, by and between the CITY 
OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California ("CITY"), and 
KITTELSON AND ASSOCIATES, INC. ("CONSULTANT").

Analysis Report Preparation Services (“Agreement”) on January 23, 2020; and
WHEREAS, on July 1, 2024, CITY and CONSULTANT executed Amendment No. 1 to extend 
the term of agreement to June 30, 2026, and include additional compensation in the amount of 
$150,000, for a total amount of $400,000; and
WHEREAS, CITY and CONSULTANT wish to amend the Agreement to extend the term of the 
agreement and include additional compensation in the amount of $200,000, for a revised total 
agreement amount of $600,000.

1.

“The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence January 22, 2020, 
and be completed on June 30, 2028, with an option for a one-year extension.”
2.
Section 5, “Payment,” of the Agreement is amended to read:
“In consideration for the full performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY 
agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee in an amount not to exceed $600,000, pursuant to 
rates stated in Exhibit B to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference.”
3.
Exhibit B, attached and incorporated by reference, hereby replaces Exhibit B of the 
Agreement in its entirety.
4.
The remaining terms of the Agreement remain in full force and effect.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  13
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-6121
File ID: 25-10780
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Parks and Recreation Department
MEETING DATE:
January 26, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Citywide Tree Planting Services — Bid Rejection
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to reject all bids received for the Citywide Tree Planting Services Project.
BACKGROUND:
On October 24, 2025, a Notice Inviting Sealed Bids was posted on the City’s website for Citywide Tree Planting Services.  A 
total of three (3) bids were received by the submission deadline on November 14, 2025. The bid results are detailed below:
Bidder
Total Base Bid
New Image Landscape Company
$232,175.00
Loral Horticultural Services Inc. 
$240,675.00
West Coast Arborists 
$273,775.00
The Request for Proposals was posted to the City Bid Posting page; however, no posting was executed to provide 
notification via the local newspaper. A notice inviting sealed bids published in a local newspaper of general circulation is 
part of the Bid Notice Requirements of the City of San Mateo’s Purchasing Policy. Therefore, staff recommends rejecting all 
bids to allow for cancellation of the project. A subset of approximately 100 trees requested by homeowners through the 
Street Tree Planting program will be planted by April 2026.  
BUDGET IMPACT:
There is no budgetary impact associated with this administrative action. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This item is not a project subject to CEQA, because CEQA does not apply to projects that are disapproved. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15270.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.
ATTACHMENTS
Att 1 – Proposed Resolution 
STAFF CONTACT
Timothy Heartquist, Manager Arborist
theartquist@cityofsanmateo.org
650-522-7425
102 of 1110

--- page 103 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ___ (2026)
REJECTING ALL BIDS FOR THE CITY-WIDE TREE PLANTING PROJECT
WHEREAS, bids were received for planting approximately 250 trees at various locations throughout the 
City for the City-Wide Tree Planting Project (“Project”); and
WHEREAS, the City received three bids on November 14, 2025; and
WHEREAS, staff recommends the rejection of all bids. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that:
1.
This bid rejection  is not a project subject to CEQA because CEQA does not apply to projects that are 
disapproved.  (CEQA Guidelines Section 15270).
2.
In accordance with the provisions of San Mateo Municipal Code Section 3.60.040(a)(4), all bids received 
on November 14, 2025 for the Project are rejected.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report

Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR

TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Parks and Recreation Department
MEETING DATE:
January 26, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Lakeshore Park Batting Cage, Ball Fields, and Concession Building – Lease Agreement 
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve a five-year lease agreement with San Mateo National Little League for the batting cage, ball fields, and 
concessions building at Lakeshore Park, for the period of February 1, 2026, through December 31, 2031, and authorize the 
Parks and Recreation Director to sign the agreement on behalf of the City in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
San Mateo National Little League (SMNLL), which serves an average of approximately 350 youth participants per little 
league season, has been a long-time user of Martens Field in Lakeshore Park as its primary field and has operated a 
concession to help offset operating costs. In addition, SMNLL installed and continues to maintain the batting cage located 
at the field. The lease agreement (Att. 1) continues the current operating relationship with SMNLL for use of these 
facilities. This lease agreement enhances community engagement through sport, provides a popular recreational service 
for San Mateo youth, and contributes to the effective utilization of City Park facilities. The terms and conditions of the 
lease agreement are consistent with those of other youth sports organizations that operate in local parks, such as San 
Mateo American Little League at Trinta Park, and with San Mateo Youth Softball Association’s at Chanteloup Field in 
Beresford Park. 
Key Provisions of the Lease Agreement: 
•
Term and Premises: Five-year lease from February 1, 2026 through December 31, 2031, for use of the batting cage, 
concession building, and designated ball fields at Lakeshore Park. 
•
Use and Operations: The League is authorized to operate youth baseball practices, games, and concessions. The 
batting cage remains City property and must be made available to other City-approved youth sports organizations 
upon request, with League use having priority. Facility use, hours, amplified sound, and operations must comply 
with City regulations and good-neighbor practices. 
•
Maintenance and Repairs: The League is responsible for routine maintenance, cleaning, and upkeep of the batting 
cage, concession building, and grass infield areas. Major repairs remain the responsibility of the City, with required 
notification by the League. 
•
Risk Management and Termination: The League must meet City insurance and indemnification requirements. The 
City may terminate the lease for cause or for convenience with 30 days’ written notice. 
Concession Provisions: 
•
Exclusive Operator: The League is designated the exclusive concessionaire at Lakeshore Park pursuant to 
Government Code Section 51038. 
•
Permitted Sales and Pricing: Concession operations are limited to food, sundries, souvenirs, and similar items, with 
pricing required to remain consistent with prevailing market rates as determined by the City. 
•
Use of Proceeds: A portion of concession revenues must be reinvested into approved improvement projects at 
104 of 1110

--- page 105 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Lakeshore Park. 
•
Health and Safety Oversight: The City retains the right to suspend or close concession operations if health or safety 
concerns arise. 
Other Provisions: 
•
Security and Access: The League may not alter locks or security systems without City approval and must ensure 
City access at all times. 
•
Improvements and Compliance: All League-initiated improvements require prior City approval and must comply 
with applicable codes, permits, and prevailing wage requirements. 
•
Inspection and Enforcement: The City retains inspection rights to ensure compliance with lease terms and 
applicable laws. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
The League will be responsible for all applicable per player fees and seasonal permit charges in accordance with the City’s 
adopted fee schedule. The City will continue to provide water service to support field and facility use, while the League will 
reimburse the City for fifty percent (50%) of electrical costs associated with the premises, invoiced on a semiannual basis. 
Overall, the agreement is structured to recover operational costs associated with League use while supporting youth 
sports programming and reinvestment in Lakeshore Park facilities. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of use. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Tracy Brumett, Community Services Section Manager
tbrumett@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7443

1 
 
LEASE AGREEMENT 
BATTING CAGE, BALL FIELDS AND CONCESSIONS BUILDING AT LAKESHORE PARK 
 
 
This Lease, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between the 
CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California 
(“CITY”), hereinafter “City” and SAN MATEO NATIONAL LITTLE LEAGUE, a California non-profit 
corporation, hereinafter “League.” 
 
RECITALS 
 
WHEREAS, City owns certain real property developed as a part of Lakeshore Park, located within 
the City of San Mateo, San Mateo County, outlined in Exhibit A and incorporated herein by reference 
("PREMISES"); and 
 
WHEREAS, the City and League agree that it is reasonable and desirable for the League, an 
established and recognized community organization, to maintain and operate certain recreational 
facilities located on the Premises ("BATTING CAGE," “BALL FIELDS) and ("CONCESSION 
BUILDING") as further described herein; and 
 
WHEREAS, under this Lease, City intends to grant the Premises lease to League for the purposes 
of maintaining and operating the Batting Cage and Building and using Ball Fields. 
 
NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the League's maintenance of the Batting Cage, 
Concession Building, and Ball Fields promises set forth above, the City authorizes League to use those 
facilities, and the parties agree as follows: 
 
1. PURPOSE: The purpose of this Lease is to: 
a. 
Allow League to maintain the Batting Cage as a baseball practice recreational facility 
for City and community group use. 
b. 
Allow League to use the Batting Cage (Batting Cage), Concession Building (Building), 
and Ball Fields (Ball Fields) located within Lakeshore Park, at 1500 Marina Ct. as shown 
in Exhibit A. 
 
2. TERM: The term of this Lease shall commence February 1, 2026, and conclude on December 31, 2031. 
 
3. USE OF BATTING CAGE: It is understood that the Batting Cage is City property; however, the 
City grants League use of the Batting Cage and authorizes League or its approved user to conduct batting 
practice in the Batting Cage. League will ensure that sufficient netting is provided inside the cage to 
reduce the noise of balls hitting the fencing. 
 
League agrees to make Batting Cage available to other City of San Mateo resident youth sports 
organizations upon request by those organizations. To clarify, when a youth sports group (other than the 
League) rents the field, it does not automatically include use of the Batting Cage. Before allowing use of 
the Batting Cage, the League must refer those potential users to the City. The City will approve the user 
and enter into a written agreement with that user requiring compliance with the City's insurance, 
indemnity, hold harmless and other facility rental requirements. City will then refer the user back to 
League to coordinate use. League use will have priority over renter use. 
 
All users of the Batting Cage must abide by the following rules: 
106 of 1110

--- page 107 ---
2 
 
 
1. Only rubber balls may be used in the Batting Cage. 
2. Use by League and scheduled users may only take place during the following times:  
• Monday-Saturday: 9am-7pm 
•   Sunday: 10am-7pm 
 
Use hours and conditions subject to change by City based upon neighborhood impact. 
 
City shall have no responsibility or liability for loss or damage to the personal property of League, its 
players, coaches, volunteers or officials that are placed or stored in the Batting Cage, including but not 
exclusive of netting, pitching machines, electrical cables, balls, player equipment or other items. 
 
4. USE OF CONCESSIONS BUILDING: The Building, including but not limited to concession 
sales goods, equipment, vehicles, supplies, money, personal effects, player equipment, and other items, 
shall be used solely for the sale of food, sundries, souvenirs, and similar articles, and for the storage of 
League equipment and supplies. The City reserves the right to specify any articles that may not be sold or 
otherwise distributed by the League at the concession stand.  Pursuant to Gov’t. Code 51038, League will 
be the exclusive concessionaire for Lakeshore Park. 
 
5. USE OF BALL FIELDS: Upon obtaining the required seasonal permits and paying all applicable 
fees, the League may use the Ball Fields for practices and games associated with the Little League season. 
Use of amplified sound equipment shall comply with all requirements of the San Mateo Municipal Code 
and good neighbor practices and is permitted only for limited purposes, such as opening day ceremonies 
and announcing upcoming batters, scores, and other game-related information. Amplified sound shall not 
be used for ongoing game commentary or for playing music before, during, or after games, except for the 
National Anthem. Complaints regarding excessive noise levels or other neighborhood impacts resulting 
from amplified sound may be grounds for the City to require discontinuation of such use. 
 
The use and storage of vehicles on the Premises must be approved in advance by the City and, if gas-
powered, shall comply with all applicable fire safety and municipal codes. Vehicles must be securely 
stored when not in use and shall be operated only under adult supervision at all times. Any vehicle 
operated on City property must be maintained in safe operating condition, including vehicles such as golf 
carts or all-terrain vehicles (ATVs). All use of the Ball Fields shall conform to the San Mateo Municipal 
Code and special park rules. 
 
6. IMPROVEMENT PROJECTS: The League agrees to identify specific projects at Lakeshore Park 
and devote a portion of the proceeds from the concession stand to complete these projects. 
Representatives of the League and the City will meet annually prior to the league opening or if by mutual 
agreement, the end of the season, to discuss the upcoming season's projects. Some projects may take more 
than one season to complete or to accumulate sufficient funds to complete. Any work done at the facility 
must comply with established Master Plans, adhere to existing codes, receive necessary permits, comply 
with state prevailing wage law, and be approved in advance by the Parks and Recreation Director or 
designee before work can begin.  
 
7. CHARGES BY LESSEE: League concession operation shall charge no more than prices 
generally prevailing for similar concessions; as such determination shall be made from time to time 
by City Manager, or their designee.   
 
 
107 of 1110

--- page 108 ---
3 
 
8. UTILITIES: The City shall provide water required by the League at the Building and Ball Fields. 
All other utilities associated with the operation of the concession stand, including electricity, gas, 
garbage, and related services, shall be the responsibility of the League. 
 
Because separate electrical meters will not be installed, the League shall reimburse the City for 50% of 
the monthly electrical cost for the Premises. The City will invoice the League for its share of electrical 
costs for two six-month periods. Any unusual or non-typical electrical usage by the City during non-
league months will be considered when determining the League’s payment amount.   
 
9. PAYMENT:  Payments, including, but not limited to per player fees and booking fees, are due one 
month (30 days) after the City releases the permits.  Payment for utilities shall be remitted to the City 
within thirty (30) days of invoice date. 
 
10. NO ASSIGNMENT: League shall not assign this Lease or any portion thereof, nor sublet 
the Building, Batting Cage or Ball Fields without the prior written consent of City. 
 
11. CONDITION OF PREMISES: The League shall ensure that the Building, backstops, 
windscreens, and adjacent areas are maintained in a clean and sanitary condition at all times, to the 
satisfaction of the City. Storage areas within the concession stand shall be kept neat, orderly, and 
clean. All doorways and windows must remain clear, unobstructed, and accessible at all times. The 
League shall keep the Batting Cage and Building secured whenever they are not in use. Any 
additional storage containers located outside of the Building, including but not limited to knack 
boxes and tool storage, must be clearly labeled, locked, securely maintained, and placed in locations 
approved by the City. 
 
12. SECURITY AND ACCESS: The League shall not change, replace, or add any locks, deadbolts, 
security devices, cameras, or similar hardware on any doors, windows, gates, or other access points to the 
Premises, including the Building, Batting Cage, or Ball Fields, whether interior or exterior, without the 
prior written consent of the City. The League shall not install any devices that prevent City access to the 
Premises as permitted under this Lease and applicable law. 
 
Any unauthorized changes to locks or security devices shall constitute a material breach of this Lease. If 
the League violates this provision, the City may, at the League’s expense, remove unauthorized devices 
and restore the Premises to their original condition. The League shall promptly provide all keys, fobs, 
access cards, or other access devices to the City upon request and upon termination of the Lease. 
 
13. MAINTENANCE: The League shall be responsible for routine maintenance, cleaning, and general 
upkeep of the Building and Batting Cage. The League further agrees to maintain the grass infield portions 
of both Ball Fields, as applicable, in accordance with the Mandatory Guidelines for Ball Field Preparation 
(Exhibit B) and the Bay Area Air Quality Management District General Requirements (Exhibit C). Such 
maintenance includes, but is not limited to, mowing, fertilization, irrigation, and field renovation when 
necessary.  League is prohibited from applying any pesticides to the premises.   
 
Any improvements installed by the League with prior City approval shall be maintained by the League. If 
the League fails to adequately maintain League-installed improvements, the City shall provide written 
notice identifying the required maintenance and a reasonable timeframe for completion. If the League 
does not complete the maintenance within the specified timeframe, the City may perform the maintenance 
and bill the League for the full cost of the work. 
108 of 1110

--- page 109 ---
4 
 
 
The League shall maintain the Batting Cage in compliance with all applicable City laws, regulations, and 
safety standards. The roof, structure, and fencing of the Batting Cage shall be kept in good repair and in 
an attractive condition at all times. The City shall have the right to enter and inspect the Batting Cage to 
determine compliance with this Lease and to verify that the site is being maintained in a reasonably safe 
manner. 
 
14. REPAIRS: The League shall promptly notify the City of any conditions requiring repair to the 
Building, Batting Cage, or Ball Fields.  Except as otherwise provided in this Lease, the City shall be 
responsible for completing repairs within a reasonable timeframe after receiving notice. The League shall 
cooperate with the City to allow access as needed to complete such repairs. 
 
15. TERMINATION FOR CAUSE: The City may terminate this lease if the City determines 
that League has failed to maintain and operate the business and Premises in accordance with the 
terms of this lease, or any consent or permission given hereunder, or with any statute or ordinance 
effective with reference to such maintenance or operation. The City may begin the termination 
process of lease for cause at any time by notifying the League of lease violations. The League will 
have 30 days from the date of such notification to correct such violations or to take corrective steps, 
if the 30-day period is, as agreed between the City Manager and the League, inadequate to correct 
such violations. If the League has not corrected violations within the time frame described above, 
termination becomes immediately effective. In the event of termination as herein above provided, a 
proration of rentals paid or due, or of any damages due, shall be determined and adjustments made 
between City and League. 
 
In the event any action of the League when operating the concession stand creates a health or safety 
hazard, City reserves the right to close the concession stand operation immediately and keep it closed 
until the unsafe situation has been corrected. 
 
If the League fails to comply with the terms of this Lease, the City may require that the Batting Cage be 
removed, and park returned to its previous condition at the expense of the League. If it is determined by 
the City that this Lease, for any reason, is to be terminated or that the Batting Cage is to be removed, 
the City agrees to contact the League to inform League of the circumstance and work towards a mutual 
resolution. Removal in this circumstance would be at the City's expense. 
 
If for any reason, the League no longer intends to use or maintain the Batting Cage, it may terminate this 
Lease. If so, the League must notify the City in writing. At City's discretion, it will either require League 
to remove the Batting Cage and restore the park to its original condition at League expense; or keep the 
Batting Cage in place and become wholly and solely responsible for its use and maintenance. 
 
16. TERMINATION FOR CONVENIENCE: This Lease may be terminated by the City at any 
time by giving the League written notice at least thirty (30) days in advance.  
 
17. WAIVER; INSPECTION; COMPLIANCE WITH LAWS: The Building shall be open to 
inspection at all times by the City Manager and authorized employees and representatives of City, and 
League shall at all times maintain and operate the business and Building in full compliance with all 
applicable laws. City's right to inspect shall in no way diminish League's duty to keep Building in good 
and safe condition and repair or to maintain and operate the business and Building in full compliance 
with all applicable laws. 
 
109 of 1110

--- page 110 ---
5 
 
18. INDEMNITY: League agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed 
officials, employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense 
arising out of League’s performance of this Lease, except for those claims arising out of CITY’s sole 
negligence or willful misconduct.  League agrees to defend City, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents against any such claims. The League’s duty to indemnify shall survive expiration 
or early termination of this Lease. 
 
19. INSURANCE:  Little League must comply with the insurance requirements, attached as Exhibit D 
and incorporated by reference. 
 
20. GOVERNING LAW AND VENUE:  This Lease shall be governed by the laws of the State of 
California and, in the event of litigation, venue will be in the County of San Mateo. 
 
21. NOTICES: All notices required to be given hereunder shall be in writing, and delivered in person 
or transmitted by First Class Mail, postage prepaid. 
 
To City: 
 
City of San Mateo, Parks & Recreation Department  
Attn: Community Services Section Manager 
330 West 20th Avenue  
San Mateo, CA 94403 
 
To League: 
 
San Mateo National Little League 
P.O. Box 5188 
San Mateo, CA 94402

22. COSTS AND ATTORNEY’S FEES:  Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be 
recoverable as costs (by the filing of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce 
the provisions of this Lease.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in 
the trial court, appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or 
actions.  It is the intent of this Lease that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Lease.

Exhibit B 
Mandatory Guidelines for Ballﬁeld Preparation 
1. Water the Inﬁeld Dirt: To effectively minimize dust, it is crucial to water the inﬁeld dirt 
thoroughly before any dragging takes place. Properly watering the inﬁeld not only reduces 
airborne dust but also creates a safer playing surface by maintaining the integrity of the dirt. 
2. Drag the Inﬁeld Dirt: Once the inﬁeld is sufficiently watered, drag the dirt to create a smooth 
and safe playing surface.  
3.  Rake the Inﬁeld Areas: Rake the inﬁeld areas near the bases, batter's box, and mound to 
eliminate any uneven spots and debris. 
4. Chalk Lines: Accurately chalk the necessary lines on the ﬁeld, including batter's box, foul 
lines and on deck circles. 
5. Put Away Equipment: After use, ensure that all equipment is returned to its designated    
storage area.   
Failure to follow these protocols may result in ﬁeld closures and the suspension of programming.  
Fines may be assessed by the Bay Area Air Quality Management District if regulations and 
requirements are not met. 
All City owned ballﬁelds have water access with a connection. If the quick connect or coupler key is 
not located, please contact the Parks & Recreation Department at 
parksandrecreation@cityofsanmateo.org or 650-522-7400. 
Acknowledge and Agree: 
 
_____________________________ 
Justin Rhodes, President SMNLL

6-1-3 
REGULATION 6 
PARTICULATE MATTER 
RULE 1 
GENERAL REQUIREMENTS 
6-1-100 
GENERAL 
 
6-1-101 
Description: The purpose of this Regulation is to limit the quantity of particulate matter 
in the atmosphere through the establishment of limitations on emission rates, emission 
concentrations, visible emissions and opacity. 
(Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-102 
Applicability of General Provisions: The general provisions and definitions in 
Regulation 1 and Regulation 6 shall apply to this rule.  
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-110 
Exemptions for Activities Subject to Other Rules and Regulations: 
110.1 
Temporary Sandblasting Operations: The provisions of this rule shall not apply 
to Temporary Sandblasting operations. Such operations are subject to the 
provisions of Regulation 12, Rule 4. 
110.2 
Open Outdoor Fires: The provisions of this rule shall not apply to emissions 
arising from open outdoor fires. Such open outdoor fires are subject to the 
provisions of Regulation 5. 
110.3 
Wood Burning Devices: The provisions of this rule shall not apply to sources 
subject to the provisions of Regulation 6, Rule 3. 
110.4 
Metal Recycling and Shredding Operations: The provisions of this rule shall 
not apply to sources subject to the provisions of Regulation 6, Rule 4.  
(Adopted 7/11/90, 12/19/90, Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-111 
Limited Exemption, Blasting Operations: Blasting operations that are conducted by 
certified blasters who have met the blasting ordinances and requirements for licensing 
and permitting by the State of California Department of Industrial Relations Division of 
Occupational Safety and Health or other applicable local permitting authority are not 
subject to Sections 6-1-307 and 6-1-506. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-112 
Limited Exemption, Portland Cement Manufacturing: Sections 6-1-307, 310 and 
311 shall not apply to particulate emissions from sources subject to the provisions of 
Regulation 9, Rule 13.  
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-113 
Limited Exemption, Total Suspended Particulate (TSP) Concentration and 
Weight Limitations: Sections 6-1-310.2, 311.2 and 504 shall not apply to particulate 
matter emissions from the following sources: 
113.1 
Commercial cooking equipment subject to the provisions of Regulation 6, 
Rule 2. 
113.2 
Salt processing operations whose TSP emissions are greater than 99 weight 
percent salt.  
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-114 
Limited Exemption, Total Suspended Particulate (TSP) Emission Limits for Fuel 
Combustion: Sections 6-1-310.2 and 311.2 shall not apply to particulate matter 
emissions from the following sources: 
114.1 
Gas-, liquid- and solid-fuel fired indirect heat exchangers, including furnaces, 
heaters, boilers, gas turbines and supplemental fuel-fired heat recovery steam 
generators, but excluding Carbon Monoxide Boilers downstream of Petroleum 
Refinery Fluid Catalytic Cracking Unit regenerators. 
114.2 
Gas-fuel fired control devices that control only gaseous emissions. 
114.3 
Section 6-1-504 shall not apply to gas-fuel fired indirect heat exchangers or 
gas-fuel fired control devices that control only gaseous emissions. Liquid- and 
solid-fuel fired indirect heat exchangers shall remain subject to Section 6-1-
504. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
116 of 1110

--- page 117 ---
Bay Area Air Quality Management District 
 
August 1, 2018 
 
6-1-4 
6-1-115 
Limited Exemption, Total Suspended Particulate (TSP) Concentration Limitation: 
Section 6-1-310.2 shall not apply to particulate emissions from a sewage treatment 
plant solid waste incinerator abated by a wet scrubber with an Air District Permit to 
Operate until July 1, 2025. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-116 
Limited Exemption, Total Suspended Particulate (TSP) Limits: Section 6-1-310.2 
and 311.2 shall not apply to particulate emissions from a carbon monoxide boiler 
abated by a water scrubber with an Air District Permit to Operate. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-117 
Limited Exemption, Total Suspended Particulate (TSP) Limits: Section 6-1-310.2 
and 6-1-311.2 shall not apply to particulate emissions from a petroleum coke calcining 
unit abated by a baghouse with an Air District Permit to Operate until January 1, 2022. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-200 
DEFINITIONS 
 
6-1-201 
Active Operations: As defined in Regulation 6-201, any activity with the potential to 
create particulate emissions from any source or fugitive dust emissions. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-202 
Bulk Material: As defined in Regulation 6-202, any unpackaged sand, soil, gravel, 
aggregate, solid construction material, solid industrial chemical or other unpackaged 
solids less than 2 inches in length or diameter. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-203 
Bulk Material Site: As defined in Regulation 6-203, any site with one or more 
stockpiles of bulk material greater than 5 feet high or with a footprint greater than 100 
square feet. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-204 
Exhaust Gas Volume: The volume of gases discharged from an operation or an 
emission point, corrected to standard conditions (as defined in Regulation 1-228), 
excluding water vapor or steam. 
(Renumbered and Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-205 
Particle: A minute quantity of solid matter or liquid droplet. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-206 
Process Weight: The total weight of all material introduced into an operation, including 
solid fuels and process air, but excluding (i) liquids and gases used solely as fuels, (ii) 
air that is not consumed as a reactant or is not critical to the process, (iii) air that is 
used only for dilution, and (iv) combustion air.  
(Renumbered and Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-207 
Process Weight Rate and Exhaust Gas Rate: A rate established as follows: 
207.1 
For continuous or long-run, steady-state operations, the total process weight 
or exhaust gas volume for the entire period of continuous operation or for a 
typical portion thereof, divided by the number of hours of such period or 
portions thereof. 
207.2 
For cyclical or batch operations, the total process weight or exhaust gas 
volume for a period that covers a complete operation or an integer number of 
cycles, divided by the hours of actual process operation during such period. 
Where the nature of any process or operation or the design of any equipment 
is such as to permit more than one interpretation of this section, that 
interpretation which results in the minimum value for allowable emission shall 
apply. 
(Renumbered and Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-208 
Regulated Bulk Material Site: A bulk material site that (i) produces, handles, loads, 
unloads, stores or uses more than 10 tons per year of bulk materials; and (ii) is subject 
to an authority to construct and/or permit to operate for bulk material storage and 
handling issued by the Bay Area Air Quality Management District. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-209 
Stockpile: As defined in Regulation 6-208, an open or unenclosed storage pile of bulk 
material, external to any barn, pit or silo. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
117 of 1110

--- page 118 ---
Bay Area Air Quality Management District 
 
August 1, 2018 
 
6-1-5 
6-1-210 
Workday: As defined in Regulation 6-210, any period, typically 8 – 12-hour shifts, 
when active operations occur on the site. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-300 
STANDARDS 
 
6-1-301 
Ringelmann No. 1 Limitation: Except as provided in Sections 6-1-303, 304 and 306, 
a person shall not emit from any source for a period or aggregate periods of more than 
three minutes in any hour, a visible emission that is as dark or darker than No. 1 on 
the Ringelmann Chart, or of such opacity as to obscure an observer's view to an 
equivalent or greater degree. 
(Amended 7/11/90, 8/1/18) 
6-1-302 
Opacity Limitation: Except as provided in Sections 6-1-303, 304 and 306, a person 
shall not emit from any source for a period or aggregate periods of more than three 
minutes in any hour an emission equal to or greater than 20% opacity. 
(Amended 7/11/90, 8/1/18) 
6-1-303 
Ringelmann No. 2 Limitation: A person shall not emit for a period or aggregate 
periods of more than three minutes in any hour, a visible emission that is as dark or 
darker than No. 2 on the Ringelmann Chart, or of such opacity as to obscure an 
observer's view to an equivalent or greater degree, or be equal to or greater than 40 
percent opacity, from the following sources: 
303.1 
Internal combustion engines of less than 25 liters (1500 in3) displacement; 
303.2 
Engines used solely as a standby source of motive power; 
303.3 
Laboratory equipment used exclusively for chemical or physical analyses or 
experimentation; 
303.4 
Portable brazing, soldering or welding equipment. 
(Amended 1/5/83, 7/11/90, 8/1/18) 
6-1-304 
Tube Cleaning: During tube cleaning, and except for three minutes in any hour, a 
person shall not emit from any heat transfer operation using fuel at a rate of not less 
than 148 GJ (140 million BTU) per hour, a visible emission as dark or darker than No. 
2 on the Ringelmann Chart, or of such opacity as to obscure an observer's view to an 
equivalent or greater degree, or equal to or greater than 40 percent opacity. The 
aggregate duration of such emissions in any 24-hour period shall not exceed 6.0 
minutes per 1055 GJ (one billion BTU) gross heating value of fuel burned during such 
24-hour period. 
(Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-305 
Visible Particles: No person shall emit particles from any operation in sufficient 
number to cause annoyance to any other person where the particles are large enough 
to be visible as individual particles at the emission point, or of such size and nature as 
to be visible individually as incandescent particles. This Section shall only apply if such 
particles fall on real property other than the property of the person responsible for the 
emission. 
(Amended 8/1/18) 
6-1-306 
Piledriving Hammers: No person shall emit visible emissions from a piledriving 
hammer that exceeds the following standards for a period or aggregate periods of more 
than four minutes during the driving of a single pile: 
306.1 
For piledriving hammers other than those specified in Section 306.2, any 
visible emission that is as dark or darker than No. 1 on the Ringelmann Chart, 
or of such opacity as to obscure an observer's view to an equivalent or greater 
degree; 
306.2 
For piledriving hammers utilizing kerosene, smoke suppressing fuel additives 
and synthetic lubricating oil, and for which fuel usage records are kept as 
required by Section 6-1-503, any visible emission that is as dark or darker than 
No. 2 on the Ringelmann Chart or of such opacity as to obscure an observer's 
view to an equivalent or greater degree. 
(Adopted July 11, 1990, Amended 8/1/18) 
 
 
118 of 1110

--- page 119 ---
Bay Area Air Quality Management District 
 
August 1, 2018 
 
6-1-6 
6-1-307 
Prohibition of Visible Emissions Within and From Regulated Bulk Material Sites: 
307.1 
Effectve July 1, 2019, the owner/operator of a Regulated Bulk Material Site 
shall not cause or allow a fugitive dust visible emission from: active operations 
at the site, a bulk material stockpile, or a bulk material spill that: 
a. Exceeds (i) 5 feet long, 5 feet wide, or 5 feet high, and (ii) 10 percent 
opacity as determined by EPA Method 203B (or half as dark in shade as 
that designated as Number 1 on the Ringelmann Chart), for a period or 
aggregate periods of more than 3 minutes in any 60-minute period; or  
b. Travels or carries beyond the site property line. 
307.2 
Effective July 1, 2019, the owner/operator of a Regulated Bulk Material Site 
shall clean up any bulk material spill of more than 12 inches high or more than 
25 square feet by the end of the workday, unless the spill is adequately wetted, 
covered, or is protected by a wind screen with no more than 50 percent 
porosity that is (i) equal to or higher than the height of the spill; and (ii) placed 
upwind of the spill at a distance no greater than the height of the wind screen. 
Cleanup activities shall not cause fugitive dust visible emissions that exceed 
20 percent opacity as determined by EPA Method 203B (or as dark in shade 
as that designated as Number 1 on the Ringelmann Chart), for a period or 
aggregate periods of more than 3 minutes in any 60-minute period. 
(Adopted 8/1/18) 
6-1-310 
Total Suspended Particulate (TSP) Concentration Limits: 
310.1 
No person shall emit TSP from any source in excess of 343 mg per dscm (0.15 
gr per dscf) of exhaust gas volume. 
310.2 
Effective July 1, 2020, Table 6-1-310.2 emission limits shall apply to any 
source with a Potential To Emit TSP (as defined in Regulation 2-1-217) greater 
than 1,000 kg per year. No applicable source shall emit TSP at a concentration 
in excess of the limit indicated for the source’s Exhaust Gas Rate in Table 6-
1-310.2:

310.3 
For the purposes of Section 6-1-310, the measured concentration of TSP in 
the exhaust shall be corrected to standard conditions (as defined in Regulation 
1-228) and (i) 12 percent carbon dioxide (CO2) by volume, minus water vapor, 
for incineration or salvage operations and gas-fired pathological waste 
incinerators; or (ii) 6 percent oxygen (O2) by volume, minus water vapor, for 
heat transfer operations. In the case of an incineration or salvage operation, 
the concentration shall be corrected as if no auxiliary fuel had been used and 
any CO2 produced from combustion of liquid or gaseous fuel shall be excluded 
from the correction to 12 percent CO2. 
(Amended 8/1/18)
