CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  16
Section Name: OLD BUSINESS
Account Number: 25-3112
File ID: 25-10745
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
January 26, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Historic Policy Effort – Update on Draft Historic Policies and Council Direction  
RECOMMENDATION:
Receive a presentation on the Draft Citywide Historic Context Statement and Draft Historic Preservation Ordinance 
Update, receive public comments, and provide staff with direction on how to proceed with updating the draft policy 
documents and the timeline for scheduling public hearings.
BACKGROUND:
San Mateo's General Plan 2040 sets the policy framework for how issues such as historic resources and preservation 
should be addressed. The Community Design and Historic Resources Element addresses the topic of historic and cultural 
resources, and includes goals, policies, and actions that outline the framework for how the City should approach historic 
resources and preservation. Specifically, Goal CD-5 addresses preserving historic and culturally important resources to 
maintain San Mateo's special identity and continuity with the past and includes seven policies and six actions to support 
achievement of this goal.
On June 17, 2024, the City Council held a study session to consider options for how to proceed with updating the City's 
local historic policies and codes pursuant to the actions in the General Plan. The study session resulted in the Council 
directing staff to prioritize updating the City's Historic Preservation Ordinance and preparing a Citywide Historic Context 
Statement. The work would include creation of a framework for the designation of historic resources and districts, 
establishment of review and permitting procedures for historic alterations and demolitions, consistency with Federal and 
State standards and guidelines, and alignment with the other goals and policies outlined in the General Plan. 
In the summer and fall of 2024, staff issued a Request for Proposals (RFP) for a qualified historic consultant to support the 
historic policy update work directed by Council. Based on overall professional experience, quality of proposal, responses to 
interview questions and composition of the consultant team, staff identified Rincon Consultants, Inc. as being the best 
qualified to support the City's historic policy update effort. On January 21, 2025, the City Council voted to appropriate 
funding to complete the historic policy update effort and to approve a professional services agreement with Rincon 
Consultants to support this historic policy work. 
On June 2, 2025, the City Council held a study session to receive an update on the policy effort and provide staff with 
direction ahead of community outreach and engagement.  Following receipt of a presentation that outlined the 
background of San Mateo’s Historic Preservation Ordinance, key issues and opportunities for improving the Historic 
Preservation Ordinance, the outreach and engagement strategy, and a review of different approaches to key elements of 
an effective historic preservation ordinance, including owner consent, the Council provided direction to shape the Historic 
Preservation Ordinance update. Direction from Council included that the Historic Preservation Ordinance update should 
include a concise set of definitions; update existing ordinance language that is too rigid, inconsistent, and/or detailed; and 
375 of 1110

--- page 376 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
establish a reviewing body. The Council also directed staff to assess if the Community Relation’s Commission (CRC) instead 
of the Planning Commission was the most appropriate reviewing body to support administration of the Historic 
Preservation Ordinance. The Council agreed that the local historic designation criteria should generally align with the 
California Register of Historical Resources criteria, but advised that the criteria could potentially include additional criteria 
to acknowledge the city’s unique history and be informed by the results of community engagement efforts. Council also 
confirmed that the Historic Preservation Ordinance should include a mechanism to designate local historic districts, and 
such designations should require owner consent but did not set the level of consent that should be required for a district 
application to move forward. Finally, Council provided direction that the Historic Preservation Ordinance update should 
include owner consent for local listing in the form of affirmative consent, i.e. the affected property owner‘s clear 
agreement, for all local listings. 
Since the June City Council study session, the project team has held a number of public outreach and engagement 
activities to introduce the project to the community and gather their feedback to be incorporated into the Historic Context 
Statement and updated Historic Preservation Ordinance, as outlined in the Outreach and Engagement Plan published in 
May 20251. To date, outreach activities have included three focus group meetings, two virtual workshops, and three pop-
up events and have reached over 200 community members. Key takeaways have included a general recognition of the 
value of San Mateo’s cultural heritage and a desire for a clear, consistent process for identifying and regulating the City’s 
historic resources. 
DISCUSSION:
Below is a summary of the work and activities that have happened over the past six months since the June City Council 
study session and an overview of some key policy questions that staff is seeking direction on before final drafts of the 
Historic Preservation Ordinance and Citywide Historic Context Statement are published and public hearings are scheduled.
Draft Historic Context Statement
The Draft Citywide Historic Context Statement (Attachment 1) was developed based on research into San Mateo’s history, 
feedback from community members and guidance from the National Park Service and California Office of Historic 
Preservation. The Historic Context Statement highlights trends and patterns of development, as well as their relationship 
to significant eras, moments, communities and people in San Mateo’s history. It is also a policy document that provides a 
valuable framework to guide consistent evaluation of the historic significance of properties and their potential eligibility 
for local listing. 
The Historic Context Statement does not include property evaluations or any in-depth surveys. The Historic Context 
Statement follows industry best practices, and is organized by significant periods, themes, and property types and 
presented chronologically. Information gathered from community outreach events where we asked people what places in 
San Mateo are meaningful to them are reflected in the property types that were identified as well as in the buildings, 
places, and development trends that were included in the narrative.  The draft is still being updated with information on 
notable architects, builders, and designers and guidance for assessing significance. The draft was made available to the 
public for review the week of November 24, 2025 and was a key focus of the January 21, 2026 community workshop. All 
community input received since the first draft was published will be considered and, as applicable, incorporated into the 
final draft that is published later this winter.
Council Direction 
Based on the draft that was published, and the subsequent public comments received, does the City Council have any 
comments or direction related to the draft Context Statement?
Community Outreach and Engagement
The Outreach and Engagement Strategy to support the Historic Policy Update Effort focused on multiple entry points for 
community participation. The strategy was designed to offer a range of opportunities for residents, organizations, and 
interested parties to share input and perspectives to inform the update process. Project outreach began in February 2025 
1 Outreach and Engagement Strategy link
376 of 1110

--- page 377 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
and will extend though the adoption hearings in Spring 2026. To date, the project team has conducted two City Council 
Study Sessions, three focus groups, two community workshops, online outreach and an engagement tool (digital time 
capsule), and three mobile workshops designed to reach a broad range of community members. The key takeaways, thus 
far, have been that the community generally recognizes that San Mateo has cultural and architectural diversity that should 
be preserved, but desires improved clarity and transparency in the process of identifying and managing resources. 
Participants in public outreach activities were generally interested in a balanced approach to policies that protect San 
Mateo’s heritage while allowing for reasonable flexibility for property owners. Additional information about the outreach 
activities, community input and key take-aways can be found in the Outreach and Engagement Summary Report 
(Attachment 2). All written public comments that have been submitted through January 16, 2026 are included as an 
appendix to the Report. Additional public comments received after this date are included as Attachment 4. 
Most recently, a virtual community workshop was held on the evening of January 21, 2026.  The event included 55 
attendees and provided an overview of the draft context statement and draft ordinance, examples of how the updated 
ordinance would be applied, multiple polling questions, and multiple comment periods to receive input and answer 
questions. One of the polling questions sought input on the consent threshold that should go into the updated ordinance, 
and 60% of the respondents (45) expressed support for requiring a super majority (66% or 75%) of the properties within a 
proposed district to affirmatively consent. Some of the reoccurring questions/comments included support for Glazenwood 
as a historic district and clarification on its current status (an eligible historic district), what properties will be subject to the 
updated ordinance (locally listed resources only), will contributors in historic districts and individually listed properties 
require Certificates of Appropriateness for exterior alterations (yes, but non-contributors will not be subject to any 
additional review), clarification that state/federal listings will require a local listing action to be added to the City’s Historic 
Resources Inventory, and clarification about when CEQA review is required per State law.  Additional details from this 
community workshop will be provided as part of the staff presentation at the meeting.
Additional planned outreach and engagement activities include two more focus groups – one focused on youth and one 
focused on underserved communities – and one additional community workshop. The purpose of the remaining focus 
groups is to provide a checkpoint with underrepresented communities to support broader dialogue on the components of 
historic preservation and add additional feedback to support the representation of San Mateo’s diverse communities 
within the historic policy effort.  The last community workshop, which is currently scheduled to happen post adoption, is 
intended to provide technical guidance on how to use the Historic Context Statement and Historic Preservation Ordinance 
and their associated tools and guidance documents. Resources to support community understanding will include screening 
criteria to assess properties for potential historic eligibility ahead of a historic resource evaluation being required, detailed 
overviews, submittal requirements for local listing and proposed projects on locally listed historic resources and updated 
FAQs.   
Council Direction 
Staff is looking for Council input and direction on the remaining community outreach and engagement activities that are 
planned. Has there been sufficient outreach and engagement for this policy update effort to move forward with public 
hearings before the Planning Commission and City Council, or are there specific additional activities that should be 
completed before public hearings are scheduled? If additional outreach and engagement are directed, the timeline for 
project completion may be extended and/or additional money from the project’s contingency fund may be necessary to 
complete the work.
Draft Historic Preservation Ordinance
The Historic Preservation Ordinance update has been drafted in response to City Council direction and community input, 
and to meet current best practices and include all essential elements for an effective historic preservation ordinance. The 
draft Historic Preservation Ordinance update includes the following features, which are missing from City’s existing 
Historic Preservation Ordinance: 
•
Establishment of a reviewing authority;
•
Procedures and criteria for designating local historic resources;
•
Consideration of the economic effects of designation; 
•
Establishment of appeals processes; 
377 of 1110

--- page 378 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
•
Provisions for enforcement; and 
•
Clear and concise definitions. 
In addition to formerly missing elements, the Historic Preservation Ordinance update includes improved and clarified 
procedures for actions subject to review, and provides a clarified process for the types of actions that would be required to 
go to the reviewing authority versus decisions that can be made at the staff level. A sentiment repeated through ongoing 
community outreach and engagement was the desire for a transparent, consistent, and clear process in the nomination 
and project review process for historic resources. The additions and improvements included in the Historic Preservation 
Ordinance update are aimed at providing that desired clarity. It should also be noted that, as currently drafted per earlier 
Council direction, the ordinance is only applicable to locally designated historic resources. Resources designated at the 
State or Federal level would not be added to the City’s Historic Resources Inventory or subject to the updated Ordinance 
unless a subsequent local designation occurs. 
Of particular interest to San Mateo residents, as heard through community input efforts, was the identification and 
treatment of local historic districts. In response to community feedback and Council direction, the process proposed for 
nominating a local historic district includes a robust notification requirement with a community meeting obligation and a 
strong element of affirmative owner consent that requires both an owner-led application process as well as a petition of 
the effected property owners, confirming that a specified percentage of affected property owners support the application. 
That percentage is currently undetermined in the draft ordinance as staff is seeking Council direction on that topic.  The 
Historic Preservation Ordinance update was made available for public review the week of December 15, 2025 and was a 
key focus of the January 21, 2026 community workshop. 
Council Direction 
Staff are looking for Council direction on the following items related to the Historic Preservation Ordinance update:
•
Thresholds for Owner Consent in Historic Districts:
o
City Council previously provided that eligibility requirements for listing local historic districts should include a 
majority threshold for owner consent but held off on setting a specific percentage. Thresholds may be a simple 
majority (50% + 1) or a supermajority (2/3 or 3/4). 
o
What percentage of property owners within a proposed historic district should be required to support a 
historic district nomination for it to proceed to City Council for designation?
•
Eligibility Criteria:
o
To date, the public outreach and engagement process has not resulted in the identification of local criteria for 
designation in addition to the CRHR criteria, which are as follows:
▪
Association with events that have made a significant contribution to the broad patterns of San Mateo’s or 
California’s history and cultural heritage;  
▪
Association with the lives of persons or communities important to San Mateo’s or California’s past;
▪
Embodies the distinctive characteristics of a type, period, region, or method of construction, or represents 
the work of an important creative individual, or possesses high artistic values; or
▪
Has yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important to prehistory or history.
o
Are there any additional criteria that should be included in the local designation criteria?
•
Naming Conventions:
o
The local inventory is drafted to be called the “San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory,” which would be 
comprised of individual locally designated properties called “Listed Resources” and locally designated historic 
neighborhoods called “Historic Districts.” 
o
Other options for naming conventions include: Landmarks, Historic Resources Inventory Property, Protected 
Resource, Designated Resource, etc.
o
Does the Council have any opinion on the drafted naming conventions?
Historic Review Body
As currently proposed in draft Historic Preservation Ordinance, the reviewing body will be responsible for tasks such as 
making recommendations on applications to add and/or remove properties to the San Mateo Historic Resources 
Inventory, reviewing applications for Certificates of Appropriateness (alterations to a Listed Resource or Contributor within 
378 of 1110

--- page 379 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
a Historic District), including evaluating proposed projects against applicable design guidelines and the Secretary of the 
Interior’s Standards for the Treatment of Historic Properties, and making recommendations on applications for historic 
preservation (Mills Act) contracts. Council previously directed staff to evaluate if the Community Relations Commission 
(CRC) would be the appropriate reviewing body to administer the Historic Preservation Ordinance as outlined above. 
The CRC’s current powers and duties, as outlined in San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) Chapter 2.21, include hearing 
administration of federal grants (CDBG), advancing the City's equity and inclusion objectives, working and coordinating 
with the County’s Human Relations Commission, supporting Housing Element implementation of policies and programs 
related to tenant protections and Affirmatively Furthering Fair Housing (AFFH). Further, the CRC is currently supported by 
Housing Division staff and is generally composed of residents who have backgrounds and experience that do not 
significantly overlap with the skills and experience to support administration of historic policies and review of historic 
projects.  Backgrounds that would typically have knowledge in this area include architects, city planners, design 
professionals, architectural historians and/or individuals with demonstrated local historical knowledge. So, in order to 
ensure that the CRC has the knowledge and expertise to review historic projects and make recommendations to the City 
Council, significant additional training would be necessary, additional Planning staff and potentially a City historical 
consultant would need to support the CRC when reviewing historic items, and the desired background experience for 
future commissioner appointments would need to be revised. 
As an alternative, the Planning Commission is an existing City commission that includes powers and duties and 
commissioner experience that more closely aligns with the experience necessary to be an effective reviewing body for the 
Historic Preservation Ordinance. The Planning Commission’s duties and responsibilities include administration of the City’s 
General Plan, Zoning Code and associated policy documents, and making recommendations to the City Council on policies 
and codes related to land use, development and urban design policy. Desired background experience for commissioners 
includes experience in architecture, construction, law, and local history. While additional training would be necessary to 
ensure that the Planning Commission has the knowledge and expertise to review historic projects and make 
recommendations to the City Council, the current work and background experience appears to be a better fit for Historic 
Preservation Ordinance administration.
Council Direction 
Which City commission should be the reviewing body for the Historic Preservation Ordinance?
CITY COUNCIL DIRECTION:
Based on the information shared in this agenda report, staff is seeking direction from Council on the following questions:
1.
Historic Context Statement – Are there any updates or revisions to this draft document?
2.
Outreach and Engagement – Are there additional activities that should be completed before public hearings are 
scheduled?
3.
Historic Preservation Ordinance 
a.
What percentage of property owners within a proposed historic district should be required to support a 
historic district nomination for it to proceed to City Council for designation?
b.
Are there any additional criteria that should be included in the local designation criteria?
c.
Does the Council have any opinion on the drafted naming conventions?
d.
Any other direction on updates or revisions to the draft ordinance?
4.
Historic Review Body – Which City commission should be the reviewing body for the Historic Preservation 
Ordinance?
NEXT STEPS:
Following this study session, staff will continue to work to refine and finalize the Historic Context Statement and the 
updated Historic Preservation Ordinance. Work will include integrating additional feedback we receive from City Council 
and the community through our ongoing outreach and engagement activities. Upcoming outreach activities include two 
additional focus groups, which are anticipated to be scheduled in February 2026, and one additional workshop to serve as 
a primer for the public to understand how to use the Historic Context Statement and Historic Preservation Ordinance and 
their associated tools. The outstanding workshop will be executed in late Spring 2026, depending on City Council direction. 
379 of 1110

--- page 380 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Updated drafts of documents will be circulated when available and are expected in late February or March, depending on 
City Council direction.  Based on the current timeline, it is anticipated that a Planning Commission public hearing will 
happen in March 2026, and a City Council public hearing for approval will happen in April 2026.
BUDGET IMPACT:
This work is being completed as part of the professional services agreement with Rincon Consultants that was approved by 
City Council on January 21, 2025. The work is funded by the Advance Planning Fund (Fund 225) and does not have an 
impact on the City's General Fund.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This Study Session is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5))
NOTICE PROVIDED:
All meeting noticing requirements were met. In addition, meeting notifications were sent out to the interested parties list, 
included in the City’s weekly eNews and pushed out through the City’s social media channels.

Introduction 
 
Historic Context Statement 
7 
1 
Introduction 
1.1 
Project Description 
The Historic Context Statement was undertaken by the City of San Mateo’s (City) Planning Division 
as part of a project to enhance and streamline the City’s historic preservation program and meet the 
goals of the Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040 and included an updated Historic Context 
Statement and development of this historic context statement. Historic context statements are 
written planning documents that help distill a community’s collective knowledge about its historic 
places into a concise and practical format by providing a narrative history of the development 
patterns, periods of development, typical architectural styles of a period and key features to 
illustrate the style. It also provides a framework that offers greater clarity and consistency, reduces 
the burden of original research for property owners and applicants, and will help to streamline 
future designation decisions, as appropriate.  
This document presents the history of San Mateo’s built environment from prehistory to present, 
and identifies important themes, subthemes, events, and patterns of development. It also describes 
different property types, architectural styles, architects and builders associated with these 
important themes. This document also presents registration requirements for evaluating 
significance and integrity that is specific to the city of San Mateo.  
1.2 
Project Team 
This report was written and researched by Rincon Architectural Historian Project Manager JulieAnn 
Murphy, MSHP. Rincon Architectural Historians Josh Bevan, AICP, MSHP, and Antonette Hrycyk, 
carried out additional research and project assistance. Cultural Resources Principal Margo Nayyar 
provided senior oversight and quality control. Each member of Rincon’s team meets the Secretary 
of the Interior’s Professional Qualifications Standards for history and architectural history (36 Code 
of Federal Regulations, Part 61).

--- page 390 ---
City of San Mateo

Methodology 
 
Historic Context Statement 
9 
2 
Methodology 
2.1 
Study Area 
The study area for the Historic Context Statement includes the entire city of San Mateo (FIGURE). 
San Mateo is located on the San Francisco Peninsula, approximately 20 miles south of San Francisco. 
Located in San Mateo County, it is the county’s most populous city. It borders the city of Burlingame 
to the north, the town of Hillsborough to the west, and the city of Belmont to the south. The city’s 
eastern boundary is defined by the San Francisco Bay at the northeast corner, and the city of Foster 
City at the southeast corner.  
2.2 
Research Methodology 
Rincon completed background and archival research in support of this study throughout January 
and September 2025. Primary and secondary source materials were consulted and included, but 
were not limited to, historical maps, aerial photographs, and written histories of the area. The 
following sources and repositories were used to develop an understanding of the city’s context:  
▪ 
San Mateo County Assessor’s Office 
▪ 
City of San Mateo Planning Division 
▪ 
San Mateo Local History Collection, San Mateo Public Library 
▪ 
California Collection, San Mateo Public Library  
▪ 
San Mateo County Historical Association Archives 
▪ 
Online Archive of California 
▪ 
Calisphere 
▪ 
Historical aerial photographs accessed via Nationwide Environmental Title Research, LLC Online 
▪ 
Historical aerial photographs accessed via University of California, Santa Barbara Library 
FrameFinder 
▪ 
Sanborn Fire Insurance Company Maps accessed through the San Francisco County Public 
Library 
▪ 
Historical United States Geological Survey topographic maps 
▪ 
Historical maps on file with the Library of Congress 
▪ 
Historical maps on file with the David Rumsey Map Collection 
▪ 
Historical newspaper clippings obtained from Newspapers.com and the California Digital 
Newspaper Collection 
▪ 
Various historical records via Ancestry.com 
▪ 
Various historical records and secondary sources via Internet Archive 
2.3 
Public Outreach and Engagement 
The project scope, schedule, and budget were presented to City Council on January 21, 2025. Rincon 
developed a robust outreach and engagement strategy to elicit community participation and 
391 of 1110

--- page 392 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
10 
feedback, which was finalized and published on a project-specific webpage on the City’s website in 
May 2025. The document was revised and re-uploaded in August 2025. Other information on the 
webpage included upcoming events and opportunities for participation, and access to an interactive 
digital time capsule for community members to share memories, photos, and stories of San Mateo.  
Rincon targeted several focus groups with keen interest in the history of San Mateo, historic 
preservation, development, and economic health. Focus groups with residents interested in historic 
preservation occurred on July 9, 2025, and on July 16, 2025. Additional focus groups with 
community business leaders were held on XXX, with underrepresented communities on XXX, and 
with community youth on XXX. A virtual community workshop held on July 29, 2025, provided a 
high-level overview of historic preservation and its history, a review of common historic 
preservation regulations, and interactive activities to understand community attitudes and priorities 
for the City’s historic preservation efforts.  
In an effort to reach people where they were to provide critical feedback on the purpose and goals 
of the project and publicize the ongoing public engagement, Rincon and City Staff held several pop-
up events. They included September Nights on B Street on September 18, 2025, Chavez 
Supermarket on September 23, 2025, and the Hacienda Farmer’s Market on October 7, 2025. In 
addition to the pop-up events, City Staff was also present at the September 19, 2025, Movies in the 
Park event to hand out information on the project and provide contact information on how to learn 
more about upcoming and ongoing outreach events.  
In addition to active engagement, the City also installed handouts and information boards in four 
static locations throughout the city, including City Hall, the Main Library, the Martin Luther King, Jr. 
Community Center, and the Senior Center. Informational flyers and postcards were also distributed 
at the Central Recreation Center.  
ADDITIONAL INFORMATION PENDING  
2.4 
Historic Context Statement 
The Historic Context Statement is arranged in chronological chapters that relate to the city’s major 
development periods from prehistory to present. The content and format of this document follows 
guidance and industry best practices, as provided by the National Park Service and California Office 
of Historic Preservation (OHP). Guidance documents consulted included the following: 
▪ 
National Register Bulletin 15: How to Apply the National Register Criteria for Evaluation1 
▪ 
National Register Bulletin 16A: How to Complete the National Register Registration Form2 
▪ 
National Register Bulletin 16B How to Complete the National Register Multiple Property 
Documentation Form3 
▪ 
Instructions for Recording Historical Resources4 
 
1 National Park Service, National Register Bulletin 15: How to Apply the National Register Criteria for Evaluation (U.S. Department of the 
Interior, 1997a): https://www.nps.gov/subjects/nationalregister/upload/NRB-15_web508.pdf.  
2 National Park Service, National Register Bulletin 16A: How to Complete the National Register Registration Form (U.S. Department of the 
Interior, 1997b): https://www.nps.gov/subjects/nationalregister/upload/NRB16A-Complete.pdf.

Methodology 
 
Historic Context Statement 
11 
▪ 
“Writing Historic Contexts”5 
▪ 
“OHP Preferred Format for Historic Context Statements”6 
Rincon staff conducted a “windshield” survey of San Mateo to orient the team’s understanding of 
the city’s built environment resources and the general patterns of development, including property 
types, architectural styles, and age of construction. All photographs in this Historic Context 
Statement were taken by Rincon in XXX 2025, unless otherwise noted.  
The Historic Context Statement is divided into six chronological periods, each of which is further 
divided into thematic subsections that reflect the significant themes identified in San Mateo. 
Following the discussion of San Mateo’s historic development, there is a summary of the various 
property types and architectural styles associated with each period of development followed by 
specific registration requirements for assessing historical significance and integrity by chronological 
period. 
2.5 
How to Use this Document 
The Historic Context Statement presents a detailed citywide context that identifies important 
themes and patterns of development, property types, architectural styles, and registration 
requirements. This document was designed to function as a tool for use by the City, its residents, 
and property owners to better understand, interpret, evaluate, and protect San Mateo’s historical 
resources. This document is organized into the following major sections:  
▪ 
Previous Surveys and Studies – This section summarizes previously conducted historic resource 
surveys, inventories, and evaluations completed in San Mateo, including results of a California 
Historical Resources Information System records search.  
▪ 
Significant Periods, Themes, and Property Types – This section includes a framework for future 
property evaluations by providing a summary of significant themes and property types 
associated with major periods of development. 
▪ 
Historic Context – The historic context includes six chapters, divided into chronological periods 
of development that are supported by important themes and patterns of development.  
▪ 
Architectural Styles – This portion of the document provides an overview of all predominant 
architectural styles identified in the study area. This section includes a representative 
photograph of each style (organized by property type), the style’s associated period of 
significance in San Mateo, and a list of major character-defining features of the style.  
▪ 
Architects, Landscape Architects, Developers, and Builders – This chapter includes an overview 
of all identified architects and builders associated with major periods of development in San 
Mateo. 
▪ 
Guidance for Assessing Significance – This section of the document presents a discussion of the 
national, state, and local designation criteria. It also has an overview of the integrity 
requirements and also identifies associated property types, architectural styles, and registration 
requirements for assessing historical significance of in San Mateo.

https://ohp.parks.ca.gov/pages/1072/files/WritingHistoricContexts.pdf.  
6 Office of Historic Preservation, “OHP Preferred Format for Historic Context Statements” (California Department of Parks and Recreation, 
n.d.): https://ohp.parks.ca.gov/pages/1072/files/Format-for-Historic-Context-Statements.pdf.

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
14 
3 
Previous Surveys and Studies 
3.1 
Previous Surveys 
3.1.1 
1989 Survey and Historic Building Survey Final Report 
In 1988–1989, the San Mateo County Historical Association undertook a historic resources survey 
effort in partnership with the OHP, with the help of professionals and volunteers. The 1989 survey 
generally focused on the east side of El Camino Real, where the bulk of the City’s pre-1940 buildings 
were present.7 Buildings were evaluated for eligibility for listing in the National Register of Historic 
Places (NRHP) and were assigned a rating of 1–6, corresponding with its potential eligibility 
according to OHP’s Survey Workbook.8  
As a result of the survey and evaluations, as outlined in the 1989 City of San Mateo Historic Building 
Survey Final Report, several buildings were recommended NRHP-eligible for individual listing, 
potentially NRHP-eligible pending further research, or eligible for local listing, and some were 
recommended ineligible for the NRHP and local listing. Additionally, two historic districts were 
documented and recommended eligible for listing in the NRHP, the Central Business District and the 
Glazenwood Residential District.9 
3.1.2 
City of San Mateo Historic Preservation Ordinance 
At the time of the 1989 survey, the City did not have a historic preservation ordinance. In 1990, the 
City adopted the Revised General Plan, thereby identifying all individually eligible and contributor 
historic structures based on the survey report. The City adopted a historic preservation ordinance in 
1993.  
In November 1993, the City passed a resolution establishing the Downtown Historic District, the 
City’s only locally designated historic district.10 The ordinance was subsequently updated in 1995 to 
expand its applicability to properties throughout the city and not only properties in the Downtown 
Area Specific Plan and Downtown Historic District.11 
3.1.3 
City of San Mateo General Plan 
As part of the General Plan 2030, the City’s historic preservation ordinance was updated in 2010 to 
expand its applicability to buildings that met the criteria for listing in the NRHP and California 
Register of Historical Resources (CRHR) and were documented in a form consistent with the 1989 
City of San Mateo Historic Building Survey.  
In 2024, the City adopted General Plan 2040, which replaced the prior General Plan 2030. The 
General Plan addressed historic preservation as part of the Community Design and Historic 
Resources Element. Within this element, the Historic Resources section includes a goal for historic 
preservation (Goal CD-5), to “preserve historic and culturally important resources to maintain San 
 
7 City of San Mateo, City of San Mateo Historic Building Survey Final Report (City of San Mateo, 1989), 
https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/DocumentCenter/View/47850/Historic-Building-Survey-1989.  
8 Today, these are known as the California Historical Resource Status Codes and were last updated in 2020 to include more detailed 
information. 
9 City of San Mateo, City of San Mateo Historic Building Survey Final Report. 
10 City of San Mateo, Establishing the Downtown Historic District, Ordinance Number 124, 1993. 
11 City of San Mateo, Amending Chapter 27.66 Regarding Historic Preservation, Ordinance Number 1995-13, 1995. 
396 of 1110

--- page 397 ---
Previous Surveys and Studies 
 
Historic Context Statement 
15 
Mateo’s special identity and continuity with the past.”12 The General Plan included corresponding 
policies and actions, including Action CD 5.8 (Historic Preservation Ordinance), which has been 
updated in conjunction with the preparation of this Historic Context Statement, and Action CD 5.9 
(Historic Resource Context Statements), the impetus for this report which states: 
Prepare a citywide historic context statement to guide future historic resource survey efforts to 
identify individually eligible resources and historic districts. If a neighborhood is identified as a 
historic district, prepare a more detailed historic context statement for that individual 
neighborhood.

several NRHP- and CRHR-listed resources, as outlined in Table 1 below.

3.2 
California Historical Resources Information Center 
Search 
On May 5, 2025, California Historical Resources Information System staff from the Northwest 
Information Center, located at Sonoma State University, conducted a records search (#24-1587) for 
the study area (Appendix A). The Northwest Information Center is the official state repository for 
cultural resources records and reports for San Mateo County. The purpose of the records search was 
to identify previously prepared surveys, known significant resource types, resource locations, and 
reporting efforts and written histories. Results of the records search have been incorporated herein, 
as appropriate.

4 
Significant Periods, Themes, and Property 
Types 
This section provides an overview of the major chronological periods discussed in the Historic 
Context Statement and a summary of the significant themes and property types associated with 
these major periods of development. 
4.1 
Chronological Periods 
The Historic Context Statement divides the prehistory and history of San Mateo into chronologically 
divided periods of development, which are further divided into overarching themes: 
▪ 
Precontact  
▪ 
Spanish and Mexican Period (1768–1848) 
▪ 
Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
▪ 
Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
▪ 
World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
▪ 
Modern San Mateo (1980–2025) 
4.2 
Significant Themes 
A significant theme is a “means of organizing properties into coherent patterns based on elements 
such as environment, social/ethnic groups, transportation networks, technology, or political 
developments of an aera during one or more period of prehistory or history.”13 For this Historic 
Context Statement, significant themes have been concentrated into six themes: 
▪ 
Residential development 
▪ 
Commercial development 
▪ 
Civic and institutional development 
▪ 
Cultural and ethnic groups 
▪ 
Transportation infrastructure 
▪ 
Industrial development 
Themes related to architectural significance are addressed in Architectural Styles (Chapter 11) and 
Architects, Landscape Architects, Developers, and Builders (Chapter 12).  
Each chronological chapter contains a summary of events, persons, and developments of each 
period in San Mateo. It then includes an in-depth analysis of themes associated with the 
chronological period. The following describes the themes that are analyzed in each chronological 
chapter. 
 
13 National Park Service, National Register Bulletin 15: How to Apply the National Register Criteria for Evaluation (U.S. Department of the 
Interior, 1997a), 8: https://www.nps.gov/subjects/nationalregister/upload/NRB-15_web508.pdf.

Significant Periods, Themes, and Property Types

17 
4.2.1 
Residential Development 
This section discusses important residential development in San Mateo within a given time period. It 
includes a presentation of important settlement patterns, population growth trends and changes, 
architectural styles, and property types. It also includes information on important persons or 
architects, as appropriate. 
4.2.2 
Commercial Development 
This section presents major businesses and important figures in the development of commercial 
enterprise in San Mateo during a given period. It also includes a discussion of events or 
circumstances that influenced new commercial development or cause existing commercial 
development to change or end. It also describes the architecture of the city’s commercial 
development during a given period. 
4.2.3 
Civic and Institutional Development 
This section discusses the growth of San Mateo’s government and significant persons and 
institutions such as schools, churches, fraternal organizations and women’s clubs, charitable 
organizations, and civic groups. It features a review of their associated property types. 
4.2.4 
Transportation Infrastructure 
This section includes major transportation themes for a given period. This includes the arrival of the 
railroad and its influence on San Mateo, major transportation routes, trolley service, bridges, 
highways, and airfields that connect San Mateo to the region and beyond. 
4.2.5 
Industrial Development 
This section discusses major industrial developments and important figures in the development of 
the city’s industrial activities during a given period. It also includes a discussion of events or 
circumstances that influenced how and when industries developed.  
4.2.6 
Cultural and Ethnic Groups 
This section examines the contributions of cultural and ethnic groups to San Mateo for a given 
period. It includes a discussion of the influence of cultural, ethnic, and immigrant communities to 
the city’s physical, cultural, architectural, and commercial development.  
4.3 
Associated Property Types 
Each significant theme includes a discussion of illustrative examples of associated property types. A 
property type may relate to one or more historic context themes. Property types are based on 
historic use and common physical or associative characteristics. Physical attributes include style, 
structural type, size, scale, proportions, design, architectural details, method of construction, 
orientation, spatial arrangement or plan, materials, workmanship, artistry, and environmental 
relationships.14 The discussion of each property types includes relevant architectural styles and 
 
14 National Park Service, National Register Bulletin 16B: How to Complete the National Register Multiple Property Documentation Form 
(U.S. Department of the Interior, 1999), 14: https://www.nps.gov/subjects/nationalregister/upload/NRB16B-Complete.pdf.

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
18 
building types prevalent in San Mateo during the identified period of development. The following 
property types were identified in San Mateo. 
4.3.1 
Residential Properties 
Residential properties are primarily intended for personal use. They include single-family residences, 
townhouses, duplexes, and condominium and apartment complexes. 
4.3.2 
Commercial Properties 
Commercial properties cater to business activities. They include retail stores, shopping 
malls/centers, motels and hotels, and restaurants. They also include auto-centered businesses, such 
as gas stations, services centers, car washes, and drive-in restaurants. Retail stores may include 
mixed-use buildings with combined residential and commercial uses. 
4.3.3 
Civic and Institutional Properties 
Institutional buildings include buildings where public or civic functions are performed. While often 
City-owned or publicly owned, institutional buildings may be privately owned (like community 
centers), but usually have a public use, accessible for congregation. They can include libraries, social 
halls, schools, police and fire stations, churches, community centers, post offices, and hospitals. 
They may also include public art, recreational properties, and designed landscapes, such as parks 
and plazas. 
4.3.4 
Transportation Infrastructure 
Transportation properties are those associated with transportation and include depots, rail lines, 
terminals, stations, roads, highways, trails, bridges, and road-related infrastructure, such as light 
posts and signposts.  
4.3.5 
Industrial Properties 
Industrial properties are where goods are made, stored, repaired, or processed. Industrial 
properties could include mills/factories, dairies, nurseries, canneries, warehouses, lumberyards, and 
light-industrial properties.  
4.3.6 
Cultural and Ethnic Group Properties 
Properties associated with cultural and ethnic groups could be represented by any property type. 
For example, they could include residences in ethnic enclave neighborhoods, commercial properties 
associated with a business that catered to or typified a specific ethnic or cultural group, community-
serving religious properties, or institutional properties associated with a specific ethnic or cultural 
group.

Historic Context Statement 
 
20 
5 
Precontact History of San Mateo 
5.1 
Precontact History 
The precontact history of indigenous groups living in San Mateo County follows general patterns 
identified within the archaeological record of the San Francisco Bay archaeological region. The San 
Francisco Bay archaeological region is generally divided by the pre-contact chronology of the Bay 
Area into five periods: Early Holocene, Early Period, Lower Middle Period, Upper Middle Period, and 
Late Period.15  
It is presumed that early Paleoindian groups lived in the area prior to 8000 before common era 
(BCE) due to evidence in Alta California and the Channel Islands.16 However, no evidence for this 
period has been discovered in the San Francisco Bay Area.17 Sites dating to this period may be 
submerged or deeply buried as a result of rising sea levels and widespread sediment deposition that 
has occurred since the Terminal Pleistocene.18 For this reason, the Terminal Pleistocene Period 
(c 11,700 to 8000 BCE) is not discussed here. 
The earliest intensive study of archaeology of the San Francisco Bay Area began with N. C. Nelson of 
the University of California, Berkeley, between 1906 and 1908. Mr. Nelson documented over 400 
shell mounds throughout the area. Nelson was the first to identify the Bay Area as a discrete 
archaeological region.19

Increased sedentism with the introduction of 
new ground stone technologies like mortar and 
pestle replaced millingslabs and handstones, 
indicating a greater reliance on processing nuts, 
especially acorns. Faunal evidence from various 
sites during this period indicates a diverse faunal

mussels and other shellfish, marine mammals, 
terrestrial mammals, and birds within sites

Lower Middle 
Period 
500 BCE to 430 CE 
The presence of chipped stone points and bone 
tools became typical during this period. 
Rectangular shell beads, common during the 
Early Period, disappear completely and are 
replaced by split-beveled and saucer Olivella 
beads. Haliotis spp. ornaments, bone tools and 
ornaments, and basketry awls also became 
typical, indicating the development of coiled 
basketry technology. Mortars and pestles 
continued to be the dominant grinding tool.  
CA-ALA-309; CA-CCO-295 
Upper Middle 
Period 
430 to 1050 CE 
Around 430 CE, Olivella saucer bead trade 
networks that had been established during 
earlier periods collapsed and over half of known 
sites occupied during the Lower Middle Period 
were abandoned. Olivella saucer beads were 
replaced with Olivella saddle beads. New types 
of material culture appear within these sites, 
including elaborate, decorative blades, fishtail 
charmstones, new Haliotis spp. ornament forms, 
and mica ornaments. Sea otter bones became 
more abundant, while salmon and other fish 
became less so, suggesting changes in faunal 
exploitation patterns from earlier periods. San 
Francisco Bay Area individuals shifted from 
hunting higher trophic-level foods in the Early 
Period to gathering foods like plants and 
shellfish in the Middle and Upper periods.  
CA-ALA-309 
Late Period 
1050 CE to contact 
The Late Period saw an increase in social 
complexity, indicated by differences in burials 
and an increased level of sedentism relative to 
preceding periods. An increase in imported Napa 
Valley obsidian occurred during this time for the 
production of smaller points, preforms and 
simple flake tools. Small, finely worked projectile 
points of the Stockton Serrated series associated 
with bow and arrow technology appear around 
1250 CE. Olivella shell beads disappeared and 
were replaced with Olivella-lipped and spire-
lopped beads in the south bay and clamshell disk 
beads in the north bay. Thicker and larger beads 
indicated higher affluence. The toggle harpoon, 
hopper mortar, and magnesite tube beads also 
appeared during this period.  
 
BCE = before common era; CE common era 
5.2 
Ethnohistoric Context 
San Mateo is in the traditional territory of the Ohlone (or Costanoan) people. According to early 
ethnographers, Ohlone territory extends along the California coast from the point where the San 
Joaquin and Sacramento rivers merge into the San Francisco Bay to Point Sur. Their inland boundary 
was limited to the interior Coast Ranges. The Ohlone language belongs to the Penutian family, with 
403 of 1110

--- page 404 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
22 
several distinct dialects throughout the region.20 Ethnographers divided it into eight regional 
dialects: Karkin, Chochenyo, Ramaytush, Awaswas, Taymen, Mutsun, Rumsen, and Chalon.21 
The pre-contact Ohlone were semi-sedentary with a settlement system characterized by base camps 
and seasonal reserve camps composed of tule reed houses with thatched roofs made of matted 
grass.22 Just outside base camps, large sweat houses were built into the ground near stream banks 
used for spiritual ceremonies and possibly hygiene.23 Villages were divided into small polities, each 
of which was governed by a chief responsible for settling disputes, acting as a war leader during 
times of conflict, and supervising economic and ceremonial activities.24 Social organization appeared 
flexible to ethnographers, and any sort of social hierarchy was not apparent to mission priests.25 
Archaeological investigations and ethnographic evidence helped inform understanding of Ohlone 
mortuary rituals. Cemeteries were set away from villages and visited during the annual Mourning 
Anniversary.26Ceremonial human grave offerings might include Olivella beads, as well as tools like 
drills, mortars, pestles, hammerstones, bone awls, and used flakes.27 Ohlone mythology includes 
animal characterization and animism, which was the basis for several creation narratives. Ritually 
burying animals, such as a wolf, squirrel, deer, mountain lion, gray fox, elk, badger, grizzly bear, blue 
goose, and bat ray, was commonly practiced. Similar to human burials, ceremonial offerings were 
added to ritual animal graves like shell beads, ornaments, and exotic goods.28  
Ohlone food sources were based on hunting, gathering, and fishing.29 Larger animals, like bears, 
might be avoided, but smaller game was hunted and snared on a regular basis.30 The acorn was an 
important staple and was prepared by leaching acorn meal in openwork baskets and in holes dug 
into the sand.31 The Ohlone also practiced controlled burning to facilitate plant growth.32 During 
specific seasons or in times of drought, the reserve camps would be used for gathering seasonal 
food and accessing food storage.33 The Ohlone fished from tule reed canoes using nets and gorge 
hooks.34 Mussels were a particularly important food resource. Sea mammals, such as sea lions and 
seals, were hunted, and beached whales were consumed.35

404 of 1110

--- page 405 ---
Precontact History of San Mateo 
 
Historic Context Statement 
23 
Seven Franciscan missions were built in Ohlone territory in the late 1700s, and all members of the 
Ohlone group were eventually forced into the mission system.36 The missions, a method of Spanish 
imperialism, separated missionized people from their indigenous and ceremonial practices, as well 
as the seasonal rhythm of their own food production practices. After the establishment of the 
missions, Ohlone population dwindled from roughly 10,000 people in 1770 to 1,300 by 1814.37 In 
1973, the population of people with Ohlone descent was estimated at fewer than 300. The 
descendants of the Ohlone united in 1971 and have since arranged political and cultural 
organizations to revitalize aspects of their culture.38 Today, the descendant communities of the 
Ohlone can be found in multiple tribes throughout Northern and Central California.  
 
36 Kroeber, Handbook of the Indians of California, Bulletin 78, 465.; Skowronek, “Sifting the Evidence: Perceptions of Life at the Ohlone 
(Costanoan) Missions of Alta California,” 675–708.; Milliken, R.T. et al., “Punctuated Cultural Change in the San Francsico Bay Area,” 99–
124. 
37 Skowronek, “Sifting the Evidence: Perceptions of Life at the Ohlone (Costanoan) Missions of Alta California,” 675–708.

--- page 406 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
24 
6 
Spanish and Mexican Period 
6.1 
Spanish Period (1769–1822) 
Although Spanish, Russian, and British explorers visited the area for brief periods between 1529 and 
1769, the Spanish Period in California begins with the establishment of a settlement at San Diego in 
1769 and the founding of Mission San Diego de Alcalá by Franciscan Father Junípero Serra, the first 
of 21 missions constructed between 1769 and 1823.39 Juan Rodriguez Cabrillo led the first European 
expedition to observe what was known by the Spanish as Alta (upper) California in 1542. For more 
than 200 years, Cabrillo and other Spanish, Portuguese, British, and Russian explorers sailed the Alta 
California coast and made limited inland expeditions, but they did not establish permanent 
settlements.40 The Spanish crown laid claim to Alta California based on the surveys conducted by 
Cabríllo in 1542 and later survey by Sebastian Vizcaíno in 1602.41 
The 1769 overland expedition by Captain Gaspár de Portolá marks the beginning of California’s 
Historic period, occurring just after the King of Spain installed the Franciscan Order to direct 
religious and colonization matters in assigned territories of the Americas.42 Portola’s 1769 
expedition is credited with the discovery of the San Francisco Bay from the hills west of present-day 
Millbrae, though members of the expedition did not appear to regard the discovery of the bay as 
anything but an obstacle at the time.43 In 1775, San Mateo’s bay line was surveyed by Juan Bautista 
Aguirre and finally in 1776, Spanish arrived in San Mateo and camped. By this time, the Spanish 
decided that San Francisco should serve as the site of the presidio and mission in the region.44 
Lieutenant Colonel Juan Bautista de Anza was charged with bringing a colony of families to settle 
the northern tip of the Peninsula and establish the site for the mission and the presidio. On March 
26, 1776, the group crossed San Francisquito Creek and continued north where party member Padre 
Pedro Font reported they “came to a good-sized village situated on the banks of the Arroyo of San 
Matheo. It has many laurels and ash trees on its banks.”45 The area was inhabited by Ohlone and 
Font commented that, “they appear to be gentle Indians, and it would seem possible to form of 
them a good and large mission”46 (Figure 1). Anza began his return trip on March 29, 1776 and sent 
his group back to “Arroyo San Matheo” the way they had come, roughly following today’s El Camino 
Real.47 It is not known why Padre Font bestowed the creek the name San Matheo, after Saint 
Matthew. 
 
39 Walton E. Bean, California: An Interpretive History (McGraw-Hill Book Company, 1968), 50. 
40 Bean, California: An Interpretive History, 16–18.

Spanish and Mexican Period 
 
Historic Context Statement 
25 
Figure 1 
Map of Shellmounds in Vicinity of San Mateo Creek 
 
Source: San Mateo County Historical Association 
The colonization of the San Francsico Peninsula began on June 17, 1776. A party commanded by 
Lieutenant José Moraga camped at San Mateo Creek on June 24 for three days before continuing to 
San Francisco to begin work on creating the Mission San Francisco de Asis and the San Francisco 
Presidio.48 The requirements for a good mission site were well-known and required: good access to 
native populations, good grazing land, and good soil and climate for crops.49 The decision to build 
the Mission San Francisco de Asis at Delores Creek was recognized as a mistake early on.50 Anza had 
reluctantly located the mission (the arm of the church) close to the presidio (an instrument of the 
government) in order to protect native converts and to enforce discipline, as persuaded by Padre 
Font and Junipero Serra. Several things were wrong with the site. There was not enough rainfall or 
freshwater to support crops, the sandy soil was not conducive for farming, and the native 
population in the vicinity was not as large as the padres had originally believed.51 
Though the circumstances saddled the mission with a permanent handicap, it also created 
circumstances unique to the mission. The Presidio, the mission, and the existing Ohlone population 
were isolated on a narrow peninsula where they had to interact. From the outset, Spanish attitudes 
 
48 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 6. 
49 Frank Stanger, South from San Francisco: The Life Story of San Mateo County (San Mateo County Historical Association, 1963), 17. 
50 Alan Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb: The Story of The San Mateo Peninsula (Star Publishing Company, 1982), 19. 
51 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb: The Story of The San Mateo Peninsula, 19. 
Image Redacted Pending Permission for Use 
407 of 1110

--- page 408 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
26 
toward the native population made their assimilation into Spanish colonial culture impossible, while 
also extinguishing the possibility to gain true understanding of native traditions or experience. 
Spanish conquistadors systemically destroyed the existing knowledge of the area.52 
Relations between the Spanish and Ohlone tribes near the mission began when the Spanish offered 
token gifts and food that attracted interest. On August 12, 1776, however, a rival tribe from San 
Mateo Creek attacked a village near the mission site, frightening its residents who fled to the 
opposite shore. Unimpressed with the Spaniards’ offer to defend them, contact was retracted.53 In 
Spring of 1777, contact was renewed, and three days before the first anniversary of their arrival, the 
mission baptized its first Bay Area Native Americans.54 
Slowly, other populations of Ohlone were brought to the mission. The mission listed 135 baptisms of 
Native Americans from the San Mateo area in 1780 and 1786.55 By 1793, virtually all native 
populations of the San Francsico Peninsula had been baptized. Less than half of the original 
Peninsula Ohlone survived by that time, having succumbed to deadly diseases like smallpox, 
whooping cough, venereal diseases, influenza, and mumps introduced by the Spanish.56  
It was common practice for California missions to establish agricultural outposts, or asistencias or 
hospices, to grow food to sustain the mission. No mission was more dependent on such outposts as 
Mission San Francisco de Asis due to its environmental challenges. In approximately 1793, the 
padres established an outpost at San Mateo Creek. The San Mateo location had the circumstances 
for a successful settlement—good climate and soil, a location between the San Francisco and Santa 
Clara missions, fresh water, and a more dense native population.57 San Mateo outpost’s function 
was three-fold: the rites of the church would be practiced there; it would function as a base of 
operations for neophytes tending cattle and sheep; and it would provide storage for agricultural 
products.58 
 
52 Stanger, South from San Francisco: The Life Story of San Mateo County, 19. 
53 Stanger, South from San Francisco: The Life Story of San Mateo County, 19. 
54 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 20. 
55 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 6. 
56 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 22.

Spanish and Mexican Period 
 
Historic Context Statement 
27 
Figure 2 
1850 Drawing of San Mateo Hospice 
 
Source: San Mateo County Historical Association 
The outpost was constructed near the Moraga campsite. Mission records describe the first building 
in San Mateo as a three-room structure with a tile roof.59 By 1800, the San Mateo outpost was the 
center of food production on the Peninsula.60 Thirty mission-trained Native Americans lived at the 
outpost by this time. In 1810, the adobe was destroyed by earthquake and subsequently rebuilt 
larger (Figure 2). Even as Mexico was achieving independence from Spain, life at the outpost carried 
on as usual. Official mission documents reveal that by 1822 at least two buildings in San Mateo 
served as granaries.61 
6.2 
Mexican Period (1822–1848) 
After more than a decade of intermittent rebellion and warfare, Mexico won independence from 
Spain in 1821. In San Mateo, change was slow. By 1822, support for the missions was cut, while the 
Mexican constitution removed the Spanish monopoly on free trade, allowing California ports to 
open to foreign merchants.62  
The secularization of the missions following Mexico’s independence from Spain did not begin until 
1834 under Governor Jose Figueroa. In the meantime, merchants from around the world were 
interested in California products such as hides and tallow. The new business created demand for 
land from Spanish soldiers, government officials, and pioneers. Former mission lands were 
subdivided for large land grant ranchos. Commonly, former soldiers and well-connected Mexican 
families were the recipients of these land grants, which now included the title to the land and often 
included mission Native Americans who became laborers in a feudal system.63 
 
59 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 9. 
60 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 23. 
61 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 10. 
62 S.F. Dallas, “The Hide and Tallow Trade in Alto California 1822-1848,” (PhD diss., Indiana University), 1955. 
63 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 13. 
Image Redacted Pending Permission for Use 
409 of 1110

--- page 410 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
28 
In San Mateo, there were reportedly about 30 native Californians at the time of secularization. San 
Mateo was claimed by the José Argüello family, who asserted that Spanish Governor Diego Borcia 
awarded José Argüello 69,000 acres of land south of Rancho Buri Buri in 1795. The land, Rancho de 
las Pulgas, ultimately comprised of 35,000 acres was not awarded until November 1835 to the 
widow of Argüello’s son, Dona Maria and their children.64 During this period San Mateo remained 
largely unchanged. In fact, the population south of San Francico declined during the interlude of 
Mexican rule and did not exceed 500 persons.65 
The rancheros were land rich, but cash poor and vulnerable to squatters and predatory money 
lenders and lawyers. As a result, after becoming entangled in years of litigation, many rancho heirs 
lost vast portions of their holdings. The Argüello’s, whose holdings spanned from present-day Mills 
Hospital to the Santa Clara County line, hired a Spanish lawyer, Simón Mezes in exchange for a 
generous share of Rancho de las Pulgas. By the 1838, the rancho contained 4,000 cattle and 2,000 
horses and began to be populated with squatters who believed the Land Commission was not going 
to approve the title to the land, and ownership would default to the government presenting an 
opportunity for ownership.66 In 1856, however, the Argüello’s were successful at defending their full 
title.67  
Despite secularization, the area north of San Mateo Creek, known as Rancho San Mateo, was 
reserved for Native Californians and the asistencia was apparently active. An embarcadero, perhaps 
the same used during mission times, continued to be employed with grain from the outpost.68 In 
1839, the entire San Francico mission Ohlone population was moved to San Mateo by Mexican 
administrator W.E.P. Hartnell, bringing the population to 89 or 90.69 By 1840, the Ohlone population 
was endangered. In 1846, Mexican governor Pio Pico awarded the 6,000-acre Rancho San Mateo to 
Cayetano Arenas of Los Angeles for his work in the governor’s office. His land occupied the area 
between Rancho Buri Buri to the north and Rancho de las Pulgas to the south and included the area 
of the former outpost.70 Arenas, however, never occupied the land and sold it to San Francisco 
merchants William D.M. Howard and Henry Mullus in 1846, perhaps assuming the land would be 
taken away by American troops who occupied the Bay Area by that time. William D.M. Howard 
arrived in San Francisco in about 1838 from New England. He opened a San Francisco store and 
acted as an agent for several New England firms. He formed a partnership with Mellus in 1845, and 
they purchased stock of the Hudson Bay Company’s failing business, just before the Gold Rush.71 
The United States went to war with Mexico in April 1846. The war ended in 1848 with the signing of 
the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo just days before the announcement of the gold discovery at 
Sutter’s Mill, ushering California into its American period.72

Spanish and Mexican Period

--- page 412 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
30 
7 
Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
Because the Peninsula was close to the epicenter of the Gold Rush, it experienced its impact and 
change sooner and more dramatically than other parts of the state. San Francisco’s ballooning 
population was demanding food and lumber, and was buying, leasing, or squatting on land to make 
it produce. The net result was the transformation of cattle lands into community farms.73 During the 
following years in San Mateo, formerly undeveloped land transformed to support a burgeoning 
population. 
7.1 
Residential Development  
San Mateo’s earliest residential development began with the establishment of country homes or 
estates of wealthy businessmen who largely made their fortunes in the early days of the Gold Rush. 
For many years, the Rancho San Mateo land purchased by Howard and Mellus remained vacant. 
Howard subsequently made a fortune selling real estate and retired from his partnership with 
Mellus in 1850.  
In 1849, Howard moved to the former ranch, drove out the remaining Ohlone population, and built 
a small villa not far from San Mateo Creek and El Camino. He began importing cattle, becoming the 
first Peninsula resident to convert a rancho into a county estate.74 One component of Howard’s 
business was purchasing prefabricated houses from the eastern United States in the 1850s. He 
constructed a village of modest cottages in Happy Valley, along Market Street in San Francisco. For 
this own country estate in San Mateo, called El Cerrito for the small hill it sat on, it appears he 
purchased several prefabricated houses and combined them for a grand building on what is now De 
Salba Road of El Camino Real (Figure 3). Howard died in 1856, leaving the southern portion of the 
rancho to his widow, Agnes Poett. He left the northern portion (much of present-day Hillsborough) 
to his father-in-law, Dr. Joseph Henry Poett, who in turn gave the western portion to his other 
daughter Julia Redington. Agnes married W.M.D. Howard’s brother, George and they went on to 
have four children they raised in El Cerrito. After his death 1878, she married Henry Bowie in 1879.75 
As a result, the Howard, Poett, Reddington families controlled most of Rancho San Mateo for 
decades, establishing many of the Peninsula’s great estates.76 El Cerrito became known as the Bowie 
Estate and was moved to Roehampton Road in 1910 and served as Hillsborough’s first town hall 
before being demolished in the 1930s.77

76 Postel, San Mateo County: A Sesquicentennial History, 203.

Early Settlement (1849–1893)

31 
Figure 3 
W.M.D. Howard’s Estate, El Cerrito 
 
Source: San Mateo County Historical Association 
Other early pioneers to San Mateo, included Captain Frederick Macondray who established an 
estate, Brookside, on the south side of the creek, facing the Howard estate on present-day El 
Camino Real. More arrived in the area to work for the Howard family and established their own 
houses, while few others began to sparsely populate the area, including Stephen Whipple who 
established a horse farm and ranch in the 1850s, no longer extant, at the present-day location of St. 
Matthew’s Catholic School, at El Camino Real and Aragon Boulevard. David McLellan bought 300 
acres from the Argüello holdings, where he set up his country home, in the Bay Meadows land, later 
replaced by a racetrack and eventual residential development. Lemuel T. Murray built a dairy ranch 
on much of the land in the current Hillsdale neighborhood.78 
With the promise and eventual arrival of the railroad in 1863, other wealthy San Franciscans moved 
to San Mateo, including Charles Pohlemus, a director of the railroad, who bought most of the future 
downtown from the creek southward and east of El Camino. He laid out town lots during the 1860s, 
reserving some for houses and some for commercial uses. The rest of the land remained 
undeveloped for many years (Figure 4). Alvinza Hayward, an ex-lawyer from New York who made a 
fortune mining in Amador County, similarly purchased a several hundred-acre tract south and east 
of Pohlemus’s tract in the early 1860s. Hayward gradually subdivided the property for development 
and constructed his own estate, at the present-day corner of 9th and Palm avenues. It was a three-
story, stick-style mansion (non-extant) surrounded by an extravagant private park called Hayward 
Park.79  
 
78 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 34–35. 
79 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 92.

City of San Mateo

1862 Map of San Mateo Prepared for Charles Pohlemus

In 1860, John Parrott, a San Francsico banker, purchased Macondray’s Brookside estate, on the west 
side of El Camino north of 5th Avenue, to serve as his summer home. Parrott updated Macondray’s 
house into a Second Empire-style mansion on the property in 1868 and named it Baywood (non-
extant) for the property’s many trees (Figure 5).80 Parrott’s son and six daughters married into other 
wealthy families, like the Donahues, Paysons, Borels, and DeGuignes. The Parrotts and the other 
estate holders of this era became a social oligarchy, whose vast houses encircled the growing 
downtown.

Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
33 
Figure 5 
Parrott House, c. 1868 
 
Source: Getty Research Institute 
For many years, San Mateo’s growth, including its residential expansion was only possible when 
large landholders subdivided their holdings for sale. Polhemus sold his San Mateo property to Peter 
Donahue in 1866 before it eventually passed to William H. Kohl in 1874. Kohl, an engineer and sea 
captain built his estate (no longer extant) in present-day Central Park, whose gates date to Kohl’s 
mansion.81 The city’s first residential subdivision was on the east side of Railroad Avenue, extending 
from San Mateo Creek southward to 5th Avenue, and eastward to present-day Eldorado Street. 
Early settlers like James Byrnes (713 First Street—extant) and John Maynard (809 Lawrence Road—
extant) built Italianate houses along San Mateo Creek. Local histories suggest William Sands built his 
residence at 45 South Delaware in the early 1860s. In 1865, John Quincy Adams Tilton bought 25 
 
81 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 49. 
415 of 1110

--- page 416 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
34 
acres of land from the Howards, north of the Polhemus town site, and constructed a residence (36 
N. Tilton Avenue—extant), but early residential development was otherwise sparse.82 
In 1888, the Howard family, capitalizing on the considerable activity brought to San Mateo from the 
nearby construction of the Crystal Springs Dam, subdivided land north of San Mateo Creek to Poplar 
Avenue and from the railroad tracks to Delaware for the Western Addition subdivision.83 The Borel 
family subdivided the area south of Monte Diablo Avenue, east of El Dorado Street to San Mateo 
Creek. Though more landholders like Howard and Borel began to parcel land for sale in the late 
1880s, during the 1890s, the city limits extended just a few blocks beyond Polhemus’s 1862 plat 
(Figure 7). These earliest residential subdivisions occurred in what is known today as San Mateo’s 
Central neighborhood and Western Addition. Although land was subdivided, it was slow to develop. 
Examples from this period of residential development typically included wood-frame one- and two-
story buildings on several blocks of Claremont and Delaware streets, and a handful of examples 
from this period are still present (Figure 6). The city’s south end, however, was generally occupied 
by stock farms and grain fields that did not become residential areas until the twentieth century, 
while the bayside was tideflats.

Early Settlement (1849–1893)

7.2 
Commercial Development 
San Mateo’s early commercial development began with the establishment of a stagecoach stop 
along the old mission trail between San Francisco and San José in 1849. As a result, a few 
commercial structures were established in the area. Recognizing the business opportunity of the 
stagecoach traffic, Nicholas de Peyster, a New York merchandiser moved into the old outpost in San 
Mateo with a partner and sold liquor and whatever merchandise he thought travelers would want.84 
He operated the location for a year before purchasing a portion of Rancho de las Pulgas, in San 
Mateo’s present downtown, and constructed the San Mateo House.85 In 1856, David S. Cook bought 
out de Peyster, which had been enlarged with a prefabricated building from the east coast.86 In 
1857, San Mateo House was sold to a partnership of Stockton and Shafter, and it was used as a 
stagecoach stop for the Butterfield Stage, which traveled between Missouri to San Francisco.87 The 
house was sold to Tony Oakes in 1863 and Captain Edward Taylor in 1863 who moved it away from 
the road for his residence and added to the house a second time (Figure 8). Located at the present-
day location of Mills Hospital at El Camino Real and 2nd Avenue, it was demolished in 1964 for a 
parking lot. 
 
84 Stanger, South from San Francisco, 65. 
85 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 26. 
86 David Fredricks, “Life of the San Mateo House,” The Daily Journal, March 3, 2014.  
87 Fredricks, “Life of the San Mateo House.”

City of San Mateo

Figure 8 
1875 Image of San Mateo House  
 
Source: Careleton Watkins, The Sun Sketches of San Mateo Album, University of California, Berkeley Bancroft Library 
By 1860, there was stagecoach service between San Mateo and the coast. San Mateo’s 
advantageous location at the east end of the Peninsula’s main road to the coast supported limited 
commercial development, and during this period San Mateo had a grain and feed store, a livery 
stable, a blacksmith, and two saloons.88 But, without a strong industry or commercial force, there 
was not enough activity to stimulate significant growth, trade, or development.89 
Commercial development emerged significantly in the years after the railroad was constructed in 
1864.90 With the arrival of the railroad, the sparse development that had begun along the old 
mission trail shifted east toward the location of the tracks. San Mateo’s commercial downtown 
began to radiate from the rail station, beginning along Main Street and Railroad Avenue. B Street, 
one block west of the railroad station, eventually developed as a major thoroughfare. During the 
1880s, however, the city’s downtown was plagued by a series of fires in 1880, 1883, and 1887 that 
destroyed early commercial buildings and their businesses.91 
By 1888, the first Sanborn fire insurance map shows that commercial development along Railroad 
Avenue included the Union Hotel (1883), a grocer, dry good stores, and a saloon (non-extant). 
Development along A and B Street between 2nd and 5th Streets included a mix of residential and 
commercial uses like general stores, boarding houses, a restaurant, and a saloon concentrated along 
 
88 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 94. 
89 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 92. 
90 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 39.
