Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
37 
B Street.92 It also included the first service storefronts or workshops, like the plumber/carpenter at 
the corner of Fourth and B Streets and Robert Wisnom’s lumberyard at B Street and 2nd Street.  
Additional commercial uses located on the east side Railroad Avenue, between 2nd and 5th Streets, 
were less dense and included lodgings, saloons, and livery stables. Buildings were individual 
buildings and generally occupied only a portion of the parcel, though mostly wood structures began 
to be replaced by more permanent brick buildings during this period (Figure 9). The Spring Valley 
Water Company, who was amid constructing the Crystal Springs Dam west of the present city limits 
to provide drinking water to San Francsico and the Peninsula, had a warehouse structure just south 
of the San Mateo Creek, along a block of rail line. The warehouse appears to have been removed 
shortly following completion of the dam in 1889.93 During this period, the streets were unpaved and 
what sidewalks that were present were wood.94  
Because of the town’s railroad stop and stage station, San Mateo was a popular place for estate 
residents to do business before going back to their estates, tourists to stop on their way to the 
coast, or coastal dwellers to stop on their way to do business in Redwood City or San Francisco. As 
such, businesses were generally limited to those that met basic needs and lodging for overnight 
trips. Despite having a railroad stop and a stage station, there was little to hold a visitor in town for 
very long.  
Figure 9 
ca.1883 View of B Street 
 
Source: San Mateo Public Library, San Mateo Local History Collection 
Though San Mateo experienced somewhat slow commercial growth in the decades after the arrival 
of the railroad in 1864, several important emerging local businesses were established during this 
period. Robert Wisnom, born in Northern Ireland, arrived in San Mateo in 1868 to work as a

carpenter at the Parrott estate. Wisnom subsequently settled in San Mateo and became the area’s 
best-known builder, responsible for the construction of Union Hall (1874; non-extant) and many 
early residences (56 North Claremont non-extant and 707 First Avenue extant). He also opened a 
successful lumber business downtown and the city block outlined by B Street, Ellsworth Street, 2nd 
Street, and 1st Street was known as the Wisnom Block (non-extant). (Figure 10).95 
In 1889, Richard Jury and Charles Kirkbride founded the town’s first newspaper, San Mateo Leader. 
It went on to be consolidated with another local paper in 1914 to be a countywide paper before 
merging with the San Mateo Times in 1926.96 In 1892, the San Mateo Gas Light Company was 
incorporated by early San Mateo residents James Byrnes, E.A. Husing, Julius Wiesnder, William 
Nolden, and John P. Weller, and became part of Pacific Gas and Electric in 1905.97

Wisnom Carpenter and Builder Shop, 1880s 
 
Source: San Mateo Public Library, San Mateo Local History Collection 
7.3 
Civic and Institutional Development 
Before San Mateo’s incorporation in 1894, its development evolved from a collection of estates to a 
small village, with a nascent population with growing practical governance and community needs.  
 
95 “Robert J. “Big Bob” Wisnom,” Find a Grave, accessed October 1, 2025,  https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/87334134/robert_j-
wisnom.  
96 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 100.

420 of 1110

--- page 421 ---
Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
39 
Before 1887, the city lacked a fire department and the series of fires in the commercial core in the 
1880s highlighted the need for an organized fire service. The initial attempt to organize a local fire 
department began in 1885 by a group of local men that included A.T. Bartlett, J.H Bickford, John 
Moore, W.J. Price, and H.F. Barrows. Barrows was elected the foreman and took the charge to 
gather equipment, which originally consisted of a small hand pump donated by Captain Kohl and 
three fire hydrants donated by Howard. Though the hydrant locations, concentrated along 3rd 
Avenue and A and B streets, were convenient, the lack of fire hoses made their widespread use 
impossible and the first attempt at a department was short-lived.98 
In 1881, an order of the Ancient Order of United Workman was organized. It was followed a few 
months later by a lodge of the Amerion Legion of Honor. In December 1883, a parlor of the Native 
Sons of the Golden West was created with county-wide representation. In 1882, the women of St. 
Matthew’s Guild wanted to establish a reading room and library. They organized that same year, 
welcomed all denominations, and became the social and cultural center of San Mateo. The building 
replaced an earlier structure, Wisnom Hall, which had served as the de facto community hall until it 
burned in 1883. The Library Hall building, constructed through donations including Antoine Borel’s 
donation of the lot on B Street, was a long, brick building that had rooms to host all the local 
organizations and a library in one room.99 
In 1887, a second attempt to establish a fire department proved more successful. Five local men, 
drugstore owner Charles M. Moore; blacksmith William H. Brown, saddlery shop owner George A. 
Bartlett, Fisher Hotel owner Charles J. Hatch, and William Babcock Lawrence of the Spring Valley 
Water Company met in Library Hall and decided to call for a public meeting to create an official fire 
department. The day the meeting was meant to happen, April 6, a fire broke out at Library Hall 
destroying the newly constructed community building.100 The blaze was extinguished with the help 
of the Redwood City fire department. Though the citizens were exhausted from the day’s events, 
the meeting occurred and the community agreed a fire department was needed. On May 7, 1887, 
the San Mateo County Board of Supervisors appointed a fire commission consisting of William Price, 
E.A. Husing, and James Byrnes. Shortly thereafter the commission raised money to buy equipment, 
and a volunteer department was established. 101 
Library Hall was rebuilt through donations from the community and Robert Wisnom constructed a 
new, two-story building that contained the fire station and livery on the ground floor with a public 
hall and library upstairs. 102 Library Hall was later destroyed in the 1906 Earthquake (Figure 11). 
 
98 Jeff Brile ed., San Mateo Fire Department 1887-1990, (San Mateo Fire Department, 1990), 10. 
99 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 87. 
100 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 91.

Schools 
By the 1860s, San Mateo had two private schools—Laurel Hall for girls and St. Matthew’s Hall for 
boys. Laurel Hall was founded by educator Miss L.A. Buckmaster. A native of New York, she arrived 
in California in 1856 and worked in the Sacramento area before moving to Oakland in 1861, 
following a flood that closed her school. Buckmaster met Charles Polhemus in 1862, and he told her 
of the railroad that would soon connect San Mateo to San Francisco. Believing it sounded like a 
good opportunity to establish a new institution, she bought 27 acres from San Mateo pioneer David 
S. Cook in 1863.103 Architect S.C. Bugbee designed a Second Empire building for the school, whose 
first trustees included many members of San Francisco and Peninsula society like George H. Howard, 
Alvinza Hayward, A.H. Houston, Frederick Billings, Benjamin Lathrop, Thomas Shelby, F.F. Low, 
James Bolton, D.O Mills, R.B. Sneath, and E.A. Easton (Figure 12).104 The school (non-extant), located 
on present-day Alameda de Las Pulgas, east of the Peninsula Gold and Country Club, opened in 1864 
with 11 students and grew to be a prestigious institution by the 1870s. An advertisement for the 
school from 1877, lauds the geographical and educational benefits of the school (Figure 13).105 In 
1875, Buckmaster returned east to publicize her efforts. The school was run by Reverend George 
Herbert Watson in her absence, and the school flourished for several years. Buckmaster returned in 
1878 but fell ill thereafter. Despite efforts by alumni to save the school, it closed in 1887. 106 
 
103 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 61. 
104 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 61. 
105 Advertisement, “Laurel Hall, San Mateo, Cal.,” The Churchman, August 11, 1877. 
106 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 61.

boys, known as Laurel Hall College (Figure 14).107 The property was acquired by the Episcopal Bishop 
of San Francisco for the operation of the Armitage Orphanage until it closed in 1912.108

1888 Advertisement for Gamble’s Laurel Hall College

In 1866, Episcopal Reverend A.L. Brewer founded St. Matthew’s Hall, a boys military academy. 
Originally located on South Ellsworth, a new three-story school was constructed in 1872 on Baldwin 
Avenue, east of San Mateo Episcopal Church (Figure 15). By the 1880s, the school reputation was 
well-known, even outside the United States. In 1881, the King of Sandwich Islands visited San Mateo 
and toured the school, which required students to be in full military dress.109 Soon after, sons from 
the most influential Hawaiian families came to San Mateo. Princes David La‘amea Kawānanakoa, 
Abel Edward Keliʻiahonui, and Jonah Kūhiō Kalanianaʻole were among Pacific Islanders who 
attended the school. During their time in the area, they apparently introduced surfing to the 
mainland, at the mouth of the San Lorenzo River in nearby Santa Cruz.110 The school subsequently 
moved to Hillsborough. The buildings were reused for St. Margaret’s School for girls, which 
shuttered in 1892.111 
In addition to private schools that drew student populations from the area’s wealthy families and 
beyond, the growing community had an established school district and one-room school building on 
County Road, present-day Notre Dame Avenue and El Camino Real, by the mid-1860s. By 1871, 
there were 75 students attending San Mateo School, and the residents realized that a more 
adequate school was needed (Figure 16). In September 1871, a bond measure passed to construct a 
new school building with two classrooms, with expansion plans for two more. The old school was 
moved away from the road and the new school was constructed in front of it. The school building

https://www.kucb.org/2025-08-31/how-3-hawaiian-teen-princes-brought-surfing-to-the-mainland.

Early Settlement (1849–1893)

burned in about 1891. Following the fire, the school operated from several locations, and planning 
for a new school began immediately. 112 
In 1892, a strategy was adopted to construct two new schools—one at the center of town and one 
in the outskirts. The school at the center of town was constructed on a 3-acre site at today’s Second 
Avenue and Baldwin Avenue, between San Mateo Drive and B Street. Fittingly named Central 
School, it was a two-story wooden building with two classrooms (Figure 17). The school opened in 
1893 with Etta Tilton serving as Principal.113 The same year, the Homestead School was constructed 
at today’s Gum Street, near the Borel Estate. Intended to accommodate the more rural students, it 
was primitive and nearly obsolete when it opened. It closed in 1914, while Central School continued 
to operate until 1922.114

Churches 
San Mateo’s first church was St. Matthew’s Catholic Church. Irish priest Father Dennis F. Dempsey 
established a parish in San Mateo in 1863. He had served his apprenticeship at St. Mary’s Cathedral 
in San Francisco and worked in Sacramento and Auburn before being assigned to San Mateo County 
Image Redacted Pending Permission for Use 
426 of 1110

--- page 427 ---
Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
45 
and portions of Santa Clara County in 1862.115 The first St. Matthew’s Catholic Church was 
constructed on land donated by Polhemus at the southwest corner of 3rd Street and Ellsworth 
Avenue. The small wood-frame Gothic Revival structure opened in February 1864.116 It was replaced 
by a more permanent brick structure in 1889 (Figure 18). The Sanborn map from 1891 depicts the 
church on the corner, with a small building to its west that was used as a school room (Figure 19). 
The same year, Episcopal Reverend G.A. Easton arrived in San Mateo, and held services in the San 
Mateo Young Ladies Institute. On his recommendation, he was proceeded by Revered A. L. Brewer 
from Detroit in 1865, who had been sent by the Episcopal Board of Mission to take control of San 
Mateo and Redwood City congregations. In June 1865, a meeting was held at George Howard’s 
residence where he pledged a portion of his property to construct a church. Other members of the 
community raised money for its construction. The building’s design was donated by Albert H. 
Jordan, who along with Howard and Edward Taylor served as church leadership. The building was 
completed and consecrated on May 23, 1866.117 It was constructed with stones quarried from the 
Howard estate, apparently becoming the first stone church in California. The building measured 40 
feet by 70 feet and had seating for 200. The ceiling was of varnished wood and the windows had 
geometric tracery (Figure 20). The glass for the chancel window was made in Belgium and the 
window frame was made in New York. The northern end of the church had a mortuary chapel with a 
massive monument to the memory of Howard’s father, William Davis Merry Howard, and his widow 
Agnes Poett Howard.118

Cornerstone Laying at St. Matthew’s Catholic Church in 1889

In 1862, San Mateo’s Congregationalists began meeting after a visit from Redwood City’s acting 
pastor J.S. Zelia. A month later Reverend J.H. Warren conducted the first service in the schoolhouse. 
In 1864, 10 members decided to incorporate into as the Congregational Church of San Mateo. 
George Howard also gifted the land for construction of the Congregational church, at the corner of 
Tilton Avenue and North Ellsworth (Figure 21).119 The land included the construction of a parsonage 
for Reverend J.H. Warren, the congregation’s first pastor, in 1864. In 1869, a simple wood church 
was constructed.120The Congregational church was the smallest of the local churches. In its first five 
years, it had four different ministers. By 1884, the church had just 15 members.121

Early Settlement (1849–1893) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
47 
Figure 21 
Congregational Church of San Mateo, 1869 
 
Source: Congregational Church of San Mateo 
Cemeteries 
The first cemetery was established in 1877 by Agnes Tilton. She founded a 20-acre cemetery near 
her house at present-day 36 North Claremont Street (extant). She named the cemetery Evergreen, 
and it served the community for about eight years, before being moved up the hill to St. John’s 
Cemetery.122 Founded in 1885 with land donated by Abby M. Parrott to the Roman Catholic Church, 
the cemetery was named St. John’s to honor her late husband, John Parrott. When founded, the 
cemetery had sections for Catholics, Protestants, and paupers.123 
7.4 
Transportation Infrastructure 
The County Road, now known as El Camino Real was the earliest regional connection. A string of 
roadhouses or mile houses developed along El Camino Real to serve stagecoaches. The first 
stagecoach service was founded shortly after the Gold Rush. Later that year, John W. Whistman 
started the first service to connect San Francisco to San José. The service was uncomfortable and 
consisted of a wagon with benches pulled by mules. In San Mateo, Nicholas de Peyster’s Half Way 
House, so named for being the half-way point between the journey from San Francsico to San José, 
was a resting stop for travelers.124  
The most important transportation development during this period was the arrival of the railroad in 
1864. Charles B. Polhemus, the director of the San Francisco-San José Railroad, formed the company 
with Timothy G. Phelps in 1859. Following approval from the California State Legislature, 
construction began to connect San Francisco, San Mateo, and Santa Clara counties. Anticipating 
growth of the railroad, Polhemus purchased several parcels of land in San Mateo, and as previously 
 
122 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 51. 
123Samantha Weigel, “Uncovering history in San Mateo: St. John’s Cemetery has lineage of caretakers, formative family,” The Daily 
Journal, October 31, 2014. 
124 Postel, San Mateo County: A Sesquicentennial History, 109.  
429 of 1110

--- page 430 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
48 
described, laid out the plan for today’s downtown. The railroad faced construction challenges 
because of the Civil War: materials were often delayed, and there were labor shortages.125 
Despite delays, by 1864, the rail line had two passenger trains and one freight train daily. It included 
four stops in Santa Clara County and seven in San Mateo County, including in San Mateo. The 
success of the line led to its purchase by the Big Four, the name given to the railroad tycoons who 
funded the Central Pacific Railroad, in 1870, as part of their consolidation of railroads after the 
completion of the transcontinental railroad in 1869.126 The line eventually became part of the 
Southern Pacific Railroad. Despite the connection of the railroad, San Mateo County, including San 
Mateo did not immediately boom with increased development. In the nineteenth century, San 
Mateo County was the slowest growing county in the Bay Area.127 This was largely because the land 
was held by a few affluent estate owners who retained land along the rail route. Their ownership 
contained development that typically encouraged economic activity. In San Mateo, the Howard, 
Poett, Redington families held tight control of their property. Generally, estate owners did not begin 
to parcel out their land until the late 1880s. 
Even with the arrival of the railroad, stagecoaches continued to furnish important freight, mail, and 
passenger service. The railroad was slow to expand, and only one line served the Peninsula. As coast 
side and bayside communities grew, stagecoaches over the mountains became more important. 
During this period, three toll roads were constructed to connect the coast to the bayside, and 
stagecoach services connected passengers arriving by rail to San Mateo from San Francisco to 
Pescadero (Figure 22).128 
 
125 Postel, San Mateo County: A Sesquicentennial History, 112.

Crocker (1822-1888).

7.5 
Industrial Development 
During this period, San Mateo saw little industrial development. Construction projects like the 
railroad in 1864 and Crystal Springs Dam in 1887 brought support services to the area for the 
duration of the projects, but San Mateo did not have a main economic driver like neighboring 
communities, such as whaling and fishing on the coast, and logging in Redwood City and Woodside.  
7.6 
Cultural and Ethnic Groups 
Irish 
The largest single ethnic group to reside in San Mateo County during this period were Irish 
immigrants.129 Though there were Irish immigrants in the region in the years before the Gold Rush, 
their population flourished after 1849. Drawn by the promise of success and escaping the Great 
Famine (1845–1852), waves of Irish immigrants arrived in the United States during this period. 
Compared to Irish who emigrated to the East Coast; however, Irish on the West Coast were able to 
take advantage of the state’s nascent status and were moved ahead economically, socially, and 
politically with more ease.130 
In San Mateo, Robert Wisnom from County Ballycarry, arrived in 1868. He worked for the Parrott 
family, building their mansion. In his first year, however, he purchased a plot of land on B Street and 
 
129 Postel, San Mateo County: A Sesquicentennial History, 144. 
130 Hynding, From Frontier to Suburb, 163. 
Image Redacted Pending Permission for Use 
431 of 1110

--- page 432 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
50 
Fourth Avenue. As previously described, he established several businesses and constructed many of 
San Mateo’s early residences. He later went on to become a prominent San Mateo resident.131  
The big work projects in the area—the railroad and the Crystal Springs Dam construction—drew 
Irish immigrant laborers, many of whom stayed in the area and settled. In the 1860s and 1870s, the 
Fenians, a political organization for Irish causes, held fundraising picnics throughout San Mateo 
County. In 1866, the Fenian picnic in San Mateo drew 15,000 people.132  
Chinese 
The second largest ethnic population in San Mateo during this era was the Chinese. Similar to the 
Irish, their presence in the area before the Gold Rush was nominal. By 1880, however, there were 
600 Chinese residents in San Mateo County. Faced with labor issues to construct the railroad during 
the Civil War, Chinese immigrants were employed to construct the San Francisco–San José Railroad. 
The success of their labor was noticed by the Big Four, and they were hired to build the western 
section of the transcontinental railroad. After the railroad was completed in 1869, however, 
thousands of Chinese laborers were released from their jobs.133  
Many came back to San Mateo County. Some worked successfully in shrimp and oyster harvesting in 
the bay. As shrimp harvesters, they set up shrimp camps along the bay often referred to as “China 
camps.”134 At the industry’s peak in 1892, the bay off San Mateo County near present-day Coyote 
Point was the leading producer of shrimp on the west coast.135 Prior to the 1880s, most cheap labor 
in the region was done by the Chinese. The Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882, which barred further 
immigration from China and reflected anti-Asian sentiment spreading countrywide. In San Mateo, 
however, racial tensions were less explosive than there were in other parts of the state and country. 
The absence of industrial working conditions and urban congestion may have been a factor, as 
immigrant labor was not a threat to livelihood. Most Chinese on the Peninsula were men from 
South China and worked on the great estates as laborers, cooks, and domestic servants. Hayward 
hired many Chinese laborers to reclaim marshlands, dig ditches, and lay pipes to carry water from 
the hills to bayside farms.136 The Sanborn map from 1891 shows that there were also several 
Chinese businesses in San Mateo during this period. They included a wash house and grocer on 2nd 
Street and B Street, a second wash house on B Street and 2nd Street, and third Chinese wash house 
on B Street and 3rd Street (Figure 23). Though the Chinese did enjoy some community acceptance 
and involvement, they were deprived of political liberty and endured segregation while their 
position steadily improved.137 
 
131 “Robert J. “‘Big Bob’ Wisnom,” Find a Grave, accessed October 1, 2025, at https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/87334134/robert_j-
wisnom.

133 Postel, San Mateo County: A Sesquicentennial History, 150.

Early Settlement (1849–1893)

51

1891 Map of San Mateo Showing Cluster of Chinese Businesses 
 
African Americans 
Before the incorporation of San Mateo in 1894, it had a small population of African Americans. After 
the establishment of the railroad in 1864, some African American families settled in San Mateo. Tom 
Ricks operated a restaurant in one of the early hotels, the Railroad House. By the end of the 1870s, 
the town’s only barber shop was run by J. J. Adams, who had also served on a Grand Jury. The 
African American population remained small in the following years and increased with the city’s 
suburban growth in the following decades.138 
 
138 Postel, San Mateo County: A Sesquicentennial History, 77.  
433 of 1110

--- page 434 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
52 
8 
Incorporation and Suburban Growth 
(1894–1940) 
The City of San Mateo incorporated in 1894. The trustees were made up of the following group of 
prominent local figures: Captain Payson, husband of Abby Parrott; businessman and builder Robert 
Wisnom; Marine architect George W. Dickie; storeowner Charles Herbst; hotel and saloon propriety 
Harry Hatch.139 The decades following incorporation and into the twentieth century marked a new 
phase of growth and development for the city as the city began to follow a typical pattern of 
suburbanization. At the beginning of this period of development, San Mateo’s population was under 
2,000 residents.140 The city’s immense growth during this period was spurred dramatically following 
the 1906 earthquake and fire on April 18, 1906. Though San Mateo did sustain damage, it was not 
equally ravaged as San Francisco. Beginning in the days after the earthquake, refugees began to 
filter into San Mateo, some arriving on foot, carrying their remaining possessions and some arriving 
via railroad.141 Following the earthquake, thousands of San Franciscans decided to move to the 
suburbs permanently and rapid suburbanization followed, including new residential developments, 
new commercial enterprises, improved and more robust transportation, and the arrival of more 
ethnic communities. By the end of this period, San Mateo’s population reached nearly 20,000 
residents, marking a significant period of population and physical growth for the city.142 
8.1 
Residential Development  
During this period, the scant residential subdivision that began in the late 1880s by Howard and 
Borel ballooned. By the late nineteenth century, the possibility of living outside cities was within 
reach of many more people. The increased availability of land and the improved ability to travel 
with ease encouraged more residential development in San Mateo.143 Subdivisions began to radiate 
from San Mateo’s business district, and many the result of the formerly large estates being split up. 
The previously platted subdivisions by Howard and Borel that made up San Mateo’s central 
neighborhood began to be more densely developed. Sanborn maps from 1897 show that, following 
incorporation, residential development increased on the east and west sides of Railroad Avenue, 
from Tilton Avenue to 5th Avenue.144 Typical development included one-to-two story wood frame 
single-family residences.145 Examples of houses during this period include those constructed by 
Robert Wisnom for William H. Brown and Dennis Brown at 2 and 5 South Delaware Street (extant) 
(Figure 24).146 
In 1895, 62 acres of the former Howard/Bowie estate was subdivided for San Mateo Heights, 
located east of El Camino Real and north of Bellevue Avenue, extending to North Delaware Street to

Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
53 
the east and State Street to the north (Figure 26).147 Suburbanization began, and the city’s proximity 
to San Francisco made commuting possible for residents who worked in the city but preferred a 
“country” environment. A newspaper article of the time noted that the “the town of San Mateo and 
its environments have advantages which other suburbs do not possess, the most prominent being 
its accessibility to San Francisco without water transportation.”148 The neighborhood began with 
“cottages and villas” with both year-round and seasonal residents.149  
Architect Ernest Coxhead built and designed numerous residences and churches in the Bay Area. He 
constructed his own summer residence in San Mateo at 37 East Santa Inez Avenue (extant) in 
approximately 1893 (Figure 25).150 Built in the First Bay Tradition, it was soon joined by other 
houses. Sanborn maps from 1897 and 1901 show that the burgeoning community spread north 
along North Ellsworth, Catalpa, Tilton Avenues, and San Mateo Drive.151 Early residents of the 
neighborhood were from the professional class and held positions such as lawyers, doctors, 
architects, and entrepreneurs. Houses were built in popular revival styles of the period and included 
Colonial Revival, Renaissance Revival, and Shingle Style.152In the first decades of the twentieth 
century, the neighborhood continued to grow with more modest houses, especially following the 
1906 Earthquake. Houses included more examples of Neo-classical and California bungalows, 
Shingle-style cottages, and later period revival houses.153

Advertisement for San Mateo Heights 
 
Source: San Francisco Call Bulletin, July 21, 1895 
The next subdivisions established during this period were San Mateo Park and Haywood Park. San 
Mateo Park was established when real estate developers Baldwin and Howell, comprised of 
Archibald S. Baldwin (1858–1924) and Josiah R. Howell (1868–1916), purchased 400 acres northwest 
of town on property previously owned by Stephen B. Whipple.154 Whipple ran a successful horse 
ranch on the property before it was transformed for an estate and eventually sold to Charles Clark 
in the 1890s.155 In 1902, the firm hired Australian Civil Engineer Davenport Bromfield (1862–1954) 
to survey the land and design the subdivision layout.156 Bromfield’s plan retained the former 
estate’s country atmosphere by designing the roads to follow the undulating landscape. Bounded by 
El Camino Real on the east, Occidental Avenue to the west, Poplar Avenue to the south, and 
Barroilhet Avenue to the north, Bromfield reinforced the country atmosphere with planting shrubs 
and trees. In 1904, Bromfield purchased a lot on Occidental Avenue to construct his own house, 537 
Occidental Avenue (extant) (Figure 27).157 In the following years, San Mateo Park continued to 
develop with houses built in the popular revival styles as well emerging styles such as the Bay 
Tradition, like the house at 626 Occidental Avenue (extant), designed by notable architect Julia

Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
57 
Alvinza Hayward died in 1904 and his estate was subsequently interested in selling his vast property. 
Baldwin and Howell were agents for the residential sales and for the sale of the former mansion to 
be transformed into the Peninsula Hotel. The Hayward Park subdivision was laid out in square 
blocks from the railroad tracks to El Camino between 9th and 16th Avenues.159 The Peninsula Hotel 
(non-extant), at the corner of 9th Avenue and Palm Avenue, was promoted as the social center of 
the neighborhood. Early advertisements for the subdivision extolled the speed that building a house 
on the lots would be with easy connections to sewer, gas, and electricity services (Figure 30).160 First 
constructed outside the city’s boundaries, San Mateo Park and Hayward Park were annexed in 1909 
and 1910, respectively (Figure 31).161 The early development of Hayward Park included many 
California bungalows, a popular housing type of the time. In the following years, the community 
continued to develop with more examples of period revival style houses in more modest 
proportions than in San Mateo Heights and San Mateo Park.162 An available historical aerial image 
from 1929 shows that the southern portion of the neighborhood was substantially developed by this 
time, with few remaining undeveloped lots (Figure 32).163

--- page 441 ---
Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
59 
The Peninsula Hotel burned in a fire in June 1920.164 The land was subsequently sold by Baldwin and 
Howell and subdivided for additional residential development, becoming the Glazenwood 
neighborhood within Hayward Park. Unlike the typical grid subdivision and rectangular lot 
development in Hayward Park, Glazenwood was subdivided with a U-shaped design with two 
curving streets, Laurel Avenue and Rosewood Drive, creating a horseshoe entry into the 
neighborhood from 9th Avenue. Its design was apparently derived from the country lanes of 
England. Founded by Herbert Jones and Walter A. Nelson, the community was planned for Spanish 
Colonial revival houses, somewhat larger than those in Hayward Park. The neighborhood was 
developed between 1922 and 1925.165 A. Born Building Company constructed the houses, many 
speculatively, besides those built for Jones and Nelson.166 After several successful sales, Born 
purchased the remaining undeveloped lots from Baldwin and Howell to continue to develop and sell 
directly.167 
The community was planned as a restricted residential tract. S. Born described that “there is no 
other tract on the peninsula which is protected to the same extent with restrictions that 
Glazenwood is.”168 The neighborhood’s status as a restricted residential tract was a selling point, 
and meant to signal a planned, exclusive, and economically harmonious neighborhood. Such 
neighborhoods often included racial covenants that explicitly excluded people from racial and 
religious groups by deed restriction. According to available census records, the early residents were 
from the professional class and included investment bankers, lawyers, salesmen, lawyers, and 
engineers and many households included live-in service staff.169 
Residential growth continued throughout the 1920s. San Mateo approved three annexation 
measures in the late 1920s. In January 1927, the city annexed an area west of El Camino and south 
of Hobart Avenue known as San Mateo City Homestead.170 The 345-acre subdivision was first 
surveyed and approved in 1870.171 Its early development, however, appears to have been slow and 
supported some small farms. By 1898, many lots remained undeveloped with unused streets.172 
Development appears to have increased in the first decades of the twentieth century, likely 
attributed, in part, to the influx of residents following the 1906 Earthquake. Nonetheless, by 1920, 
development in the San Mateo City Homestead area was less dense than those subdivisions closer 
to the center of the city. A 1920 Sanborn map depicts limited residential development on lots 
closest to El Camino Real, along present-day Hobart Avenue (formerly 1st Avenue), Madison Avenue 
(formerly Middle Avenue), Barneson Avenue (formerly 2nd Avenue) as well as along Maple Street 
and Nadina Street (former Nut Avenue), while the lots to the south of Barneson Avenue were

undeveloped.173 Though infill development occurred in the following years, the southern lots

Figure 33 
1920 Sanborn (left) and 1929 aerial (right) of San Mateo City Homestead 
Development 
 
In April 1927, the City voted to annex the former Parrott Estate located at the northwest corner of 
the city and bounded by the town of Hillsborough to the north.175 The estate passed to John 
Parrott’s widow, Abby Parrott after his death in 1884. She continued living on the estate until her 
death in 1917.176 Due to the vast amount of land owned by the Parrott family throughout San 
Francisco and the Peninsula, the heirs established the Parrott Estate Company.177 In 1927, the 
former Parrott Estate was subdivided by the Dunn-Williams Company. The Baywood Park Company 
was created the same year to sell the lots. Similar to the San Mateo Park subdivision, streets were 
designed following the natural topography and landscape. J.A. Williams of the Baywood Park 
Company described that, “we have instructed our engineers in planning streets to avoid sacrificing 
trees,” he continued, “with this thought in mind, they have worked out a magnificent plan, 
particularly for Baywood Drive. This broad artery, which will have no equal anywhere, will in reality 
be a triple avenue. The center section, which will be lined on both sides by magnificent oaks, 
eucalyptus and other fine trees, will be about 20 feet wide.”178 By late 1927, roads were constructed

Over the next several years, the neighborhood continued constructing single-family houses built in 
the popular revival styles of the period. In 1930, the San Francsico Chronicle sponsored a model 
home in the neighborhood. Built especially for display purposes, “The Californian,” was designed by 
the architecture firm Grimes and Schoening in the Monterey Colonial Revival style at 639 Fordham 
Road (extant) and staged with furniture and appliances from O’Connor, Moffatt, a luxury 
department store. The open house attracted over 30,000 visitors and served as a boost to draw 
more residents to the neighborhood, marketed as a charming neighborhood at a moderate cost.180 
Though Baywood initially only included single-family houses by design, in 1929 builder Irma B. 
Downing oversaw the construction of the Casa Baywood Apartments (extant) at the corner of El 
Camino Real at the entrance to the neighborhood.181 Designed by local architect Ernest L. Norberg, 
he also designed another apartment building, Del Cerrito at 212 Eaton Road (extant) for owner 
Gordon C. Hess.182 Other apartment buildings from this period included Sequoia at 146 West 3rd 
Avenue and Casa Hermosa Apartments at 106 West 3rd Avenue (extant) (Figure 35). These larger 
suburban-style apartment buildings were built in the same popular revival styles of the period. As a 
building type, they were becoming more popular during this period, though multifamily housing 
existed in downtown San Mateo by the early twentieth century. These examples, however, differed

San Mateo’s physical expansion continued in 1929, when the city annexed the area formally known 
as the Beresford District. The Beresford District was subdivided outside the city in 1884 and included 
the area south of 15th Avenue.184 A 1910 survey map of the county illustrates Beresford Park 
located on the west side of El Camino Real and subdivided into 21 large lots.185 A 1927 map of San 
Mateo County depicts further subdivision spanning from approximately present-day 20th to 31st 
avenues. It was bound by the Beresford Country Club (the present-day location of the Peninsula Golf 
and Country Club) to the west, while the area between the Baywood neighborhood to the north and 
Beresford Park still included several large unsubdivided tracts of land.186 Additionally, a portion of 
Beresford east of El Camino, known as the Town as Beresford, bound by McLellan Avenue to the 
north and Poinsettia Avenue to the south, that was served by its own rail station. The maps indicate 
Beresford Park and Beresford Town were sparsely populated with 200 residents. 187 The annexation 
two years later added 600-700 new residents to San Mateo and increased the size of the city by

In 1929 work began on the Aragon Tract, a 10-block subdivision south of Baywood and north of the 
San Mateo Homestead Tract. The Aragon Tract is located on approximately 100 acres of land from 
the former Tobin Clark Estate (extant in neighboring Hillsborough).189 Developed by Meyer Brothers, 
houses were open for display beginning in summer 1929.190 Display houses were assigned names 
like “the Cordova,” “the Kenmore,” and “the York” and reflected their architectural stylings—
Spanish Colonial Revival in the Cordova and Tudor Revival in the Kenmore and the York.191 
Development continued in 1933 when Meyers Brothers transferred approximately 82 acres of the 
tract to the Aragon Company, a syndicate of prominent San Mateo residents that included W.S. 
Leadley, Louis Lengfeld, F.W. Loewe, Emil Zwierlan, and Fred P. Calleson.192 Advertisements for the 
second phase of development for the Aragon Tract depicted curving roads and Revival style houses, 
describing the neighborhood as “the Peninsula’s choiciest homesites.”193 Sales continued for the 
next several years. Unlike Meyers Brothers, the Aragon Company was not a developer/builder, and 
houses were built by a number of different local construction companies and included more

The new and annexed subdivisions helped the city’s overall population grow from 5,979 residents in 
1920 to 13,444 in 1930, a nearly 125 percent increase.195 The city continued growing over the next 
decade due to subdivision development.  
In 1938, the Sunnybrae subdivision was approved and construction began shortly thereafter.196 
Located south of 10th Avenue from South Delaware Street to Bayshore Highway, the tract was built 
by the G.W. Williams Company and developed by American Homes, Inc. The neighborhood, as 
envisioned, included 700 houses and community amenities like parks, schools, and shopping centers 
and was planned with the help of the Federal Housing Administration (FHA).197 Unlike the strict grid

Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
65 
in the city’s first residential neighborhoods and the curving roads of the subdivisions carved from 
the former estates, Sunnybrae was laid out with long blocks and weaving streets designed to slow 
traffic and minimize accidents, and appears to have adopted many of the principles established by 
the FHA by this period as published in their guidance Planning Neighborhoods for Small Houses. The 
neighborhood included other features typical of FHA-encouraged designs like use of the “suburban 
curb,” a curved curb that continued to the gutter and did not require new curb cuts for driveways 
for each house built. The neighborhood’s main artery, Sunnybrae Boulevard, continues diagonally 
through the neighborhood providing convenient access to principal centers, as also recommended 
by FHA guidance.198 The first houses were designed by architect John Knox Ballentine, Jr. The first 
phase of building included 11 houses; five were wood frame and six were “Unibilt” steel frame 
houses. All the houses were designed to meet the demand for modern, low-cost housing and unlike 
earlier development in this period were not revival style, but were built in California ranch and 
minimal traditional styles (Figure 38). 199  
Figure 38 
Sketch of Sunnybrae House 
 
Source: “The Golden Gate Exposition Model Homes,” Pencil Points, May 1939. 
Toward the end of this period, residential development of formerly semi-rural land began. Laurel 
Creek Farm, located south of Beresford Park at the city’s southern limit at present-day 31st to 42nd 
Avenues, was first subdivided in 1889. By 1927, it had been further subdivided, but remained 
sparsely developed.200 Further subdivision and development began in spring 1940, beginning on 
Arbor and Briar Lanes as part of David D. Bohannon’s Hillsdale development, however it remained 
largely undeveloped by the early 1940s.201  
After starting his firm in 1928, and surviving the stock market crash in 1929, David D. Bohannon of 
the David D. Bohannon Organization, purchased the St. Cyr Estate, formerly the Howard property. 
He built the El Cerrito Manor development, a 10-acre portion of the former St. Cyr estate. His work 
in San Mateo continued in 1937, when he purchased 848 acres of the Beresford area. The 
development of the first 241 acres of what is now known as Hillsdale was completed in April 1940.

66 
Unlike El Cerrito Manor, which largely resembled the city’s other suburban development, Hillsdale 
called for the construction of 5,000 inexpensive, well-built houses that came to represent post-
World War II housing.202 
8.2 
Commercial Development 
Commercial development following San Mateo’s incorporation was characterized by rapid 
expansion and growth to respond to San Mateo’s increasing population. Sanborn insurance maps 
show that by 1897 wood-frame commercial buildings typical of the previous period were replaced 
by more permanent brick and stone frame buildings along San Mateo’s commercial corridor, 
particularly along B Street and Railroad Avenue.203 The city’s commercial district experienced 
significant damage from the 1906 earthquake, which was a contributing factor to the commercial 
core’s rebuilding and subsequent expansion (Figure 39). In the following decades, commercial 
businesses extended along B Street from Baldwin to Fourth Avenue and radiated to side streets. 
Second Avenue opened through the former Taylor property and connected to El Camino Real, 
where a number of important commercial businesses were also located. In addition to the 
connection to El Camino Real, a bridge was built across San Mateo Creek, connecting San Mateo 
Drive and Second Avenue.204 
Figure 39 
Damage to San Mateo Stable Building at 1st Avenue and B Street, 1906 
 
Source: San Mateo Public Library, San Mateo Local History Collection 
Many of San Mateo’s first hallmark businesses were founded during this period. In 1894, San 
Mateo’s first bank was organized by Philip Roedel along with San Mateo’s existing business leaders. 
In 1896, the Bank of San Mateo building was erected on Main Street. The same year, he helped 
establish the San Mateo Mutual Building and Loan Association Roedel built a grand Tudor Revival 
 
202 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 211.  
203 Sanborn Map Company, Sanborn Fire Insurance Map from San Mateo, San Mateo County, California, 1897. 
204 Frank Stanger, Levy Bros: A Centennial History, 1872-1972, (Stanger, 1972), 22. 
448 of 1110

--- page 449 ---
Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
67 
house, called Seven Oaks at 20 El Cerrito Avenue (extant) in 1905. After his death in 1905, both the 
bank and house were purchased by A.P. Giannini. 205 
Giannini retained the bank’s name and officers, which included Robert Wisnom as Vice President. 
The bank’s location was moved to the corner of B and Third streets by 1905 and was renamed the 
National Bank of San Mateo in 1909. In 1918, Giannini changed the name of the bank at 3rd and B 
streets to Bank of Italy (present-day Bank of America). The original bank building was demolished in 
the 1930s. In 1923, the Bank of San Mateo’s original directors spilt from Giannini and revived the 
Bank of San Mateo name with a new building at 164 South B Street (extant). Designed by architect 
William Henry Weeks (1866–1936), the Beaux Arts building, was a monumental addition to 
downtown San Mateo.206 
During this period, the Levy brothers established a store in San Mateo, Levy Bros. The brothers 
arrived from France in 1869 and first established stores on the San Mateo coast side and purchased 
the stage line to San Mateo before establishing a store in downtown San Mateo in 1898. The 
brothers—Joseph, Fernand, Adrien, and Armand—operated from the Wisnom Block on B Street, 
occupying most of the buildings by 1906 (Figure 40).207 The store catered to all of the community’s 
needs and included four connected stores—hardware, grocery, dry goods, and men’s furnishings. In 
addition to the store, they also offered door-to-door sales and delivery from San Bruno to Belmont. 
By 1917, Levy Bros were looking to expand in San Mateo and with the opening of the former Parrott 
estate lands for development, arranged to build a new building at Ellsworth and Second Avenues. 
Despite difficulty during the Great Depression, the business survived.  
During this period, San Mateo’s business district began to shift away from Railroad Avenue and B 
Street toward El Camino Real. In 1923, Chistian de Guigne’s heirs agreed to sell their property, 
which occupied most of the land between Ellsworth and El Camino on the south side of Third 
Avenue. The sale of the property, organized by D.A. Raybould, helped him realize his vision to 
connect El Camino Real with the railroad, via a wide commercial thoroughfare. In 1924, Third 
Avenue was developed to be 80 feet wide, allowing for diagonal parking and becoming the widest 
business district in the Peninsula.208 Lots began to be developed on the south side of Third Avenue, 
beginning with the Caldwell building at 149 Third Avenue (non-extant) and continuing in the 
following years with Noah’s Ark restaurant (non-extant), the San Mateo Theater (1925, extant, but 
altered), and Benjamin Franklin Hotel (1927, extant). In an effort to improve business, Levy Bros 
decided to move to the new commercial core. In 1931, the moved into a new store, designed by the 
San Franciso firm Hyman and Appleton with a 150-foot frontage, on the north side of Third Avenue 
(extant) (Figure 41).209 The gamble to build a new store in the midst of the Great Depression did not 
immediately help the business, but they were able to survive in San Mateo for many more decades.

--- page 451 ---
Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
69 
The introduction and popularity of automobile travel during the early twentieth century influenced 
San Mateo’s commercial growth. In addition to the city’s commercial expansion shifting toward El 
Camino Real, it began to take the form of an auto-oriented commercial corridor as the early decades 
of the twentieth century passed. Buildings related to servicing and repair of automobiles began 
appearing along major roadways and downtown streets. By 1920, garages and machine shops were 
built in the vicinity of 4th and B streets, along with the California Garage at 146 2nd Avenue (non-
extant), and at least two additional garages just beyond the incorporated city in nearby Homestead, 
including the former Homestead Garage at 10 Madison Avenue (extant, but altered).210  
Gas stations, too, appeared by 1920, and were increasingly common by the end of this period. 
Between 1920 and 1950, four gas stations were developed fronting El Camino Real, between East 
3rd and East 4th avenues, framing one gateway into the downtown.211 One such gas station was a 
Spanish Colonial Revival service station at the corner of Palm Avenue and South Bend Boulevard 
(extant, 1641 Palm Avenue).  
The city’s most popular commercial businesses, Wisnom’s Hardware and Levy Bros were also early 
adopters of the automobile business. Edmond Levy, son of Fernand Levy, traveled to Detroit by train 
in 1916 and drove cross-country back to San Mateo to prove the durability of new Buicks. Levy Bros 
then purchased the first rail shipment of Buicks and were agents until 1927.212 The Wisnom family, 
also a multigenerational San Mateo business, continued Robert Wisnom’s legacy in 1905 when 
Robert J., his brother William, and Robert Bonner opened Wisnom-Bonner Hardware at 2nd and 
Ellsworth Avenues (201 Second Avenue, extant).213 The Wisnom-Bonner Hadware store was a Dodge 
dealership, and also provided service for Dodge automobiles (Figure 42). 
Figure 42 
c. 1910 Image of Wisnom-Bonner Hardware at 2nd and Ellsworth with Dodge 
Dealership Sign 
 
Source: San Mateo Public Library, San Mateo Local History Collection  
Beyond meeting the city’s growing population’s necessities, the commercial development during 
this period began to include enterprises related to entertainment and recreation. The San Mateo 
Theater opened on Third Avenue in 1925. Designed by architects William I. Garren and Irving F. 
 
210 Sanborn Map Company, Sanborn Fire Insurance Map from San Mateo, San Mateo County, California, 1920.

Marrow, the 1200-seat theater had a popular Neo-Classical design with Art Deco elements 
(Figure 43).214 A second movie theater was added to downtown in 1931 when Baywood Theater was 
built (159 B Street, non-extant). Designed by Simeon Charles Lee, it featured an Art Deco design.215 
Other entertainment venues during this period included other businesses along B Street and Third 
Avenue, like billiards halls.  
Figure 43 
San Mateo Theater, 1925 
 
Source: Los Angeles Public Library, Library Legacy Collection 
Other entertainment businesses also began appearing outside of the downtown area. Before the 
city’s incorporation, Coyote Point was beyond the city limits. First inhabited by Native Americans, it 
was eventually sold to the Mellus & Howard shipping firm in 1850, who connected the once-island 
to the mainland. In the 1880s, it had a swimming tank and bath houses popular with weekenders 
from San Francisco.216 Still owned by the Howard Estate Company in 1922, its manager David 
Stollery saw an opportunity for an entertainment business. A group of investors formed the Pacific 
City Corporation and purchased nearly 350 acres, 250 of which was submerged land, to create a 
theme park to rival New York’s Coney Island. By late Spring 1922, Pacific City included landscaping, 
ferris wheel, rides, bath house, band stand, pier, and playground in addition to the beach. A success 
in its first year, it did not last, due in part of contamination from the growing bayside communities 
of San Mateo and Burlingame into the bay that restricted swimming.217 It closed in 1925 and the 
Howard Estate Company purchased the land back, eventually selling it to the city in 1940.218

https://tessa2.lapl.org/digital/collection/photos/id/111652.  
215 “Baywood Theater,” Cinema Treasures, accessed October 10, 2025, at https://cinematreasures.org/theaters/9547.  
216 “A History of Coyote Point,” CuriOdyssey at Coyote Point, accessed October 10, 2025, at https://curiodyssey.org/wp-

--- page 453 ---
Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
71 
In 1934, William P. Kyne (1887–1957) opened Bay Meadows racetrack on a portion of Dave 
McLellan’s former Beresford ranch. A powerhouse in the racing industry, Kyne was responsible for 
introducing pari-mutuel betting, the daily double, night racing, and quarter horse racing to 
California. Bay Meadows (non-extant) became one of the busiest racetracks in the country and 
hosted many of the most famous horses, including Seabiscuit.219 Bay Meadows was the only 
racetrack on West Coast that operated during World War II. As part of its agreement for continued 
operation, over 90 percent of the track’s profits were leveraged to support the war effort, with over 
$4 million donated to war relief and service organizations.220  
8.3 
Civic and Institutional Development 
The need for expanded services was stark in the aftermath of the 1906 earthquake. Elisabeth Mills 
Reid, daughter of Darius Ogden Mills and Jane Templeton Mills, was visiting her family from New 
York in 1906 and saw a need for an adequate hospital. Up to this point, the city’s medical needs 
were met by doctors making house calls.  
In 1905 the San Mateo Medical Society was formed by a group of 16 doctors, but there were no 
hospitals or emergency facilities between Palo Alto and San Francisco. Reid, having organized the 
New York chapter of the Red Cross in 1881, recruited Dr. W.C. Chidester to develop an emergency 
care facility. The effort was supported by Neptune Blood William Gallwey, rector at the Episcopal 
Church of St. Matthew. Between 1906 and 1907, a corner of the church property was set aside for a 
modest building to house the parish nurse and a patient’s room for emergencies. Reid, back in New 
York by this time, lead the search for a head nurse, and hired Miss Beatrice H. “Woody” Woodard.221 
Woody arrived in 1907 to a high demand for services. By 1908, the six-bed facility operated as the 
first private hospital in San Mateo County. First called the Church of St. Matthew Red Cross Guild, it 
had no connection to the Red Coss and, though on church property, served everyone. At the 
hospital’s dedication in 1908, the Bishop stated that it was “to meet the needs of suffering 
humanity, irrespective of religious or racial affiliations.”222  
Immediately popular, the facilities were expanded. In 1909, Reid and her cousin Henry H. Taylor 
purchased the old San Mateo Hotel for a nurses’ residence. In 1910 Reid purchased the Taylor 
estate land at El Camino Real to construct a facility to accommodate 50 patients.223 Architect Lewis 
Hobart designed the new building, which was completed in 1914 in a French Gothic style (extant, 
northwest portion of hospital).224 The hospital was expanded in 1928, with a Hobart-designed 
addition, increasing capacity to 147 beds.225 Renamed the Church of St. Matthew Mills Memorial 
Hospital, it continued to expand in the following decades, with notable growth after World War II. 
The city’s medical services further expanded when the San Mateo County Community hospital was 
planned in 1919. Land for the hospital was purchased by the county, and architect William Toepeke 
designed the building (non-extant) which opened in 1923 to serve patients with communicable 
 
219 Ringler, San Mateo, USA: The Golden Years, 35.

221 M. Morrison, The Hospital Elisabeth Built, (Mills Memorial Hospital, n.d), 6.  
222 Morrison, The Hospital Elisabeth Built, 8. 
223 Morrison, The Hospital Elisabeth Built, 20. 
224 Morrison, The Hospital Elisabeth Built, 22. 
225 Morrison, The Hospital Elisabeth Built, 23.

--- page 454 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
72 
diseases.226 It was expanded in 1929 with a tuberculosis unit and expanded again in 1934 with a 51-
bed third unit. It was extensively updated following World War II.227 
In addition to medical services, the city’s municipal services expanded significantly after 
incorporation and during the period of exponential growth after the 1906 earthquake. In 1910, 
following a campaign lead by the San Mateo Women’s Club, the city received $10,000 from the 
Carnegie Foundation for the construction of a library at San Mateo Drive and Second Avenue (non-
extant).228 In 1914, a City Hall building was constructed at 215 South B Street (extant, altered). Built 
by Caldwell & Wisnom, it served as City Hall until 1961.229 The city’s police department was 
established in 1917 when Thomas F. Burke was hired as the first Marshal and then Police Chief in 
1922.230 Burke, who also served as Fire Chief, professionalized both bodies. First operating out of 
City Hall, by 1939, the Police Department headquarters was in a one-story building at 11 South B 
Street (extant) (Figure 44). The Fire Department headquarters was at 2nd Avenue and Main Street in 
the City Hall building before being moved to a new Spanish Colonial Revival-style station at 120 
South Ellsworth (extant) in 1939.231

San Mateo Police Headquarters 1939

the former St. Margaret’s School for Girls campus on Baldwin Avenue in 1903 until a new building

73 
was constructed in 1911.232 The new, three-story concrete reinforced building at San Mateo Drive 
and Baldwin Avenue (non-extant) had 25 classrooms, lab space, and an assembly hall and was 
designed by Havens & Toepke architects.233 In 1926, to accommodate a growing student population, 
a new San Mateo High School was built on Delaware Street (extant).234 Designed by architects and 
brothers Col. E.L. Norberg and John Norberg, it was meant to resemble England’s Hampton Court.235 
Elementary education facilities also expanded significantly during this period. In 1914, the city’s first 
Parent Teacher Association was established.236 They established the city’s first kindergarten. During 
this period, several neighborhood schools were founded including Borel School in 1929 (extant as a 
middle school) and Baywood Elementary School in 1939.237 San Mateo also established higher 
education institutions during this period with the founding of San Mateo Junior College in 1922. First 
housed in the San Mateo High School building downtown, it moved to William H. Kohl’s former 
mansion (non-extant) in 1923 for four years before returning to the former San Mateo High School 
building after it relocated to its new campus (Figure 45). In 1939, a new campus opened at North 
Delaware and Peninsula Avenues, but development was curtailed by World War II. It moved to its 
current campus following the war in 1963.238

--- page 456 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
74 
During this period, the fraternal and society groups that were emerging in the previous period grew 
and expanded, and many catered to the city’s elite. In 1906, Edward Howard founded the San 
Mateo Polo Club, which used several private estate lands for matches.239 The club’s first clubhouse 
is in present-day Hillsborough (Hillsborough Racquet Club). In 1926, the Borel family sold a portion 
of their estate to the San Mateo Polo Club for fields and grandstands, but the city’s new sanitation 
orders in 1931 made horse ownership more difficult and the land was subdivided for residential 
development.240 In 1911, wealthy Jewish residents founded the Beresford Country Club (present-
day Peninsula Golf and Country) in response to restrictive membership policies at Burlingame 
Country Club. By 1914, the land south of San Mateo included a Tudor Revival clubhouse and an 18-
hole golf course. 241 
Several other civic groups and institutions flourished during this period, including the San Mateo’s 
Women’s Club, established in 1903, who were instrumental in getting the library constructed. Other 
civic grounds were dedicated to improvements like roads and beautification, like the Civic Club and 
the Auxiliary Civic Club.242 The city’s first municipal park, San Mateo Central Park, was established 
when the city purchased the former Kohl estate in 1922. Early improvements included a baseball 
field and grandstands for the city’s amateur baseball club, the San Mateo Blue.243 By the 1920s, San 
Mateo had an Elks Lodge, Masonic Lodge, and a Rotary Lodge. The Masonic Lodge built a temple at 
303 Tilton Avenue (extant), while the Elks Lodge owned 336 B Street (non-extant). 
As seen in the previous period, communities organized places of worship. Many of the churches 
were damaged in the 1906 earthquake. The Congregation Church of San Mateo’s steeple collapsed, 
but avoided more significant damage and was expanded with a new building in 1931.244 St. 
Matthew’s Catholic Church was damaged and had to be dismantled and rebuilt.245 The roof of 
Episcopal Church of St. Matthew was extensively damaged in the quake, and the church decided to 
rebuild the church rather than repair or restore it. The new Gothic-style church building, designed 
by architect Willis Polk, was consecrated on May 15, 1910.246  
As the city’s population became more diverse, more faiths also established churches in San Mateo. 
The First Methodist Church opened at 300 East Santa Inez in 1905 and is purported to be the oldest 
continuously used church building in San Mateo (extant, now the Unitarian Universalists of San 
Mateo) (Figure 46).247 The First Church of Christ, Scientist began in San Mateo when two residents 
opened their house at the corner of Tilton Avenue and Elm Street for Christian Science services in 
1897. By 1907, the congregation had grown and the church was incorporated. The small group first 
met at Froebel Hall on Ellsworth Avenue before moving to the newly erected Masonic Lodge in 
1910. The church purchased a lot at the corner of El Camino Real and Monte Diablo Avenue in 1919

8.4 
Transportation Infrastructure 
San Mateo’s suburbanization during this period was made possible by its evolving transportation 
options and favorable location near San Francisco and surrounding communities. The improved 
transportation connections supported an emerging middle class. In 1896, transportation options 
increased with the arrival of the newly organized San Francisco and San Mateo Electric Railway.249 
As the region’s first electric railroad, it was granted franchise into San Mateo to serve as its 
terminus, in 1900. Sold to United Railroads of San Francsico in 1902, construction on the San Mateo 
line, or interurban, began in October of that year. By 1910, 54 cars left San Mateo each weekday, 
traveling along the current Caltrain alignment.250 It boosted San Mateo’s residential development, 
similar to many of the streetcar suburbs that emerged throughout the country during this time. By 
1915, however, the Peninsula Rapid Transit Company began running a bus line. Unlike the streetcar, 
it did not have to pay for the franchise. The No. 40 streetcar line continued to run into the 1940s, 
when it was supplanted by the popularization and increased accessibility of the automobile.251 
The availability of the personal automobile in the 1920s lead to El Camino Real being established as 
the first highway in San Mateo. By the early 1920s, however, as the only connection between San 
Francisco and San José, traffic became intolerable to drivers. In 1924, the first section of the San 
Mateo County section of the highway was planned to span from South San Fransico to San Mateo.252 
In October 1929, Bayshore Highway opened, and relieved some of the pressure on El Camino Real. 
Built to the highest standard of the time, the highway was 100 feet wide with three lanes in each 
 
248 “About Our Church,” First Church of Christ, Scientist, accessed October 7, 2025, at http://www.cschurchsanmateo.org/about-our-
church/.  
249 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 125.

251 “Meet Me in San Mateo on the #40 Line,” The Daily Journal, April 2, 2025, https://www.smdailyjournal.com/news/local/meet-me-in-

direction (Figure 47). It did not, however, have a center median and drivers commonly traveled 
above the posted speed limit, making it one of the most dangerous roads in the county. By 1940, 
traffic congestion and growing fatalities lead to the establishment of the Bayshore Freeway from 
San Francisco to Palo Alto. It was a separate high-speed roadway with on-ramps.253  
Figure 47 
Bayshore Highway to Coyote Point, 1928 
 
Source: San Mateo Public Library, San Mateo Local History Collection 
During this period, trans-bay automobile travel also became possible when the San Mateo-Hayward 
Bridge (also called the San Francisco Bay Toll Bridge) opened in 1929.254 The 7-mile bridge was 
comprised of 1,278 25-foot spans resting on concrete and wood composite piles across the shallow 
bay. The bridge was designed by Raymond Concrete Pile Co. and was planned to take approximately 
18 months to construct.255 Bogged down by several delays, when completed in 1929, it was the 
longest continual vertical lift highway bridge in the world.256 Despite the popularity of the personal 
automobile, the bridge was not heavily used during its first years of operation and suffered low 
usage and low toll fares during the Great Depression (Figure 48).257

8.5 
Industrial Development 
From the turn of the twentieth century to the years immediately preceding World War II, industrial 
development in San Mateo consisted of salt refining and harvest along the bay shore, agriculture, 
and local businesses providing lumber milling and storage, and blacksmithing.258 By 1897, locally 
prominent builder, Robert Wisnom, maintained a lumber yard on the west side of B Street, just 
north of Second Avenue. By 1920, Wisnom Lumber Co. developed a larger complex with a mill and 
cabinet shop with a spur connecting to the Southern Pacific Railroad at 4th Avenue and South 
Railroad Avenue. The San Mateo Planing Mill Co. developed its own facility at 5th Avenue an C 
Street, immediately east of the railroad.259 
In 1903, C.E. Whitney, who worked in the dredging business for August H. Schilling, acquired 200 
acres of land east of the railroad tracks in San Mateo where he established a salt harvesting 
operation known first as C.E. Whitney Company and later the Leslie Salt Refining Company. Whitney 
employed many Japanese laborers at his San Mateo plant, which was located just north of the 
present-day Highway 92 overpass; a site that provided extensive marshland and was close to the 
 
258 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 125. 
259 Sanborn Map Company, Sanborn Fire Insurance Map from San Mateo, San Mateo County, California, 1920, revised 1950. 
459 of 1110

--- page 460 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
78 
railroad. In 1907, the Leslie Salt Company was formed through the merger and consolidation of 
Whitney’s company with the businesses of other industrialists. The plant continued to operate until 
1931, when C.E. Whitney’s successor Arthur Whitney died.260  
Excluding the salt works, San Mateo did not factor in significantly to other major industries such as 
logging or agriculture, beyond a concentration of flower farms that emerged during the twentieth 
century. Many of the flower farms relied heavily on Japanese labor, as the Leslie Salt Company 
had.261 
For a short period, San Mateo sought to be a new center for the film industry following an 
aggressive booster campaign that began after World War I. Pacific Studios was constructed in 1920 
at a 103-acre parcel east of the railroad and south of Peninsula Avenue by architects Roy Purnel and 
Harold Stoner. The studio included plans for sets, labs, cutting rooms, and offices. Only one stage 
set and an administration building (extant, 430 Peninsula Avenue) was completed, however.262 
Open from 1920 until 1927, only one feature length film, White Hands (1921), was shot at the 
studio. Work on several unfinished films continued for several years, but the arrival of talking 
movies made the set obsolete and the property was sold.263 
8.6 
Cultural and Ethnic Groups 
As San Mateo grew significantly during this period, so did the diversity of its ethnic makeup. In 
1935–1940, an agency of the federal government, the Home Owners’ Loan Corporation, graded the 
residential security of neighborhoods across the United States. The practice, which came to be 
known as redlining, assigned a risk level to neighborhoods that was used by banks and mortgage 
lenders to decide on what neighborhoods to invest. The practice included declaring neighborhoods 
with high concentrations, or “infiltrations,” of African Americans, immigrants, and Jewish people as 
hazardous.264 Though the Home Owners’ Loan Corporation map was not published for San Mateo 
County, a related practice to redlining—the use of restrictive covenants—was common. The use of 
restrictive covenants excluded minority access to several of the city’s neighborhoods and promoted 
a Whites only policy.265 During this period, immigration bans like the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 
was still in place; alien land laws banned those ineligible for U.S. citizenship from owning property, 
and anti-miscegenation laws barred interracial marriage.266 Despite all of the challenges faced by 
non-White and immigrant populations during this period, several cultural and ethnic communities 
emerged in San Mateo. 
Chinese 
The Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 significantly limited Chinese immigration to the United States. 
The anti-Chinese laws were so severe that the Chinese population in California declined by 40 
percent from 1880 to 1900.267 During this period, however, San Mateo’s Chinese population

Historic Context Statement 
79 
changed very little and there was a small Chinatown around First Avenue. By 1897, Chinese 
businesses included laundries and a grocer along blocks nearby the Southern Pacific Depot in San 
Mateo.268 A 1920 Sanborn map describes several buildings as having Chinese uses, while the 
neighboring block of buildings was designated as Japanese (Figure 49). This concentration 
demonstrates a statewide trend during this period where distinct Chinatowns, Japantowns, and 
Filipinotowns developed in towns and cities. They often developed close to each other and other 
ethnic communities in the less desirable areas where minorities were relegated by law.”269  
The Leong family migrated to San Mateo during this period, having first arrived from China to 
Oakland in 1913. Sam Shun Leong bought a successful fruit and vegetable route in San Mateo in 
1931. His son, Gordon, who had been delivering flowers, borrowed $150 from him in 1933 to open a 
flower shop, named Ah Sam in honor of his father. The shop, eventually located on El Camino Real 
and 28th Avenue, soon included Gordon’s siblings, children, and extended family. The store, still 
operating today, is in its third generation of Leong ownership.270

1920 Map Showing San Mateo’s Chinese and Japanese Businesses 
 
African Americans 
At the turn of the century, many African American laborers worked as Pullman porters on the 
railroad. The city’s African American population grew as the city suburbanized. Noah Williams, a

Historic Context Statement 
 
80 
famous railroad chef, opened a restaurant, Noah’s Cafeteria, at 139 South B Street in 1920.271 
Following up his success at his restaurant in San Francisco’s renowned Palace Hotel, Noah’s was 
instantly popular. In 1923, he moved the restaurant to 3rd Avenue and renamed it Noah’s Ark. The 
restaurant was noted for its fried chicken and baked ham, as well as its impressive décor including 
large format paintings of jungle scenes. The paintings are on display at the San Mateo County 
Historical Association Museum.272 During this period, Williams also appears to have owned a grocery 
store with his wife, Mabel, who worked as a cashier. They lived at 114 South Delaware Street 
(extant).  
Their neighbor at 112 South Delaware Street, Harry Cox, was also African American and worked as a 
clerk for a cafeteria, potentially Williams’.273 In addition to neighbors, Williams also employed 
several family members. The restaurant served primarily Whites as Noah’s wife worked the front of 
house area and could pass as White, while Noah worked in the kitchen. Noah “closed” the 
restaurant on Mondays for the exclusive use of Black customers. The National Association for the 
Advancement of Colored People held meetings at Noah’s Ark, but the restaurant closed in 1931 due 
to the Great Depression (Figure 50).274 Noah’s family, however, remained in San Mateo. Noah’s son, 
Les, became a well-known dancer in the 1930s. During World War II, Les went on to become one of 
the 996 African American airmen known as the Tuskegee Airmen.275 Les attended Standford 
University on the G.I. Bill after the war and opened a dance studio on San Mateo Drive.276 After City 
Council approved his permit for the dance school by a vote of 3 to 2, a woman threw her purse at 
him in anger. Other San Mateans, however, sent him unsolicited checks and enrolled their children 
in his classes in support.277

During this period, San Mateo’s African American population was concentrated in the North Central 
neighborhood, along Monte Diablo Avenue, North Fremont Street, and Cypress Avenue. According 
to available census records, families who lived in the neighborhood worked as cafeteria workers, 
barbers, cooks, and were employees at the salt works.278 By 1919, the community organized a 
church. Community leaders like Robert Holmes, Jesse Selby, Anita Miles, Robert Williams, Kelly 
Mack, Rose Jones, William Pinkstone, Mary J. Boutee, E.M. Brown, Mamie Davis, and Harry Cox, lead 
the effort with the help of Reverend J.B. Holmes from Oakland.279 The church, African M.E. Zion 
Church of San Mateo, grew out of temporary quarters within the Congregational Church at Tilton 
Avenue and San Mateo Drive and in 1927 the congregation constructed a new building at 803 
Monte Diablo Avenue, named St. James African Episcopal (now African Methodist Episcopal 
[A.M.E.]) Zion Church. In 1938, the church moved to 825 Monte Diablo Avenue (extant, altered).280  
The Pilgrim Baptists faced opposition during planning to construct a church in 1935. The 
congregation wanted to exchange a lot they owned on Cypress Avenue for a City-owned corner lot 
at Monte Diablo Avenue and North Grant Street. In November 1935, Revered Sample went to City 
Council for permission to construct the church. City Council expressed concern, questioning if a 
dance hall was included in the plans. Sample replied, “dancing feet and bended knees don’t go 
together.”281 Just as council was to approve the exchange and permission to construct the church in 
January 1936, 21 neighboring property owners filed a protest requesting Council to reconsider the 
matter, apparently concerned that two African American churches on the same block would

somehow damage the neighborhood.282 At the February 1936 City Council meeting, a one-hour 
debate occurred, but the Council were united in their support of the project.283  
After the Council hearing, Revered Sample penned a Letter to the Editor of the San Mateo Times 
outlining the need for the church stating:  
“we feel that it is not generally understood by the majority of people that this is the only Negro 
Baptist church between San Francisco and San Jose consequentially we serve the spiritual needs 
of five to six hundred people in San Mateo and the surrounding communities.”284  
Upon project approval, Sample thanked the Council saying, “you have been very kind to us, and we 
have been very patient with you.”285 One City Councilman, Aylett Cotton, who went on to become a 
Superior Court judge, supported the cause early on and was invited to speak at the church when it 
opened in 1937 (extant, altered).286 
Japanese 
By the turn of the twentieth century, Japanese had settled in San Mateo, having arrived during the 
1890s. Similar to San Mateo’s Chinese and African American communities during this period, may 
Japanese immigrants worked as support staff such as gardeners, maids, and cooks at the many area 
estates. Like California’s Chinese communities of the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, 
Japanese communities were predominantly home to single men who emigrated from Japan and 
provided agricultural labor. The 1907–1908 Gentleman’s Agreement between the United States and 
Japan resulted in an increase in female immigration.287 In San Mateo, many domestic workers 
worked short hours in private houses to attend language classes. Some became permanent, full-
time workers. When domestic workers married, it was common for husband and wife to work in the 
same household.288  
As San Mateo welcomed more commerce and industry in the early 1900s, many Japanese found 
work outside domestic labor. In 1901, a notable number of Japanese people immigrated to San 
Mateo County to work at the salt works, West Shore Company and Leslie Salt Works .289 By 1910, a 
Japanese employment agency, helped match newly arrived Japanese to local work opportunities.290  
Workers at Leslie Salt Works were single men who typically worked 12-hour days, six days a week. 
Sahioue Kawakita tended the boiler at the plant from 1910 until 1930 and witnessed hazardous 
working conditions that often caused injury to workers.291 In addition to the working conditions, the 
associated labor camps (non-extant) for worker housing were often crude, unsafe, and spread 
communicable diseases like tuberculosis. Despite the hardship, workers saved money to send to 
family or to purchase their own property.292
