Incorporation and Suburban Growth (1894–1940) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
83 
Tomisuke Ito, among the earliest known Japanese residents of San Mateo, worked for the Mitsui 
Company in Japan and immigrated to the United States in the 1880s, where he found work as a 
domestic servant.293 In 1903, he became the first Japanese to purchase land in San Mateo County 
and built a one-story apartment building, nicknamed nagaya or “long house” on a three-lot 
property at 112 North Fremont Street (non-extant). The nagaya “was a way station for many 
Japanese families who stayed until they found other housing.”294The property also hosted meetings, 
parties, and included a teahouse and bathhouse.295 
Figure 51 
Nagaya at 112 North F Street (now Fremont Street) on 1920 Sanborn Map 
 
Source: Sanborn Map Company 1920, Insurance Maps of San Mateo, California, May 1920, Sheet 2. 
Additionally, Japanese who saved money and/or obtained loans from fellow community members 
through rotating credit, tanomoshi, established businesses that served the Japanese community. 
These included the Yokohama Laundry at Tilton Avenue and North El Dorado Street, established as 
the city’s first Japanese laundry in 1900 by S. Sato and K. Katoaka; Tetsuo Yamanouchi’s Imperial 
Laundry at 214 First Avenue; Konosuke Ito’s Depot Hand Laundry at 247 Railroad Avenue; and 
Kyusuke Yamaguchi’s Sunrise Cleaners at 151 B Street, which were established between 1908 and 
1912 (all non-extant). Into the Great Depression years of the 1930s, there were nine Japanese-
operated cleaning or laundry businesses in San Mateo, in addition to informal operations based in 
homes.296  
In addition to cleaning and laundry, Japanese proprietors worked as tailors, grocers, and in the floral 
industry. Businesses included the Hata Merchant Tailor Shop (non-extant) at 122 B Street, opened in 
1901, and Tokutaro Takahashi’s Oriental grocery and merchandise store, originally located on B 
Street, and relocated several times under multi-generational ownership, and most recently to 221 
South Claremont Street by 2003 (Figure 52).297

Yen-Mei Wong, (San Mateo Chapter JACL 2003), 5.

City of San Mateo

Figure 52 
Takahashi Market 221 South Claremont 
 
Source: TBD 
The floral industry, primarily chrysanthemums, emerged on the Peninsula after the 1906 
Earthquake. San Mateo became known as the “Floral City” beginning the in early 1900s, because 
growers sought the advantageous climate offered in San Mateo and Santa Clara counties while 
maintaining access to markets in San Francisco.298 The floral industry in San Mateo County grew 
significantly between 1910 and 1925 due to the physical expansion of farms and production of 
roses, carnations, gardenias, and lilies, in addition to chrysanthemums.299  
Fields in the Beresford and Homestead areas were leased for flower cultivation. Renting land was 
the only choice for people after the passage of the Henry-Webb Alien Land Law in 1913 that 
prohibited non-citizens from owning land.300 Into the mid-1920s, the present-day Hillsdale area was 
among the chrysanthemum-growing locations in San Mateo, with San Mateo-based nursery 
proprietors including Seishiro Mayeda, Tokujiro Tanaka, and Saikichi Yamada.301 In 1935, San 
Mateo’s Bay Meadows racetrack hosted the San Mateo County Products and Floral Fiesta, where it 
was noted that “Japanese gardeners sent a pyramid of yellow chrysanthemums.”302 The Floral Fiesta 
was the precursor to the San Mateo County Fair. By 1939, 38 percent of Northern California’s 
Japanese flower growers were in San Mateo County.303 
Japanese settlements grew with the establishment of cultural institutions such as Buddhist temples, 
Christian churches, Japanese language schools (gakuen) and other businesses that served Japanese 
residents.304 San Mateo’s Japanese built community through religious associations and organized 
local Buddhist and Christian churches. Imperial Laundry owner Tetsuo Yamanouchi served as the

Historic Context Statement 
85 
Buddhist church’s founding spiritual leader, and his wife Yoshiko organized the church’s Sunday 
School and Women’s Auxiliary. In 1910, the San Mateo’s Buddhist church was established through 
affiliation with the San Francisco Buddhist Church and began operating independently with 60 
members in 1925. Services were first held in the Yamanouchi home at 1st Avenue and Ellsworth 
Street, then they relocated to an adjacent store before moving to the Japanese Language School at 
504 Second Avenue. Property was acquired at 504 2nd Avenue in 1940 to construct a temple, 
however, World War II halted construction until after the war.305  
In 1924, the Japanese Christian Church was founded and held services at the home of Hidematsu 
Tamura on Grant Street, before relocating to worship space in the Congregational Church on Tilton 
Avenue. In 1927, the congregation, led by physician Dr. Ernest Adolphus Sturge, became the 
Japanese Independent Union Church of San Mateo. The congregation acquired property at 25 South 
Humboldt Street in 1930 and received funds from Dr. Sturge’s estate supporting construction of the 
Sturge Memorial Cottage in 1938. Like the San Mateo Buddhist Temple, World War II impacted the 
full development of the Sturge Memorial Cottage property.306 
Japanese and Japanese-Americans also participated in social organizations. The Japanese 
Association of America was founded in 1900 and San Mateo’s chapter organized in 1906. Its first 
meeting included 50 people, led by President Sekko Shimada. The organization sponsored social 
events like annual picnics and Sumo tournaments. Shimada arrived from Japan in 1902 as a guest of 
prominent San Matean Henry Bowie, who studied art in Kyoto.307 During this period, Bowie, then 
considered a leading expert in Japan, designed a Japanese tea garden at his San Mateo residence. A 
portion of Bowie’s estate, including a portion of the Japanese tea garden, was sold to Eugene Henry 
de Sabla in 1906. de Sabla constructed his own estate house on the property and expanded the 
“Shin” style gardens with the assistance of Japanese master designer Makoto Hagiwara (1854–
1925), who also designed the Japanese Tea Garden in Golden Gate Park. Japanese gardener 
Naoharu Aihara (1870–1940) may have also been involved in de Salba’s garden design, which is 
extant.308 
Between 1904 and 1942, 589 children were born to Issei, or first generation Japanese immigrants.309 
Many Issei wanted to return to Japan and wanted their children to be fluent in reading and writing 
Japanese. To address the need, several Japanese Language Schools opened in San Mateo County 
during this period. A Japanese Language School (gakuen) opened in San Mateo in 1916 at the corner 
of South Delaware Street and 2nd Avenue. The schools also taught Japanese music, literature, art, 
and social customs and traditions.310 In addition to Japanese school, second generation Japanese, or 
Nisei, participated in other children’s activities like athletic leagues or judo and kendo clubs.311 
Nisei in San Mateo County organized around issues surrounding their community, such as civil 
rights. In 1930 the Japanese American Citizens League (JACL) became a national organization. The 
San Mateo chapter was founded in May 1935. Aside from organizing around issues like citizenship 
and employment opportunities, JACL was also a social group that hosted dances and meetings.312 
Many Nisei in JACL emerged as leaders during World War II.

Historic Context Statement

Filipino 
The first recorded entry of Filipinos to the Americas (and land within present-day California) 
occurred in 1587 at Morro Bay during a Spanish expedition. Filipinos also settled in the United 
States in the late 1700s as crewmembers or escapees from Spanish galleons. The Spanish-American 
War in the late 1890s and subsequent Philippine-American War resulted in the formation of an 
American colonial regime in the Philippines that lasted into the 1930s. The Americanization of 
Filipinos was integral to the American colonization process. In particular, the Pensionado Act of 
1903 allowed for sponsorship of Filipino students at higher education institutions in the United 
States. These students, known as pensionados, were expected to return to the Philippines and share 
and spread their American influence; however, some stayed in the United States.313 
Agricultural labor was another primary factor of Filipino immigration during the first half of the 
twentieth century. The Gentleman’s Agreement of 1907–1908 between the United States and Japan 
was aimed at restricting immigration of Japanese laborers and opened pathways to agricultural 
labor in Hawaii and California for many Filipino men.314 Filipinos were considered nationals, rather 
than aliens, meaning they were not subject to the same immigration quotas imposed on other Asian 
nations during the early twentieth century. Many entrants passed through San Francisco where they 
found an emerging Manilatown community established alongside Chinatown. Filipino immigrants 
typically labored in agricultural areas of California seasonally, based on harvest cycles. During off 
seasons, Filipinos often worked in restaurants, hotels, and as domestic servants in population 
centers including San Francisco and Stockton. San Mateo was home to several Filipinos, including 
students at San Mateo Junior College.315 
In 1920, approximately 2,700 Filipinos resided in California, and by 1930, that number rose to over 
30,000. During this period of increased immigration and settlement, several Filipino students 
formed a Filipino Club at San Mateo Junior College.316 Despite increased settlement during the 
1920s, immigration slowed in the 1930s as the Great Depression hit. Furthermore, anti-Filipino 
sentiment in California rose and influenced federal policy related to controlling Filipino immigration. 
The Tydings-McDuffie Act (also known as the Philippine Independence Act of 1934) provided for 
Philippine Independence on July 4, 1946, following a transitional period of Commonwealth 
Government. However, the Act also resulted in changing Filipinos’ status as nationals to aliens with 
a quota of 50 immigrants per year, effectively ending Filipino immigration for the foreseeable 
future.317

Documentation Form (Washington D.C.: US Department of Interior, National Park Service, 2024), 58.

9 
World War II and Post-War Development 
(1941–1979) 
During World War II, the San Francisco Bay Area played a major role in coastal defense, military 
training and transport, and wartime industries, including shipbuilding.318 After the Japanese 
bombing of Hawaii’s Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, the United States declared war on Japan 
and joined World War II soon after. In San Mateo County, the impact of the attack brought 
immediate and heightened security along the Pacific coast and included expansion or rapid 
construction of defense installations throughout the Peninsula.319  In 1942, the United States 
Merchant Marine Cadet Basic Training School was constructed at the former Pacific City park at 
Coyote Point.320 
The war created 17 million jobs nationwide and pulled the country out of the Great Depression. 
However, beyond impacts to military and industry, the war created lasting social impact through 
racist policies. San Mateo, like many communities along the West Coast, witnessed the forced 
relocation of residents of Japanese descent to assembly centers, including Tanforan Racetrack in 
nearby San Bruno. Federal relocation policy also affected residents of German, Italian, Hungarian, 
Romanian, and Bulgarians descent, but was particularly impactful to the city’s Japanese 
community.321  
In the immediate post-World War II decades, San Mateo’s population growth and suburbanization 
mirrored broader regional and national trends.322 New neighborhoods emerged from the 
subdivision of former estates. The downtown transformed with new architectural styles and 
buildings including mid-twentieth century-style bank branches and City Hall, Modern-era corporate 
office towers, and contemporary streetscaping. Some buildings in the central business district 
represented completely new construction, while others were remodeled with on-trend exteriors.  
The bulk of the city’s residential, commercial, transportation infrastructure, and civic and 
community buildings extant today were constructed in the two and a half decades following World 
War II.323 
This period also brought significant transitions in cultural and institutional development, as well as 
in transportation systems that served the city, Peninsula, and in some cases the greater Bay Area.

World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
89 
Several new congregations were joined in contributing to the city’s diversity of religious institutions, 
while the College of San Mateo (formerly San Mateo Junior College) consolidated from three 
campuses to one. The San Mateo-Hayward Bridge and the Interstate 280/Highway 92 Interchange, 
as well as emergent Caltrain and San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) systems influenced 
the spread of commerce and industry beyond the downtown.  
9.1 
Residential Development  
The post-World War II building boom expanded San Mateo’s suburban character beyond its earlier 
developed residential neighborhoods. The city’s population grew from just under 20,000 in 1940 to 
over 77,000 in 1980.324 The geographic boundary of the city also expanded with annexation of newly 
subdivided areas. Post-World War II housing was largely characterized by tract developments—
houses constructed in large numbers and marketed to prospective buyers. Merchant builders like 
David Bohannon of the Bohannon Development Corporation subdivided land and constructed 
homes on a massive scale. Bohannon’s company developed over 10,000 houses throughout the Bay 
Area, including in San Mateo’s Hillsdale neighborhood. Marking for new suburban neighborhoods 
included newspaper advertisements describing related amenities, such as parks, churches, and 
access to modern shopping centers. While the newspaper adverts showcased alluring amenities, 
new housing in post-World War II neighborhoods often came with restrictions and exclusionary 
lending and sales practices targeted at non-White minorities.  
Construction of Bohannon’s Hillsdale neighborhood began in 1939, suspended in 1941 with the 
onset of World War II, and continued in the late 1940s. Located on 800 acres in the southern extent 
of the city, Hillsdale was developed immediately west of El Camino Real and adjacent to Laurel 
Creek. The neighborhood included single-family houses, Hillsdale Garden Apartments, and sites for 
churches, schools, and parks. It also included Hillsdale Center, the Peninsula’s first mid-century,  
Like many post-World War II suburban developments, Hillsdale was among San Mateo 
neighborhoods that restricted residency to Whites. Deed restrictions for new houses contained 
exclusionary clauses such as “No persons other than members of the Caucasian or White race shall 
be permitted to occupy any portion of said property, other than as domestics in the employ of the 
occupants of the premises.”325 The architecture of the houses and apartment buildings in Hillsdale 
included one-story, Minimal Traditional and Post-War Minimal style single-family houses. 
Occasionally the exteriors featured applied minimalist ornamentation. In 1946 House Beautiful’s 
Editor, Elizabeth Gordon launched the Pace Setter House Program, an annual series of exhibition 
houses that showcased modern architecture in post-war America. In 1950, one of Bohannon’s 
Hillsdale houses was included in the program, showcasing its design described as a “cautious” pace-
setter (Figure 53). The Hillsdale Branch Library at 205 West Hillsdale Boulevard leans more into the 
Contemporary style, which typically featured post-and-beam construction elements.  
The Hillsdale Garden Apartments was sold to prominent investor Albert G. McCarthy in 1953 after 
recognition as an innovative example of family-friendly garden apartments with playgrounds and a 
nine-hole putting course for adults.326

Pace Setter House #3, 1950

In addition to Bohannon, developer L.C. Smith became synonymous with post-World War II-era 
housing in the region. He also emerged as one of the Peninsula’s most prolific contractors for 
sidewalk, street, and highway development.327 In 1955, Smith’s Concord Ranch Enterprises acquired 
land along 19th Avenue from the Leslie Salt Company and contracted with Moorepark Company to 
develop 19th Avenue Park. The Moorepark Company was owned by renowned tract house developer 
Joseph Eichler. In addition to 19th Avenue Park, Eichler constructed housing in The Highlands, 
located just outside San Mateo’s western municipal boundary. Overall, roughly two-thirds of the 
11,000 houses Eichler built in California were located in San Mateo and Santa Clara counties.328 
Additionally, Eichler’s housing was not racially restricted allowing 19th Avenue Park to develop as a 
relatively diverse neighborhood. Eichler’s houses featured the Contemporary style post-and-beam 
architecture, and were built from several architect-designed models, often with a central atrium or 
courtyard and clerestory glazing above street facing exteriors or garage bays.  
Between 1964 and 1965, Smith developed Lauriedale, a community of garden apartments and 
single-family houses built southeast of the downtown. Lauriedale was annexed to the City of San 
Mateo in 1966.329 Smith hired San Francisco-based Modernist architects Campbell & Wong to design 
apartment buildings in the community.330 The apartment buildings exhibit Second and Third Bay

91 
Tradition-style architecture notable for their wood shingle cladding and multiple stories. The single-
family residential areas of the development feature Ranch and Minimal Traditional-style 
architecture with one to two stories, hipped roofs, stucco exteriors, and brick or stone veneer 
accents.  
Fiesta Gardens, located  south of 19th Avenue and north of Borel Creek and the Bay Meadows 
racetrack, was developed by the Culligan Development Company during the 1950s. In addition to 
single-family residences and apartment buildings with swimming pools, Fiesta Gardens featured 
Fiesta Center (built 1955), a moderately sized Ranch-style shopping center at the northeast corner 
of Bermuda Drive and Ginnever Street.331 One advertisement for the subdivision noted builder-
developer Thomas J. Culligan had constructed 5,000 houses on the Peninsula by 1955.332 
Initially developed during the interwar years between 1918 and 1939, additional single-family 
developments were constructed in Beresford Park during the 1940s and 1950s. San Mateo Knolls, 
developed by San Mateo Investment Company between the early 1940s and mid-1950s, included 
single-family houses constructed east of Alameda de Las Pulgas and in the vicinity of 42nd Avenue. 
Buyers chose between three different Ranch-style house models within blocks of San Mateo Knolls 
Grammar School, P.J. Abbot School, Hillsdale High School, and St. Gregory’s Parochial School.333 
Additionally, a shopping center at the intersection of 42nd Avenue and El Camino Real was 
integrated into the neighborhood’s development. Subsequent residential development occurred 
west of Alameda de Las Pulgas in the 1960s and 1970s. 
While much new construction occurred west of the Bayshore Freeway between the 1940s and early 
1960s, construction east of the freeway included additional tract housing in the Lakeshore and Los 
Prados neighborhoods during the 1960s. Los Prados opened around 1960, with waterfront living, 
parks, a playground, schools, and single-family and duplex residences.334 In 1965, the Los Prados 
Shopping Center at Hillsdale Boulevard and Bayshore Highway marked the completion of its first 
phase of construction. This shopping center has since been redeveloped from its mid-century 
form.335  
Joseph Greenbach of the Newbridge and Belle Haven Realty Companies began development on 
Shoreview, just south of Coyote Point, in 1945. Development continued into the late 1940s and 
included construction of 1,200 residences in 1949 alone. By 1952, Shoreview housed 3,000 San 
Mateo families in houses typically representative of the Minimal Tradition, Post-war Minimal, and 
the generally modest aesthetic common to post-World War II tract housing developments.336  
In 1966, Woodlake Apartments, a large complex of nearly 1,000 apartments with central courtyards 
and an outdoor recreation area with lake, opened immediately north of San Mateo High School’s 
campus (Figure 54). The prominent architecture firm, Wurster Bernardi & Emmons, was contracted

9.2 
Commercial Development 
In the immediate years after World War II, new commercial developments included neighborhood 
and regional shopping centers, modern bank branches in the central business district, 
neighborhoods adjacent to commercial corridors, and several commercial office towers that 
supported growing professional sectors (Figure 55).  
Figure 55 
View of San Mateo’s Downtown Commercial District, 1952

World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
93 
Hillsdale Shopping Center, among the most prominent projects in post-World War II San Mateo, was 
developed by the Bohannon Development Corporation. Hillsdale Center was planned as a 42-acre 
shopping center on part of the acreage Bohannon acquired from the Burleigh H. Murray Ranch in 
1939.339 By 1941, Bohannon’s development included housing in the Hillsdale and Park Western 
neighborhoods, as well as the Andrew Williams Stores grocery (rebranded Mayfair Market in 1951) 
at Hillsdale Boulevard and El Camino Real.340 Planning began in the early 1940s, but construction for 
Hillsdale Center commenced in the early 1950s. The shopping center was master planned by the 
noted architecture firm Welton Becket & Associates who concurrently planned San Francisco’s 
Stonestown shopping center.341 Consistent with the typical features of post-World War II regional 
shopping centers, Hillsdale Shopping Center included anchor department stores, smaller retail 
shops, adjacent office buildings, and ample parking with capacity for 10,000 vehicles.342  
Hillsdale Shopping Center’s Macy’s store was designed by architect John Bolles, with 5 acres of floor 
space, making it Macy’s largest suburban store at the time.343 The center also featured a three-story 
Sears-Roebuck department store that opened in 1951. In 1954, the completed shopping center was 
formally dedicated, with over 50 stores.344 Between the mid-1950s and 1970s, an Emporium 
department store, cinema, and additional shops were added, and Macy’s and Sears expanded.345 
Originally, the Hillsdale Shopping Center was designed as an outdoor shopping center with wide 
integrated pedestrian pathways and storefronts set beneath broad canopies. It was expanded and 
redesigned as an enclosed shopping mall at the end of the post-World War II period, a nationwide 
trend (Figure 56). 
Figure 56 
Hillsdale Shopping Center c. 1950s

Hillsdale Shopping Center served as a regional market while smaller shopping centers and strip malls 
embedded in neighborhoods or anchored alongside commercial corridors served a more localized 
customer base. In 1949, West 25th Avenue, west of El Camino Real to Hacienda Street was widened 
for an improved neighborhood commercial district to serve the Hacienda, Beresford, and Hillsdale 
neighborhood. By that time, it included Manor Theater and Tudor Revival-style shops and a parking 
tract at the corner of Mary Lou Lane and Flores Street.346 Subsequent development introduced mid-
twentieth century commercial buildings alongside those from the pre-World War II period, and 
eventual development of the parking tract for residential use(Figure 57).347 During the same period, 
a residential shopping center was developed on 42nd and Olympic avenues adjacent to the growing 
San Mateo Knolls neighborhood. This commercial zone featured two strips of preexisting Tudor 
Revival-style shops similar to the pre-World War II commercial fabric along West 25th Avenue and 
newer central parking lot and clusters of surrounding shops (Figure 58).348  
The Villa Hotel (now The Villa at San Mateo Apartments), a large property with integrated retail and 
restaurant uses, was built on an L-shaped site spanning from 4000 South El Camino Real to 41st 
Avenue in 1955-1956. Designed by Robert M. Sherman, the hotel, which incorporated a Googie 
architectural design, was built next door to the Villa Cartier restaurant (non-extant), which opened 
in 1925 and the Lanai Tiki Bar (non-extant), established in 1950. Together, the businesses operated 
as the Villa Square. In 1965, the restaurant was added. Designed by Armet & Davis, it first housed 
the Villa Coffee Shop (extant, Maverick Jacks; Figure 59 and Figure 60).349

Shopping District along West 25th Avenue with a mix of pre- and post-World 
War II Commercial Architecture

Beyond shopping centers and strip malls, San Mateo’s mid-century commercial development 
included several Modern corporate-office buildings, bank branches, and storefront remodeling 
featuring the brick, concrete, and metallic materials prevalent in many American downtowns during 
the period. The bank branches were primarily constructed in a three-square-block downtown area 
north of Central Park and along or east of El Camino Real. The brick-clad bank building at 100 East 
4th Street, originally a branch of American Trust Co. and was later incorporated into Wells Fargo 
Bank, was among the earliest post-World War II banks constructed in the downtown (Figure 61).350 
 
350Sanborn Map Company, Sanborn Fire Insurance Map from San Mateo, San Mateo County, California, 1950.

In 1950, Hibernia Bank, then among the West Coast’s oldest financial institutions, opened its first 
branch outside of San Francisco at present-day 195 East 4th Street (extant, altered). The building 
was designed to harmonize with the “suburban atmosphere prevailing in all new buildings along 
Fourth Avenue,” as reported in a local newspaper.351  
Bank of America continued expanding in San Mateo after World War II. Beyond its original San 
Mateo location at 277 B Street, branches opened in Hillsdale at 2900 South El Camino Real (1947), 
Bayshore/19th Avenue at 1860 South Norfolk Street (1956), and the A.P. Giannini Memorial Branch 
at 300 South El Camino Real (1963) (Figure 62). This branch was designed in the New Formalist style 
by the architectural firms of Miller & Steiner and Wurster and Bernardi & Emmons. The façade, 
recessed within an arcaded plaza, features murals by artist Louis Macouillard and mosaics by 
Alphonso Pardinas of Byzantine Mosaics. Both depict A.P. Giannini’s career and the history of Bank 
of America.352

Another example of New Formalist design is the former American Savings & Loan branch building at 
195 East 4th Street. Built in 1973 it features tapered concrete columns and double-height bays filled 
with curtain walls or marble panels (extant).353 Both the Bank of America Branch and the former 
American Savings & Loan buildings express modernist approaches to interpreting the classical forms 
and detailing of earlier bank buildings. Additional examples of bank branches constructed during the 
post-World War II period include 444 South El Camino Real (extant) and 491 South El Camino Real 
(extant).  
Retail buildings from the post-World War II period also claimed a place in San Mateo’s downtown. 
The building at 88 East 4th Avenue was completed in 1949 for prominent San Francisco Bay Area 
women’s clothing retailer Joseph I. Magnin (Figure 63).354 The Streamline Moderne-style building 
succeeded an earlier Joseph I. Magnin store at 43 3rd Avenue and was reported as the largest 
suburban store for women’s clothing in the nation at the time of its opening. The building was 
originally designed to house various departments in separate “shops” across a single floor, with a 
dramatic corner window, green marble-clad exterior, recessed planting boxes at each window, and 
brown- and magenta-striped awnings.355

Former Joseph I. Magnin Store at 88 East 4th Street

In 1952, a four-unit retail building was built at the northeast corner of East 4th Avenue and 
Ellsworth Avenue for the Algodon Company, a corporation of the Judge Aylett R. Cotton family. The 
company later redeveloped of a portion of the Antoine Borel estate as Borel Place. The building was 
designed by architects E. Jay Miller and Robert D. Steiner, who also designed Borel Place buildings, 
and was constructed by Oscar Cavanagh & Son. The building was located opposite the Levy Bros. 
department store (built 1952) and later redeveloped as Draeger’s market (extant).356  
Construction of the Algodon Company’s building at East 4th Avenue and Ellsworth Avenue occurred 
after the demolition of the George Bartlett residence, a pioneer and former San Mateo fire chief. 
The redevelopment foreshadowed future commercial redevelopment in this area of San Mateo. 
Portions of the Algodon building’s storefronts, canopy, and brick accent cladding along Ellsworth 
Avenue remain intact. The city’s mid-century bank branches and office buildings stood alongside 
department stores that were built during the first half of the twentieth century prior to the 
development of shopping centers. These buildings included the City of Paris at 130 2nd Street 
(extant), Levy Bros. at 55 3rd Street (extant), Loewe & Zwierlein at 317 B Street (extant), and J.C. 
Penney & Co. at 200 East 3rd Street, and represent surviving pre-World War II downtown 
commerce.357

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
100 
While most of San Mateo’s post-World War II bank buildings were one to two-stories, commercial 
architecture of the period also included several taller towers expressing varying traits of Modern 
design. These buildings typically served corporate occupancy and gained popularity during this 
period as San Mateo became the executive headquarters for national and regional companies, like 
Franklin Templeton Investments.358 Prominent office complexes were developed on the former 
Borel and Hayne estates; the former located on lands south of the downtown and the latter 
opposite Central Park.  
The Hayne Estate at 5th Avenue and El Camino Real (opposite Central Park) was initially proposed 
for redevelopment as a luxury resort hotel.359 However, plans from 1953 were abandoned and the 
property was redeveloped in the early 1960s with an I. Magnin store and office high rise buildings.360 
The buildings at 500 and 520 South El Camino Real and 85 West 5th Avenue (extant) represent the 
redevelopment of the former estate. 
Borel Place Office Center, located northwest of the El Camino Real and Highway 92 interchange was 
constructed between 1962 and 1967. Developers Aylett B. Cotton and Eric B. Bovet, grandsons of 
financier and Borel estate namesake, Antoine Borel, planned construction around the estate’s trees 
to preserve portions of the wooded setting. The first three buildings in the complex were designed 
by local architects E. Jay Miller and Robert D. Steiner.361 The first building in the complex was 
completed around 1962 after construction of the 19th Avenue Freeway required partitioning of the 
Borel estate. The first two buildings were nearly fully occupied by 1966 when construction of a third 
building commenced, concurrent with planning for an eventual seven buildings. The architecture of 
the complex ranged from two-stories to taller mid-rise construction, with materials including 
redwood and pebble-dash intended to blend with the woodsy setting (Figure 64). Later buildings in 
this office park feature concrete or glass-clad exteriors, spanning the spectrum of regional Bay Area 
modernism to more overtly contemporary design, often referred to as Late Modern.362

An additional example of office construction includes 2555 Flores Avenue. It was completed in 1962 
and designed by architect Vincent G. Rainey, who was known in the San Francisco Bay Area as a 
theater designer.363 Rainey’s design features a first-story lobby level with pointed arch windows and 
elevations featuring octagonal punched window openings and octagonal spandrel detailing running 
between each story (Figure 65).364

A greater concentration of mid- and high-rise office buildings were built downtown on sites adjacent 
to El Camino Real between the mid-1960s and mid-1970s. Examples include Mills Square Tower at 
100 South Ellsworth Street, the former Crocker Bank building at 181 2nd Avenue (Figure 66), 
Heritage Bank of Commerce at 400 South El Camino Real, 444 South El Camino Real, 491 South El 
Camino Real, 520 South El Camino Real, and 4 West 4th Avenue (all extant). Within this group, styles 
include New Formalism and International with evident influence from architect Ludwig Mies van der 
Rohe, through elements like grid-like, exposed steel-fame exteriors and rectangular forms. 
484 of 1110

--- page 485 ---
World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
103 
Figure 66 
Former Crocker Bank Building, 1982 
 
Source: San Mateo Public Library, San Mateo Local History Collection 
By 1971, five hotels and 10 motels provided over 1,400 rooms. Several of the motels were located 
along the former Bayshore Highway, east of U.S. 101, and were heavily used for conventions and 
conferences held in the area.365 In the 1970s, commerce in San Mateo grew, with a population of 
roughly 80,000 at the start of the decade, despite facing challenges from the nationwide inflation 
and energy crises. The Chamber of Commerce noted revenues tied to convention- and business-
related visitation increased compared to 1950 numbers. This reflected a pattern of growth in 
commercial-office space in the city during post-World War II decades, during which time many 
companies established headquarters in San Mateo.366 Likewise, Silicon Valley emerged during this 
period and influenced the vast technology-related commerce throughout the region.367  
 
365 Polk’s San Mateo City Directory, 171, R.L. Polk & Co. Publishers, 1971. 
366 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 265.

https://computerhistory.org/stories/silicon-valley/.

--- page 486 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
104 
In 1973, Franklin Resources, Inc., a financial consultancy, relocated to San Mateo from its previous 
headquarters in New York City. The firm’s offices were within the mid-rise office building at Borel 
Place, now named Bovet Office Centre at 155 Bovet Road.368 Franklin Resources, Inc. has since 
become park of Franklin Templeton, which is based at a corporate office campus at 1 Franklin 
Parkway, developed on a portion of the Bay Meadows racetrack.  
The Bay Meadows racetrack, as the only operating West Coast racetrack during World War II and 
into post-war decades, remained one of San Mateo’s largest employers between the late 1940s and 
late 1970s.369  
9.3 
Civic and Institutional Development 
During the post-war period, San Mateo upgraded civic facilities to accommodate its growing 
population. Additionally, institutional uses, including medical and educational facilities, schools, 
libraries, fraternal organizations, and churches were all significantly expanded during this period. 
In 1942, an 11-building training facility, the Merchant Marine Cadet Basic Training School at Coyote 
Point was rapidly constructed.370 Several thousand cadets graduated from the school and served in 
the Merchant Marines who operated ship fleets and delivered troops and materials during World 
War II. At the height of the war, in 1944, the United Service Organization (USO) established the Tri-
Cities USO at 3rd Avenue and El Camino Real, providing a recreational outlet for service people and 
their families (non-extant). The facility on El Camino Real remained active until 1946 when it was 
sold to the College of San Mateo, which operated from three campuses in the area.371  
Over the next decade and a half, the College of San Mateo used the former military buildings and 
adapted a surplus building for use by the San Mateo County Junior Museum. The college also 
developed Coyote Point Yacht Harbor and Coyote Point Marina later selling the property to San 
Mateo County in 1962. The college opened its existing, centralized campus west of downtown San 
Mateo in 1963.372 Coyote Point became part of the County’s regional park system in 1963, while 
Coyote Point Marina and the park lands were further developed by the late 1960s. Planning for a 
new Junior Museum began around 1973, coinciding with its renaming as Coyote Point Museum and 
ongoing park improvements.373  
City Hall offices, located in a building connected to the San Mateo Fire Department’s headquarters 
at 1st Avenue and South Ellsworth Street (non-extant), were relocated in 1962 to the former 
headquarters of Mobil Oil at present-day 330 West 20th Avenue. The facility on West 20th Avenue 
brought City Hall outside the downtown and away from a cluster of civic and institutional buildings 
located along a two-block stretch of South Ellsworth Street including the fire department

World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979)

headquarters, Post Office, and an American Legion Hall.374 The City Hall building at 330 West 20th 
Street was remodeled and expanded in 1984.375 
During this period, San Mateo’s citizens organized around several issues, including the fight to keep 
Sugarloaf Mountain as open space. In 1971, residents organized a grassroots campaign to save 
Sugarloaf Mountain from development by Cargus Corporation. After a protracted fight, a scaled-
back condominiums and office space development with reserved open space was approved. In 
1978, the City expanded its parks with the opening of Laurelwood Park.376 
Medical 
Institutions including Mills Hospital expanded during the mid-twentieth century, and many modern 
medical buildings were constructed in San Mateo, particularly along North San Mateo Drive. Mills 
Hospital’s West Wing was completed in 1950, as a continuation of periodic expansion from the first 
half of the twentieth century.377 The Baywood Medical Building at 1 Baywood Avenue was 
constructed in 1957. It features several elements of Modern architectural design including 
horizontal massing, uniform rectangular fenestration, and a screened bezel around its windows 
(Figure 67).378 By 1950, North San Mateo Drive contained multiple dental and medical offices along 
the 0, 100, 200, 300, 400 blocks that were close to San Mateo Hospital.379 By 1965, these blocks 
contained even more offices with medical-office uses, indicating that San Mateo continued to hold a 
central position in regional medical services.380 Buildings evidencing this pattern of post-World War 
II development include 101 North San Mateo Drive and 256 North San Mateo Drive (Figure 68).

Schools 
By 1948, San Mateo maintained four private or parochial schools and 13 public schools, ranging 
from elementary/grammar to high school level.381 By 1955, the three private or parochial schools 
were within the city, and the number of public schools grew to 22.382 The growth pattern aligned 
with overall population growth and the development of additional residential neighborhoods. By 
the 1960s, the post-war growth, lead to the creation of the San Mateo-Foster City School District.383 
Racial segregation impacted San Mateo’s schools just as it impacted its residential neighborhoods. 
As noted by the San Mateo County Historical Association:  
Resistance to racial intolerance took place in San Mateo County during the 1960s, perhaps most 
notably at its new College of San Mateo campus. […] Beginning in 1959, Professor Rudolph Lapp 
offered classes in Black History. The College of San Mateo was the first institution of higher learning 
outside of the South to have such a course.384  
In 1963, the California legislature passed the Rumford Fair Housing Act, which outlawed race-based 
housing discrimination and restrictive covenants. The real-estate industry responded by funding the 
Proposition 14 campaign to overturn the Rumford Act, arguing that owners should be able to sell, or 
refuse to sell, their houses to whomever they chose. Although Proposition 14 passed in 1964, in 
1966 the California Supreme Court ruled that Proposition 14 violated the U.S. Constitution. This 
ruling was confirmed by the U.S. Supreme Court in 1967, one year prior to the passing of the federal 
Fair Housing Act of 1968.385 
Efforts to bus students to different schools throughout nearby schools materialized in the mid-
1960s. By that time, the racial makeup of elementary schools reflected the segregation in 
neighborhoods. Some schools were mostly Black or Asian, while other were mostly White. In 1966, a 
community conference to address the subject included approximately 700 people, and in December 
 
381 Burlingame, Hillsborough, San Mateo City Directory 1947-1948, Coast Directory Co., 421.  
382 Polk’s San Mateo City Directory, 1955, R.L. Polk & Co. Publishers, 1955, 1217.

Valley Community Foundation, May 2012), 7.

8, 2025, at https://historysmc.org/exhibits/san-mateo-countys-african-american-history/.

World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
107 
1966 an “Administrative Transfer Plan” was adopted.386 As a result of implementation of the plan, 
45 students from Lawrence and Turnbull Schools were selected as the first to be bused to 10 other 
schools as part of the desegregation process. Based on the success of this busing program, the San 
Mateo Elementary Teachers Associated passed a resolution to support the plan and called for 
desegregation in all city schools.387 
Library 
A modern San Mateo Public Library was constructed at 3rd Avenue and Dartmouth Road in 1969, 
and replaced the previous library at 2nd Avenue and San Mateo Drive, which was later demolished 
for the Mills-Peninsula Health Center’s parking lot. The 1969 library building was demolished in 2003 
and replaced another updated library building in the same location.388 
Fraternal 
The Benevolent Protective Order of Elks (Elks) Lodge No. 1112 was established in San Mateo in 1908 
with 30 members. Originally based out of a building on B Street, the lodge’s membership grew to 
3,300 by 1958. The existing Elks Lodge at 229 West 20th Avenue was constructed in 1954 and 
designed by architects E. Jay Miller and R.D. Steiner.389 
Churches 
During the post-World War II period, many of San Mateo’s oldest religious institutions continued 
serving their constituents, while the count of religious properties grew apace with the spread of 
new neighborhoods and the diversifying demography of the city. While many congregations 
remained rooted at preexisting locations, several undertook projects for new buildings or 
modernized existing sacred spaces after the World War II, sometimes continuing projects that had 
been halted due to Depression-era economics and/or wartime construction limitations. These 
religious properties appeared throughout the city, adjacent to commercial districts, in master-
planned neighborhoods where developers reserved sites for educational or religious properties, or 
in some cases on somewhat isolated sites. City directories listed 11 religious properties in San 
Mateo in 1948, 27 by 1955, and 38 by 1971.390 
Long-time congregation St. Matthew Catholic Church constructed a modern sanctuary and hall at 
1 Notre Dame Avenue between 1948 and 1953 and opened the existing church and rectory in 1966 
(extant).391 During this time, St. Timothy Church at 1515 Dolan Avenue in San Mateo’s Shoreview

neighborhood was opened as a mission church of St. Matthew Parish. The Gothic Revival-style 
church was built in 1954 (extant).392 
The San Mateo Buddhist Temple at 2 South Claremont Street was constructed in 1952 as the first 
permanent facility serving Japanese Buddhists from San Mateo, neighboring Belmont, and San 
Carlos (Figure 69). The temple’s completion followed a World War II-related development pause at 
the property. The Temple facility was expanded in 1980 to provide an education wing and a new 
parking lot (extant).393 
Figure 69 
Japanese Buddhist Temple at 2 South Claremont Street 
 
Source: Rincon Consultants, Inc. 2025 
The Sturge Presbyterian Church at 25 South Humboldt Street was constructed in the 1950s, for a 
primarily Japanese congregation that celebrated the construction of Sturge Memorial Chapel on the 
same property just before World War II. The existing mid-twentieth century church building appears 
to have replaced the cottage entirely.394  
The neighboring Community Baptist Church at 15 South Humboldt Street was established in 1948 
and initially focused on serving San Mateo’s Chinese community (Figure 70). The church 
incorporated as the San Mateo Chinese Baptist Community Church in 1958. It became Community 
Baptist Church in 1962 (extant). Cantonese- and Mandarin-speaking services were added in 1990 
and 2002.395

The original Temple Beth El in San Mateo was developed between 1951 and 1952 to serve a growing 
community of Reform Jews, becoming the city’s first Synagogue.396 The 5-acre site at 1700 Alameda 
de las Pulgas was expanded with a sanctuary, fellowship hall, administrative, educational and library 
spaces, between 1955 and 1956 (extant).397 
The First Presbyterian Church of San Mateo erected a modern sanctuary in 1958, with elements of 
Gothic Revival styling, on Hacienda Street where the street meets the West 25th Street commercial 
district (extant) (Figure 71).398 The First Baptist Church at 2801 Alameda de las Pulgas and Grace 
Lutheran Church 2825 Alameda de las Pulgas were constructed on adjacent parcels near Beresford 
Park in 1960 and 1967, respectively (extant).399 Hope Evangelical Lutheran Church, featuring an 
Expressionistic design that appears similar to an ark, was built in the San Mateo Knolls 
neighborhood in 1965 (extant) (Figure 72).400 These churches offer additional examples of the 
variety of contemporary ecclesiastical architecture developed in San Mateo during the post-World 
War II decades.

9.4 
Transportation Infrastructure 
Post-World War II population growth and the increased prevalence of the automobiles in daily life, 
influenced the development of transportation infrastructure during this period. Much of this 
infrastructure supported travel at a regional scale: road, freeway, or rail. Additionally, development 
492 of 1110

--- page 493 ---
World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
111 
in the downtown included major projects designed to accommodate vehicular traffic and parking 
demand. 
The Bayshore Highway connected communities along the Peninsula’s eastern shore for nearly three 
decades and was reconstructed in the 1950s and designated as part of U.S. Highway 101, because 
the California Department of Highways and Public Works considered the four-lane highway, without 
a central median, accident prone. Improvements including a median and separation from surface 
streets began on the segment in San Francisco in 1950, and construction continued toward San José 
until 1962. The original Bayshore Highway, now parallels the Bayshore Freeway/U.S. 101 and is 
known as Bayshore Boulevard.401  
Concurrently, streetcar service between San Francisco and San Mateo was gradually reduced, as 
ridership fell rapidly after the World War II. After incorporation into San Francisco’s Municipal 
Railway in 1944, the No. 40 interurban route’s service was reduced to shuttle service between San 
Mateo and Daly City, limited service to San Francisco, and “high school trippers” that served San 
Mateo and Burlingame High School students. The San Francisco Board of Supervisors terminated the 
route in 1948, and San Mateo County rejected and option to continue the route under its own 
management, resulting in termination and a final ceremonial trip from San Mateo’s Southern Pacific 
Depot in 1949.402  
As the Bayshore Freeway was redeveloped regional planning for greater connection between San 
Francisco, Oakland, the Peninsula, North Bay, and East Bay sub regions also began. Several concepts 
for increased trans-bay connectivity began in the early twentieth century and included monumental 
projects like the construction of the San Francsico-Oakland Bay Bridge in 1937. Acknowledgement of 
rapid population growth and increased automobile use by the mid-twentieth century led a joint 
Army-Navy review board to conclude that additional trans-bay connection was needed to prevent 
San Francisco-Oakland Bay Bridge congestion in the near future.403 The State Legislature formed the 
BART Commission and then the San Francisco BART District in 1957. However, San Mateo County 
withdrew from the plan in 1961, citing potentially high costs of the new system and what was 
deemed as an adequate existing Southern Pacific commuter train service. Marin County withdrew 
one year later. In subsequent decades, BART connected San Francisco to nearby stations in northern 
San Mateo County, and eventually San Francisco International Airport.  
While planning for BART was underway in the 1960s, the 1929 San Mateo-Hayward Bridge was 
replaced with a new bridge constructed between 1961 and 1967. Its steel girders were built across 
the Bay in Richmond, California by Murphy Pacific Bridge Builders. The bridge received an award 
from the American Society of Civil Engineers for Outstanding Civic Engineering Achievement in 
1968.404 
In 1971, Amtrak took over operation of the nation’s intercity passenger trains and rerouted its Coast 
Daylight line from a terminal in San Francisco to one in Oakland; Southern Pacific’s Del Monte line 
was also discontinued. This resulted in limited commuter train passenger service between San 
Francisco and San José.405 In 1977, one year after San Mateo’s historic Southern Pacific Depot was 
demolished for a parking structure, Southern Pacific petitioned the State Public Utilities Commission 
 
401 Eva Knowles, “Highway 101,” FoundSF the San Francisco Digital History Archive, accessed September 10, 2025, 
https://www.foundsf.org/Highway_101#:~:text=The%20portion%20of%20the%20freeway,runs%20parallel%20to%20U.S.%20101.  
402 Ward Hill, Historic Architecture Evaluation Report: 30 Ingold Road, Burlingame, CA. March 2020, 
https://cms6.revize.com/revize/burlingamecity/Appendix%20H_HAER.pdf; Walter Rice, Ph.D. and Emiliano Echeverria, San Francisco’s 40-
line,” The Museum of the City of San Franciso, n.d., accessed September 2025, at https://sfmuseum.org/hist10/interurban.html.  
403 “A History of BART: The Concept is Born,” San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District, 2025, at https://www.bart.gov/about/history. 
404 Postel, San Mateo: A Centennial History, 254. 
405 “Early Milestones,” Caltrain, accessed September 10, 2025, https://www.caltrain.com/earlymilestones. 
493 of 1110

--- page 494 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
112 
and the Interstate Commerce Commission to discontinue its Peninsula Commute Service.406 Months 
of negotiation with San Francisco, San Mateo, and Santa Clara counties and the California 
Department of Transportation (Caltrans) led to an agreement under which Southern Pacific 
provided commuter service, and public agencies covered most of the operating costs.407  
State Route 92, completed in 1964, connected San Mateo to Half Moon Bay and Highway 380, 
further improving the city’s connection to neighboring communities. The project, however, was not 
without controversy. Conservation groups interested in preserving coastlines were opposed to 
connecting the coast to the Peninsula’s communities.408 
While moving residents, workers, and tourists were factors in shaping expanding regional 
transportation networks, additional parking capacity in the downtown remained a major concern. 
As regional shopping centers drew increasing traffic and offered the convenience of abundant 
parking, downtown commercial districts sought solutions to retain patronage to local businesses. In 
1955, the City’s Central Parking District was created following nearly two decades of studies and 
debate over approaches to parking.409 Bounded by Railroad Avenue, El Camino Real, 2nd Avenue, 
and B Street, the envisioned district included a larger parking lot or structure at its center, which 
required razing buildings including a former Elks Lodge, several retail shops, and at least one 
dwelling. By 1962, a stretch of mid-block surface-level parking existed, indicating some clearance 
had been completed.410 By 1964, plans for the parking district evolved to include a 60-space lot at 
2nd Avenue and El Camino Real, a large garage structure at center, and a separate underground 
parking garage. In November 1964, the first section of the Central Parking Garage, a thru-block 
structure extending through three city blocks between 3rd and 4th avenues, opened to the public. 
An underground parking structure was built in tandem with and below new tennis courts at Central 
Park.411 Development of the Central Parking Garage continued into 1965, as the structure was 
extended southwestward beyond San Mateo Drive and its upper level of parking was finished 
(Figure 73).412

9.5 
Industrial Development 
During the mid-twentieth century, San Mateo retained several local nurseries, where the cut flower 
industry maintained a strong presence during decades leading up to World War II. City directories 
indicate most nurseries were located on lands adjacent to El Camino Real or Bayshore Highway, 
where intensive residential development continued throughout the post-World War II period.413 The 
Pederson & Arnold Company at 4th Avenue and South Claremont Street and the neighboring San 
Mateo Lumber Co. at 501 South Claremont Street (extant) continued to operate lumber milling and 
lumber yard facilities. Additional lumber yards including Foley & Boettcher at 19 Bayshore Highway 
and Horne & Winfrey at 2701 El Camino Real supported the local construction industry.414 These 
lumber yards, and additional industrial properties including a few iron and sheet metal works and 
light-manufacturing plants were located along the U.S. 101 corridor, including on frontage roads 
South Amphflett Boulevard, North Amphflett Boulevard, and adjacent to the Southern Pacific 
Railroad corridor further to the west. 
As indicated by the relatively limited industrial development in San Mateo during post-World War II 
decades, San Mateo became a center of commerce along the Peninsula, where corporations from 
several industries established headquarters or regional offices, rather than an industrial hub.  
 
413 Polk’s San Mateo City Directory, 1948, R.L. Polk & Co. Publishers, 1948, 416. 
414 Polk’s San Mateo City Directory, 1948, R.L. Polk & Co. Publishers, 1948, 414, 418. 
495 of 1110

--- page 496 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
114 
9.6 
Cultural and Ethnic Groups 
African Americans 
In the years before World War II, San Mateo’s small African American population was generally 
concentrated east and north of downtown in the city’s Central neighborhood.415 The Black 
population grew during the war when many came to the area to work in shipyards in South San 
Francisco. Discriminatory real estate practices made it nearly impossible for African Americans to 
settle in many Bay Area communities, causing many to settle in San Mateo’s Central 
neighborhood.416 The African American population grew in the years following World War II.  
Based on 1950 Census records, San Mateo’s African American community continued to reside 
primarily in the area east of Railroad Avenue, west of the Bayshore Freeway/U.S. 101, and north of 
San Mateo Creek (roughly 2nd Avenue). While this area was apparently racially integrated, with 
several White, Japanese, Chinese, and Filipino residents appearing on 1930, 1940, and 1950 census 
schedules, it remained one of the few areas where African Americans and Asian Americans were 
allowed to live in San Mateo. In an interview with La Peninsula, African American San Mateo 
resident, Basil Emery explained, “to the west of the 101 freeway, to the east of the railroad tracks, 
and then Poplar to the north and 3rd was to the south. That was [the neighborhood] we could buy 
in.”417 
In addition to limited housing opportunities, African Americans faced difficulties with employment 
opportunities during this period. During World War II, a Mr. Cullen ran the San Mateo city dump and 
“was the second Black employee of the city, the first one having been a janitor for the Police 
Department,” as noted by scholar Eve Simister in La Peninsula.418 While some fair employment 
practices protests were successful, others appear to largely have resulted in the mere appearance of 
change. For example, when negotiations and promises were made by the Hillsdale Shopping Center 
in San Mateo and the Stanford Shopping Center in Palo Alto, actual implementation was minimal.419 
By the 1960s, the city, like the rest of the country, experienced civil unrest. After the assassination 
of Dr. Martin Luter King, Jr. in April 1968, a thousand high school and college students marched on 
City Hall. In response to the dialogue following King’s death, East Park and the soon-to-be-
constructed East Park Recreation Center at present-day 725 Monte Diablo Avenue were renamed in 
honor of the late civil rights leader.420 As part of the effort in renaming the park, Reverand Rufus 
Cooper of the nearby St. James A.M.E. Zion church, conveyed the support of his congregation and 
expressed the “hope that the park and center will not be segregated in any way.”421 A 1969

World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
115 
newspaper article reported the recently opened King Recreation Center served Black, White, and 
Asian residents, with amenities including meeting spaces.422  
In the summer of 1969, City Council formed the 25-member Human Relations Commission to assist 
in reinforcing state and federal anti-discrimination laws. They addressed complaints of youth against 
police, individual cases of housing discrinimination, and employment issues. Into the 1970s, the 
model of public involvement in the Human Relations Commission was repeated in the Public Works 
Commission, the Youth Advisory Council, the Greater Downtown Commission, and the Housing 
Advisory Appeals Board.423 
Chinese 
World War II was a major turning point for the Chinese residents in the United States and more 
broadly for Asian American and Pacific Island communities. The United States viewed the Republic 
of China, who had been at war with the Empire of Japan since 1937, as an ally, following the attack 
on Pearl Harbor in 1941.  
In 1943, exclusionary legislation limiting Chinese immigration was repealed. New legislation enabled 
additional opportunities for Chinese immigrants to enter the country, or for those already in the 
country to gain citizenship.424 The Magneson Act enabled Chinese residents in the United States to 
become naturalized citizens, which was previously limited to free, White immigrants, based on 
legislation from 1790. The Magneson Act set an annual immigration limit for Chinese immigrants at 
105, based on the Immigration Act of 1924.425  The War Brides Act of 1945 allowed Chinese women 
to immigrate as new brides or to reunite with husbands who had been members of the armed 
services. This led to a demographic shift in Chinese communities, which had historically been largely 
male. The immigration quota tied to the Immigration Act of 1924 was repealed with the 
Immigration Act of 1965, which greatly increased immigration from China, in addition to other Asian 
countries.426 Additionally, second and third generation English-speaking Chinese Americans and 
educated Chinese immigrants sought opportunities to establish careers and residence beyond local 
Chinatowns.  
In San Mateo, the cluster of Chinese and Japanese-used buildings located on South Railroad Avenue 
between 1st and 2nd Avenue prior to World War II, was largely no longer identified with Chinese 
and Japanese uses by 1950. Several buildings formerly associated with the Chinese and Japanese 
populations were demolished, while a smaller group of stores and dwellings, including a Chinese 
laundry, remained (Figure 74).427 This local pattern aligned with wider trends of shrinking 
Chinatowns and Japantowns, due to relocation of residents, redevelopment, and clearance due to 
perceived blight.

view/52467428/?match=1&terms=New%20Center%20is%20Popular%2C%E2%80%9D%20.

1950 Sanborn Map Depicting Area of Former Chinese and Japanese Stores  
 
Source: Sanborn Map Company 1950 
After World War II, the Civil Rights Movement and immigration reform led to major changes in 
immigration policy, including a shift to geographic region-based quotas, allowing for greater 
numbers of immigrants. Racial and ethnic discrimination was also abolished.428 San Mateo County’s 
Chinese population doubled from just under 2,500 to over 5,000 between 1960 and 1970.429 
Ah Sam Florist at 28th Avenue and El Camino Real continued to operate through the Great 
Depression and World War II, including taking over the lease of The Towne Florist, a Japanese-
owned business faced with the forced relocation of its proprietors during the war. The former 
Japanese business was eventually acquired by Ah Sam Florist owners but was closed in 1949 with 
focus reverting to the Ah Sam Florist business. Ah Sam Florist continued to expand after the war, 
with acquisition of additional land behind their store where greenhouses, lathhouses, parking, and a 
warehouse were developed beginning in 1953.430  
Japanese 
The United States’ entry into World War II marked a turning point for the future of federal 
immigration policy and opened up opportunities for some Asian immigrants; however, the war was 
significantly impactful to Japanese communities in across the nation. The West Coast had long been 
an important region for the settlement and formation of Japanese immigrant communities, with 
 
428 Albert A. Acena, “Chinese Americans in San Mateo County,” La Peninsula Vol. XLIII, No. 2, 2015, 4. 
429 Albert A. Acena, “Chinese Americans in San Mateo County,” La Peninsula Vol. XLIII, No. 2, 2015, 4. 
430 Nellie W. Leong, “Ah Sam,” La Peninsula Vol. XLIII, No. 2, 2015, 19. 
498 of 1110

--- page 499 ---
World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
117 
over 50 Japantowns across California alone. By the interwar years, Japanese people significantly 
contributed to agriculture across the state as rice growers in Northern California, at coastal 
vegetable farms, and as laborers and proprietors working in the cut flower industry in the Bay Area, 
including chrysanthemum farms in San Mateo.431 
Following the attack on Pearl Harbor, anti-Japanese sentiment and federal policy resulted in the 
forced relocation and incarceration of Japanese and Japanese Americans, halting the growth of 
Japanese communities in the Western United States. Less than three months after the attack, 
President Roosevelt issued Executive Order 9066 on February 19, 1942, which governed conditions 
for enemy aliens, including all individuals of Japanese descent, including American citizens, as well 
as non-citizen Italian and German residents. Between 1942 and 1945, the U.S. government 
incarcerated over 120,000 Americans of Japanese ancestry, first in temporary assembly centers 
adapted from facilities at racetracks and fairgrounds before sending to War Relocation Centers in 
isolated areas of the West Coast, two of which were in California.432  
During this period, Attorney General Earl Warren actively prompted the creation of maps detailing 
landholdings of Japanese and Japanese-Americans county-by-county. These maps were sent to 
important officials to convince them of the danger the community posed to the state. The maps 
created for San Mateo show Japanese land ownership largely concentrated in the city’s central 
neighborhood, east of the railroad tracks (Figure 75). In San Mateo, evacuation orders given to 
Japanese and Japanese-American residents on May 3, 1942 required them to report to the Masonic 
Temple Building at 100 North Ellsworth.433 In response to the order, the San Mateo County JACL 
released a statement saying, “today, as evacuation orders affect Japanese aliens and American 
citizen Japanese, the sorrow at the thought of leaving their homes is eased by the knowledge that 
by cooperation and sacrifice, they are aiding the United States war effort.”434 On May 9, 1942, the 
last busload of San Mateo County Japanese residents was transported to Tanforan Assembly Center 
in San Bruno.435 
 
431 Page & Turnbull, “Asian Americans and Pacific Islanders in California, 1850-1995” National Register of Historic Places Multiple Property 
Documentation Form (Washington D.C.: US Department of Interior, National Park Service, 2024), 36–39.

1942 “San Mateo Land Occupied by Japanese”

Some cultural institutions stored the belongings of those incarcerated, and in some cases later 
served as hostels for returning members; however, not all property was secured. Many lost their 
possessions, places of business, and residences. After the war ended, many sought residency 
beyond their pre-war communities, including in the Eastern and Midwestern states. While some 
arranged for their properties to be held and secured by sympathetic neighbors or friends, others 
lost their businesses or returned to find their housing occupied by wartime workers who had settled 
in the Bay Area to support shipbuilding and other wartime industries. In San Mateo, the Yamaguchi 
family apparently returned from internment in Topaz to find their house and belongings intact 
(Figure 76).436 Altogether, California lost nearly 10,000 residents of Japanese descent from its 
prewar population of nearly 94,000, per the 1950 Census. During the period of incarceration, San 
Mateo’s Japanese Protestant congregation affiliated with Sturge Memorial Cottage offered and 
turned over their facility to local Chinese. After the war, Chinese and Japanese religious properties 
expanded along South Humboldt Street.437

Mrs. Saiki Yamaguchi and Susan Yamaguchi at the Tanaka House, 38

Sanborn maps from 1950 show that the former Japantown, located alongside Chinatown, as 
nonextant. The one-story apartment building and gathering area, referred to as the Nagaya by the 
Japanese community appeared on the 1920 Sanborn map as “Japanese,” and contained several 
dwellings, a shed, and a more recently built garage for automobiles.438 By 1950, the Nagaya was 
redeveloped with residential buildings. After World War II, the first permanent facility for Japanese 
Buddhists in San Mateo was constructed in 1952.  
An active member of San Mateo’s Buddhist congregation was Yoshiko Yamanouchi (1895–1973), 
wife of Tetsuo Yamanouchi (1878–1936) who founded Imperial Laundry at 214 First Avenue. 
 
438 Sanborn Map Company, Sanborn Fire Insurance Map from San Mateo, San Mateo County, California, 1950. 
501 of 1110

--- page 502 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
120 
Following his death, she continued to run the family laundry business until her family returned to 
San Mateo at the end of World War II. In 1957, following her retirement, Yoshiko Yamanouchi 
purchased property at 1007 East 5th Avenue to build a new house. The Ranch Style house was 
designed by William H. Rowe and built by Hugh H. MacDonald. Following completion of the house, 
Yamanguchi oversaw the construction and design of a traditional Japanese style garden 
implemented by renowned landscape architect Nagao Sakurai. The garden was expanded in 1968 
with a contemporary Japanese garden by Kodo Matsubara and construction of the Katsura Building, 
inspired by the Katsura Imperial Villa in Kyoto. It is connected to the house via a raised walkway 
(ryoka), which is fronted at the streetside by a Japanese style gateway. In addition to its traditional 
and contemporary Japanese landscape, the property (extant) is a significant example of post-war 
Japanese American life in San Mateo and is associated with the life of early San Mateo Japanese 
resident Yoshiko Yamanguchi.439 
Nagao Sakurai’s work is also present in San Mateo’s Central Park. Restrictions on Japanese 
immigration eased in the 1950s and 1960s and the concept of sister cities became popular to foster 
connections between different cultures. In California, there are over 75 sister-city relationships with 
Japanese cities.440 In 1963, San Mateo proposed a sister city affiliation with Toyonaka, a city in the 
Osaka Prefecture. The relationship helped to reinvigorate an earlier effort by the San Mateo 
Gardeners’ Association, made up of mostly Japanese-Americans, to landscape and maintain a 
garden in a public location. Following the creation of the relationship with Toyonaka, a one-acre site 
in Central Park was selected for a Japanese Garden.441 An organization was formed to raise funds, 
and Nagao Sakurai was hired to design it. Construction was mostly carried out by volunteers and 
Toyonaka donated a five-story pagoda for the garden.442 The Japanese Garden (extant) opened in 
1966. 
Filipino 
On December 8, 1941, Japanese forces attacked the Philippines, several hours after the attack on 
Pearl Harbor. As the United States entered World War II, it created Filipino military regiments, and 
many Filipinos residing on the West Coast enlisted. In 1946, the Luce-Cellar Act was passed, and 
granted citizenship eligibility to persons of Asian Indian and Filipino descent. On July 4, 1946, 
President Harry Truman proclaimed Philippine independence and signed the Treaty of General 
Relations, which relinquished U.S. sovereignty, dating to 1898, over the Philippines. While this policy 
still enabled the United States to maintain presence in the Philippines, a second wave of Filipino 
immigration to the United States followed, with some 33,000 Filipinos emigrating by 1965.443  
Whereas previous period of Filipino immigration primarily included single males, the wave of post-
World War II immigration included many women and children, including brides and families of 
Filipino servicemen with U.S. citizenship. Many Filipinas came to the United States for nursing 
training as participants of the Exchange Visitor Program of the State Department, “whose general 
objective was to promote a better understanding of the U.S. abroad through the exchange of 
people, education, and skills, received a monthly stipend for their work at U.S.-sponsored 
 
439 Denise Bradley and Ward Hill, “Yoshiko Yamanhoushi House,” National Register of Historic Places Nomination Form (Washington, DC: 
U.S. Department of the Interior, National Park Service, 2023).

World War II and Post-War Development (1941–1979) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
121 
institutions”.444 The Exchange Visitor Program offered foreign nursing graduates an opportunity for 
two-year postgraduate study and clinical training in U.S. hospitals. The program significantly 
transformed the American nursing labor force, including hospital staff in San Mateo County.  
Additionally, the 1965 Immigration Act (Immigration and Nationality Act) set the stage for a major 
increase in immigration by removing preexisting quotas allowing 20,000 people per year from each 
country in the Eastern Hemisphere. Between 1970 and 1980, San Mateo County’s Filipino 
population more than quadrupled from over 5,000 to over 24,000.445 
Tongan 
The Kingdom of Tonga has been an independent nation and a member of the British 
Commonwealth since 1970. After World War II, Tongans began immigrating increasingly to New 
Zealand, Australia, and the United States to pursue educational and employment opportunities.446 
Immigration to the United States was influenced by the Immigration Act of 1965. Since the 1960s, 
Tongans have settled throughout the Bay Area with relatively high populations in San Mateo and 
neighboring San Bruno and East Palo Alto. As noted by scholars Cathy A. Small and David L. Dixon, 
“The first Tongan migrants were young single men, and occasionally women, who left home […] to 
earn high overseas wages that would help feather nests back in Tonga.” 447 As these migrants 
established permanence, they supported their family’s migration, leading to the formation of 
Tongan communities in Australia, New Zealand, and in the United States. While a relatively small 
community relative to the city’s overall population, San Mateo’s Tongan community remains one of 
the largest in California and the United States.  
Latino 
California experienced small waves of Latino immigration, primarily from Mexico, beginning in the 
mid-nineteenth century. An estimated 10,000 Mexicans came to California during the Gold Rush. 
While many returned home after the Gold Rush, others decided to stay in the United States, with an 
estimated 8,000 Mexicans settling in California during this period. Similar to other immigrant 
populations, Mexican immigrants worked in the state’s early railroad industry and its agricultural 
economy. As a result of limitations on Asian immigration during that period, there was a growing 
reliance on Mexican labor.448 In the following decades, the improved connection between Mexico 
and California, especially by rail made immigration more fluid. In 1910, the Mexican Revolution 
began, resulting in increased violence and instability, and persuading many to relocate to the United 
States as a means of improving their situation.449 
In the early twentieth century, most Mexicans migrating to the United States originated from 
central and southern Mexico and traveled by rail to border towns and settled in Texas and 
California. Once in California, common settlement locations included the Imperial Valley and San 
Joaquin Valley, becoming significant ethnic groups in those communities. The primary urban area 
for Mexican settlement was Los Angeles. Other California cities attracted Mexican populations, 
 
444 Page & Turnbull. Asian Americans and Pacific Islanders in California, 1850-1995, 66. 
445 Albert A. Acena, “’Invisible Minority’ No More: Filipino Americans in San Mateo County,” La Peninsula Vol. XXXVII, No. 1, (Summer 
2008), 17. 
446 Cathy A. Small and David L. Dixon, “Tonga: Migration and the Homeland,” February 1, 2004, The Online Journal of the Migration Policy 
Institute, at https://www.migrationpolicy.org/article/tonga-migration-and-homeland. .

Statement, (California Office of Historic Preservation, 2015), 3. 
449 California Office of Historic Preservation, Latinos in the Twentieth Century California, 4.

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
122 
including nearby San Francisco and San Jose, but in smaller numbers.450 During World War II, 
attitudes toward Mexican labor changed, as war efforts resulted in labor shortages. In an effort to 
relieve the labor shortages the United States and Mexico created a guest worker program officially 
called the Emergency Farm Labor Agreement, known as the Bracero Program, in 1942. The 
agreement allowed Mexican farm workers to enter and work in the United States legally for limited 
amounts of time. The program was hugely successful and was extended for 22 years, resulting in 
lasting effects for the U.S. economy.451 It was not uncommon for workers to arrive on the program, 
stay after the end of their contract, find work in the country, and settle. In increasing numbers, 
those who found work moved to cities.452 
During the post-war period, California’s economy was boosted by the military spending in research, 
technology, and defense during the Cold War. The influx of capital along with the availability of large 
acreages of land contributed to a major development boom in the state. It also stimulated the 
demand for labor in manufacturing, the service industry, and construction.453 In 1965, the U.S. 
immigration policy was overhauled, and strict quotas were set for the number of authorized 
immigrants from different regions of the world. This, in combination with the end of the Bracero 
Program and limited economic opportunities in Mexico, resulted in an increase in unsanctioned 
migrations as the second half of the twentieth century continued. The continued demand for labor 
caused a spike in legal and illegal immigration during the 1970s and 1980s.454  
By the 1970s, the makeup of the Latino immigrant population became more diverse, with more 
populations from Central America. Before the 1970s, immigrant populations from Central American 
were small and mostly from the upper and middle classes from Nicaragua, Costa Rica, Guatemala, 
and El Salvador. Some Central American workers came to the United States through the Bracero 
Program by pretending to be Mexican because the program was limited to Mexican workers. Those 
who stayed in this region, settled in a San Francisco barrio neighborhood developed by the 1920s 
within San Francisco’s Mission District. In the mid-twentieth century American companies invested 
heavily in Central American countries, establishing manufacturing facilities and plants. When the 
global economy stagnated in the 1970s, Central American workers transferred their skills to 
manufacturing jobs in the United States.455 During this period, many Central American immigrants 
found work as domestic labor, too. During this period, South American immigration to the area did 
not materialize in the same magnitude as Mexican and Central American immigration. In San Mateo, 
by 1980, the city had the state’s 14th highest concentration of Mexican immigrant population.456
