Modern San Mateo (1980–2025) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
123 
10 
Modern San Mateo (1980–2025) 
The continued development of San Mateo during the modern period, or recent past, is largely 
defined by its proximity to the expansion and growth of Silicon Valley’s technology industry.  
10.1 
Residential Development  
This period is marked by more limited residential development than in the decades following World 
War II due to several factors, including rising interest rates, economic shifts in the construction 
industry, land use and zoning policies, and the passage of Proposition 13 in 1978. Proposition 13 is 
an amendment to the California Constitution that established limitations on the tax rate and 
assessment increase for real property according to base year values.457 The assessed value of a 
property is limited to a 2 percent annual increase and reassessment on base year values when there 
is a change in ownership or new construction occurs.458 Historically, however, market values of real 
estate have increased more than 2 percent a year. When house values increase by more than 2 
percent a year, homeowners gain from remaining in the same house because their taxes are lower 
than they would be on a different house of the same value. A widely accepted consequence of this 
law is reduced property tax revenue and a tendency for owner-occupiers to not sell their houses, 
thereby reducing the turnover of owner-occupied houses.459 
The city’s residential building stock is characterized by older developments in mostly single-family 
neighborhoods. Approximately 73 percent of houses in the city were built before 1979.460 
Residential development slowed in the two decades before 1980 and continued on a more dramatic 
downward trend between 1980 and 1999.461 People remaining in their owner-occupied houses, 
coupled with the slowing of residential development in the city, contributed to the rising cost of 
housing in the city in recent years. The cost of housing has been further affected by the health of 
the local economy. Since 1980, the Silicon Valley’s technology sector has been a driving economic 
force in the local economy and stimulated population growth. The city’s population was 
approximately 80,000 by the late 1980s, a four-fold increase from 1940, and grew to over 95,000 by 
late 1990s.462 The influx of capital and scarcity of housing contributed to the rising cost of housing 
and the city’s poverty rate. During the 10-year period between 1970 and 1980, the average value of 
a house in San Mateo increased 307.4 percent and has continued to rise.463  
More recent residential developments have generally trended away from single-family only 
neighborhoods common during San Mateo’s suburbanization and post-war expansion to more

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
124 
multifamily housing. Major developments since the 1980s included housing within the mixed-use 
development for Mariner’s Island, which brought the city’s easternmost neighborhoods and a mix of 
multifamily housing types alongside Foster City and the San Mateo-Hayward Bridge/Highway 92 
corridor. Other development included Edgewater Isle and Harbortown. Edgewater Isle was 
condominium developed between 1984 and 1987 by the Anden Group/O’Brien & Hicks developers. 
Marketed to emphasize its bayside location, original floorplans for the North Association were 
named Capri, Malibu, Del Mar, and Newport. The South Association, also called the Cove, had 
nature-based named floor plans like the Peninsula, the Windward, and the Landing.464 Harbortown 
and Mariner’s Isle were developed in 1979–1981 by Mairner’s Isle Associates and are comprised of 
condominiums and townhouses. Harbortown was designed by architect Rodney Friedman and used 
a cluster pier design inspired by New England fishing villages.465 
In 1981, the San Mateo Redevelopment Agency organized. The agency subsidized building out 
apartments in vacant spaces above ground-floor commercial uses, supported façade improvements, 
and provided loans for seismic retrofitting of unreinforced masonry buildings. 466 In 1989, Rotary 
Club’s Rotary Hacienda, a low-income elderly housing complex, was constructed on Hacienda 
Boulevard between 27th and 28th streets (extant). It was constructed with redevelopment funds, 
contributions from the Rotary Club of San Mateo, and with the help of federal tax credits.467 Infill 
residential development of former commercial properties, such as Bay Meadows racetrack, includes 
a combination of multifamily, townhouse, and adjacent corporate offices since the early 2000s. The 
first phase of Bay Meadows redevelopment was funded by redevelopment funds. However, the 
Redevelopment Agency, along with some 400 other similar agencies across the state were dissolved 
through statewide legislation in 2012, restraining the availability of redevelopment funds.468 
Despite the loss of redevelopment funds, additional residential construction has occurred through 
development of formerly commercial or industrial properties through different financing options. 
Recent residential developments focused on transit-oriented opportunities such as San Mateo’s 
three existing Caltrain stations. Recently constructed examples include Kiku Crossing (2025), 
Montara (2020), and Station Park Green (2018).469  
10.2 
Commercial Development 
In 1982, the Hillsdale Shopping Center expanded with a Nordstrom and a Mervyn’s store, as well as 
a second-story addition.470 It became an enclosed shopping mall following a nationwide trend of 
converting outdoor shopping malls to indoor with airconditioned destinations. During this time, the 
downtown was “creeping along,” as stated by former Mayor Claire Mack. The downtown struggled 
with attracting visitors and competing with new shopping center developments that catered to the

Modern San Mateo (1980–2025) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
125 
city’s suburban neighborhoods. As a result, the downtown’s commercial core stagnated in growth 
and redevelopment.471  
Franklin Resources, Inc., a financial consultancy, experienced greater success in the 1980s. After 
relocating to San Mateo in 1973, the company expanded to 1,700 employees, making it the largest 
employer in the city.472 The company grew through mergers in the 1990s and 2000s and relocated 
to the 1 Franklin Parkway office complex located on a portion of the former Bay Meadows 
Racetrack. 
The Bay Meadows racetrack entered its final decades of operation in the 1980s, ultimately closing in 
the early 2000s. By the end of the 1980s, Bay Meadows had more than 600 employees and was 
second to only San Francisco International Airport in contributing to the county’s tax base.473 
However, attendance at races declined due to the popularity of remote betting. Between 2006 and 
2007, the facility’s owner failed to secure an extension on required replacement of dirt track with 
required synthetic track materials, which ultimately spelled closure of the facility in 2008.474 The 
former site of Bay Meadows continues to undergo redevelopment with office, residential, and 
recreational uses.  
Along the city’s eastern perimeter, Mariners Island Shopping Center, later renamed Fashion Island, 
opened in 1982 adjacent to the Interstate 280 and State Route 92 interchange off San Mateo-
Hayward Bridge. A project of Southern California-based developer Ernest Hahn, the shopping center 
was developed to compete with Hillsdale Shopping Center. Fashion Island originally featured a 
Bullock’s store, Montgomery Ward, J.C. Penney, and Liberty House as anchor tenants, with over 100 
additional stores. However, the shopping center faced steep competition from the expanding 
Hillsdale Shopping Center and the demise of several anchor tenants, including Liberty House and 
Bullock’s, by the mid-1980s. In 1995, Sandhill Properties of San Mateo acquired the property and 
redeveloped Fashion Island as Bridgepoint Shopping Center.475  
Commercial office development grew during this period, mostly outside of the residential 
neighborhoods and along the city’s transportation corridors including State Route 92, Highway 101, 
El Camino Real, and in the Bay Meadows racetrack development. A notable commercial 
development from this period is the Century Centre project at 1400 Fashion Island Boulevard 
(extant) constructed by developer Joaquin De Monet between 1985 and 1986, an early commercial 
office development on Fashion Island.476  
10.3 
Civic and Institutional Development 
The city’s public services were expanded during this time. The San Mateo Fire Department updated 
all their existing facilities in 1989 to accommodate women firefighters.477 On October 17, 1989, the 
Loma Prieta Earthquake hit San Mateo, causing millions of dollars in property damage, but no loss of

life.478 In 2017, the San Mateo Police Department replaced their building at 200 South Delaware 
with a new state-of-the art facility, housing the city’s emergency services and dispatch facilities.479 
In 2006, after a comprehensive public outreach effort, the city replaced its main library at the corner 
of Dartmouth Road and Third Avenue with a new, energy-efficient facility with ample space for 
collections and improved access to technology.480 The San Mateo Arboretum Society, who has 
occupied the old Kohl pumphouse in Central Park since 1977, has undertaken several improvements 
to the facility.481 In 1985, architect Leonard Michaels of Leonard Michaels Associates was recognized 
with an award by the American Institute of Architects San Mateo chapter for the then 20-year-old 
Central Park Recreation Building.482 Park improvements in the city continued in the following years. 
In 2000, the former East Third Avenue landfill was redeveloped for Seal Point Park, as part of a 
regional trend to close and redevelop the Bay Area’s shoreline landfills with parks.483 Today, San 
Mateo has 35 parks and 81 percent of San Mateo residents live within a 10-minute walk of a park, 
which is higher than the national average.484 
In 2005, the San Mateo County Expo Center completed a $3 million renovation and was renamed 
the San Mateo County Event Center of the San Franciso Peninsula. The 1930s facility was updated 
with teleconferencing capabilities, event space, and new exhibit space.485 At Coyote Point, the 
Coyote Point Museum was constructed by Spencer Associates with exhibit specialist Gordon 
Ashby.486 Though the museum nearly closed in 2006 due to financial difficulties, fundraising efforts 
were successful and it was rebranded CuriOdyssey in 2011.487 
10.4 
Cultural and Ethnic Groups 
Since 1980, San Mateo has continued to diversify. The population grew from 77,561 residents in 
1980, 85 percent of which was White, to 85,486 residents in 1990, by which time the city’s 
Asian/Pacific Islander population had increased to represent 13 percent of the city’s population.488 
Between 1980 and 1990, the city’s Hispanic/Latino population grew from 9 to 15 percent.489  
By 2020, San Mateo’s population had grown to 105,661 residents in 40,263 households. The 
population was 38 percent White, 26 percent Asian, and 26 percent Hispanic/Latino.490 The city’s 
Hispanic/Latino population is mostly concentrated in the North Central neighborhood. While the

Modern San Mateo (1980–2025) 
 
Historic Context Statement 
127 
rest of the city’s population grew 8 percent between 1990 and 2000, the North Central 
neighborhood experienced an 18 percent growth.491 By 2010, the North Central neighborhood was 
comprised by a majority Hispanic/Latino population.492 The city’s expanding Asian and 
Hispanic/Latino populations resulted in an influx of Asian and Hispanic/Latino owned businesses, 
markets, and restaurants.  
10.5 
Transportation Infrastructure 
In the recent past the city’s transportation infrastructure has grown. The railroad continues 
providing access to the surround areas, and continues to be an important link for commuters and 
residents alike. On July 1, 1980, Caltrans assumed responsibility for the management of the 
Peninsula Commute Service, formerly controlled by Southern Pacific Railroad. Caltrans continued 
management of the service until 1992 when the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board assumed 
operation of Caltrain and contracted with Amtrak to operate the commuter service.493 Today the 
city has three Caltrain stations—Downtown, Hayward Park, and Hillsdale. In 1999, Hayward Park 
Station opened at 401 Concar Drive, south of the original location, which was known as Leslie 
Station prior to 1936.494 In addition to Caltrain, the city’s public transit includes bus service provided 
by SamTrans. SamTrans operates express bus service at the U.S. 101/State Route 92 park-and-ride 
lot, which was developed in 1987.495 
Vehicular motorways have expanded in recent years. The State Route 92/U.S. 101 interchange 
linking the San Mateo-Hayward Bridge with 19th Avenue and College of San Mateo was completed 
in 1982–1985.496 Today, U.S. 101 (Bayshore Freeway), State Route 92 (J. Arthur Younger Freeway), 
and State Route 280 all provide regional access to the community. Within the city, arterials like El 
Camino Real, San Mateo Drive, Hillsdale Boulevard, Norfolk Street, and Alameda de las Pulgas are 
the major thoroughfares for vehicular travel. In more recent years, the city has improved and 
expanded its bike networks to connect neighborhoods to schools, parks, and train stations. 
10.6 
Industrial Development 
Since 1980, San Mateo’s economy shifted from agriculture, manufacturing, and retail and financial 
services toward professional services, mostly related to the region’s technology economy. In the 
1990s and 2000s, the rise of the technology and information economy has driven the region’s 
economy. Between 2010 and 2019, available jobs based in San Mateo grew by 33 percent, with 
nearly 53,000 people employed in the city as of 2024. Professional services and technology account 
for 20 percent of jobs in San Mateo.497 In addition to the major healthcare providers in San Mateo, 
several technology companies are based in or have headquarter facilities in the city. With the 
increase in professional and business economy, house values and local wages have markedly

Architectural Styles 
 
Historic Context Statement 
129 
11 
Architectural Styles 
This section describes architectural styles that contribute to the character of San Mateo’s built 
environment. The identified styles are arranged in chronological order of their approximate periods 
of popularity during periods of the city’s development, while acknowledging some examples of each 
style may have been built outside of their periods of popularity. Additionally, buildings within the 
city may incorporate features of more than one style, reflecting the tastes of the owner, design 
professional, and approaches to building construction that evolved through the periods studied in 
this historic context statement.  
Buildings appearing in this section are included as local examples of particular architectural styles 
that retain many of the style’s character-defining features. However, inclusion of a building in this 
section does not necessarily indicate that it meets the historic significance and integrity 
requirements for eligibility. 
11.1 
Gothic Revival (Carpenter’s Gothic) 
The Gothic Revival began in eighteenth-century England as medieval houses were remodeled and 
new houses with Gothic features, such as pointed-arch windows, steep gable roofs, carved 
bargeboards, and embattlements (a feature derived from castles) were constructed. The style was 
used for houses in the United States primarily between the 1840s and 1860s, as house designers 
(not necessarily professional architects), including Andrew Jackson Davis and later Andrew Jackson 
Downing, influenced the development of the Carpenter Gothic sub-style. Davis built a Gothic Revival 
style house in Maryland in 1832 and authored Rural Residences in 1837. The book included plans for 
houses and helped introduce the style. He influenced Andrew Jackson Downing, who published and 
more intensively promoted several pattern books in the 1840s, which carpenters and builders used 
in constructing houses for clients. Although some examples of Gothic Revival/Carpenter Gothic 
houses were built in cities, these houses were in most cases built in rural areas at the time of 
construction, as the style was promoted as suited to rural areas where integration with natural 
landscapes played an important role in housing design. In the United States, Greek Revival and 
Italianate style were more commonly applied to houses constructed during the prime years of the 
Gothic Revival style’s use.499 
 
 
 
499 Virginia Savage McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, Alfred A. Knopf, 2013, 266–280. 
Image to be replaced with local 
example

--- page 512 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement

Period of Significance: 1840s–1860s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Typically one- to two-and-a-half stories in height 
▪ 
Building footprints are square, rectangular, or L-shaped, sometime with projecting bays 
▪ 
Roofs are gable with steep pitches and often include a center cross gable, or in rare cases two or 
three cross gables 
▪ 
Eaves overhang the building footprint, with gabled ends often trimmed with carved 
bargeboards/vergeboards (commonly referred to as “gingerbread”) 
▪ 
Exterior finishes include smooth stucco (sometimes scored to mimic stone), or wood siding in 
horizontal or vertical (board-and-batten) configurations 
▪ 
Windows are typically double-hung with wood sashes (2-over-2 glazing is most common); other 
glazing patterns such as intersecting muntins (diamond-shaped) are more rare 
▪ 
Pointed-arch gable windows are common 
▪ 
Front porches with brackets at frame corners 
▪ 
Bracketed eaves

Historic Context Statement 
131 
11.2 
Folk National 
The Folk National style broadly captures variations of traditional, vernacular residential architecture 
built in the United States between the mid-nineteenth century and early twentieth century. The 
expansion of railroad networks in the United States during the mid-1800s, along with the 
introduction of balloon framing, increased the efficiency of supply chains and brought building 
materials to lumber yards in developing communities. As stylistic trends evolved during the late 
1800s, local builders, artisans and carpenters, constructed vernacular residences that incorporated 
traditional gable-front, gable-front-and-wing, pyramidal, and other one- or two-story variants.500 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1870s–1900s 
Associated Property Types: Residential 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Typically one- to two-stories in height 
▪ 
Building footprints are rectangular, or L-shaped (gable-front-and wing) 
▪ 
Roofs have moderate overhangs and are typically not ornamented with brackets or 
bargeboards/vergeboards 
▪ 
Front porches are common and sometimes recessed into a corner 
▪ 
Exterior finished with wood siding 
▪ 
Windows are typically wood, with double-hung sashes and often one-over-one glazing 
▪ 
Exteriors feature little to no ornamentation, but may feature some molded wood trim 
 
500 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 133–147. 
513 of 1110

--- page 514 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
132 
11.3 
Folk Victorian 
During San Mateo’s first few decades, vernacular construction methods, which relied on the skills 
local builders, artisans and carpenters, and regionally available materials, were adapted to build 
residences, commercial buildings, and other structures that were not necessarily representative of 
an architectural style. Folk Victorian is a late nineteenth century vernacular trend that featured 
houses of one- to two-story heights, simple floor plans ranging from square, rectangular, to L-
shapes, and roofs with gable or hip (sometimes pyramidal) forms. Exteriors were often finished with 
horizontal wood siding, board-and-batten, or stucco. Although some detailing was common, such as 
a decorative bargeboard on a gabled end, or spindlework or scroll-cut brackets at porches, 
ornamentation was limited when compared to high-style examples of Italianate, Stick, or Queen 
Anne style architecture. Some very early surviving examples of residential architecture in San Mateo 
can convey aspects of Folk Victorian architecture. However, the representation of this building 
approach in the present-day is dependent on the degree of alteration vernacular buildings have 
been subject to over time.501 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1870s–1900s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Typically one- to two-stories in height 
▪ 
Building footprints are square, rectangular, or L-shaped, sometime with a projecting bay 
▪ 
Recessed front porches are common 
▪ 
Exterior finished with wood siding (horizontal or board-and-batten) or stucco 
▪ 
Windows are typically wood, with one-over-one or two-over-two glazing 
▪ 
Ornamentation is limited, but may include bargeboards or spindlework at porches 
▪ 
Roofs appear in gable, hip (sometimes pyramidal) forms 
 
501 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 396–405.

Architectural Styles 
 
Historic Context Statement 
133 
11.4 
Italianate Style 
During San Mateo’s early settlement, more elaborate examples of country houses included the 
Italianate Style, a popular Victorian era style. American Italianate houses were inspired by Northern 
Italian farmhouses. The style was first adapted by English architects and became known in the 
United States through English pattern books. During the style’s popularity it was considered 
appropriate for a rural setting because of its asymmetry and picturesque characteristics. Some of its 
elements are drawn from Classical detailing and the Gothic Revival style. The style includes a range 
of expressions from single family houses to modest Flat-Front Italianate commercial buildings. Some 
early surviving examples of residential architecture in San Mateo can convey the Italianate Style, but 
the representation of this building approach in the present-day is dependent on the rarity of 
surviving examples and the degree of alteration surviving examples may have been subject to over 
time. 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1860s–1880s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Typically one- to two-stories in height 
▪ 
Rectangular massing and form 
▪ 
Horizontal exterior wood cladding 
▪ 
Roof with gently sloping sides and deep eaves 
▪ 
Tall and slim window openings, often rounded at the top 
▪ 
Columned entryways 
▪ 
Decorative brackets below the roofline 
▪ 
Bay windows 
▪ 
Balustraded balconies and verandas 
▪ 
Ornamental features like wood corner quoins imitating stone, wood water tables, and wood 
pediments 
515 of 1110

--- page 516 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
134 
11.5 
Queen Anne  
The Queen Anne style was introduced to the United States in 1876, during the Centennial Exposition 
in Philadelphia, after it originated and was named in England by a group of architects including 
Richard Norman Shaw. This style was used most frequently in Southern California between 
approximately 1885 and 1895. Although named for Queen Anne, buildings constructed in this style 
did not strongly mimic those built during Queen Anne’s reign in the 1700s. Rather, Queen Anne 
style buildings had more similarity to those built during Queen Elizabeth I’s reign but typically lacked 
stone and brick materials associated with Elizabethan architecture. The Queen Anne style buildings 
introduced to the United States by England during the 1876 Centennial Exposition in Philadelphia, 
featured half-timbering and patterned masonry that was tied into the growing Arts and Crafts 
Movement. As the style was popularized in the United States, varying forms and subtypes 
incorporated elements of Victorian period architecture, representing a culmination of styles such as 
Italianate, Stick, and Eastlake, while also adding Classical details such as columns Palladian windows, 
swags, and garlands. The style also combined rectangular or square plans with projecting bays 
featuring circular, rectangular, hexagonal, or octagonal footprints to create varied compositions. 
Additionally, roof forms often combined intersecting or layered gables, dormers, and steeply 
pitched towers. Exteriors were finished with horizontal wood siding and molded trim, but also 
regularly featured shingles of varying profile as primary cladding materials or as accents at gabled 
ends or projecting bays. The style was introduced by architects but was popularized through pattern 
books and mail-order catalogs providing example plans, which could be delivered across expanding 
rail networks. The Queen Anne style remained popular into the 1890s, but was succeeded by a 
return to Neoclassical and Craftsman styles by the turn of the twentieth century.502 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1876–1890s 
Associated Property Types: Residential 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
One- to three-stories height 
▪ 
Asymmetrical façade composition 
 
502 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 344–370; Anne Greening and Elena Moore, Victorian Era Styles (1870-1910), San

Historic Context Statement 
 
136 
11.6 
American Colonial Revival 
The American Colonial Revival style appeared throughout the United States from the late 
nineteenth century through the mid-twentieth century and reinterpreted traditional architectural 
motifs of the American Colonial era that spanned parts of the seventeenth and eighteenth 
centuries. Architecture in North American colonies often adopted the construction methods, 
materiality, and aesthetic preferences of the nations who colonized areas now within the United 
States. The American Colonial Revival style is primarily associated with domestic architecture in New 
England, the Mid-Atlantic, and Midwest regions, but also appeared on the West Coast with less 
frequency, where Spanish colonial influence played a major role in the development of regional 
architecture. The American Colonial Revival style draws heavily on English and Dutch influences. The 
Centennial Exposition in Philadelphia of 1876 and the World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago of 
1893 popularized the American Colonial Revival style on a national scale. Both expos used colonial 
architecture in their displays as a way of idealizing the colonial era as a time when everything was 
simpler and purer. This desire for the past turned into an enthusiasm for all forms of colonial 
nostalgia, ranging from clothing to furniture.  
By the turn of the twentieth century, this trend matured as a distinct style that was more faithful to 
eighteenth-century American colonial architecture. Between 1910 and 1930, the percentage of new 
houses built in this style in the United States was around 40 percent. Elements of this style were 
applied to Minimal Traditional houses constructed between the 1930s and 1950. Likewise, American 
Colonial Revival styling is applied to new construction in the twenty-first century.503 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1880s–1930s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Rectangular plan 
▪ 
Primarily feature brick cladding, usually with secondary stucco or concrete exterior surfaces, 
and terracotta or cast stone ornament at the base 
▪ 
Flat roofs with parapets or hip roofs, typically with clay tiles and overhanging eaves 
▪ 
Tripartite vertical organization (base, shaft, capital) 
 
503 Teresa Grimes and Elysha Paluszek, “Theme: American Colonial Revival, 1895-1960,” from Survey LA: Los Angeles Historic Resources 
Survey Los Angeles Citywide Historic Context Statement, Los Angeles Department of City Planning, December 2015, 3–22. 
https://planning.lacity.gov/odocument/d26d7637-d6da-4466-aa74-992d63a284dc/American%20Colonial%20Revival%2012-2-15_0.pdf.

138 
11.7 
Classical Revival 
The Classical Revival style draws inspiration from many Classical architectural sources, including 
ancient architecture of Greece, Rome, and Italian architecture constructed during the Renaissance 
and Baroque periods. During the late nineteenth century, the profession of architecture matured 
and relied increasingly on academic influences and training, which included study of historical 
approaches to design. Some architects studied at institutions such as the Ecole des Beaux Arts in 
Paris, France, where they learned formal principles of design. The institution is also associated with 
the development of Beaux Arts Classicism, or the Beaux Arts style, which resulted in very formal and 
often monumental designs; for example, San Francisco’s City Hall. While Classical Revival style 
shares some aspects with the Beaux Arts style, Classical Revival style buildings incorporate classical 
architectural vocabulary with simpler approaches intended to adapt features such as columns, 
porticos, entablatures, and cornices to a variety of building scales: single-family residences, 
apartment buildings, banks, schools, etc.504  
 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1890s–1920s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Symmetrically balanced primary elevation with rectangular massing 
▪ 
Primary elevation dominated by full-length porch with roof supported by classical columns 
▪ 
Entries typically have elaborate doors with decorative surrounds based on Greek Revival, 
Federal, or Georgian designs 
▪ 
Rectangular multipane double hung windows, topped with broken pediment detailing 
▪ 
Ornamental and decorative elements include columns, cherubs, fluting, and boxed-eave 
cornices with dentils 
 
504 Elena Moore et al., Progressive Era & Early Revival Styles (c. 1890-c. 1930), San Francisco Planning Department, January 18, 2023, 37–
39.

11.8 
Renaissance Revival 
The earliest interpretations of the Renaissance Revival style in the United States date to the second 
half of the nineteenth century and consist mostly of architect-designed residences that were 
inspired by Italian architecture of the sixteenth century. By the 1890s, more detailed documentation 
on Italian architecture was available, and therefore, Renaissance Revival architecture tended to 
more accurately replicate aspects of the architecture that it was influenced by than the earlier 
Italianate architecture of the 1860s and 1870s. By the 1920s, the style was also used to design high-
rises, commercial buildings, and civic buildings in downtowns, with several examples in the Bay 
Area. After 1930, the style’s popularity declined. In San Mateo, most of the best examples of the 
Renaissance Revival style are downtown commercial buildings constructed during the 1920s, though 
commercial and institutional properties also represent the style. 505 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1890s–1920s 
Associated Property Types: Commercial, Residential 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Houses are typically two to three stories, sometimes with a raised basement 
▪ 
Tripartite composition of base, shaft, and capital 
▪ 
Recessed entrances 
▪ 
Rusticated first story, with typically smooth stucco or brick exterior above 
▪ 
Details including quoins at corners, molded window surrounds and hoods, and sometimes 
columns 
 
505 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 496–509. 
521 of 1110

--- page 522 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
140 
11.9 
Mission Revival  
The Mission Revival style is an architectural style that was influenced by romanticism surrounding 
former Spanish missions, churches, and other adobe buildings constructed during the colonial 
period. Many of these buildings were in poor condition by the late nineteenth century but served as 
symbols of California’s early settlement by Europeans. They inspired architects, corporations, and 
institutions who sought architecture that was could be considered authentically Californian. The 
application of Spanish colonialist architectural motifs also was a way of developing a regional style 
that varied from other parts of the country. Until this point, West Coast architects had adopted East 
Coast styles, not embracing their own history and surroundings until the Mission Revival style. 
Furthermore, as interest in Victorian-era styles (such as Italianate and Queen Anne) was surpassed 
by the growing Arts and Crafts Movement that emphasized basic forms and an appreciation for 
natural materials, the architecture of the Spanish colonial period was rediscovered. The style 
developed and spread through California, the American Southwest, and eventually as far as the East 
Coast, as it was adopted by institutions and corporations who sought architecture that was 
identifiable with California. Spanish colonial buildings also received attention through the 
publication of literature and art in the late eighteenth and early twentieth centuries. For instance, 
several railroad companies selected Mission Revival style for many railroad depots, which brought 
the style into many California communities as transcontinental travel increased.506 
After 1915, the Mission Revival style was eclipsed by the more widespread Spanish Colonial Revival 
style. The Mission Revival style also influences architecture of the recent past and present day, with 
elements such as stucco, tiled roofs, arcaded facades, and shaped parapets appearing often as a 
regional vernacular (or design language).507  
 
 
Period of Significance: 1890s–1910s 
Associated Property Types: Commercial 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Espadaña: a curved or scalloped gable end or parapet extension that functions as an extension 
of the gabled end wall above the roof line, with openings for bells 
▪ 
Corner towers 
 
506 Karen J. Weitze, California’s Mission Revival, (Hennesy & Ingalls, Inc., 1984), David Gebhard 1967, Los Angeles Department of City 
Planning 2016, McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 2013.  
507 Karen J. Weitze, California’s Mission Revival, (Hennesy & Ingalls, Inc., 1984), David Gebhard 1967, Los Angeles Department of City

Historic Context Statement 
 
142 
11.10 Craftsman 
A style driven by a search for the “simplicity” of pre-industrial times, the Craftsman style was greatly 
influenced by the Arts and Crafts Movement. The style emphasized natural materials and 
craftsmanship associated with construction and design. The bungalow became a typically single-
story, mass-produced house grouped in middle class neighborhoods. The Craftsman style 
incorporated elements of Swiss, Japanese, and English architecture and the philosophies of the Arts 
and Crafts Movement to create site-specific buildings that were modest in form while still 
embracing craftsmanship and artistic tradition. By the start of World War I, the style declined in 
popularity due to a change in tastes. Nonetheless, bungalows relying less on wood materiality and 
more so on economical stucco exteriors were built in high numbers during the 1920s.508 In San 
Mateo, the Craftsman tradition is expressed in many bungalows, an often low-cost form of the style. 
The popular sub-type, the California Bungalow, was constructed between 1910 and 1925. Common 
bungalow typologies include a front or side gable roof with a projecting porch or a hipped roof 
cottage, which often features a central dormer. Also seen in San Mateo is the Classic Box variation, 
which has a boxy design with a square footprint and is usually two stories with a hipped roof, central 
dormers, and a projecting front porch.  
 
 
Period of Significance: 1890s–1930s 
Associated Property Types: Residential 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
One- to two-story height 
▪ 
Exteriors typically clad with shingles or wood siding; stucco exteriors were increasingly common 
from mid-1910s to 1920s  
▪ 
Low pitched gabled roofs with wide and overhanging eaves, sometimes with flared, Japanese-
influenced profiles 
▪ 
Exposed structural or decorative wood roofing elements: roof beams, rafters, brackets, braces 
▪ 
Broad front porches spanning the half or full width of the building  
▪ 
Square or tapered (pyramidal) columns, typically constructed of wood or stone 
▪ 
Stone (“river rock”) or brick accent materials 
 
508 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 567–578; Teresa Grimes, “Arts and Crafts Movement, 1895-1930” from Survey LA: Los

at https://planning.lacity.gov/odocument/18037253-197d-483a-8b13-c85fcd553fe8/ArtsandCraftsMovement_1895-1930.pdf.

144 
11.11 Spanish Colonial Revival 
The Spanish Colonial Revival is an architectural style prevalent in California, which was inspired by 
the growing interest in the vernacular architecture of Andalusia in Southern Spain in the 1900s. The 
Spanish Colonial Revival style incorporates details inspired by Moorish, Byzantine, Gothic, or 
Renaissance architecture, as well as roof tiles common to historical construction in the 
Mediterranean region. The style’s form fit California’s warm climate and was greatly adaptable to a 
variety of size and site conditions. The 1915 Panama-California Exposition in San Diego introduced 
the style to a wide audience, and along with attention paid to European architecture during World 
War I, encouraged its popularity. Houses in this style were constructed with side-gabled, cross-
gabled (L-), or compound plans featured gabled and hip roof forms. Exteriors were typically smooth 
of moderately textured stucco with ornamentation reserved for columns (often separating 
windows), paneled wood doors with elaborate surrounds, gable vents set behind patterned screens 
or grilles or made of cylindrical pipe. More so than detail, the picturesque composition of the 
building itself was emphasized, with features such as porches, small balconies, towers or turret 
bays, and prominent focal windows appearing in strong examples of the style.509 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1915–1930s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Stucco exteriors, usually smooth, but sometimes with moderate texture 
▪ 
Low-pitched gable and/or hip roofs with little to no eave overhang 
▪ 
Clay roof tiles 
▪ 
Arched windows and openings 
▪ 
Occasional tower used as a vertical accent 
▪ 
Enclosed outdoor spaces 
▪ 
Wrought iron railings for balconies and window grilles (rejas) 
▪ 
Picturesque massing, comping rectangular and cylindrical masses 
▪ 
Asymmetrical façades 
 
509 Daniel Prosser, “Subtheme: Spanish Colonial Revival 1912-1942” from Survey LA: Los Angeles Historic Resources Survey Los Angeles 
Citywide Historic Context Statement, Los Angeles Department of City Planning, November 2018, 14-25, at

fd140763de90/MediterraneanandIndigenousRevivalArchitecture_1893-1948.pdf.

façade) 
▪

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
146 
11.12 Tudor Revival 
The Tudor Revival style is an eclectic mixture of English building traditions of the Tudor, Elizabethan, 
and Jacobean eras, drawing inspiration from housing types ranging from cottages to large mansions. 
Historians have also referred to the style as “Jacobethan” given its historical influences. In the 
United States, the style emerged as an adaption of Tudor, Elizabethan, and Jacobean features to 
Queen Anne style houses, eventually evolving to more faithfully revive English historical styling. It is 
often identified by steeply pitched gable roofs and distinct half-timbered surfaces, where stucco or 
brick fill wall areas between visible timber beams. Through explicit reference to English country 
architecture, the flexible and easily adaptable style with great versatility in floor planning was a 
popular choice for middle class houses in the late 1920s and 1930s, possibly accounting for around 
25 percent of houses built during the 1920s. Like the Queen Anne style that preceded it in 
popularity in the United States, the Tudor Revival style also relied on asymmetry, intersecting and 
projecting roof forms in creating picturesque massing. Other common, prominent features included 
massive chimneys, leaded-glass or wood casement windows in single, paired, and other multiple 
sash configurations, stained glass, and arched openings at entrances.510  
 
 
Period of Significance: 1890s–1930s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
One- to three-story height 
▪ 
Varied plans, steeply-pitched cross-gabled roofs  
▪ 
Half-timbering at the exterior, often on the face of projecting bays or gabled ends 
▪ 
Brick or stucco primary cladding with brick or stone accents  
▪ 
Large, elaborate chimneys often crowned by decorative chimney pots  
▪ 
Entrances with arched openings 
▪ 
Hand-hewn detailing on wood beams framing porches or entrances 
▪ 
One or more groupings of wood or metal casement windows usually in groups of three or more 
▪ 
May have rectangular or diamond-shaped panes 
 
510 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 448–474. 
528 of 1110

--- page 529 ---
Architectural Styles 
 
Historic Context Statement 
147 
11.13 French Eclectic 
Similar to other revival styles, French Eclectic drew on inspiration from the architecture, history, and 
literature of preindustrial periods in Europe. Primarily used in single-family residential 
neighborhoods, it is often the least decorative of the residential revival styles, and often only 
involved the application of one or two design elements on an otherwise minimalist building. 
Inspired by rambling French farmhouses, it became popular in the United States after World War I. 
Variations of the style are called “French Norman” or “French Provencial”, and uses combinations of 
materials including stucco, half-timbering, stone, and wood-shingle to suggest development over 
time. More complex examples of the style feature a prominent tower entry at the primary 
elevation.  
 
 
Period of Significance: 1920–1940 
Associated Property Types: Residential 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
One- to two-story height 
▪ 
Irregular massing and fenestration 
▪ 
Horizontal massing 
▪ 
Roofs with long pitches, may be steeply pitched, hipped, clipped 
▪ 
Stucco, brick, and stone exteriors 
▪ 
Half-timbering 
▪ 
Dormers

City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
148 
11.14 Art Deco 
Art Deco, which takes its name from the Exposition Internationale des Arts Décoratifs et Industriels 
Modernes (International Exhibition of Modern Decorative and Industrial Arts) held in Paris in 1925, 
was a short-lived response to modernism that quickly spread to the United States. American 
architects designed elaborate buildings that broke from the rigid structure of the Beaux Arts 
classical tradition. Art Deco captured the spirit of modernity, while still incorporating expensive and 
sophisticated forms and materials in a way that exuded a sense of luxury and splendor. At the time, 
the recent discovery of King Tutankhamun’s tomb, in part, prompted a cultural fascination in non-
Western design, leading Art Deco architects to draw inspiration from Ancient Egyptian, 
Mesopotamian, or pre-Colombian cultures, including the Maya of Central America. Some buildings 
were designed with features such as shaped parapets, zig-zag shaped detailing, and ornate cornices 
or other elements that showcased Art Deco styling, while some buildings simply featured applied 
ornamentation that lent buildings with generally less distinctive forms a bit of Art Deco flair. Further 
development of the style was truncated by the Great Depression and its ostentatious and wealthy 
silhouettes quickly faded away from American architecture.511 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1925–1930s 
Associated Property Types: Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
One- to three-story height 
▪ 
Varied plans, steeply-pitched cross-gabled roofs  
▪ 
Half-timbering at the exterior, often on the face of projecting bays or gabled ends 
▪ 
Brick or stucco primary cladding with brick or stone accents  
▪ 
Large, elaborate chimneys often crowned by decorative chimney pots  
▪ 
Entrances with arched openings 
▪ 
Hand-hewn detailing on wood beams framing porches or entrances 
▪ 
One or more groupings of wood or metal casement windows usually in groups of three or more 
▪ 
May have rectangular or diamond-shaped panes 
 
511 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 580–585. 
530 of 1110

--- page 531 ---
Architectural Styles 
 
Historic Context Statement 
149 
11.15 Streamline Moderne 
After the Art Deco style entered the American architectural scene in the mid-1920s, the harsh 
realities of the Great Depression subdued the sense of optimism associated with the years 
immediately after World War I. Despite economic downturn, scientific and technological 
advancements continued to be made during the interwar years between the early 1920s and early 
1940s, forming what has been described as the American Machine Age. Design professionals sought 
methods of balancing design with economic constraints and influenced by the design of Modern 
machines (cars, planes, trains, steamships). The Streamline Moderne style took the principles 
emphasized by industrial designers such as Norman Bel Geddes (the “father of streamlining”) and 
replaced the geometric and exotic aspects of Art Deco with smooth exteriors, curved forms, and an 
emphasis on horizontal lines that lent architecture a sense of movement. More affordable materials, 
such as glass block, aluminum, and stainless steel, were incorporated into Streamline Moderne 
designs, and favored over more expensive materials that contributed to earlier Art Deco designs. 
With the onset of World War II and related limitations on building construction, the Streamline 
Moderne style’s period of popularity came to a close. By the mid-1940s, newly emergent trends in 
Modern architecture took hold.512 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1930s–1940s 
Associated Property Types: Commercial, Industrial, Institutional, Residential  
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Horizontal massing and emphasis, including ribbon windows or speed stripes that lead the eye 
along the exterior 
▪ 
Rounded corners and curved surfaces 
▪ 
Flat or nearly flat roof, sometimes with a flat parapet that is trimmed with plaster or metal 
coping 
▪ 
“Speed stripes” at wall surfaces or on canopies that appear at entrances or over services areas 
(for example at gas stations) 
▪ 
Horizontal moldings and continuous sill courses 
▪ 
Smooth stucco cladding 
 
512 Katie E. Horak et al., “Sub-theme: Streamline Moderne, 1935-1945,” from Survey LA: Los Angeles Historic Resources Survey Los Angeles 
Citywide Historic Context Statement, Los Angeles Department of City Planning, August 2021, 65–78, at 
https://planning.lacity.gov/odocument/4f67bd39-631a-4f26-9a52-cd5809a66655/LA_Modernism_1919-1980.pdf.

▪ 
Windows with metal sash, often casements, sometimes arranged in ribbons, but typically with 
horizontal orientation 
▪

▪ 
Unadorned wall surfaces and with minimal ornamentation

Architectural Styles

151 
11.16 Minimal Traditional 
Fueled by the economic constraints of the Great Depression and the subsequent housing shortage 
of the immediate post-war era, the Minimal Traditional architectural style was a deliberate design 
and construction trend intended to create simple, economic, single-family residences for the 
masses. In the Federal Housing Administration’s 1940 version of Planning Small Houses, the word 
“simple” appears frequently in descriptions of the exterior design of the Minimal Traditional house. 
Efficient floor plans and speedy construction enabled the mass production of these houses: first 
between 1940 and 1945 as houses for relocating wartime production-plant workers and then 
between 1946 and 1949 for returning servicemen promised affordable housing through the G.I. Bill. 
Instant suburban communities appeared across the country, sometimes only using subtle variations 
of building prototypes replicated over and over to house thousands. By about 1950, Ranch houses 
began to replace Minimal Traditional Style houses, as post-war prosperity facilitated the building 
and financing of larger homes.513  
 
 
Period of Significance: 1930s–1950s 
Associated Property Types: Residential 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Low or moderately pitched roof, often gabled or hipped 
▪ 
Small, usually single-story massing 
▪ 
Roof eaves with little to no overhang 
▪ 
Double-hung windows 
▪ 
Minimal decorative or architectural detail, often incorporating some traditional Colonial Revival 
or Classical elements (column, dentil, pedimented front door) 
▪ 
Principal subtypes include gable and wing roof, side gabled roof (also commonly called Cape 
Cod), hipped roof, and front gabled houses 
 
513 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 586–595. 
533 of 1110

--- page 534 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
152 
11.17 Ranch Style 
“Ranch House” is a broad term that refers to a property type and an architectural style most often 
applied to residential buildings. Examples of Ranch houses appeared in limited numbers prior to the 
1920s, while the 1930s through 1970s were the predominant years of the type’s construction. 
Ranch houses and Ranch styling were influenced by nineteenth-century vernacular residences in the 
American West and Southwest. Haciendas built in Southern California and wood-frame houses built 
by early American pioneers in Northern California, Texas, and elsewhere in the United States 
informed the style’s development. Haciendas featured adobe walls, low-pitched shed or gable roofs, 
and decorative wood windows grilles (rejas) or lattices (celosias) and featured asymmetrical plans 
that opened to an interior courtyard, which drew on Spanish precedents. Early American 
farmhouses were also typically asymmetrical in plan, with front porches, board-and-batten siding, 
and wood shake (long shingle) roofs. By the early 1900s, architects in California were designing 
houses drawing on these influences. William Wurster, an influential Bay Area Modernist architect, 
designed an early Ranch house in Scotts Valley, Santa Clara County known as the Gregory 
Farmhouse, which was featured in Sunset magazine. These early examples in the style preceded the 
influential work of architect Cliff May, who is strongly linked with the Ranch house’s popularization.  
The ranch houses designed between the 1920s to 1940s were often architect-designed, custom 
houses on relatively large sites. After World War II, Ranch houses gained popularity as publications 
such as Sunset magazine and its related Western Ranch Houses, as well as House Beautiful and 
House and Garden. As the style was further publicized, developers began to favor it for mass-
produced suburban communities. Into the 1970s, the style was adapted to commercial and 
institutional functions such as motels, gas stations, supermarkets, churches, civic buildings, and 
schools. Circa 1975, the Ranch style’s popularity declined due to a scarcity of expansive buildable 
land resulting from the previous decade’s suburbanization of many areas and a growing desire for 
more formal and traditional styles.514  
 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1930s–1950s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional  
Character-Defining Features: 
 
514 Katie E. Horak et al., “Theme: The Ranch House 1930-197,” from Survey LA: Los Angeles Historic Resources Survey Citywide Historic 
Context Statement, December 2015, 4-26, at https://planning.lacity.gov/odocument/1acefe03-5615-425f-9182-
d58a79014901/The_Ranch_House%2C_1930-1975.pdf; McAlester, A Field Guide to Modern American Houses. 597–611.  
534 of 1110

11.18 Mid-Century Modern  
The term Mid-Century Modern is a broad classification used to describe a variety of post-World War 
II architectural and design styles that adapted the International Style, which was among the pre-
World War II architectural styles important to the development of Modern architecture. The 
decades after World War II brought about the United States’ longest period of continuous growth 
and the rise of Mid-Century Modernism as the dominant mode of post-war architecture. The style 
embraced new technology and materials, the philosophy of indoor-outdoor living, and stylized clean 
simplicity. Found in a wide assortment of building types, the lack of ornament and inclusion of 
historical detailing was extremely versatile, and designers could meet the needs of almost any 
client. Mid-Century Modern architecture has been applied to residential building types, is often 
seen in California downtown districts that saw extensive post-World War II expansion or 
redevelopment, and was also adapted to institutional and industrial buildings, typically with more 
restrained forms. Beyond new construction during the post-World War II period, Mid-Century 
Modern architectural elements were also used in designing additions to earlier buildings, or for 
example, in modernizing features such as storefronts, entrances, and exterior cladding. These 
patterns were seen in many communities in California, as downtowns that emerged during the late 
nineteenth and early twentieth centuries adapted to broader planning and developmental patterns, 
such as embracing the automobile (and related parking needs) and incorporating signage that could 
be seen by passing car traffic. A very exuberant sub-style of Mid-Century Modern architecture is the 
Googie style, made famous by California roadside commercial buildings with attention-getting 
rooflines and signage.515 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1940s–1960s 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Exposed wood, concrete, and or/or steel structural components, especially posts and beams 
▪ 
Simple geometric volumes 
▪ 
Unornamented wall surfaces 
▪ 
Flat, cantilevered roofs  
 
515 Mary Brown, San Francisco Modern Architecture and Landscape Design 1935-1970 Historic Context Statement, San Francisco Planning 
Department, 2011, 121–125, at 
https://sfplanninggis.org/docs/Historical_Context_Statements/Modern%20Architecture%20Context%20adopted%20Jan%202011.pdf.

11.19 New Formalism 
New Formalism is an American architectural style that combined updated classical elements with 
modernist forms and technologies. First appearing in Edward Durrell Stone’s American Embassy in 
New Delhi in 1954, New Formalism broke from the minimalism of the modernist International style 
and worked classical forms, proportions, and motifs into the style. Focusing on monumental forms, 
the style takes liberties with the aesthetics and philosophies of the past, while adapting the 
technology and features of the present into massive forms often used in the context of civic and 
institutional buildings. New Formalism represents a radical and historically oriented departure from 
the rigid culture of the modernist movement through the embracing of classical precedents.516 
 
 
Period of Significance: 1954–1970s 
Associated Property Types: Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Large volumes 
▪ 
Rectangular massing 
▪ 
Strictly symmetrical facades that use full height colonnades 
▪ 
Formally landscaped plaza 
▪ 
Classically derived details such as arches, columns, and entablatures 
▪ 
Modern-inspired details such as honeycomb screens, shell forms, and folded plates 
▪ 
Often three or more stories in height  
▪ 
Integrated parking lot: subterranean or above grade 
▪ 
Flat roof, usually with a heavy and extending overhand  
▪ 
Set on a podium 
 
516 McAlester, A Field Guide to American Houses, 654–692; Katie E. Horak et al.“Sub-theme: New Formalism, 1960-1975” from Survey LA: 
Los Angeles Historic Resources Survey Citywide Historic Context Statement. August 2021, 190–201, at

11.20 Bay Tradition Regional Styles 
The Bay Tradition (also known as Bay Area Regional Tradition) developed in three phases between 
the late nineteenth century and mid-twentieth century. These styles are regional interpretations 
and began with the First Bay Tradition between the 1880s and 1920s. This style adapted the East 
Coast Shingle style to the Bay Area’s climate and brought an eclectic mix of forms to residential and 
institutional architecture that varied from the formality of the Classical Revival and Beaux Arts. The 
First Bay Tradition featured the natural materials (shingles and shakes) of the Shingle style and 
asymmetry. It was common to see houses with brick bases and shingles exteriors with limited 
classical or Gothic-influenced ornamentation.  
The Second Bay Tradition developed during the growth of the Modern Movement, from the 1930s 
to the 1950s. Buildings with clean, Modern forms were clad with wood siding (horizontal or 
vertical), or shingles, often flat roofs, and adapted to emphasize indoor-outdoor connection within 
the site, or to adapt to complex site conditions. The Third Bay Tradition emerged in the 1960s and if 
typically traced to The Sea Ranch development along the coast of northern Sonoma County. Also 
clad in wood materials, this third iteration of region style often feature forms that were similar to 
barns, with gable or pitched roofs. More so than the First and Second Bay Traditions, the Third Bay 
Tradition became favored for use in multifamily residential architecture, such as condominium 
developments and in some cases campus housing. 
 
 
Period of Significance: First Bay Tradition (1890s–1920s), Second Bay Tradition (1930s–1950s), Third 
Bay Tradition (1960s–1970s) 
Associated Property Types: Residential, Commercial, Institutional 
Character-Defining Features: 
▪ 
Exposed wood, concrete, and or/or steel structural components, especially posts and beams 
▪ 
Simple geometric volumes 
▪ 
Unornamented wall surfaces 
▪ 
Flat, cantilevered roofs  
▪ 
Wide overhanging eaves common but not required 
▪ 
Use of bright or contrasting colors  
▪ 
Spandrel glass in floor to ceiling windows 
▪ 
Open floor plans 
▪ 
Integration with the outdoors through overhanging trellises, pergolas, atriums, and planters

Guidance for Assessing Significance 
 
Historic Context Statement 
161 
13 
Guidance for Assessing Significance 
13.1 
Designation Criteria 
13.1.1 National Register of Historic Places 
The NRHP was authorized by Section 101 of the National Historic Preservation Act and is the 
nation’s official list of cultural resources worthy of preservation. The NRHP is the official Federal list 
of districts, sites, buildings, structures, and objects significant in American history, architecture, 
archeology, engineering, and culture. NRHP properties have significance to the history of their 
community state, or the nation. Properties which are listed in or have been formally determined 
eligible for listing in the NRHP are automatically listed in the CRHR. Per 36 Code of Federal 
Regulations, Part 60.4, a property is eligible for listing in the NRHP if it meets one or more of the 
following criteria: 
Criterion A: Is associated with events that have made a significant contribution to the broad 
patterns of our history 
Criterion B: Is associated with the lives of persons significant in our past 
Criterion C: Embodies the distinctive characteristics of a type, period, or method of installation, or 
that represent the work of a master, or that possess high artistic values, or that 
represent a significant and distinguishable entity whose components may lack 
individual distinction 
Criterion D: Has yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important in prehistory or history 
Integrity 
In addition to meeting at least one of the above designation criteria, resources must also retain 
integrity. The National Park Service recognizes seven aspects or qualities that, considered together, 
define historic integrity. To retain integrity, a property must possess several, if not all, of these 
seven qualities, defined as follows:  
Location: 
The place where the historic property was constructed or the place where the 
historic event occurred 
Design: 
The combination of elements that create the form, plan, space, structure, and style 
of a property 
Setting: 
The physical environment of a historic property 
Materials: 
The physical elements that were combined or deposited during a particular period 
of time and in a particular pattern or configuration to form a historic property 
Workmanship: The physical evidence of the crafts of a particular culture or people during any given 
period in history or prehistory 
Feeling: 
A property’s expression of the aesthetic or historic sense of a particular period of 
time 
Association: 
The direct link between an important historic event or person and a historic 
property 
543 of 1110

--- page 544 ---
City of San Mateo 
Historic Context Statement 
 
162 
Certain properties are generally considered ineligible for listing in the NRHP, including cemeteries, 
birthplaces, graves of historical figures, properties owned by religious institutions, relocated 
structures, or commemorative properties. Additionally, a property must be at least 50 years of age 
to be eligible for listing in the NRHP. The National Park Service states that 50 years is the general 
estimate of the time needed to develop the necessary historical perspective to evaluate 
significance. Properties which are less than 50 years must be determined to have “exceptional 
importance” to be considered eligible for NRHP listing. 
13.1.2 California Register of Historical Resources 
The CRHR was established in 1992 and codified by PRC Sections 5024.1 and Title 14 Section 4852. 
The CRHR is an authoritative listing and guide to be used by state and local agencies, private groups, 
and citizens in identifying the existing historical resources of the state and to indicate which 
resources deserve to be protected, to the extent prudent and feasible, from substantial adverse 
change (Public Resources Code, 5024.1[a]). The criteria for eligibility for the CRHR are consistent 
with the NRHP criteria but have been modified for state use in order to include a range of historical 
resources that better reflect the history of California (Public Resources Code, 5024.1[b]). Unlike the 
NRHP however, the CRHR does not have a defined age threshold for eligibility; rather, a resource 
may be eligible for the CRHR if it can be demonstrated sufficient time has passed to understand its 
historical or architectural significance. Furthermore, resources may still be eligible for listing in the 
CRHR even if they do not retain sufficient integrity for NRHP eligibility. Generally, the OHP 
recommends resources over 45 years of age be recorded and evaluated for historical resources 
eligibility.  
A property is eligible for listing in the CRHR if it meets one of more of the following criteria: 
Criterion 1: Is associated with events that have made a significant contribution to the broad 
patterns of California’s history and cultural heritage 
Criterion 2: Is associated with the lives of persons important to our past 
Criterion 3: Embodies the distinctive characteristics of a type, period, region, or method of 
construction, or represents the work of an important creative individual, or possesses 
high artistic values 
Criterion 4: Has yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important in prehistory or history

Rincon Consultants, Inc.
66 Franklin Street, Suite 300
Oakland, California 94607
510-834-4455 
Engagement Summary
Version 2 Updates are in Purple Text
Overview 
The City of San Mateo is in the process of developing a Citywide Historic Context Statement and 
updating its Historic Preservation Ordinance. To inform this work, the City developed an Outreach and 
Engagement Strategy focused on multiple entry points for community participation and offering a 
range of opportunities for residents, organizations, and interested parties to share input and 
perspectives that inform the update process. Through the engagement conducted thus far, community 
members have shared their views of the future of historic preservation in San Mateo and identified 
places and themes that hold historical and cultural interest.
The project started in February 2025, with outreach initiated in the summer, and will extend through 
Spring 2026. To date, the project team has conducted a City Council Study Session, two three focus 
groups, one community workshop, online outreach, and three mobile workshops. These activities 
correspond to the first two three engagement phases:
x
Project Launch and Early Focus Groups, and
x
Community Storytelling and Draft Development, and
x
Release of Draft Documents for Review
The project is now entering the Release of Draft Documents for Review Feedback on Drafts phase, 
during which additional community feedback will continue to refine and strengthen the draft materials.
In addition to defined engagement events, the City has received numerous emails and comments from 
community members, demonstrating San Mateo’s strong interest and investment in shaping the future 
of historic preservation.
Summary of Participation and Activities 
The City implemented a multi-faceted strategy that combined digital communications, printed 
materials, and in-person events to raise awareness and invite participation in the project. The 
approach emphasized accessibility and visibility by creating multiple entry points for residents, 
stakeholders, and organizations to learn about the project and share their input.
Participant Identification and Outreach Methods
Participants were reached through a blend of targeted outreach and citywide communications:
557 of 1110

--- page 558 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
2 
x
Targeted invitations for workshops and website participation were sent to members of 
neighborhood associations, local preservation advocates, cultural organizations, and prior 
participants in planning and preservation efforts.
x
Broader community participation was encouraged through general City communications including 
e-blasts, newsletters, and social media posts. 
x
Mobile workshop pop-up locations were strategically selected to engage residents from different 
parts of the city and to reach people who might not typically participate in formal meetings.
Community Engagement Activities
The City hosted several opportunities for direct community participation:
x
City Council Study Session
x
Two Three virtual focus groups with targeted stakeholder audiences
x
One virtual community workshop open to all residents
x
Three in-person mobile workshops held at community events and public spaces  
x
A Digital Time Capsule, an online interactive platform to collect stories and historic information 
from the community
These activities built momentum across engagement phases, beginning with focused discussions 
among key stakeholders, followed by broader community engagement through workshop events. 
Outreach and Communications
To sustain visibility throughout the engagement period, the project team coordinated a range of digital 
and physical outreach tools, including:

Three project e-blasts distributed through City email lists

Three e-blasts announcing the release of draft documents for review and promoting the draft

Senior Center.

Distribution of flyers and postcards at the Central Recreation Center
