Comparison to Outreach & Engagement Strategy Goals
The Outreach and Engagement Strategy established participation goals of:
x
Reaching at least 20 attendees per workshop
x
Collecting 50 Digital Time Capsule contributions
x
Engaging 8-10 participants per focus group
x
Expanding email sign up list from a baseline of 36 subscribers by 10%
To date, the project has exceeded goals for community workshops, reaching approximately 137
residents through the first workshop and subsequent mobile workshop events. Pivoting from a second 
traditional workshop to a series of mobile workshops proved effective in diversifying the voices heard 
in this round of engagement. 
The project’s email list has grown from 36 to 90 178 subscribers, far surpassing the growth target and 
demonstrating strong interest in staying informed as the project advances. To date, the Digital Time 
Capsule has received 11 community posts and 13 reactions, marking early but meaningful online 
participation. The first two focus groups were intentionally smaller in scale, providing opportunities for 
key stakeholder groups, including representatives from Less Red Tape and the San Mateo Heritage 
Alliance, to share their perspectives directly. The third focus group reached its goal of engaging at least 
8 members involved in the business and development community. 
Activities Overview 
The engagement program included a mix of focus groups, a community workshop, mobile workshops, 
and digital engagement. Each activity was designed to gather input on key themes while ensuring that 
participation opportunities were accessible, inclusive, and interactive.
Focus Groups 
The City hosted two virtual focus groups in July 2025 with residents representing different viewpoints 
and experiences related to historic preservation in San Mateo. The City hosted a third virtual focus 
group in December 2025 with members of the business and development community. Each session 
lasted approximately one hour and was facilitated by the consultant team with support from City staff. 
Discussions were recorded for note-taking purposes only and analyzed thematically to identify shared 
concerns, priorities, and ideas. The full analysis for each focus group is attached to this report as 
Appendix A, with summaries provided below.
Focus Group #1: Residents Cautious of Preservation Efforts
Participants, primarily from or interested in the Baywood neighborhood, discussed concerns about the 
fairness and transparency of historic designation processes and their impact on property rights. They 
emphasized the need for owner consent, clear and objective design standards, and predictable 
procedures for evaluating historic resources.
While participants expressed frustration with perceived inconsistencies and costs of compliance with 
historic preservation regulations, they also supported developing a Historic Context Statement so long 
as it could not be used to justify new designations without consent. The discussion highlighted the 
importance of education and communication to promote public confidence in the process and ultimate 
project deliverables. 
Key Takeaways: 
560 of 1110

--- page 561 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
5 
x
Desire for transparency, owner consent, and local control
x
Concern about financial and procedural burdens
x
Conditional support for a context statement focused on education, not regulation
Focus Group #2: Residents Supportive of Preservation
This group emphasized the importance of preservation in maintaining San Mateo’s character and 
identity. Participants called for stronger implementation tools, professional oversight, and clearer 
design compatibility standards to ensure consistency between planning and building review.
They also encouraged a more inclusive approach to preservation that recognizes cultural and social 
histories, as well as opportunities to align preservation with housing, sustainability, and equity goals 
through adaptive reuse and education.
Key Takeaways: 
x
Preservation seen as essential to community identity
x
Need for stronger tools, standards, and professional oversight
x
Support for inclusive, educational, and adaptive approaches
Focus Group #3: Business/Development Community
Participants stressed the need for clear, practical guidance and flexibility in the ordinance to avoid 
stifling development. They expressed concerns about economic and business viability due to 
uncertainty and rigid rules that can make projects financially unfeasible.
They also called for updates to plans and surveys to reflect current architectural trends and economic 
conditions while still honoring historic character. Additionally, participants noted that overlapping 
planning documents create friction and lack clarity, and they expressed a strong desire for an 
integrated, unified regulatory framework.
Key Takeaways: 
x
Need for flexibility and clear, practical guidance in the ordinance
x
Preservation of cultural identity while encouraging creative modernization
x
Establish a clear hierarchy among the Historic Preservation Ordinance, Downtown Specific Plan, 
and other zoning codes
Overall Observations
Together, the two three focus groups reflect a spectrum of perspectives, from those seeking flexibility 
and property rights protection to those prioritizing stronger safeguards for historic resources and 
inclusiveness to ensure the context statement reflects San Mateo’s diverse communities. Both All 
groups underscored the need for clarity, transparency, and education, reinforcing the City’s goal of 
developing balanced policies that protect community character while maintaining fairness and trust.
Community Workshop
The first community workshop, Preservation and Regulation 101, introduced residents to the City’s 
Historic Policy Update process and built a shared foundation for future discussions. The virtual event 
aimed to educate participants on key preservation concepts, including historic context statements, 
561 of 1110

--- page 562 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
6
ordinance structure, and the relationship between preservation and development, while collecting 
early feedback on community priorities. Spanish interpretation was available during the meeting.
The session combined a presentation on preservation laws and local regulations with three interactive 
activities designed to encourage reflection and discussion. The activities are summarized below, with 
compiled contributions available in Appendix B.
Warm Up: I Can’t Imagine San Mateo Without…
Participants began by sharing short reflections on what defines San Mateo’s character and what 
aspects of the city they most value. Common responses included references to beautiful 
neighborhoods, Central Park, trees, and the diverse community and historical figures of San Mateo.
Participants highlighted beloved places such as the Japanese Tea Garden, the Benjamin Franklin 
Hotel, and the Downtown Historic District. 
Digital Time Capsule: People, Places, and Events
The next activity invited participants to identify people, places, and events they believe future 
generations should remember about San Mateo. Submissions highlighted well-known local institutions 
such as San Mateo’s schools, Talbot’s Toys, and Draeger’s Market, as well as community figures like 
Claire Mack, Tom Brady, and members of the city’s Japanese community. Participants also highlighted 
beloved everyday places that hold personal or collective meaning, including the Central Library, 
Downtown San Mateo Post Office, B Street, and Bay Meadows. The exercise linked historical 
storytelling with the technical goals of the context statement, which include highlighting patterns of 
development, centralizing historical information, providing a consistent framework for evaluating a 
property, and offering a more robust picture of San Mateo’s history -- capturing the city’s lived 
experience alongside its architectural heritage.
Preservation Priorities Exercise
In small breakout groups, participants discussed four key themes shaping preservation policy: 
flexibility for homeowners, protection of historic landmarks, cultural equity and representation, and 
transparency and predictability. Using a “Preservation Dollars” framework, groups were asked to 
allocate limited resources across these priorities, sparking discussion about trade-offs and shared 
values. Most groups emphasized transparency and predictability as their top priority, followed closely 
by protection of historic landmarks and cultural representation. The activity revealed broad community 
interest in clearer processes and more inclusive recognition of San Mateo’s heritage.
Participation and Outcomes
Approximately 42 residents attended the workshop, representing a range of neighborhoods and 
interests. An exit survey was provided via the virtual platform; however, only seven participants 
provided responses, limiting the usefulness of the results for a broader analysis of representation. The 
virtual format also presented challenges: some attendees experienced technical difficulties, and 
several expressed a preference for a more traditional Q&A format rather than the interactive activities 
used. We incorporated this feedback into our planning for the second workshop, which led us to shift 
to a series of in-person mobile workshops designed to provide more direct interaction and 
opportunities for real-time questions and discussion. 
Key takeaways included:
x
Broad recognition of San Mateo’s cultural and architectural diversity
x
Desire for transparent, consistent, and educational preservation processes
562 of 1110

--- page 563 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
7
x
Support for inclusive storytelling and representation in the Historic Context Statement
x
Interest in balanced policies that protect heritage while allowing reasonable flexibility for property 
owners
Full results from group activities and whiteboard outputs are included in Appendix B.
Mobile Workshops
Following the first community workshop, the City shifted from a traditional meeting format to a series 
of mobile workshops, sometimes referred to as “pop-ups,” designed to reach residents in familiar, 
everyday settings. This pivot reflected lessons from early engagement. While the virtual workshop 
successfully built understanding among those already interested and was available to residents who 
may not feel safe or comfortable attending a gathering in person, broader participation required 
meeting people where they are.
The mobile format provided more accessible, casual opportunities for conversation and participation, 
particularly for residents who may not attend formal meetings or have limited time to engage online. 
As shown in Table 1, offering touchpoints at varying times of day helped reach residents with differing 
schedules. Rather than convening another single event, the City opted for multiple small-scale, in-
person touchpoints that collectively expanded reach and visibility across the community.
Activities and Content
Each pop-up featured a set of multilingual (English, Chinese, and Spanish), visually oriented displays 
introducing the two components of the Historic Policy Update, the Historic Context Statement and the 
Historic Preservation Ordinance, along with printed postcards with QR codes linking to the project 
website and Digital Time Capsule. Staff and consultants were present to answer questions, collect 
input, and provide educational context about the project.
The core activity invited participants to respond to the same people, places, and events prompts 
introduced during the virtual workshop. Using sticky notes, attendees shared the names of individuals, 
landmarks, and local stories that they felt best represented San Mateo’s heritage. This approach 
allowed the team to continue building the community narrative started online and identify recurring 
themes across engagement formats.
563 of 1110

--- page 564 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
8
Outcomes and Observations
The mobile workshops generated meaningful one-on-one conversations about the City’s history, 
identity, and preservation goals. Many participants used the opportunity to learn about the broader 
Historic Policy Update effort and how it connects to housing, land use, and community character 
policies under other City efforts.
The mobile workshop format proved effective in:
x
Broadening participation to include residents encountered in public spaces rather than through 
formal events;
x
Reinforcing education on the City’s preservation framework; and
x
Gathering qualitative feedback that complemented earlier workshop and digital engagement 
results.
These events demonstrated the value of informal, interactive outreach in maintaining visibility for the 
project and building trust through direct dialogue. The mobile workshop materials and results are 
available in Appendix C. 
Online Engagement
Digital engagement opportunities ensure that residents can participate in the Historic Policy Update 
at their own pace and through familiar online platforms. The City’s Project Website and Digital Time 
Capsule served as the primary interactive tools, inviting community members to sign up for updates 
and share stories, memories, and reflections about the people, places, and events that define San 
Mateo’s identity. 
As of November 2025, the Digital Time Capsule has received 11 posts and 13 reactions, representing 
a small but insightful sample of community input. The content reflects a wide range of input, from 
neighborhood landmarks and local businesses to historic photos of families and homes, that will 
inform the Historic Context Statement.
564 of 1110

--- page 565 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
9
Beyond the Time Capsule, the City maintained a consistent online presence to keep residents 
informed throughout the process. Project updates were shared through the City’s website, email and 
print newsletters, and social media channels, helping to build awareness and invite continued 
participation in upcoming project phases. Together, these tools provided accessible entry points for 
engagement and helped sustain visibility and transparency between live events.
Upcoming Engagement
The next community workshop is scheduled for January 21st, 2026, and will be a two-hour draft review 
session. Participants will review the Draft Historic Context Statement and Draft Historic Preservation 
Ordinance with a historic preservation expert, with ample opportunities to ask questions throughout
the session.
A second City Council Study Session is scheduled for January 26th, 2026, and will focus on reviewing 
the draft documents.
In February, two focus groups are planned: one with members of the Youth Activities Commission and 
another with residents from underrepresented communities. The City will continue to distribute e-blast 
updates to the project email list, providing information on the project, key dates, and opportunities for 
public feedback.
Key Takeaways
Community input from the focus groups, workshop, mobile pop-ups, and digital engagement activities 
revealed a consistent set of priorities centered on trust, transparency, and inclusivity in the City’s 
historic preservation efforts. Participants expressed a strong desire for clear, fair, and predictable 
processes that balance property owner rights with the shared value of protecting San Mateo’s 
neighborhood character.
565 of 1110

--- page 566 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
10 
Across all activities, residents emphasized the importance of broadening preservation narratives to 
include the city’s cultural, social, and architectural diversity. Many encouraged the City to recognize 
not only landmark properties, but also everyday places that hold community meaning, such as 
gathering spaces like Central Park, local businesses, and institutions that reflect San Mateo’s evolving 
identity. Participants also identified the need to align preservation with housing goals. A recurring 
theme across all engagement formats was the need for education and clear communication to 
improve public understanding of how historic preservation works and have confidence that the process 
is fair and objective. 
These insights have directly informed the Historic Context Statement and Historic Preservation 
Ordinance update. Detailed summaries and raw data from all engagement activities are included in 
the appendices.

Focus Group 1 – Thematic Analysis
Community Group: Less Red Tape
Date: July 9, 2025
Location: Virtual
Facilitators: Kassandra Gale and JulieAnn Murphy
Overview 
This document presents a thematic analysis of the first focus group conducted as part of the San 
Mateo Historic Policy Update effort. The session included residents affiliated with the Less Red Tape 
group, primarily from the Baywood neighborhood. The discussion centered on concerns related to 
historic preservation, property rights, and the implications of state and local designation processes.
Emergent Themes 
Distrust in the Historic Designation Process
Participants repeatedly expressed skepticism about how historic designations are determined.
x
Quotes:
o
“We think the whole thing is being weaponized against us in Baywood.”
o
“They will find a way of making Baywood historic when it doesn’t have the attributes.”
o
“The drive-by assessment by Page and Turnbull is grossly inaccurate.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Inconsistent application: Reference to Glazenwood being treated as historic without formal 
designation.
o
Lack of site-specific knowledge: Concerns about “drive-by” assessments and lack of permit 
review.
o
State-level rubber-stamping: Perception that the State approves nearly all nominations without 
scrutiny.
o
Consultant bias: Belief that consultants are hired to justify preordained outcomes.
This theme reflects a deep concern about fairness, transparency, and accuracy in the designation 
process.
Demand for Owner Consent and Local Control
A strong, consistent demand emerged for affirmative, written consent before any property is 
designated historic.
x
Quotes:
o
“No home would be included… without individual consent.”
o
“No third-party nominations.”
o
“Why not 100% approval like an HOA?”
x
Sub-themes:
568 of 1110

--- page 569 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
2
o
Affirmative consent vs. passive inclusion: Strong opposition to opt-out or assumed consent 
models.
o
Third-party nominations: Rejection of any process that allows non-owners to initiate 
designations.
o
Consent thresholds: Suggestions for 80% or 100% thresholds before a district nomination can 
proceed.
This theme emphasizes personal agency and property rights as central to participants’ values.
Fear of Financial and Practical Burdens
Participants described the cost, complexity, and impracticality of maintaining or modifying historic 
homes.
x
Quotes:
o
“It costs a lot of money… extremely difficult, extremely time-consuming.”
o
“Trying to pick it up and put a new foundation under it would be worse than tearing it down.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Cost of compliance: Preservation described as 4-5 times more expensive than rebuilding. One 
participant said, “I will never do that again.”
o
Structural feasibility: Examples of homes with foundational issues or extensive modifications 
that are impractical to preserve.
o
Time and complexity: Preservation seen as time-consuming and technically difficult.
o
Impact on resale and inheritance: Concerns about burdening future owners or heirs.
This theme reveals a pragmatic concern about the real-world implications of preservation.
Desire for Clear, Protective Ordinances
Participants want specific, enforceable local rules that protect homeowners from unintended 
consequences.
x
Quotes:
o
“We want ordinances that give us the maximum flexibility.”
o
“What kind of protections can the city give us so we’re not lost?”
o
“Objective design standards… that trumps CEQA.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Objective design standards: Proposed as a way to balance flexibility with neighborhood 
character.
o
Appeals process reform: Frustration with perceived “unlimited appeals” and lack of finality.
o
Flexibility within designation: Questions about whether homeowners can rebuild in different 
styles.
o
Examples from other cities: Huntington Beach cited as a model for more permissive policies.
This theme reflects a proactive interest in shaping policy to reflect community needs.
569 of 1110

--- page 570 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
3
Confusion and Need for Clarity
There was frequent mention of uncertainty and lack of understanding about how designations work 
and what they mean.
x
Quotes:
o
“We don’t understand the process.”
o
“All the uncertainty of it is very troubling.”
o
“We want to know what our right is to challenge a contributor status.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Terminology confusion: Terms like “contributor” and “non-contributor” were not well 
understood, and participants wanted to know how these labels affect their rights.
o
Process opacity: Participants didn’t know how decisions were made or who had authority. “We 
don’t understand the process” was a recurring sentiment.
o
Desire for education: Several participants asked for clearer criteria and examples of what 
qualifies as historic.
This theme highlights a gap in communication and education between officials and residents.
Conditional Support for Historic Context Statement
Participants showed interest in local history, but only if the context statement is not used to justify 
unwanted designations.
x
Quotes:
o
“There is value there… pulling the history together will be very interesting.”
o
“As long as that’s not a starting point for somebody to make a nomination.”
o
“We’re not anti-history.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Interest in local history: Participants were genuinely curious about San Mateo’s past—
mentioning schools, estates, parks, and notable residents.
o
Fear of misuse: The context statement was seen as a potential “launchpad” for unwanted 
designations. “As long as that’s not a starting point for somebody to make a nomination…”
o
Desire for caveats: Participants wanted explicit language stating that the context statement 
cannot be used to justify designations without consent.
o
Examples of valuable history: Mention of schools, estates, parks, and local celebrities.
This theme reflects a nuanced position: appreciation for history, but concern about how it’s applied.
Social Division and Emotional Strain
Participants described emotional distress and neighborhood conflict resulting from the historic 
designation debate.
x
Quotes:
o
“This has been tearing our neighborhood apart.”
o
“People wouldn’t even look each other in the eye.”
o
“You’ve got frightened people on this call.”
570 of 1110

--- page 571 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
4
x
Sub-themes:
o
Neighborhood conflict: Participants described how the historic designation debate had “torn 
the neighborhood apart.”
o
Loss of trust: Some felt betrayed by neighbors or advocacy groups. “People wouldn’t even look 
each other in the eye.”
o
Urgency and fear: The tone was often anxious, even combative. “We’re already at war here,” 
one participant said.
This theme underscores the human and relational cost of policy decisions.
Contextualization 
Ordinance Reform as a Tool for Protection
x
Participants view the ordinance update as their primary line of defense against unwanted 
designations and restrictions.
x
The update must address classification clarity, appeals processes, and design flexibility, especially 
for neighborhoods like Baywood that are already under scrutiny.
Context Statement as a Double-Edged Sword
x
While the historic context statement is intended as a planning and educational tool, participants 
fear it could be used to justify future designations.
x
The city must clearly define the purpose and limits of the context statement to avoid further 
erosion of trust.
Community Engagement and Trust Building
x
The focus group itself is part of the city’s community engagement strategy, but the emotional 
tone of the discussion reveals deep mistrust.
x
The update process must go beyond technical revisions and include relationship 
repair, transparent communication, and ongoing dialogue.
Economic and Practical Realities
x
The ordinance must account for the financial and logistical burdens of preservation, especially 
in older neighborhoods like Baywood.
x
Policies that ignore these realities risk being seen as punitive or elitist.
Need for Education and Clarity
x
Participants’ confusion about terms like “contributor” and “non-contributor” underscores the need 
for plain-language explanations in both the ordinance and public materials.
x
The update effort should include educational components to demystify the process and criteria.
Social Cohesion and Neighborhood Health
x
From the participants perspective, the Baywood case illustrates how historic preservation efforts 
can fracture communities when not handled with care.
571 of 1110

--- page 572 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
5
x
The policy update must be sensitive to social dynamics, offering voluntary pathways and conflict 
resolution mechanisms.
Historic Examples 
Buildings and Structures
x
St. Matthew’s Catholic Church – located on the border of Baywood; noted for its architectural and 
historical significance.
x
Former Mayor’s House on Delaware – possibly already historic; mentioned as a point of interest.
x
Gatehouse of the Parrott Estate – near Crystal Springs Lake; described as a quaint, surviving 
structure from the estate
Parks and Natural Features
x
Arroyo Park – near Baywood, along Crystal Springs Creek; noted for its charm and a historical 
anecdote about a famous person staying there.
x
Crystal Springs Creek – Runs through Arroyo Park and the Parrott Estate area
x
Crystal Springs Lake – Near the Parrott Estate gatehouse
Schools
x
San Mateo High School – Attended by notable figures like Merv Griffin and Lynn Swann.
x
Serra High School (Junípero Serra High School) – Originally located at the site of St. Bartholomew’s; 
later moved to 20th Avenue.
x
Aragon High School – Mentioned in the context of local school history.
Estates and Historical Areas
x
Parrott Estate – A large historical estate that extended into Hillsborough; includes the gatehouse 
and was once owned by Elon Musk.
x
Tobin Clark Estate – Mentioned in relation to the location of St. Matthew’s Catholic Church.
Commercial and Cultural References
x
Downtown San Mateo – Referenced for its historical shops and commercial evolution.
x
Hillsdale / Bohannon Family – Noted for their role in San Mateo’s development, especially in the 
Hillsdale area.
Notable People and Residences
x
Tom Brady’s Childhood Home – Suggested as potentially historic.
x
Merv Griffin’s Childhood Home – Also suggested as potentially historic.
x
Lynn Swann – Mentioned as a notable alumnus of San Mateo High School.
x
Barry Bonds – Attended Serra High School.

Focus Group 2 – Thematic Analysis
Community Group: San Mateo Heritage Alliance
Date: July 16, 2025
Location: Virtual
Facilitators: Kassandra Gale and JulieAnn Murphy
Overview 
This document presents a thematic analysis of the second focus group conducted as part of the San 
Mateo Historic Policy Update effort. The session included residents affiliated with the San Mateo 
Heritage Alliance group. The discussion centered on community experiences with historic preservation, 
challenges in current policies and processes, and ideas for making preservation more inclusive, 
effective, and aligned with broader city goals.
Emergent Themes 
Sense of Place and Community Identity
Participants emphasized the emotional and aesthetic value of San Mateo’s historic neighborhoods, 
expressing concern that new development is eroding the city’s unique character.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“San Mateo was known as the City of Beautiful Homes and City of Trees”
o
“A lot of people are just really tired of the same retail chains, no matter where they go. San 
Mateo really is unique. It has a unique asset, and we should be doing everything we can to 
protect it and value it for the value it has for the community.”
o
“I think that’s why there is such high value in a lot of these neighborhoods—because they are 
unique, and they’ve been well taken care of for decades.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Nostalgia for past eras: Participants referenced specific decades as benchmarks for 
neighborhood character.
o
Visual coherence: Concerns about mismatched signage and architectural scale disrupting the 
city’s aesthetic.
o
Loss of uniqueness: Fear that new development is making neighborhoods feel generic.
Frustrations with the Preservation Process
Participants described procedural gaps and inconsistencies in how preservation is implemented, 
particularly between planning and permitting stages.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“There’s always the gap between when planning approves something and building permits it. 
And the building department is often left out of the learning.”
o
“Having the ordinance require building staff to understand more.”
x
Sub-themes:
573 of 1110

--- page 574 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
2
o
Process breakdowns: Disconnect between planning and building departments.
o
Loss of institutional knowledge: Perception that staff support has declined over time.
o
Ambiguity in rules: Ordinance seen as unclear or inconsistently applied.
Call for Stronger Tools and Oversight
Participants advocated for clearer standards, enforcement mechanisms, and professional oversight 
to ensure preservation goals are met.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Need a mechanism that’s not a guideline, that’s a standard, to require compatible building.”
o
“The oversight body should have educated people, architectural historians, and regulatory 
specialists that understand the city’s current regulations and the Secretary of the Interior’s 
Standards for rehabilitation.”
o
“I think we need a different tool.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Accountability mechanisms: Desire for both incentives and penalties.
o
Professionalization: Oversight should be led by experts, not generalists.
o
Clarity and consistency: Need for enforceable standards for compatibility.
Concerns About Development Pressures
Participants expressed anxiety about the pace and nature of development, especially when it 
threatens historic structures or neighborhood cohesion.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“It looks like a lot of these neighborhoods, frankly, are becoming profit centers.”
o
“Prometheus owns individually eligible buildings which is a threat to the downtown.”
o
“These developers come and go. They take what they can, and they’re off to the next 
communities.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Loss of historic assets: Specific sites cited as at risk.
o
Unbalanced growth: Concern that development is outpacing preservation.
o
Support for adaptive reuse: Seen as a compromise between growth and conservation.
Education and Public Awareness
Participants highlighted the need for broader public understanding of preservation and more visible 
recognition of historic and cultural assets.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“These are values that really matter. The education has to be there.”
o
“We need consistency of signage in the downtown historic district.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Community stewardship: Education as a tool for cultivating care and responsibility.
o
Cultural visibility: Signage and storytelling to honor diverse histories.
o
Transparency and trust: More open processes to build public confidence.
574 of 1110

--- page 575 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
3
Inclusive and Cultural Preservation
Participants emphasized the importance of expanding preservation efforts to include cultural and 
social histories, particularly those of underrepresented communities.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Our city is 130 years old, we have historic resources across the city, in all neighborhoods.”
o
“We would like to see more plaques and signs to reflect some of the ethnic communities here 
in San Mateo.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Beyond buildings: Emphasis on intangible heritage and lived experiences.
o
Equity in recognition: Inclusion of marginalized communities in preservation narratives.
o
Redefining significance: Broader criteria for what is worth preserving, reflecting diverse 
cultural values.
Alignment with Broader City Goals
Participants saw opportunities to integrate preservation with housing, sustainability, and equity goals, 
rather than treating them as competing priorities.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Adaptive reuse – protecting historic downtowns, encourage and maintaining front facades?”
o
“From an environmental standpoint, you’re tearing down—and a lot of times, most of the time—
they’re throwing away amazing timber and tiles.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Adaptive reuse as a bridge: Supports both housing and environmental goals.
o
Preservation as sustainability: Reuse of materials and embodied energy.
o
Policy alignment: Desire for integrated planning across city departments.
Contextualization 
Acknowledge the Emotional and Cultural Significance of Historic Resources
x
Refine to emphasize the community-wide emotional impact, not just individual attachment, 
reinforcing the need for preservation as a shared value.
x
Highlight the importance of communicating both the risks of loss and the benefits of preservation 
in clear, relatable terms.
Rebuild and Sustain Public Trust with Transparent, Empowering Policies
x
Expand to include ongoing engagement mechanisms beyond ordinance adoption, such as advisory 
committees, or regular public reporting.
x
Stress the need for clarity in decision-making processes to avoid perceptions of arbitrary or biased 
enforcement.
Establish Clear, Enforceable Design Compatibility Standards
x
Stress integrating preservation as a key strategy to maintain community character amid housing 
expansion pressures.
575 of 1110

--- page 576 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
4
x
Encourage flexible, innovative approaches such as adaptive reuse and context-sensitive infill to 
achieve dual goals. 
Center Community Interests to Mitigate Developer Influence
x
Specify mechanisms like community benefit agreements or enhanced public review to ensure 
developers are accountable.
x
Recommend policies that support local investment and partnerships to build long-term 
neighborhood stewardship.
Strengthen Legal Frameworks with Clear Protections and Enforcement
x
Emphasize closing loopholes with explicit, codified standards rather than relying on discretionary 
guidelines.
x
Include provisions for monitoring, reporting, and penalties to ensure consistent enforcement.
Preserve and Enhance the Social and Economic Fabric Connected to Historic Places
x
Highlight the role of preservation in supporting economic vitality through small businesses and 
tourism.
x
Suggest incorporating strategies to improve public spaces and pedestrian infrastructure that 
complement historic character.
Places Referenced 
Buildings and Structures
Individually eligible buildings owned by Prometheus
Referenced as part of a concern about development pressures and the need for better tools to protect 
historic assets.
Important Buildings for Ethnic Communities
x
Chingley Laundry at South Claremont and 1st Avenue
x
Noah’s Diner
Good examples of façade maintenance or rebuilds that maintain the original architectural style
x
Saint Matthew’s Hotel
x
Belmont Building on 4th Avenue
x
West part of the 100 block of 3rd Avenue
x
Casa Baywood
x
5456 North Ellsworth

Parks and Natural Features
Heritage trees
Mentioned in the context of preservation guidelines and the desire to protect natural features that 
contribute to neighborhood character
Historic Areas
Historic downtowns
Discussed in relation to adaptive reuse and maintaining front facades.
B Street
Cited as an example of a historically significant area being affected by development, with concerns

Commercial and Cultural Areas
Retail sameness and signage in downtown
Examples of how commercial development is undermining historic character and visual coherence.
Plaques/signage for ethnic communities
Suggested as a way to honor cultural history and increase visibility of underrepresented narratives.

Focus Group 3 – Thematic Analysis
Community Group: Business/Development Community
Date: December 11, 2025
Location: Virtual
Facilitators: Kassandra Gale and JulieAnn Murphy
Overview 
This document presents a thematic analysis of the third focus group conducted as part of the San 
Mateo Historic Policy Update effort. The session included business owners and members of the 
development community. The discussion centered on community experiences with historic 
preservation, challenges in current policies and processes, and ideas for improving preservation to be 
more practical, inclusive, and aligned with city goals.
Emergent Themes 
Need for Clear, Practical Guidance
Participants repeatedly emphasized the importance of clarity in the Historic Preservation Ordinance 
and related processes, noting that ambiguity and conflicting codes discourage investment and slow 
projects.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Provide a roadmap, and then everyone knows what can and or can’t be done”
o
“Needs to be really clear…give clear guidelines to the staff.”
o
“Create supplemental application materials that make it clear on, like a checklist on what’s
required.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Lack of transparency: Developers struggle with unclear timelines and requirements.
o
Desire for practical tools: Checklists, flowcharts, and FAQs were suggested.
o
Frustration with overlapping plans: Participants want integration of zoning, design standards,
and historic ordinances.
Flexibility vs. Rigidity in Historic Preservation
Participants expressed concern that overly rigid rules could stifle creativity and economic vitality, 
advocating for adaptive reuse and modern design elements that respect historic character.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“You have to be really careful when you write the ordinance not to stifle cool development.”
o
“There has to be some kind of flexibility there.”
o
“Allow modern additions like rooftop lounges or glass elevators if they complement historic
intent.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Adaptive reuse: Support for creative modernization within historic contexts.
o
Avoiding “freeze-dried” preservation: Participants want interpretation, not replication.
578 of 1110

--- page 579 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
2 
o
Economic pragmatism: Flexibility seen as essential for attracting tenants and revitalizing
downtown.
Conflicts Between Codes and Standards
Participants highlighted systemic issues where ADA compliance and Historic Building Code 
requirements clash, creating barriers to renovation and occupancy.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“ADA code and Historic Code have no ability to harmonize”
o
“The language is vague, and it leaves a lot of authority with the local building official.”
o
“Every tenant walked away, and it is still vacant to this day.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Regulatory tension: Lack of harmonization between accessibility and preservation standards.
o
Local discretion: Outcomes depend heavily on building officials’ interpretation.
o
Business impact: Code conflicts can lead to abandoned projects and vacant properties.
Economic and Business Viability Concerns
Participants stressed that uncertainty and rigid rules increase costs and deter investment, making 
projects financially unfeasible.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Prevents offices or businesses from coming to the area.”
o
“Wherever clarity doesn’t exist… we have to hold a more significant contingency.”
o
“Ordinance should not kill business development.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Risk management: Developers factor in contingencies when rules are unclear.
o
Ordinance as an economic tool: Preservation policies must consider financial realities.
o
Tenant attraction: Flexibility and clarity are key to revitalizing commercial spaces.
Outdated Plans and Ordinances
Participants noted that existing plans and surveys are decades old and fail to reflect current 
architectural trends or economic conditions.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Maybe it’s time for another survey?”
o
“The old downtown plan… doesn’t necessarily age with time.”
o
“Signage ordinance feels outdated—why can’t we have neon signs?”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Aging standards: 1989 survey and design guidelines no longer align with today’s needs.
o
Cultural evolution: Desire for modern aesthetics alongside historic character.
o
Regulatory refresh: Calls for updating signage and façade rules to encourage vibrancy.
Community Information and Clarity
Previous residential historic resources designation efforts have sparked tension and misinformation, 
creating social friction and uncertainty for the current project. 
579 of 1110

--- page 580 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
3 
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Some neighbors don’t talk to other neighbors because of this.”
o
“I can’t separate fact from fiction.”
o
“People think they won’t be able to remodel their homes.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Polarization: Strong opinions for and against historic districts.
o
Knowledge gaps: Misunderstandings about what designation entails.
o
Need for education: Clear communication to dispel myths and reduce conflict
Desire for Coherence Across Multiple Plans
Participants questioned how various planning documents interact and called for integration to reduce 
complexity.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“How do these different plans… play together?”
o
“There are multiple plans and guidelines that govern how a project gets done.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Fragmentation: Overlapping documents create inefficiency.
o
Hierarchy confusion: Lack of clarity on which plan takes precedence.
o
Consolidation: Strong desire for a unified regulatory framework.
Aesthetic and Cultural Identity
Participants valued San Mateo’s historic character but also wanted vibrancy and modernization to 
keep downtown appealing.
x
Quotes and Paraphrases:
o
“Make downtown cool.”
o
“Respect existing architecture.”
o
“Allow creative signage and storefronts.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Preservation of charm: Maintain scale and sense of place.
o
Modern vibrancy: Incorporate contemporary design elements.
o
Cultural branding: Historic identity as an economic and social asset.
Contextualization 
Provide Clear, Practical Guidance
x
Develop explicit, codified standards supported by user-friendly tools such as checklists, timelines,
and flowcharts.
x
Consolidate overlapping plans (zoning, design standards, historic ordinances) into a single
roadmap to reduce confusion.
580 of 1110

--- page 581 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
4
Embed Flexibility While Preserving Historic Character
x
Allow adaptive reuse and modern design elements that complement historic intent, such as 
rooftop additions or creative façades. 
x
Include language that permits deviations when they meet preservation goals and enhance 
economic vitality.
Resolve Conflicts Between Codes
x
Create clear pathways for reconciling ADA compliance with Historic Building Code requirements. 
x
Provide training for local officials on flexibility provisions and negotiation under the California 
Historic Building Code.
Support Economic Viability
x
Incorporate economic impact considerations into ordinance revisions to avoid creating financial 
disincentives. 
x
Streamline approval timelines and clarify appeal processes to reduce risk and encourage 
investment.
Modernize Outdated Standards
x
Update design guidelines and signage rules to reflect contemporary aesthetics while maintaining 
cultural identity. 
x
Refresh historic surveys and context statements to align with current architectural trends and 
community needs.
Foster Community Understanding and Reduce Division
x
Launch educational campaigns to clarify what historic designation means for property owners. 
x
Provide transparent communication tools to dispel myths and reduce neighborhood tensions.
Integrate Multiple Planning Documents
x
Establish a clear hierarchy among the Historic Preservation Ordinance, Downtown Specific Plan, 
and other zoning codes. 
x
Develop a unified guidance resource that explains how these plans interact during project review.
Preserve Cultural Identity While Encouraging Vibrancy
x
Highlight the role of historic character in shaping San Mateo’s unique sense of place and economic 
appeal. 
x
Encourage creative signage, storefront improvements, and public space enhancements that 
complement historic architecture.
581 of 1110

--- page 582 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
5
Places Referenced 
Downtown San Mateo
B Street 
x
Context: Participants noted it as the area “where it’s at” for historic character and referenced past 
city-led work (e.g., the Vendome Hotel renovation).
Vendome Hotel
x
Mentioned as a significant historic resource the city previously “redid,” signaling precedent for city-
backed preservation and reuse.
Baywood Theater (former San Mateo Theater)
x
Case study raised about its modernized façade and questions on whether such updates would still 
be approved under the revised ordinance. 
x
Highlights tension between preservation and contemporary design.
Benjamin Franklin Hotel / Ben Franklin Building (downtown)
x
Brought up in the context of ground-floor use (retail expectations vs. other uses) and ideas for 
rooftop activation and a glass elevator as a modern addition that distinguishes new from old while 
animating the street.
Medical Arts Building (downtown)
x
Referenced regarding painting and refresh processes and how guidance can conflict or lack clarity, 
used as an example of the need for straightforward procedural steps.
Wisdom Hardware > First Watch/Baywatch storefronts (B Street area)
x
Noted in an architectural reminiscence tied to earlier renovations (e.g., moment frame addition) 
and storefront transformations--useful as a micro-case of historic-era commercial ground floors 
evolving.
Old City Hall on the plaza
x
Discussed as a site where participants imagined more creative ground-floor interventions, while 
respecting the historic narrative (including existing interpretive art on the façade). 
Other San Mateo districts and landmarks (beyond downtown)
Glazenwood/Hayward Park
x
A historic neighborhood referenced by residents; involved in recent incidents with the entry pillars 
at 9th Avenue (one toppled by a vehicle)
x
Raised questions about responsibility and replacement in the right-of-way; relevant to ordinance 
clarity and maintenance protocols.
582 of 1110

--- page 583 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
6
Baywood Historic District (residential)
x
Repeatedly cited as an area where misconceptions about historic designation affect neighbor 
relations and remodel expectations.
25th Avenue Business District
x
Perceived by participants as having a “walk back in time” architectural feel – suggested to be 
included in citywide context and guidance.
37th Avenue and 41st Avenue Corridors
x
Mentioned as additional commercial areas with historic elements that merit consideration in the 
context statement and potential ordinance impacts. 
External examples informing local discussion (outside San Mateo)
San Carlos Depot/Depot Café (San Carlos train station plaza)
x
Cited as a cautionary tale: multiple prospective tenants walked away due to unresolved conflicts 
between ADA requirements and historic code, underscores the need for reconciliation pathways in 
San Mateo’s ordinance.
Vine Street Victorians (Paso Robles)
x
A participant’s 1888 local-register Victorian home used to illustrate a pragmatic approach: interior 
modernization was feasible while the city focused on exterior review, reinforcing the value of 
flexible, common-sense preservation practices.

598 of 1110

--- page 599 ---
November 5, 2021, Unitarian Universalists of San
Mateo Congregational Building, 300 E. Santa Inez Ave.
(southeasterly corner of N. Ellsworth St.). Displayed is
the Progress Pride yag, with 11 representational colors,
as created in 2018 by Oregon-based designer Daniel
Quasar. Also displayed is the "Black Lives Matter -
Unitarian Universalists of San Mateo" banner.
Unitarian Universalists of San
Mateo Sanctuary (Constructed 1905)
The Unitarian Universalists of San Mateo (UU San
Mateo or UUSM) have met in the historic Sanctuary
of the former Methodist Church campus at 300 E.
Santa Inez Ave. (corner of N. Ellsworth) since 1952.
Constructed in 1905, the Sanctuary building
withstood the 1906 Earthquake - intact, and, as
of September 2025, is the longest continuously-
serving place of worship in the City of San Mateo.
Unitarian Universalists of San Mateo is "A religious
community of open hearts and open minds, working
together to transform ourselves and the world." -
Source: uusanmateo.org website, September 2025.
300 East Santa Inez Avenue, San Mateo, CA 94401
2021
Grace Lutheran Church
The history of Noah Ark built by the US Army 1944-1945
the memories are held at Golden Gate National Cemetary
2825 Alameda de las Pulgas, San Mateo, CA
1942

--- page 600 ---
Pictured on the San Mateo Drive frontage of the
historic home and retail shop next door (occupied by
Nouvelle Vogue Bridal Boutique), the members of the
walking group from the Unitarian Universalists of San
Mateo pose mid-walking-tour, October 23, 2022.
29 N. San Mateo Drive, Corner of St.
Andrews Dr., and 27 N. San Mateo Dr.
Starting in 2020, during the Pandemic, many of the
congregational members of Unitarian Universalists of San
Mateo (and their friends) joined together for walks - many
of which originated at our Sanctuary at 300 E. Santa Inez
Ave., just north of the historic Downtown of San Mateo.
The Unitarian Universalists of San Mateo (UU San
Mateo or UUSM) have met in the historic Sanctuary
on the former Methodist Church campus at 300 E.
Santa Inez Ave. (corner of N. Ellsworth) since 1952.
Constructed in 1905, the Sanctuary at 300 E.
Santa Inez withstood the 1906 Earthquake.
As of September 2025, the UUSM Sanctuary
is the longest continuously-hosting place of
worship in the City of San Mateo.
29 North San Mateo Drive, San Mateo, CA
2022
1975 downtown walking tour
pamphlet shows original "town" lines
Downtown, San Mateo, CA
1975
600 of 1110

--- page 601 ---
900 Fourth Ave.
The Feely family in their rental on 900 Fourth
Ave., later changed to Grant Street. They
moved there in 1940 and rent was $50 a month.
1st Avenue and B Street - Courtesy of
Hasuko Yamanouchi Watanuki Collection
In 1908, Tetsuo Yamanouchi opened the Imperial Laundry at
218 - 1st Avenue San Mateo. The space was rented from
the Wisnoms a pioneer San Mateo family. In addition to the
family of xve children, the laundry also employed as many
as seven other people. The Yamanouchi home was at the
corner of 1st Ave and Ellsworth Street. Tetsuo Yamanouchi
had been educated and trained as a Zen Buddhist priest in
Japan, and he became the spiritual leader in 1909. The living
room of the home was the beginning of the San Mateo
Buddhist Church. His wife Yoshiko organized the Buddhist
Sunday School, the Buddhist Women’s Association, and the
Young Buddhist Association. When the small congregation
outgrew the living room, an adjacent storefront on 1st
Avenue was rented for $40 a month by Tetsuo
Yamanouchi. When Tetsuo Yamanouchi died in 1936, his
wife Yoshiko took over the laundry business and
successfully operated it until the family returned to San
Mateo at the end of World War II. It was unusual in those
times for the wife to take over a business, but Yoshiko was
determined the business would go on.
In 1940, the business was moved from its original location
to a new facility at 46 B Street.
During World War II, the family was incarcerated in Topaz,
Utah. The business was leased by friends during the war.
Hasuko and Masashi Max Watanuki, the daughter and son-
in-law took over the management of the business after the
war and the name of the business was changed to Blu-
White Laundry because the connotation of the word
“imperial” may have been perceived as a negative. The
name likely came from the appearance of the building
which was white with blue trim. The new building was
601 of 1110

--- page 602 ---
designed by William H Rowe and built by Hugh H.
MacDonald.
Yoshiko Yamanouchi remained active in the San Mateo
Buddhist Temple and in her Buddhist and Civic life.
Imperial Laundry in 1909 - Courtesy of
Hasuko Yamanouchi Watanuki Collection
See 1st Avenue and B Street post
1909
Yoshiko Yamanouchi House -
Historic District - Katsura Walkway
In December 2023, the Yoshiko Yamanouchi House at 1007
East 5th Avenue San Mateo was listed on the National
Register of Historic Places at the local level of signixcance
under Criteria A & B in the areas of Ethnic
Heritage/Asian/Japanese and social History/Woman’s
History for its association with a prominent member the
Issei generation in San Mateo’s Japanese American
Community, from early 20th century to today.
The picture below is the Yoshiko Yamanouchi House
Historic District which has nine contributors:µ The
Entrance Gate, House, Katsura Building, Katsura Garden,
Katsura Walk-way, Storage Room and Pump House, Patio
and Swimming Pool, West Side Garden. The Japanese style
Hill and Pond and Garden was completed in 1958 by
landscape designer Nagao Sakurai. The second structure
and garden on this site was completed in 1968, by architect
and designer Kodo Matsubara, scholar of Japanese sukiya-
styled architecture. The Yoshiko Yamanouchi House is one
of only three properties nationwide spotlighted in the
recent National Register publication “Best Practices
Review.”
An added note:µ After the completion of the Japanese Hill
and Pond Garden at the Yoshiko Yamanouchi House, Nagao
Sakurai assisted the Japanese gardeners who built the
Japanese Tea Garden in Central Park in San Mateo.

Subject: Less Red Tape's official response to the SMHA's restarting of the Baywood Historic District  
Dear City Council and City Manager,
Less Red Tape has received confirmation this week that the SMHA has restarted the Baywood National 
Register of Historic Places (NRHP) nomination at the OHP this week. While we don’t know yet exactly what 
was filed, this act by the SMHA is a clear breach of the deal we reached to pursue a local process in lieu of the 
state-level NRHP process. In light of this development, and the City Council’s special session on Mar 17 2025 
to discuss sending a letter to the OHP asking them to defer the Baywood nomination, we would like to ask 
three things from the City: 
1. Not one penny spent on the Rincon contract or a local historic district process until the SMHA 
withdraws the Baywood nomination. When I got up in front of City Council in January and called the 
SMHA “bad actors,” some criticized me, saying the SMHA was cooperating with the City. We knew 
better. The SMHA never intended to stop their application. The SMHA simply wants unchecked power 
to create
historic districts around the city as cheaply and easily as possible. Trying to negotiate with or appease 
the SMHA is a waste of time and money. What is the point of developing a local process if the SMHA 
will keep subverting it by going directly to the state? 
2. City cooperation with Less Red Tape on opposing the Baywood nomination. 
605 of 1110

--- page 606 ---
2
We have been fighting this since the beginning, pouring thousands of hours and tens of thousands of 
dollars of our own money. We have worked closely with members of the City Council, City staff, and 
developed strong grassroots networks throughout the city. We have made great progress, but in 
conjunction with the City, we can, together, be a much more effective force. Having the unified 
opposition of the municipal government and the affected homeowners will be very powerful. Indeed, the 
only historic district nomination to ever be pulled by the OHP, in Alameda, was such a case. If this is 
amenable to the Council, we can discuss what forms this collaboration might take. 
 
3. Councilmember Lisa Diaz Nash must formally absent herself from the March 17 special session 
as part of her “recusal,” or else formally end her pretense at recusing herself. 
As you know, Councilmember Nash has been “recusing” herself from votes specifically related to the 
Baywood historic district. We use quotes because she has been an active participant in discussions, 
and her ardent support for the district is documented in PRA requests. While Nash has no legal reason 
to recuse, at least according to the City Attorney, she has tried to appear “neutral” on the matter by 
claiming to "recuse" herself. Since the March 17 special session is solely dedicated to Baywood, Lisa 
Diaz Nash should not attend in any capacity, much less cast a vote. Alternatively, she can end her 
phony “recusal” and stop this farce. 
 
Thank you for listening. We are deeply concerned about these new developments and wish to try to move 
forward in a constructive way. We want peace among our neighbors and historic preservation done the right 
way: with consent and input from local residents about what is worth preserving and how to preserve it. The 
SMHA are bullies and bad actors who are exploiting a weak set of state laws to attempt to enact their own 
agenda.  
 
 
Thank you, 
 
Andrew Ryan on behalf of Less Red Tape, a Baywood community organization

From: Andrew Ryan 
  
Sent: Tuesday, April 15, 2025 12:32 PM 
To: Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Cc: Christina Horrisberger <chorrisberger@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; 
 Sung Kwon <skwon@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Council (San Mateo) 
<CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Re: Planning Department procedures for Historic Districts 
 
Hi Zachary, 
 
Thank you for your response. However, I believe there are a few misunderstandings that we hope the City can 
clarify. 
 
First, it seems we both agree that Glazenwood is not a formally adopted historic district and is not 
protected under any City ordinance. This is a good starting point. Surely you can see why we are deeply 
concerned that if Rincon’s work is allowed to proceed, even if it does not specifically identify new historic 
districts, the results of Rincon’s work could be easily used by the City to create new de facto historic districts, 
just like the City has done with Glazenwood. 
 
Regarding CEQA, I would like to clarify the process for identifying historic resources. Under CEQA, there are 
three types of “historic resources,” each with specific requirements: 
1. Mandatory Resource / CEQA Guideline 15064.5(a)(1): A mandatory resource must either be "listed 
in, or determined to be eligible by the State Historical Resources Commission." Glazenwood has 
neither been listed nor determined eligible by the State Historical Resources Commission, so this 
provision is inapplicable. 
2. Presumed Resource / CEQA Guideline 15064.5(a)(2): A presumed resource must be specifically 
identified in a local register as "officially designated or recognized as historically significant by a local 
government" pursuant to a local ordinance or resolution (PRC Sec. 5020.1(k)). As we agree, 
Glazenwood has not been formally designated as a district by the City of San Mateo. Moreover, since 
Glazenwood’s survey is over five years old, it cannot be included in the State Historic Resources 
Inventory in its current form. Therefore, this provision also does not apply. 
607 of 1110

--- page 608 ---
2
3. Discretionary Resource / CEQA Guideline 15064.5(a)(3): This provision allows the lead agency to 
determine, on a case-by-case basis, that a property not listed on a local register and not found 
eligible for the California Register can still be considered a historic resource for CEQA purposes. 
However, this determination must be “supported by substantial evidence,” and such decisions can be 
appealed to the City Council. The City cannot simply presume that any property in Glazenwood 
qualifies as a historic resource. Each property must be individually evaluated, and the determination 
must be backed by substantial evidence. If the Planning Department makes such a determination, it 
could be subject to appeal. 
 
Therefore, when the City states that it “currently has two identified districts” in its Historic Resources handout, 
this is inaccurate. To the extent the City has authority to apply historic resource standards to Glazenwood, it 
can only assert such authority under CEQA and because it has not been formally listed locally and/or deemed 
eligible by the State Historical Resources Commission. 
 
Thank you for your attention to these points. I look forward to discussing this further. I am available to meet in 
person or virtually, whatever works for you. 
 
Best regards, 
Andrew Ryan 
 
 
On Mon, Apr 14, 2025 at 10:49 AM Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org> wrote: 
Morning Andrew, 
  
I’m following up on your earlier email inquiry. The City’s Historic Preservation Ordinance is only used when a 
property is formally designated as a resource or a district.  However, the status of “eligible” means that a property 
hasn’t been designated by the City but that it meets certain criteria (like age and historical importance) that make 
it a candidate for listing at the state and/or national level, or for local historic designation.  This means that the 
City could consider it for local designation but that it also has to be evaluated under CEQA when a project on the 
property is proposed.  Also, there’s no such thing as “formally eligible.”  A property either does, or does not, meet 
the criteria for being eligible, and this is based on an evaluation using the National Register of Historic Places and 
the California Register of Historical Resources standards.  
  
With regards to Glazenwood, you are correct that staff has not be able to find any record that it was locally 
designated as a historic district, so it is not an officially designated historic district.  However, based on the 
documented information and evidence on the Glazenwood Neighborhood, including in the City’s past General 
Plan, it does meet the criteria for being an eligible historic district.  Thus, this information has to be considered 
when a project is proposed that is subject to environmental review. Also, as has been communicated previously 
to LRT and at public meetings, Rincon is working on the preparation of a Citywide Historic Context Statement, but 
this work does NOT include identification of potential historic districts.  
  
City Council and City Attorney included as a BCC.

From: Andrew Ryan 
  
Sent: Monday, April 7, 2025 12:17 PM 
To: Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Cc: Frank Elliott 
 City Mgr <citymgr@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Council (San Mateo) 
<CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Planning Department procedures for Historic Districts
  
Dear Mr. Dahl,
As the local historic designation process proceeds, we urge the City to affirm that restrictions for historic 
districts and individual historic resources will apply only to those areas that are officially recognized as being 
“formally eligible” or “listed” on a local, state, or federal registry, starting with Glazenwood.
Glazenwood currently has no legal historic district status at the federal, state, or local levels. Yet, the City of 
San Mateo’s Planning Department treats it as if it were listed as “eligible for the National Register of Historic 
Places.” The Planning Department has simply adopted an opinion and turned that opinion into code 
enforcement, which is not how the law is supposed to work. The ambiguous status enforced by the City 
benefits neither the City nor homeowners, but merely adds unnecessary expense, delays, and uncertainty to 
development and remodeling projects.
We respectfully request that the City instruct the San Mateo Planning Department to immediately cease 
treating Glazenwood—and similarly, Baywood—as part of a Historic District.
For reference, on page 32 of the “1989 Historic Building Survey” conducted by the City, Glazenwood is noted 
as "appears eligible for nomination as a National Register district.” However, merely stating that Glazenwood 
“appears eligible” confers no legal status as a historic district and no legal “eligibility” status, as “eligibility” is a 
specific legal status with regards to the National Register of Historic Places which can only be granted by the 
Department of the Interior. Numerous other individual properties in San Mateo are also in this same “gray 
area” status.
Should the City decide to pursue a local, state, or federal process to formally designate Glazenwood as a 
historic district, per the Council’s direction in January 2025 that any local process include affirmative 
homeowner consent, we request that the residents of Glazenwood be asked to provide affirmative consent for 
such a designation.
As demonstrated by the issues in Baywood, lacking strong community support can lead to significant conflicts 
among neighbors and within the broader community. Historic district status can confer certain rights but also 
imposes considerable obligations on residents and the City, and therefore should be implemented only with 
careful deliberation.
As the local process moves forward—and as Rincon identifies potential historic districts through their context 
statement work—we request that the planning department apply historic district procedures only to those 
areas that are officially recognized.
609 of 1110

--- page 610 ---
4
We welcome the opportunity to meet with you at your earliest convenience to discuss this matter in more 
depth. 
  
Regards, 
  
Andrew Ryan 
A member of Less Red Tape, a Baywood community organization 
https://lessredtape.com

Subject:
Please take the SMHA propaganda out of the City"s "Historic Policy Update Effort FAQs"
Date:
Tuesday, June 17, 2025 9:48:18 AM
The City website is a new, separate entry for "Historic Policy Update Effort FAQs" --
https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/DocumentCenter/View/97533/FAQs-Historic-Preservation-
Ordinance -- on the city's historic policy web site
(https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/4877/Historic-Policy-Update-Effort) that contains
misinformation and missing information.
We ask that you  thoroughly review this FAQ and remove all misinformation, errors, and half-
truths. Leaving this misinformation up confuses residents Specifically:
1. Remove all information about "historic districts" from this FAQ and keep it strictly
about the mechanics of the development of the new ordinance. There is already a
Historic Districts FAQ on your website which is decent and factual.
2. One piece of misinformation is in the question "Would being in a historic district
change my property value?". The answer implies that being in a historic district
increases your property values. The truth is -- and you know this --  no one knows for
sure, it's much more complicated, and this answer doesn't go into any of the many
negatives of being in a historic district that DEFINITELY come along with being in a
historic district. Implying that being in a historic district raises property value is straight
up SMHA propaganda.
3. Another piece of misinformation is in the question "Who decides what is historic?"
Nowhere in this answer is "Owner Consent" mentioned, yet the City Council has
reaffirmed TWICE now that owner consent will be a mandatory part of the ordinance.
4. Still another piece of misinformation is in the question "Will I still be able to renovate
my house if it is designated as historic?". The answer here is incomplete and wrong, and
more clearly answered in your other FAQ under the entry "Are there added permits or
construction requirements that could increase costs for a proposed project affecting a
contributor to a historic district?"
5. Yet another piece of misinformation: under the question "Will this affect what kinds of
buildings or businesses are allowed in the future?" the answer states "Preservation
focuses on the physical character of buildings and neighborhoods, not land use." We all
know this is false, so stop printing the SMHA's propaganda. You'll never be able to re-
zone a historic district.
If this FAQ was written by Staff, then we have a serious training and knowledge problem
inside City Hall.
If this FAQ was written by Rincon, then we are in big trouble because they are supposed to be
the "experts."
I believe you have people inside City Hall who know what's up, but for whatever reason you're
deliberately choosing to take the SMHA's side here every time and try to ignore what the
people, and the City Council, have told you that they want.

P.S. Here is the update given to council: 
Mills Act Program - The City has two active properties with Mills Act (Historic Preservation) Agreements. 
Staff is currently engaged with the owners of both properties (70 de Sabla Rd. and 37 E. Santa Inez Ave.) 
to prepare updated workplans that detail property maintenance and preservation activities over the next 
ten years, as required by their agreements. Initial windshield surveys of the sites show that both are in 
compliance with the requirements of the agreements and well maintained with historic characteristics 
intact. Staff anticipates that the updated workplans will be completed in September, followed by detailed 
property inspections. As part of the Historic Policy Update, staff is working with Rincon to develop a 
complete Mills Act Program that addresses the process for designation and ongoing administration to 
maintain compliance. No new Mills Act applications will be accepted until the program has been 
developed and the Historic Policy Update Effort has been completed.

Chantal
 
From: Andrew Ryan 
 
Sent: Monday, October 20, 2025 1:12 PM
To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>
Cc: Frank Elliott 
 Chris Kusber <ckusber@sanmateopark.org>
Subject: The "Historic Policy Update" is a disaster in slow-motion, will you help?
 
Councilmember Cwirko-Godycki (and others), how would you feel if the City were to spend
$300K on a new set of flooding ordinances, and both the City staffer in charge as well as
the firm creating those ordinances (1) never met with Shoreview residents and (2) did not
include any specifics about how flooding would be handled specifically in the Shoreview
neighborhood? You’d be very disappointed and angry, I’d expect.
 
Well, that’s exactly what the Planning department under Zach Dahl is doing with our
Historic Policy Update. Just like “flooding risk,” “Historic Risk” is not distributed
equally in San Mateo. The primary neighborhoods at risk for historic designation are:
1. Baywood: already nominated for the National Register of Historic Places, under very
high risk of being turned into a 444 home historic district against neighborhood
consent
617 of 1110

--- page 618 ---
2. Glazenwood: already considered a historic district by the City, even though this is an
error in the San Mateo General Plan, and the City is refusing (why?) to change
designation
3. San Mateo Park: Even older than Baywood, contains many possibly individually
historic homes as well as the historic medians designed by John McClaren of Golden
Gate Park fame
 
A proper education and outreach plan would involve meeting directly and
consistently with residents of these neighborhoods to give them clarity on how their
rights and obligations as homeowners will be affected if they are designated historic.
Yet, none of those meetings have occurred.
 
Even worse, when my colleague Frank Elliott recently met with Mr. Dahl, he was told that,
assuming Baywood is eventually placed on the National Register, Mr. Dahl has no idea
how homes in Baywood will be treated under City law and expects bureaucrats at the
OHP in Sacramento to guide us! If that’s true, how is that better than the status quo, and
what is San Mateo spending money on here exactly? 
 
Council, are you really telling us that we are spending all this money and residents are
getting no additional clarity or input about what we can and can’t do with our homes? This
is outrageous. The Council is heading for yet another expensive disaster if we do not
engage in more outreach and give property owners in this city more clarity.
The three questions I’m asking you to ask yourselves and ask Staff:
1. Why has there not been direct neighborhood outreach to the neighborhoods at
greatest risk of being declared historic?
2. Do we even have the right person from the City leading this effort, or should we find a
replacement for Mr. Dahl?
3. Is the City really getting any value for its money from Rincon, or do we need to re-
examine our commercial relationship and contractual compliance with them?
 
If the Council takes action now, I think we can correct this situation. No laws have been
made or changed, and there is still time to engage with key stakeholders. But if you fail to
act, I can guarantee that you are creating big problems for years to come that will come
back to haunt city residents and future governments and cause more acrimony and even
larger expenses.
 
Andrew Ryan
Baywood Homeowner
 
618 of 1110

--- page 619 ---

Cc: SMPNA Board of Directors <Board@sanmateopark.org> 
Subject: San Mateo Park participation in historic ordinance outreach (info + hosting offer) 
 
Dear City Council, City Clerk, Mr. Dahl, and Mr. Khojikian, 
On behalf of the San Mateo Park Neighborhood Association (SMPNA), thank you for your continued 
work on City ordinances and for your efforts in keeping the community informed. 
Over the past week, we’ve heard from San Mateo Park residents who are eager to learn more about 
the historic ordinance process, updates, and how to participate. In that spirit - and true to our mission 
to share information with our community - we’re reaching out to request a few details and to offer our 
help. 
Specifically, could you please share: 
x 
Schedule & format – Dates, times, locations, and formats for the upcoming pop-up events 
and the three planned stakeholder focus groups.  
x 
Communications & notification – The City’s planned outreach (channels, timing, and any 
mailers) so we can share and potentially amplify, as needed.  
x 
Timeline & next steps – Key milestones over the coming months, anticipated dates for a draft 
ordinance and the citywide historic context statement, and how public input will shape 
revisions before Council consideration. 
We’d also be happy to host. SMPNA can help coordinate a City-led event in San Mateo Park - either 
as one of your pop-ups or as a dedicated, in-person neighborhood session focused on learning and 
input. We’re happy to handle logistics (venue coordination, on-site volunteers) and to promote the 
event through our email list, website, and social channels to help ensure broad, welcoming 
participation. 
Our goal is simple: to be a reliable vessel of information so Park neighbors can learn, ask questions, 
and share feedback constructively. 
If helpful, our board is meeting next Monday, 10/6, and can be made available to compare calendars 
and discuss dates and logistics.  
Thank you for your partnership and for all you do for our community. 
Chris  
Chris Kusber  
President 
San Mateo Park Neighborhood Association  
 
 
sanmateopark.org

From: Connie Weiss 
  
Sent: Monday, June 2, 2025 12:21 PM 
To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Historic Policy Study Session, June 2, 2025 
 
 
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Council Members, 
  
Thank you for addressing San Mateo’s ordinance for historic resources. Like many of you, my love for 
San Mateo is rooted in its beautiful historic architecture and is a key reason why San Mateo is such a 
highly desirable place to live. 
  
There have been several “generations” of the City Council working on the General Plan over the 
years; even with widely diverse opinions of each Council body, it is resoundingly clear that they 
believed historic architecture was important to preserve throughout each of the General Plan 
iterations. 
  
In revising the ordinance, the City should respect the expertise of our state and national historic 
bodies, which have processes developed over many years. 
  
The City ordinance should flow easily from the state process so homeowners of historic homes will 
understand how the processes work together for a cohesive policy. 
  
While it seems magnanimous to include owner consent in the City’s ordinance, it is actually 
duplicative of the state process. We should not challenge a process that has taken years to put 
together with something that sounds good, but has no real value for San Mateo, and actually defeats 
the purpose of what the General Plan is trying to accomplish. 
  
And for me, an owner of a historic home, I have already had to acquiesce to the City when we 
remodeled our home in 2017. We made several changes to our plans without complaint as we agreed 
that it was important for our home to continue to align with the rest of the neighborhood. Our consent 
was not an option; the requirement was complied with. 
  
There are two Heritage Sycamore trees in our front yard. These trees are not ours; they are the 
City’s. Even though they are on our property, we have no options as to their care, we are tasked by 
the City to care for them, which we willingly do because it is so important to the overall look and feel 
623 of 1110

--- page 624 ---
2
of this gorgeous neighborhood. We have never been allowed to opt-out or consent on this, although it 
is a costly requirement, and we never expect to be given choices by the City. 
  
With respect to the General Plan, Council should not consider the Planning Commission as the 
governing body for the ordinance as this body has a reputation for unilaterally opposing historic 
preservation because of the false notion that it prevents housing. A more balanced body of leaders 
needs to be identified to truly carry out the work the General Plan prescribes. 
  
Thank you, 
  
Connie Weiss 
San Mateo Resident  
 
* PRIVILEGE AND CONFIDENTIALITY NOTICE: This message, together with any attachments, is intended 
only for the use of the individual or entity to which it is addressed. It may contain information that is 
confidential and prohibited from disclosure. If you are not the intended recipient, you are hereby notified
