Mr. Rob Newsom, Mayor 
Mr. Prasanna Rasiah, City Attorney 
City of San Mateo 
330 W. 20th Avenue 
San Mateo, California 94403 
 
Subject: Defamation of Heritage Alliance by the City of San Mateo 
 
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Attorney Rasiah: 
 
We, the Board of Directors of the San Mateo Heritage Alliance, are writing to you about the 
serious and defaming allegations made against Heritage Alliance on March 17, 2025, and the 
use of false information as a basis for voting to send a letter to the California State Historic 
Preservation Office (SHPO).  The City Council members repeatedly claimed SMHA reneged on 
a supposed “agreement.” We are asking for a retraction of your false statements, a retraction of 
the SHPO letter, and a public apology. 
 
During the City Council Special Meeting discussion of Agenda Item #5, “Letter to California 
Office of Historic Preservation – Approval”, Council Member Nicole Fernandez requested the 
City Council approve a letter to SHPO requesting an 18-month deferral of the review of the 
Baywood Historic District nomination. What follows are direct quotes and time stamps from the 
video recording.  
 
Council Member Danielle Cwirko-Godycki spoke in support of the letter, stating at 1:38:33, “The 
Heritage Alliance said they would not continue with submission of their application if the City 
moved forward with an expedited local process and updating our historic ordinance. 
Unfortunately, the San Mateo Heritage Alliance has gone back on their word, and submitted 
their application anyway”. That allegation is patently false; we never said this, therefore we 
never went back on our word.   
 
Council Member Loraine continued the false narrative, declaring at 1:46:34, “I am disappointed 
in the Heritage Alliance’s decision to continue this application because the idea of what came 
before Council was with that agreement in mind.”  The Heritage Alliance states again, for the 
record, there was NO such agreement ever made.  
 
Mayor Newsom goes on to say at 1:52:00, “I would have appreciated if the pause was at least 
held until we had another meeting.”  Respectfully, Mayor Newsom, Heritage Alliance slowed the 
nomination for eight months, but did not agree to stop all work or wait until the end of the City 
711 of 1110

--- page 712 ---
Mr. Rob Newsom 
 
 
Mr. Prasanna Rasaiah 
 
 
March 25, 2025 
 
 
Page 2 
 
process. There was never an agreement between the City of San Mateo and the Heritage 
Alliance to enter into any such pause.   
 
It is important to note that Heritage Alliance did choose to delay submitting the revised Baywood 
nomination to SHPO in May 2024 and to not pursue consideration by the State Historic 
Resources Commission in August 2024.  Heritage Alliance independently delayed the submittal 
as a demonstration of good faith for the benefit of our initial discussions for the City to prioritize 
the General Plan requirement to update the historic preservation ordinance and prepare a 
context study in 2024, rather than waiting another five years.   
 
The potential agreement was requested by the City as a way to calm down the rhetoric and 
consternation in the community. The discord is a direct result of the incorrect information LRT 
was spreading and continues to spread in the community. We had been working towards an 
agreement; however, Less Red Tape abruptly pulled out of negotiations, the process towards 
an agreement ground to a halt, and was never resumed. The effort to collaboratively forge an 
agreement between Heritage Alliance, the City, and LRT failed because the City, as well as 
LRT, did not agree to the actions required on their part to make the agreement viable, and a 
true agreement on a pre-defined pause, possible. Heritage Alliance requested that LRT stop 
spreading inaccurate information and cease their divisive campaign against the historic district 
and that the City provide accurate information to counteract LRT’s inaccurate information.  
 
While we voluntarily paused our nomination activity, Heritage Alliance repeatedly told the City 
Manager that our pause was not a long-term pause, we would not stop all work, and we would 
not wait until the end of the City process. It is not clear what the City Manager communicated to 
Less Red Tape and the Council members. It is clear, however, there was no specific 
communication to Council members as to the nature of the pause. Heritage Alliance also 
communicated to each member of the City Council what our pause consisted of on numerous 
occasions.  
 
Heritage Alliance asked City Manager Alex Khojikian (via email the day after the 3/17/25 City 
Council special meeting) to provide evidence of the agreement referenced throughout the 
Council meeting. Mr. Khojikian replied: 
  
”The only agreement I am aware of is the Rincon Agreement for Historic 
Preservation Services that the Council approved on January 21, 2025.”  
 
On that we agree. There was no agreement. Yet, Mr. Khojikian sat silent throughout the Special 
Meeting proceeding, never mentioning that the City did not have such an agreement with the 
Heritage Alliance. As confirmation, please see the attached email from the City Manager to 
Laurie Hietter. 
 
712 of 1110

--- page 713 ---
Mr. Rob Newsom 
 
 
Mr. Prasanna Rasaiah 
 
 
March 25, 2025 
 
 
Page 3 
 
Every Council member on the dais repeated the misinformation about the agreement to pause 
the nomination process, giving it more credence when it shouldn’t have had any. This 
misinformation was used as the basis to approve a motion for Mayor Newsom to send the letter 
to SHPO.  
 
The repeated false statements that Heritage Alliance reneged on an agreement defames the 
Heritage Alliance and harms our reputation in the community. Heritage Alliance sees this as a 
serious matter that needs to be addressed and rectified immediately. We ask that the City take 
these actions:  
 
1. Retract or rescind the letter sent to the California SHPO. Delaying the State’s decision 
will only extend the false statements, discord, and animosity. 
2. Retract the false statements made at the City Council Special Meeting alleging bad faith 
by our organization. The City needs to make a public statement announcing that there 
was not “an agreement to pause” the Baywood nomination by Heritage Alliance.  
3. Mayor Newsom should issue an apology, both in public and in writing, to the Heritage 
Alliance, its Board, and its membership for the false comments made that defame our 
organization. The apology should be on behalf of the entire City Council and City staff. 
 
Board President Laurie Hietter is available to you to provide any additional information or 
documentation needed. 
 
We look forward to your prompt response and action on this matter.  
 
Sincerely, 
 
 
Laurie Hietter, for 
The San Mateo Heritage Alliance

January 16, 2025 
City Council

SUBJECT: Support for Context Study and Historic Preservation Ordinance Update 
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Council Members:
The San Mateo Heritage Alliance strongly urges the approval of the proposed scope of work to prepare 
a context study and to update our historic preservation ordinance. The current ordinance, dating back 
to 1993, and the outdated 1989 Historic Building Survey are no longer sufficient to meet the demands of 
our growing city while preserving its rich historical legacy. 
The Importance of a Context Study
x
Economic and Efficient: A context study is far more cost-effective than a detailed survey of 
each historic property, providing a broad understanding of our city's historical assets at a 
fraction of the cost. 
x
Framework for Preservation: It will lay down the groundwork necessary for a citywide historic 
resources understanding, informing not just preservation but also future development.
x
Policy Informed by Study: The study will directly influence the updating of our preservation 
policies and ordinance, ensuring they reflect the current state and significance of our historical 
resources.
x
Strategic Planning: By understanding the type, quantity, and location of our historic resources, 
we can make informed decisions that balance growth with preservation. 
The Necessity to Update the Historic Preservation Ordinance
x
Cultural and Economic Benefits: As noted in our current ordinance, preserving our history is 
not just about nostalgia; it's crucial for enhancing our community’s economic, cultural, and 
aesthetic value. 
x
Criteria for Preservation: An updated ordinance will clearly define processes and criteria for 
designating and protecting local historic districts, something missing in our current framework. 
The current ordinance does not recognize or apply to the new Yoshiko Yamanouchi House 
Historic District or the pending Baywood Historic District.
x
Legal Clarity and Protection: It will provide a robust legal framework to recognize, protect, and 
manage historic resources, ensuring they are considered in development reviews. 
714 of 1110

--- page 715 ---
City Council 
January 16, 2025 
Page 2 
 
 
 
2 
The Urgency of Action 
x 
Time is of the Essence: Over three decades have passed since significant action was taken on 
historic preservation in San Mateo. Delaying further will only increase costs and risks losing 
more of our heritage. 
x 
Resource Allocation: With budgets potentially tightening, acting now is prudent to leverage 
existing funds for maximum impact. 
Balancing Historic Preservation with Housing Needs 
x 
Compatibility with Modern Needs: Historic properties and districts can accommodate modern 
housing solutions like ADUs, expansions, and remodels, proving that preservation does not 
hinder but can complement urban development. 
x 
Selective Designation: The criteria for historic designation are stringent, ensuring only truly 
significant structures or neighborhoods are protected, thus not broadly impeding new housing 
developments. 
 
In conclusion, the time to act is now to safeguard San Mateo’s historical narrative while fostering 
growth. Approving this scope of work will not only protect our past but also enrich our future. We 
urge you to ignore the misinformation and support this initiative for the benefit of San Mateo's cultural 
heritage and its residents. 
Thank you for your consideration. 
Sincerely, 
 
Laurie Hietter 
President 
San Mateo Heritage Alliance 
 
 
715 of 1110

--- page 716 ---
From:
Martin McTaggart
To:
Zachary Dahl; Sung H. Kwon
Subject:
FW: Historic Ordinance
Date:
Monday, June 16, 2025 11:49:14 AM
 
 
From: Steve Lesley 
 
Sent: Tuesday, June 10, 2025 8:36 AM
To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>
Subject: Historic Ordinance
 
City of San Mateo Historic Code
 
Dear City Council
I read that the City of San Mateo is considering a Residential Historic Ordinance.
As a 35 year resident of San Mateo and as an architect, I ask that you please consider the long
term consequences of historic designations to houses. In my opinion, a blanket historic label
of an area or neighborhood is the wrong approach. Once designated “Historic”, then any
remodeling or additions can become very complicated, expensive,  or prohibited. There is
value to modernizing within the already strict zoning codes that can require designing within
the existing style of the house or neighborhood. I have experienced designing a standard 100
year old junkie non-descriptive looking house with other jurisdictions that was located within a
historic neighborhood that became a very expensive and lengthy process. At some point old
homes in disrepair need to be remodeled and updated.
 
I hope that this ordinance will be very careful in designating a house as “Historic”, and only be
used on an individual basis and not cover an entire neighborhood.
 
Steven Lesley
San Mateo, CA 94403

FW: Historic Ordinance
Date:
Monday, June 16, 2025 11:49:14 AM
 
 
From: Steve Lesley 
 
Sent: Tuesday, June 10, 2025 8:36 AM
To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>
Subject: Historic Ordinance
 
City of San Mateo Historic Code
 
Dear City Council
I read that the City of San Mateo is considering a Residential Historic Ordinance.
As a 35 year resident of San Mateo and as an architect, I ask that you please consider the long
term consequences of historic designations to houses. In my opinion, a blanket historic label
of an area or neighborhood is the wrong approach. Once designated “Historic”, then any
remodeling or additions can become very complicated, expensive,  or prohibited. There is
value to modernizing within the already strict zoning codes that can require designing within
the existing style of the house or neighborhood. I have experienced designing a standard 100
year old junkie non-descriptive looking house with other jurisdictions that was located within a
historic neighborhood that became a very expensive and lengthy process. At some point old
homes in disrepair need to be remodeled and updated.
 
I hope that this ordinance will be very careful in designating a house as “Historic”, and only be
used on an individual basis and not cover an entire neighborhood.
 
Steven Lesley
San Mateo, CA 94403

Glazenwood historic district designation

From Takako Matsuyama <i

To Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org >; City Council
(San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear members of city council,

My family and | have proudly called Glazenwood home for nearly 40 years.

When we purchased our property, we were unaware that it was located within a designated historic
district.

We only discovered this when we undertook a remodeling project, which required compliance with
historic preservation guidelines and resulting in thousands of dollars in additional expenses due to the
involvement of the historical society.

Although the added costs and requirements were unexpected and burdensome at the time, we have
since come to fully appreciate and value the historic designation.

We are genuinely pleased that our home and neighborhood possess recognized historical significance.
This designation preserves the unique characters of the area, enhances its appeal and contributes
meaningfully to property values for all residents.

We love our neighborhood, take pride in its rich history.

| understand that there are current discussions about potentially removing Glazenwood from historic
district.

| am writing to firmly express our strong opposition to any such change.

One of the key reasons we chose to purchase and remain in this home for 4 decades is the extensive
historic quality of the neighborhood.

Removing the designation would diminish an important aspect that we - and many other long time
residents- have grown to cherish deeply and it would understandably cause significant disappointment
and concern.

Please give serious weight to the views of longtime homeowners like us who strongly support
maintaining the historic status of Glazenwood.

We urge you to preserve this designation in order to protect the special heritage and character of our
community.

Thank you for your consideration and for your efforts in safeguarding our city’s historic neighborhoods.
Sincerely,

Takako Matsuyama

Initial Draft Update 
San Mateo Historic Preservation Ordinance 
San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 27.66 
 
As part of the City’s Historic Policy Update Effort, the Historic Preservation Ordinance will be updated 
to align with current statewide best practices, General Plan 2040 and community priorities.  This initial 
draft update to the Ordinance is responsive to early public comments and aligned with City Council 
direction. The goal is to publish this draft for community review and input in December and January.  
This input will be considered ahead of the final draft that is published ahead of Planning Commission 
and City Council public hearings in 2026.

27.66.010 Purpose and Intent 
The intent of the Historic Preservation Ordinance is to promote the protection, enhancement, 
preservation, and use of historic resources within San Mateo, recognizing they are of economic, 
cultural, and aesthetic benefit to the community. It is further recognized that the economic, cultural 
and aesthetic character of the City will be enhanced by honoring its heritage and ensuring that future 
generations can experience and appreciate it. The purposes of this chapter are as follows: 
(a) Designate, preserve, protect, and enhance the City’s historic resources. 
(b) Foster public awareness and encourage public understanding of the City’s past through the 
recognition and use of historic and cultural resources. 
(c) Preserve a variety of architectural styles, patterns of development, and design trends that 
reflect phases of the City’s history and encourage complementary design and construction to 
inspire a diverse and livable urban environment. 
(d) Enhance the visual and aesthetic character of the City by encouraging the preservation and 
maintenance of historic resources.  
(e) Avoid demolition of the City’s historic resources, where feasible, through preservation, 
rehabilitation, or other available and appropriate alternatives. 
(f) Fulfill the City’s responsibility under state and federal regulations, including the California 
Environmental Quality Act and Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act. 
27.66.020 Definitions 
(a) Alteration, major. Any significant changes or modifications of a Listed Resource or Contributor 
within a Historic District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory, including but not 
limited to changes to or modifications of structural, architectural details or visual 
characteristics; additions; and placement or removal of any significant objects affecting the 
significant exterior visual and/or historical qualities of the property. Examples include: 
(1) Any demolition or relocation of a historic building or structure, or removal of a 
significant feature of a Historic Resource.  
(2) Any project that requires a permit that significantly alters or changes the street-facing 
elevation or side elevation of a Historic Resource, including major changes to windows 
721 of 1110

--- page 722 ---
3 
and doors and their openings, the application of new exterior cladding or coating that 
changes the appearance, design, or texture of a property, and the addition of dormers 
and other architectural features. 
(3) Any addition of square footage to a building elevation that faces a street. 
(4) New construction on a property containing a Listed Resource or a Contributor within a 
Historic District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory that could affect 
its visual appearance.  
(b) Alteration, minor. An alteration that has been determined to have limited potential to affect 
the defining character and architectural style of a Listed Resource or Contributor within a 
Historic District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory. In no case shall minor 
alterations involve actions including new construction or full or partial demolition of a Listed 
Resource. Examples include: 
(1) Any demolition or removal of insignificant exterior features of a Listed Resource, 
including additions, windows, doors, and exterior siding material that is non-original or 
otherwise lacking in historic integrity. 
(2) Any project that requires a permit that does not substantially change the exterior 
character-defining features of a Listed Resource, including re-roofing in a different 
material that replicates the existing or original roofing, replacement windows and doors 
matching the design and materials of the existing or original windows and doors (when 
it is infeasible to repair) and minor additions on secondary or rear elevations. 
(3) In Historic Districts listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory, demolition 
and alteration of detached garages and other accessory structures built within the 
period of significance on contributor properties and new construction of such 
structures on any Listed Resource (districts and individual properties). 
(4) Any alteration determined minor by the Community Development Director. 
(c) Certification of Appropriateness. The administrative approval document issued by the City to 
approve an alteration to a Listed Resource or Contributor within a Historic District listed in the 
San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory.  
(d) Character-Defining Feature. Character-defining features are visual aspects, physical qualities, 
and characteristics of a historic resource that help to convey its significance and may include 
the building’s shape, structural details, materials, and decorative features. 
(e) Construction. The act of expanding an existing building or structure or the erection of a new 
principal or accessory building or structure on a property.  
(f) Contributing Resource or Contributor. A building, structure, site, or object within a Historic 
District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory that retains enough of its original 
location, setting, design, materials, workmanship, feeling, and association to convey the 
district's historical or architectural significance.  The resource contributes to the designated 
district's historical significance and dates to its period of significance.  
(g) Demolition. The complete destruction or removal of a building, structure, or improvement, 
removal of more than 50 percent of the perimeter walls, or substantial removal of a structural 
wall of a street-facing elevation of a structure or building that may have an adverse effect on 
the significance of a property. 
(h) Eligible Historic Resource. Resources found to be eligible for designation as a historic resource 
at the federal, state, or local level based on substantial evidence, but not formally designated. 
722 of 1110

--- page 723 ---
4 
(i) Historic District. An area containing a concentration of historic buildings, structures, sites, 
objects, or other resources that are unified historically, culturally, or architecturally.  A historic 
district may contain both “contributing” and “non-contributing” resources.  
(j) Historic Resource. Includes, but is not limited to, any object, building, structure, site, area, 
place, sign, district, or landscape feature which is historically, culturally, architecturally and/or 
archaeologically significant. Historic resources may also include, but are not limited to, 
resources listed in the: 
(1) National Register of Historic Places; 
(2) California Register of Historical Resources; 
(3) San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory.  
(k) Historic Resources Evaluation (HRE). A historic resources evaluation (HRE) is a report, 
prepared by a qualified professional, which documents, identifies, and evaluates, based on 
the appropriate evaluative criteria, potential historic resources, including buildings, structures, 
objects, and districts, for their eligibility for designation.  
(l) Improvement. Any building, structure, landscape feature, or object physically located on real 
property or that constitutes a physical addition or physical alteration of real property or any 
part of such alteration.  
(m) Integrity. The ability of a property, structure, site, or building to convey its identity through its 
physical features and how they relate to its historic context and the basis of its significance. A 
measure of integrity is not the same as an assessment of condition. 
(n) Listed Resource. Individual resource listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory. 
(o) Non-contributing Resource. A building, structure, site, or object within a historic district that 
does not possess significant qualifications or characteristics of a contributing resource due to 
such factors as age or level of alteration, but which has been included within the geographic 
boundary of a historic district. 
(p) Owner. The person or entity (public or private) appearing on the last equalized assessment roll 
of the County of San Mateo or person/entity providing proof of ownership. 
(q) Qualified Professional. An individual who meets the Secretary of the Interior’s Professional 
Qualification Standards (36 CFR Part 61, Appendix A) in history, architectural history, or historic 
architecture.  
(r) San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory. A list of individual Listed Resources and Historic 
Districts in San Mateo, designated by the City under the provisions of this chapter. 
(s) Secretary of the Interior’s Standards for the Treatment of Historic Properties. The Secretary of 
the Interior’s Standards for the Treatment of Historic Properties codified in 36 CFR, Part 68, 
which serve as the guidance for work on historic resources.   
27.66.030 San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory 
(a) The resolutions adopting designations and deletions of Listed Resources shall be known as 
the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory.  
(b) Listed Resources and Historic Districts locally designated as of the date of enactment of 
Ordinance No. 2022-10 §§ 2, 3; Ord. No. 2010-15 § 15; Ord. No. 1995-13 § 3; Ord. No. 1993-
21 § 1, inclusive of the Downtown Historic District, codified in this chapter are included in the 
San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory. 
723 of 1110

--- page 724 ---
5 
(c) Properties listed in the National Register of Historic Places or California Register of Historic 
Resources adopted by local resolution.  
27.66.040 Designation Criteria  
Prior to approval for listing in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory, the City Council shall find 
that the historic resource satisfies one or more of the criteria outlined below, and retains its historic 
integrity.  
(a) Is associated with events that have made a significant contribution to the broad patterns of 
San Mateo’s or California’s history and cultural heritage; or 
(b) Is associated with the lives of persons or communities important to San Mateo’s or California’s 
past; or 
(c) Embodies the distinctive characteristics of a type, period, region, or method of construction, 
or represents the work of an important creative individual, or possesses high artistic values; 
or 
(d) Has yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important to prehistory or history. 
27.66.050 Eligibility Requirements for Listing  
(a) Listed Resources. To be eligible for consideration as a Listed Resource, the resource must be 
at least fifty (50) years old and meet at least one of the criteria outlined in Section 27.66.040.  
(b) Historic District. To be eligible for consideration as a Historic District, at least XX percent of the 
buildings in the proposed historic district (excluding accessory buildings) must be at least fifty 
(50) years old or otherwise meet the requirement of Section 27.66.040. In addition, no more 
than XX percent of the buildings in the proposed district may be non-contributing. Non-
contributing buildings may be included as part of a historic district only to the extent that they 
are determined to be essential to the geographic integrity of the district. The [reviewing body] 
shall make determinations identifying non-contributing buildings as part of the review process. 
27.66.060 Procedure for Designating Listed Resources  
(a) Application for Listed Resources. The City shall make available an application for listing historic 
resources in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory.  
(1) Owner Consent. Nominations to add a Listed Resource to the San Mateo Historic 
Resources Inventory shall be made only by application of the property owner or 
property owners representing a majority or controlling interest on which the resource 
is located. The property owner may request that a resource be listed in the San Mateo 
Historic Resources Inventory by submitting a completed application to the Planning 
Division. No application shall be accepted without the written consent of the property 
owner. 
(2) Application Review. Listed Resource applications shall be reviewed by the Planning 
Division to assess if all application materials are provided and if the application is 
complete, including sufficient information to support the finding that the resource 
meets the requirements for designation set forth in Section 27.66.050.  
(3) Designation Recommendation. Listed Resource applications, once deemed complete 
by the Planning Division, shall be reviewed by the [reviewing body] at a public meeting. 
The [reviewing body] shall determine whether the resource meets one (1) or more 
criteria for approval as a historical resource to be listed on the San Mateo Historic 
Resources Inventory and, based on this determination, provide a recommendation to 
724 of 1110

--- page 725 ---
6 
the City Council. The [reviewing body] shall adopt a resolution stating its 
recommendation, focusing on the criteria set forth in Section 27.66.40, and 
incorporating its reasons for the recommendation. 
(4) Designation Action. Following the recommendation from [reviewing body], the City 
Council shall hold a public hearing to consider the application. Following the public 
hearing, the City Council shall adopt a resolution to approve or deny the application 
based on the criteria set forth in Section 27.66.40. If the application is approved by 
City Council, the resource shall be added to the San Mateo Historic Resources 
Inventory. 
27.66.070 Procedure for Designating Historic Districts  
(a) Application for Historic District Designation. The City shall make available an application for 
listing a Historic District in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory.  
(1) Owner Notification and Consent. Nominations to add Historic Districts to the San 
Mateo Historic Resources Inventory shall be made by a property owner or group of 
property owners in a potential historic district. The property owner(s) may request that 
a district be listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory by submitting a 
completed application to the Planning Division. The application shall include the 
following: 
(A) The application form and requisite support materials outlined in the application 
instructions. 
(B) Documentation that notification of the pursuit of the application was provided 
to all potentially affected property owners in the proposed district via mailed 
notice. Mailed notice shall be provided to every occupant and owner of property 
as listed in San Mateo County’s most recent property ownership assessment 
roll within the proposed Historic District.  
(C) Documentation by petition establishing at least XX percent of property owners 
in the proposed historic district as listed in San Mateo County’s most recent 
property ownership assessment roll support the potential designation by 
affixing their signatures to the petition.  
(2) Application Review. Historic District applications shall be reviewed by the Planning 
Division to assess if all application materials are provided and if the application is 
complete, including confirmation of owner consent and sufficient information to 
support the finding that the Historic District meets the requirements for designation 
set forth in Section 27.66.050.  
(3) Community Meeting. Once an application has been deemed complete, the applicant 
will host a community meeting prior to any public meetings to consider the designation. 
The purpose and intent of the meeting is to provide information about the proposed 
Historic District and designation process. Mailed notice shall be provided to every 
occupant and owner of property as listed in San Mateo County’s most recent property 
ownership assessment roll within the proposed Historic District. Such notice shall be 
provided at least 10 days prior to the community meeting date.  
(4) Designation Recommendation. Once an application has been deemed complete by the 
Planning Division and a community meeting has been held, it shall be reviewed by the 
[reviewing body] at a public meeting. The [reviewing body] shall determine whether the 
historic district meets one (1) or more criteria for approval as a historical resource to 
be listed on the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory and, based on this 
725 of 1110

--- page 726 ---
7 
determination, shall be recommended to the City Council that the application be 
approved or denied. The [reviewing body] shall adopt a resolution stating its 
recommendation, focusing on the criteria set forth in Section 27.66.40, assessing the 
historical significance of those contributing resources identified in the proposed 
district, affirming that no more than XX percent of the buildings in the district are non-
contributing, and incorporating its reasons in support or denial of the application. 
Mailed notice shall be provided to property owners and occupants within the Historic 
District at least 10 days prior to the [reviewing body] meeting date. 
(5) Designation Action. Following the recommendation from [reviewing body], the City 
Council shall hold a public hearing to consider the application. Following the public 
hearing, the City Council shall adopt a resolution to approve or deny the application 
based on the criteria set forth in Section 27.66.40. If the application is approved by 
City Council, the Historic District shall be added to the San Mateo Historic Resources 
Inventory. Mailed notice of the public hearing shall be provided to property owners and 
occupants of properties within the Historic District at least 10 days before the meeting 
date. Mailed notice of the decision shall be provided to property owners and occupants 
of properties within the Historic District within 30 days following the decision. 
27.66.080 Criteria and Procedure for Removing a Listed Resource or Historic District 
Designation 
(a) The City Council may approve removal of a Listed Resource from the San Mateo Historic 
Resources Inventory by one of the following methods: 
(1) Application for Removal. Removal of a Listed Resource designation may be sought by 
the owner of a property on which a Historic Resource is located. Applications must be 
accompanied by such documentation as required by the Planning Division and 
demonstrate that the Listed Resource does not possess, or no longer possesses, a 
minimum level of significance necessary for listing as outlined in Section 27.66.040. 
(2) Removal Following Destruction. The Planning Division may seek removal of 
designation to the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory where a Listed Resource 
has been destroyed. 
(b) Delisting Recommendation. Upon completion of an application to remove a Listed Resource, 
the request shall be reviewed by the [reviewing body] at a public meeting. The [reviewing body] 
shall determine if the Historic Resource meets the conditions to warrant removal and provide 
a recommendation to City Council that the application be approved or denied. 
(c) Delisting Action. Upon receipt of a recommendation from the [reviewing body] to remove a 
Listed Resource from the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory, the City Council will hold a 
public hearing on the application. If the City Council approves an application, such approval 
shall be made by resolution.  
(d) Historic District Delisting. The process to remove a Historic District from the San Mateo Historic 
Resources Inventory shall follow the same process that is required to designate a Historic 
District as outlined in Section 27.66.070. 
27.66.090 Procedure for Development Proposals 
Community Development Director Review. The Community Development Director may require a 
Historic Resource Evaluation for any development proposal involving the demolition or modification of 
character-defining features of buildings or structures over 45 years old that are not designated or have 
not been identified in a previous historic resource survey adopted by the City Council. A Historic 
Resource Evaluation may inform the adoption of the appropriate environmental determination 
pursuant to the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA).  
726 of 1110

--- page 727 ---
8 
27.66.100 Certificate of Appropriateness  
(a) No person shall demolish, construct, move or alter any Listed Resource or Contributor within 
a Historic District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory without first obtaining 
a Certificate of Appropriateness. Approval of such work shall be required even if no other 
permits or entitlements are required by the City. In approving an application, the final approval 
body may impose conditions or restrictions as it deems necessary or appropriate to ensure 
that the integrity of the resource is maintained.  
(1) Design Standards. The Secretary of the Interior’s Standards for the Treatment of 
Historic Properties, as amended from time to time, are incorporated by this reference. 
Work proposed for a Listed Resource or Contributor within a Historic District shall be 
in compliance with these standards. Where applicable, locally adopted design 
standards or guidelines shall apply in addition to The Secretary of the Interior’s 
Standards for the Treatment of Historic Properties. 
(b) Zoning Administrator Review. The Zoning Administrator has the authority to review and 
approve, conditionally approve, or disapprove Certificates of Appropriateness for the following 
types of projects, provided that the projects do not result in any significant impacts pursuant 
to the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), subject to public notice and to appeal of 
the final action to the [reviewing body]: 
(1) Minor Alterations. 
(2) Projects that: (1) do not adversely affect a Listed Resource or Contributor within a 
Historic District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory, or (2) are 
consistent with the Secretary of the Interior’s Standards for the Treatment of Historic 
Properties, and for which no public purpose would be served by requiring [reviewing 
body] review.  
(c) Exemptions. A Certificate of Appropriateness is not required for the ordinary maintenance and 
repair of any Listed Resource or Contributor within a Historic District listed in the San Mateo 
Historic Resources Inventory, so long as maintenance and repair does not involve change in 
exterior design, material, or appearance. A Certificate of Appropriateness is not required for 
construction, reconstruction, alteration, or demolition that the Building Official certifies is 
required to protect public health or safety because of an unsafe or dangerous condition. 
(d) Economic Hardship. At the time of an application for a Certificate of Appropriateness, the 
applicant may apply for an Economic Hardship Exception. The application shall include proof 
of economic hardship from the owner of the property, and documents and/or testimony to 
substantiate a claim of economic hardship as required by the Community Development 
Director.  The approval of an Economic Hardship Exception shall be based on the findings that: 
(1) The owner of the property is qualified as low-income or very-low income; and 
(2) Denial of the Economic Hardship Exception would deprive the owner of all reasonable use 
of, or economic return on, the designated resource; or 
(3) Denial of the Economic Hardship Exception would cause the owner to suffer unreasonable 
damage in comparison to the benefit conferred to the community by the designated 
resource.  
27.66.110 Design Guidelines  
In addition to the Certificate of Appropriateness requirements outlined above, all exterior modifications 
of individually eligible and contributor buildings in the Downtown Retail Core subarea of the Downtown 
Area Plan shall conform with the Downtown Retail Core and Downtown District Design Guidelines.  
727 of 1110

--- page 728 ---
9 
27.66.120 Incentives for Preserving Historic Resources 
To foster preservation and encourage participation in the City’s historic preservation efforts, the 
following incentives are available to Listed Resources or Contributor within a Historic District listed in 
the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory: 
(a) State Historical Building Code. Any alteration made for preservation, rehabilitation, restoration, 
or relocation of such properties shall be made according to the requirements of the state 
historical building code pursuant to the California Health and Safety Code Section 18950, et 
seq. 
(b) Mills Act. The City Council may authorize the use of contracts pursuant to California 
Government Code Section 50280 et. seq., also known as the Mills Act. Such contracts may be 
entered into at the sole discretion of the City Council based on the recommendation of the 
[reviewing body] for the specific intent to promote the continued preservation of historic 
properties. 
(c) Additional Incentives. The City Council may authorize the use of additional incentives, as 
appropriate, to encourage the preservation, reuse, and rehabilitation of the City’s historic 
resources. 
27.66. 130 Duty to Keep in Good Repair 
The owner of a resource listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory shall keep in 
good repair the exterior portions of all such buildings, structures, or improvements, and all 
interior portions thereof whose maintenance is necessary to prevent deterioration of any 
exterior improvement or essential exterior architectural features. They shall also maintain 
properties in compliance with San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 7.14 and the protective 
treatment standards of the International Property Maintenance Code. 
27.66.140 Enforcement and Penalties 
(a) Time to Correct. Prior to assessment or initiation of any penalty for any violation of this chapter, 
the Zoning Administrator shall provide written notice of noncompliance to property owners. 
Notice shall be sent by certified and regular mail. Following mailing of the notice, property 
owners shall have 30 calendar days to correct the violation or to inform the City that there is a 
financial hardship, or why an extension is warranted. Additional time to correct the violation 
may be allowed where the property owner is exercising due diligence in acting to correct 
noticed violations. The Zoning Administrator shall have the authority to place reasonable 
and/or necessary conditions on such an extension to ensure timely correction of the violation.  
(b) Work Stoppage. In addition to any other fines, penalties or enforcement provisions set forth in 
this chapter, failure to comply with an approved application shall constitute grounds for 
immediate stoppage of the work involved in the noncompliance until the matter is resolved. 
(c) Violation Penalty. In addition to the foregoing remedies, and any other remedies available by 
law, the City Attorney may enforce the provisions of this article by seeking injunctive relief to 
restrain or enjoin or to cause the correction or removal of any violation of this chapter.

Rincon Consultants, Inc.

Engagement Summary
Version 2 Updates are in Purple Text
Overview 
The City of San Mateo is in the process of developing a Citywide Historic Context Statement and 
updating its Historic Preservation Ordinance. To inform this work, the City developed an Outreach and 
Engagement Strategy focused on multiple entry points for community participation and offering a 
range of opportunities for residents, organizations, and interested parties to share input and 
perspectives that inform the update process. Through the engagement conducted thus far, community 
members have shared their views of the future of historic preservation in San Mateo and identified 
places and themes that hold historical and cultural interest.
The project started in February 2025, with outreach initiated in the summer, and will extend through 
Spring 2026. To date, the project team has conducted a City Council Study Session, two three focus 
groups, one community workshop, online outreach, and three mobile workshops. These activities 
correspond to the first two three engagement phases:
x
Project Launch and Early Focus Groups, and
x
Community Storytelling and Draft Development, and
x
Release of Draft Documents for Review
The project is now entering the Release of Draft Documents for Review Feedback on Drafts phase, 
during which additional community feedback will continue to refine and strengthen the draft materials.
In addition to defined engagement events, the City has received numerous emails and comments from 
community members, demonstrating San Mateo’s strong interest and investment in shaping the future 
of historic preservation.
Summary of Participation and Activities 
The City implemented a multi-faceted strategy that combined digital communications, printed 
materials, and in-person events to raise awareness and invite participation in the project. The 
approach emphasized accessibility and visibility by creating multiple entry points for residents, 
stakeholders, and organizations to learn about the project and share their input.
Participant Identification and Outreach Methods
Participants were reached through a blend of targeted outreach and citywide communications:

x
Targeted invitations for workshops and website participation were sent to members of 
neighborhood associations, local preservation advocates, cultural organizations, and prior

x
Broader community participation was encouraged through general City communications including 
e-blasts, newsletters, and social media posts. 
x
Mobile workshop pop-up locations were strategically selected to engage residents from different 
parts of the city and to reach people who might not typically participate in formal meetings.

These activities built momentum across engagement phases, beginning with focused discussions 
among key stakeholders, followed by broader community engagement through workshop events.

Comparison to Outreach & Engagement Strategy Goals
The Outreach and Engagement Strategy established participation goals of:
x
Reaching at least 20 attendees per workshop
x
Collecting 50 Digital Time Capsule contributions
x
Engaging 8-10 participants per focus group
x
Expanding email sign up list from a baseline of 36 subscribers by 10%
To date, the project has exceeded goals for community workshops, reaching approximately 137
residents through the first workshop and subsequent mobile workshop events. Pivoting from a second 
traditional workshop to a series of mobile workshops proved effective in diversifying the voices heard 
in this round of engagement. 
The project’s email list has grown from 36 to 90 178 subscribers, far surpassing the growth target and 
demonstrating strong interest in staying informed as the project advances. To date, the Digital Time 
Capsule has received 11 community posts and 13 reactions, marking early but meaningful online 
participation. The first two focus groups were intentionally smaller in scale, providing opportunities for 
key stakeholder groups, including representatives from Less Red Tape and the San Mateo Heritage 
Alliance, to share their perspectives directly. The third focus group reached its goal of engaging at least 
8 members involved in the business and development community. 
Activities Overview 
The engagement program included a mix of focus groups, a community workshop, mobile workshops, 
and digital engagement. Each activity was designed to gather input on key themes while ensuring that 
participation opportunities were accessible, inclusive, and interactive.
Focus Groups 
The City hosted two virtual focus groups in July 2025 with residents representing different viewpoints 
and experiences related to historic preservation in San Mateo. The City hosted a third virtual focus 
group in December 2025 with members of the business and development community. Each session 
lasted approximately one hour and was facilitated by the consultant team with support from City staff. 
Discussions were recorded for note-taking purposes only and analyzed thematically to identify shared 
concerns, priorities, and ideas. The full analysis for each focus group is attached to this report as 
Appendix A, with summaries provided below.
Focus Group #1: Residents Cautious of Preservation Efforts
Participants, primarily from or interested in the Baywood neighborhood, discussed concerns about the 
fairness and transparency of historic designation processes and their impact on property rights. They 
emphasized the need for owner consent, clear and objective design standards, and predictable 
procedures for evaluating historic resources.
While participants expressed frustration with perceived inconsistencies and costs of compliance with 
historic preservation regulations, they also supported developing a Historic Context Statement so long 
as it could not be used to justify new designations without consent. The discussion highlighted the 
importance of education and communication to promote public confidence in the process and ultimate 
project deliverables. 
Key Takeaways: 
732 of 1110

--- page 733 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
5 
x
Desire for transparency, owner consent, and local control
x
Concern about financial and procedural burdens
x
Conditional support for a context statement focused on education, not regulation
Focus Group #2: Residents Supportive of Preservation
This group emphasized the importance of preservation in maintaining San Mateo’s character and 
identity. Participants called for stronger implementation tools, professional oversight, and clearer 
design compatibility standards to ensure consistency between planning and building review.
They also encouraged a more inclusive approach to preservation that recognizes cultural and social 
histories, as well as opportunities to align preservation with housing, sustainability, and equity goals 
through adaptive reuse and education.
Key Takeaways: 
x
Preservation seen as essential to community identity
x
Need for stronger tools, standards, and professional oversight
x
Support for inclusive, educational, and adaptive approaches
Focus Group #3: Business/Development Community
Participants stressed the need for clear, practical guidance and flexibility in the ordinance to avoid 
stifling development. They expressed concerns about economic and business viability due to 
uncertainty and rigid rules that can make projects financially unfeasible.
They also called for updates to plans and surveys to reflect current architectural trends and economic 
conditions while still honoring historic character. Additionally, participants noted that overlapping 
planning documents create friction and lack clarity, and they expressed a strong desire for an 
integrated, unified regulatory framework.
Key Takeaways: 
x
Need for flexibility and clear, practical guidance in the ordinance
x
Preservation of cultural identity while encouraging creative modernization
x
Establish a clear hierarchy among the Historic Preservation Ordinance, Downtown Specific Plan, 
and other zoning codes
Overall Observations
Together, the two three focus groups reflect a spectrum of perspectives, from those seeking flexibility 
and property rights protection to those prioritizing stronger safeguards for historic resources and 
inclusiveness to ensure the context statement reflects San Mateo’s diverse communities. Both All 
groups underscored the need for clarity, transparency, and education, reinforcing the City’s goal of 
developing balanced policies that protect community character while maintaining fairness and trust.
Community Workshop
The first community workshop, Preservation and Regulation 101, introduced residents to the City’s 
Historic Policy Update process and built a shared foundation for future discussions. The virtual event 
aimed to educate participants on key preservation concepts, including historic context statements, 
733 of 1110

--- page 734 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
6
ordinance structure, and the relationship between preservation and development, while collecting 
early feedback on community priorities. Spanish interpretation was available during the meeting.
The session combined a presentation on preservation laws and local regulations with three interactive 
activities designed to encourage reflection and discussion. The activities are summarized below, with 
compiled contributions available in Appendix B.
Warm Up: I Can’t Imagine San Mateo Without…
Participants began by sharing short reflections on what defines San Mateo’s character and what 
aspects of the city they most value. Common responses included references to beautiful 
neighborhoods, Central Park, trees, and the diverse community and historical figures of San Mateo.
Participants highlighted beloved places such as the Japanese Tea Garden, the Benjamin Franklin 
Hotel, and the Downtown Historic District. 
Digital Time Capsule: People, Places, and Events
The next activity invited participants to identify people, places, and events they believe future 
generations should remember about San Mateo. Submissions highlighted well-known local institutions 
such as San Mateo’s schools, Talbot’s Toys, and Draeger’s Market, as well as community figures like 
Claire Mack, Tom Brady, and members of the city’s Japanese community. Participants also highlighted 
beloved everyday places that hold personal or collective meaning, including the Central Library, 
Downtown San Mateo Post Office, B Street, and Bay Meadows. The exercise linked historical 
storytelling with the technical goals of the context statement, which include highlighting patterns of 
development, centralizing historical information, providing a consistent framework for evaluating a 
property, and offering a more robust picture of San Mateo’s history -- capturing the city’s lived 
experience alongside its architectural heritage.
Preservation Priorities Exercise
In small breakout groups, participants discussed four key themes shaping preservation policy: 
flexibility for homeowners, protection of historic landmarks, cultural equity and representation, and 
transparency and predictability. Using a “Preservation Dollars” framework, groups were asked to 
allocate limited resources across these priorities, sparking discussion about trade-offs and shared 
values. Most groups emphasized transparency and predictability as their top priority, followed closely 
by protection of historic landmarks and cultural representation. The activity revealed broad community 
interest in clearer processes and more inclusive recognition of San Mateo’s heritage.
Participation and Outcomes
Approximately 42 residents attended the workshop, representing a range of neighborhoods and 
interests. An exit survey was provided via the virtual platform; however, only seven participants 
provided responses, limiting the usefulness of the results for a broader analysis of representation. The 
virtual format also presented challenges: some attendees experienced technical difficulties, and 
several expressed a preference for a more traditional Q&A format rather than the interactive activities 
used. We incorporated this feedback into our planning for the second workshop, which led us to shift 
to a series of in-person mobile workshops designed to provide more direct interaction and 
opportunities for real-time questions and discussion. 
Key takeaways included:
x
Broad recognition of San Mateo’s cultural and architectural diversity
x
Desire for transparent, consistent, and educational preservation processes
734 of 1110

--- page 735 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
7
x
Support for inclusive storytelling and representation in the Historic Context Statement
x
Interest in balanced policies that protect heritage while allowing reasonable flexibility for property 
owners
Full results from group activities and whiteboard outputs are included in Appendix B.
Mobile Workshops
Following the first community workshop, the City shifted from a traditional meeting format to a series 
of mobile workshops, sometimes referred to as “pop-ups,” designed to reach residents in familiar, 
everyday settings. This pivot reflected lessons from early engagement. While the virtual workshop 
successfully built understanding among those already interested and was available to residents who 
may not feel safe or comfortable attending a gathering in person, broader participation required 
meeting people where they are.
The mobile format provided more accessible, casual opportunities for conversation and participation, 
particularly for residents who may not attend formal meetings or have limited time to engage online. 
As shown in Table 1, offering touchpoints at varying times of day helped reach residents with differing 
schedules. Rather than convening another single event, the City opted for multiple small-scale, in-
person touchpoints that collectively expanded reach and visibility across the community.
Activities and Content
Each pop-up featured a set of multilingual (English, Chinese, and Spanish), visually oriented displays 
introducing the two components of the Historic Policy Update, the Historic Context Statement and the 
Historic Preservation Ordinance, along with printed postcards with QR codes linking to the project 
website and Digital Time Capsule. Staff and consultants were present to answer questions, collect 
input, and provide educational context about the project.
The core activity invited participants to respond to the same people, places, and events prompts 
introduced during the virtual workshop. Using sticky notes, attendees shared the names of individuals, 
landmarks, and local stories that they felt best represented San Mateo’s heritage. This approach 
allowed the team to continue building the community narrative started online and identify recurring 
themes across engagement formats.
735 of 1110

--- page 736 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
8
Outcomes and Observations
The mobile workshops generated meaningful one-on-one conversations about the City’s history, 
identity, and preservation goals. Many participants used the opportunity to learn about the broader 
Historic Policy Update effort and how it connects to housing, land use, and community character 
policies under other City efforts.
The mobile workshop format proved effective in:
x
Broadening participation to include residents encountered in public spaces rather than through 
formal events;
x
Reinforcing education on the City’s preservation framework; and
x
Gathering qualitative feedback that complemented earlier workshop and digital engagement 
results.
These events demonstrated the value of informal, interactive outreach in maintaining visibility for the 
project and building trust through direct dialogue. The mobile workshop materials and results are 
available in Appendix C. 
Online Engagement
Digital engagement opportunities ensure that residents can participate in the Historic Policy Update 
at their own pace and through familiar online platforms. The City’s Project Website and Digital Time 
Capsule served as the primary interactive tools, inviting community members to sign up for updates 
and share stories, memories, and reflections about the people, places, and events that define San 
Mateo’s identity. 
As of November 2025, the Digital Time Capsule has received 11 posts and 13 reactions, representing 
a small but insightful sample of community input. The content reflects a wide range of input, from 
neighborhood landmarks and local businesses to historic photos of families and homes, that will 
inform the Historic Context Statement.
736 of 1110

--- page 737 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
9
Beyond the Time Capsule, the City maintained a consistent online presence to keep residents 
informed throughout the process. Project updates were shared through the City’s website, email and 
print newsletters, and social media channels, helping to build awareness and invite continued 
participation in upcoming project phases. Together, these tools provided accessible entry points for 
engagement and helped sustain visibility and transparency between live events.
Upcoming Engagement
The next community workshop is scheduled for January 21st, 2026, and will be a two-hour draft review 
session. Participants will review the Draft Historic Context Statement and Draft Historic Preservation 
Ordinance with a historic preservation expert, with ample opportunities to ask questions throughout
the session.
A second City Council Study Session is scheduled for January 26th, 2026, and will focus on reviewing 
the draft documents.
In February, two focus groups are planned: one with members of the Youth Activities Commission and 
another with residents from underrepresented communities. The City will continue to distribute e-blast 
updates to the project email list, providing information on the project, key dates, and opportunities for 
public feedback.
Key Takeaways
Community input from the focus groups, workshop, mobile pop-ups, and digital engagement activities 
revealed a consistent set of priorities centered on trust, transparency, and inclusivity in the City’s 
historic preservation efforts. Participants expressed a strong desire for clear, fair, and predictable 
processes that balance property owner rights with the shared value of protecting San Mateo’s 
neighborhood character.
737 of 1110

--- page 738 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
10 
Across all activities, residents emphasized the importance of broadening preservation narratives to 
include the city’s cultural, social, and architectural diversity. Many encouraged the City to recognize 
not only landmark properties, but also everyday places that hold community meaning, such as 
gathering spaces like Central Park, local businesses, and institutions that reflect San Mateo’s evolving 
identity. Participants also identified the need to align preservation with housing goals. A recurring 
theme across all engagement formats was the need for education and clear communication to 
improve public understanding of how historic preservation works and have confidence that the process 
is fair and objective. 
These insights have directly informed the Historic Context Statement and Historic Preservation 
Ordinance update. Detailed summaries and raw data from all engagement activities are included in 
the appendices.

Focus Group 1 – Thematic Analysis
Community Group: Less Red Tape
Date: July 9, 2025
Location: Virtual
Facilitators: Kassandra Gale and JulieAnn Murphy
Overview 
This document presents a thematic analysis of the first focus group conducted as part of the San 
Mateo Historic Policy Update effort. The session included residents affiliated with the Less Red Tape 
group, primarily from the Baywood neighborhood. The discussion centered on concerns related to 
historic preservation, property rights, and the implications of state and local designation processes.
Emergent Themes 
Distrust in the Historic Designation Process
Participants repeatedly expressed skepticism about how historic designations are determined.
x
Quotes:
o
“We think the whole thing is being weaponized against us in Baywood.”
o
“They will find a way of making Baywood historic when it doesn’t have the attributes.”
o
“The drive-by assessment by Page and Turnbull is grossly inaccurate.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Inconsistent application: Reference to Glazenwood being treated as historic without formal 
designation.
o
Lack of site-specific knowledge: Concerns about “drive-by” assessments and lack of permit 
review.
o
State-level rubber-stamping: Perception that the State approves nearly all nominations without 
scrutiny.
o
Consultant bias: Belief that consultants are hired to justify preordained outcomes.
This theme reflects a deep concern about fairness, transparency, and accuracy in the designation 
process.
Demand for Owner Consent and Local Control
A strong, consistent demand emerged for affirmative, written consent before any property is 
designated historic.
x
Quotes:
o
“No home would be included… without individual consent.”
o
“No third-party nominations.”
o
“Why not 100% approval like an HOA?”
x
Sub-themes:
740 of 1110

--- page 741 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
2
o
Affirmative consent vs. passive inclusion: Strong opposition to opt-out or assumed consent 
models.
o
Third-party nominations: Rejection of any process that allows non-owners to initiate 
designations.
o
Consent thresholds: Suggestions for 80% or 100% thresholds before a district nomination can 
proceed.
This theme emphasizes personal agency and property rights as central to participants’ values.
Fear of Financial and Practical Burdens
Participants described the cost, complexity, and impracticality of maintaining or modifying historic 
homes.
x
Quotes:
o
“It costs a lot of money… extremely difficult, extremely time-consuming.”
o
“Trying to pick it up and put a new foundation under it would be worse than tearing it down.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Cost of compliance: Preservation described as 4-5 times more expensive than rebuilding. One 
participant said, “I will never do that again.”
o
Structural feasibility: Examples of homes with foundational issues or extensive modifications 
that are impractical to preserve.
o
Time and complexity: Preservation seen as time-consuming and technically difficult.
o
Impact on resale and inheritance: Concerns about burdening future owners or heirs.
This theme reveals a pragmatic concern about the real-world implications of preservation.
Desire for Clear, Protective Ordinances
Participants want specific, enforceable local rules that protect homeowners from unintended 
consequences.
x
Quotes:
o
“We want ordinances that give us the maximum flexibility.”
o
“What kind of protections can the city give us so we’re not lost?”
o
“Objective design standards… that trumps CEQA.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Objective design standards: Proposed as a way to balance flexibility with neighborhood 
character.
o
Appeals process reform: Frustration with perceived “unlimited appeals” and lack of finality.
o
Flexibility within designation: Questions about whether homeowners can rebuild in different 
styles.
o
Examples from other cities: Huntington Beach cited as a model for more permissive policies.
This theme reflects a proactive interest in shaping policy to reflect community needs.
741 of 1110

--- page 742 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
3
Confusion and Need for Clarity
There was frequent mention of uncertainty and lack of understanding about how designations work 
and what they mean.
x
Quotes:
o
“We don’t understand the process.”
o
“All the uncertainty of it is very troubling.”
o
“We want to know what our right is to challenge a contributor status.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Terminology confusion: Terms like “contributor” and “non-contributor” were not well 
understood, and participants wanted to know how these labels affect their rights.
o
Process opacity: Participants didn’t know how decisions were made or who had authority. “We 
don’t understand the process” was a recurring sentiment.
o
Desire for education: Several participants asked for clearer criteria and examples of what 
qualifies as historic.
This theme highlights a gap in communication and education between officials and residents.
Conditional Support for Historic Context Statement
Participants showed interest in local history, but only if the context statement is not used to justify 
unwanted designations.
x
Quotes:
o
“There is value there… pulling the history together will be very interesting.”
o
“As long as that’s not a starting point for somebody to make a nomination.”
o
“We’re not anti-history.”
x
Sub-themes:
o
Interest in local history: Participants were genuinely curious about San Mateo’s past—
mentioning schools, estates, parks, and notable residents.
o
Fear of misuse: The context statement was seen as a potential “launchpad” for unwanted 
designations. “As long as that’s not a starting point for somebody to make a nomination…”
o
Desire for caveats: Participants wanted explicit language stating that the context statement 
cannot be used to justify designations without consent.
o
Examples of valuable history: Mention of schools, estates, parks, and local celebrities.
This theme reflects a nuanced position: appreciation for history, but concern about how it’s applied.
Social Division and Emotional Strain
Participants described emotional distress and neighborhood conflict resulting from the historic 
designation debate.
x
Quotes:
o
“This has been tearing our neighborhood apart.”
o
“People wouldn’t even look each other in the eye.”
o
“You’ve got frightened people on this call.”
742 of 1110

--- page 743 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
4
x
Sub-themes:
o
Neighborhood conflict: Participants described how the historic designation debate had “torn 
the neighborhood apart.”
o
Loss of trust: Some felt betrayed by neighbors or advocacy groups. “People wouldn’t even look 
each other in the eye.”
o
Urgency and fear: The tone was often anxious, even combative. “We’re already at war here,” 
one participant said.
This theme underscores the human and relational cost of policy decisions.
Contextualization 
Ordinance Reform as a Tool for Protection
x
Participants view the ordinance update as their primary line of defense against unwanted 
designations and restrictions.
x
The update must address classification clarity, appeals processes, and design flexibility, especially 
for neighborhoods like Baywood that are already under scrutiny.
Context Statement as a Double-Edged Sword
x
While the historic context statement is intended as a planning and educational tool, participants 
fear it could be used to justify future designations.
x
The city must clearly define the purpose and limits of the context statement to avoid further 
erosion of trust.
Community Engagement and Trust Building
x
The focus group itself is part of the city’s community engagement strategy, but the emotional 
tone of the discussion reveals deep mistrust.
x
The update process must go beyond technical revisions and include relationship 
repair, transparent communication, and ongoing dialogue.
Economic and Practical Realities
x
The ordinance must account for the financial and logistical burdens of preservation, especially 
in older neighborhoods like Baywood.
x
Policies that ignore these realities risk being seen as punitive or elitist.
Need for Education and Clarity
x
Participants’ confusion about terms like “contributor” and “non-contributor” underscores the need 
for plain-language explanations in both the ordinance and public materials.
x
The update effort should include educational components to demystify the process and criteria.
Social Cohesion and Neighborhood Health
x
From the participants perspective, the Baywood case illustrates how historic preservation efforts 
can fracture communities when not handled with care.
743 of 1110

--- page 744 ---
Rincon Consultants
San Mateo Historic Policy Update Effort
5
x
The policy update must be sensitive to social dynamics, offering voluntary pathways and conflict 
resolution mechanisms.
Historic Examples 
Buildings and Structures
x
St. Matthew’s Catholic Church – located on the border of Baywood; noted for its architectural and

Former Mayor’s House on Delaware – possibly already historic; mentioned as a point of interest.

Arroyo Park – near Baywood, along Crystal Springs Creek; noted for its charm and a historical

Crystal Springs Creek – Runs through Arroyo Park and the Parrott Estate area

Serra High School (Junípero Serra High School) – Originally located at the site of St. Bartholomew’s;

x
Aragon High School – Mentioned in the context of local school history.

x

Commercial and Cultural References

Hillsdale area.
Notable People and Residences
x

x
Merv Griffin’s Childhood Home – Also suggested as potentially historic.
x
Lynn Swann – Mentioned as a notable alumnus of San Mateo High School.
x
Barry Bonds – Attended Serra High School.
