1/13/26, 11:37 AM Historic Policy Effort - Zachary Dahl - Outlook

oad Outlook

Significant Concerns Regarding Public Process and Policy Options for Historic Districts

From Frank Elliott Po

Date Thu 1/8/2026 1:17 PM

To Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash
<Idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-
Godycki <Danielleforsanmateo@ gmail.com>

Cc = Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org>; Andrew Ryan

Dear Mayor and City Council Members,

| am writing to highlight a critical concern regarding the current staff process for drafting the Historic
District ordinance. | have spent dozens of hours reviewing the draft and preparing comments, but |
fear this substantive input will be ignored, as it has been throughout the process so far.

@ BOTTOM LINE UP FRONT: If you read no further, | ask that you insist staff clarify and
improve their public engagement process immediately. Knowledgeable participants must be
afforded a real opportunity to advance substantive input, ensuring that viable policy alternatives are
not administratively ruled out before the Council even has the chance to hear them. While that may be
the plan going forward, it has not been the reality thus far.

1. The Context: A Missed Opportunity & Fiscal Responsibility

It is critical to state clearly why this local process was expedited: The San Mateo Heritage Alliance
(SMHA) is advancing a state nomination for the Baywood district without the consent of property
owners. One of the primary drivers for this ordinance update—championed by the City Manager
specifically to reduce contention—was the expectation that the SMHA would withdraw their state
nomination.

While that withdrawal did not happen, the City still retains a critical opportunity to reduce contention
by providing relief to homeowners in designated districts, to the extent legally viable. We must not
miss this opportunity. Reducing this contention would be a win for everyone involved.

Finally, this is an issue that should concern all Council members. If we fail to reduce contention
after spending between $300/500K on this effort, a large constituency will rightfully ask: What was the
point? This failure could severely undermine the public trust required to secure support for the future
tax measures the City hopes to pursue.

2. The "Engagement" Illusion

The draft ordinance as written reflects absolutely no substantive input from the public on the actual
regulations. While the "Engagement Summary" provided by staff claims that community insights
"directly informed the... Historic Preservation Ordinance update", this is a misrepresentation.

From: Gene Alston 
  
Sent: Monday, June 2, 2025 4:49 PM 
To: Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki <danielleforsanmateo@gmail.com>; Adam 
Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash 
<ldiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Prasanna Rasiah 
<prasiah@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirco-Godycki 
; Zachary Dahl 
<zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Community supported historic preservation 
 
Dear Mayor Newson and Councilmembers, 
 
I urge you to follow through on the previous commitment to include a clear homeowner consent requirement (above 80% 
affirmative consent) in any update to the Historic Preservation Ordinance, especially for creating historic districts. 
 
As your own staff report shows, cities like Glendale and South Pasadena require homeowner support before designating 
districts. San Mateo should do the same.  
 
Requiring consent is not a legal mandate, but it is good policy.  A consent-based process ensures that historic 
preservation is achieved through collaboration, not coercion. 
 
Without consent, as the report also clearly outlines, residents could face costly permit hurdles, CEQA reviews, or resale 
complications, without ever agreeing to be part of a historic district. 
 
Preservation should be a partnership, not an imposition. Please protect property rights and require homeowner consent 
for any future historic district designations. 
 
Thank you for your leadership on this issue, 
Gene Alston 
Baywood Homeowner

San Mateo Historic Ordinance Draft (Code Chapter 27.66)

From Jay Kuhre

Date Thu 1/8/2026 6:47 PM

To Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash
<Idiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez <nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org >; Danielle Cwirko-
Godycki <Danielleforsanmateo@gmail.com>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Zachary Dahl
<zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org >

Dear Mayor and City Council Members,

The San Mateo Historic Ordinance Draft does a fine job of listing the enhancements that historic
preservation offers, but it completely ignores the challenges that come with it. It would be far better
to acknowledge those challenges and deal with them, than to mislead directly and/or by omission. To
preserve a historical resource is difficult, time consuming and costly! | know this to be fact because
| have done it both personally and professionally for decades. | do not suggest that you remove the
language that "sells" the "benefits" of historic preservation. | simply insist that the negative side of the
process be acknowledged as well. That can be handled simply by inserting the following two
sentences between the first and second sentence in the introductory paragraph of 27.66.010 "Purpose
and Intent".

It is recognised that to accomplish the goal of Preserving History, the process and the work involved is
restrictive and difficult. As such, additional time and money is required to complete preservation

projects.

Regarding the balance of the Ordinance Draft, my input is simple:

27.66.050 (b) XX % my suggestion = 90% further on XX % = my suggestion = 10%
27.66.070 (1) (c) XX % = my suggestion = 85%

27.66.070 (4) XX % = my suggestion = 10%

Increase the time notifications in both subsections of 27.66.070 above

If you choose not to recognize the negative aspects of Historic Recognition in writing, the rhetoric
regarding the benefits must be eliminated from the Ordinance. Please don't perpetuate the "feel
good" misconception being sold that Historic Recognition is simply. an honor with benign
requirements. That is SIMPLY NOT TRUE! | presented a TED Talk style lecture to the OHP & SHRC on
12/11/2025. It documents actual facts | have encountered regarding work difficulties, time
consumption, costs and more. | would be happy to repeat that presentation for you.

Please let me know if any of this is not clear,

Jay Kuhre

Preserve Our Glazenwood Historic District:

From Jennifer Schellenberg i nnn

Dear Council members

We are property owners at MM living in the historic Glazenwood district. We wanted to email
to voice our strong support for maintaining the historical district designation that retains what remains
of the neighborhood archetectual cohesiveness. We have remodeled our 1924 spanish style home
several times over our 22 year ownership, and not once has the historical requirement been an issue. |
believe the prior owner was required to return the exterior to its historical look and that is why we
purchased the property because it looked old and had continuity with the neighboring houses.

| want to voice my opposition to changing the designation in Glazenwood and ask that the council
separate our status and our neighborhood from issues effecting adjacent neighborhoods like Baywood.

Sincerely,
Jennifer and Marc Schellenberg

From: Jeremy Levine 
  
Sent: Monday, June 2, 2025 4:28 PM 
To: Rob Newsom <rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>; Nicole Fernandez 
<nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Lisa Diaz Nash <ldiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org>; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki 
<dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org>; City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Cc: Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Prasanna Rasiah <prasiah@cityofsanmateo.org>; Zachary Dahl 
<zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Public Comment on Special Meeting Agenda Item 1: Historic District 
 
Good evening city council members. Thank you to staff and the council for your work navigating the 
complexities of historic preservation.  
 
In order to ensure historic preservation harmonizes with the housing needs of current and future San 
Mateo residents, HLC supports the following components of any historic ordinance:  
x 
Affirmative consent by the owner for any property to be designated history, and supermajority 
(2/3) affirmative consent of all property owners included within a proposed district, with the costs 
for officially acquiring such consent to be born by the applicants seeking to create a historic 
resource. 
x 
Allow ADUs and duplexes to be built ministerially within historic districts 
x 
Embed decision-making authority in the planning commission rather than creating an 
independent commission 
x 
Clarify the criteria for listing a historic resource to be more specific and tailored to the needs of 
San Mateo than the broad, easily abused California Register of Historic Resources Criteria. HLC 
can provide suggestions for language as the process evolves.  
The council should set a high bar for the standards of historic preservation to ensure San Mateo can 
honor its heritage without smothering its future. As the staff report notes, "CEQA restrictions apply 
whether a resource is determined to be eligible for or actually listed on the CRHR. These CEQA 
restrictions require environmental review to determine whether a proposed development project may 
have a “substantial adverse change” in the significance of the resource." Historic preservation will only 
843 of 1110

--- page 844 ---
2
maintain its integrity so long as it clearly emphasizes making history accessible for all, not making 
housing more difficult to build.  
 
Thank you for your consideration, 
Jeremy

Jeremy Levine (he • him) 
Policy Manager 
Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County 
2905 El Camino Real 
San Mateo, CA 94403

Historic Preservation Ordinance Update - Glazenwood

From Joann Ares a

Date Fri 1/9/2026 3:14 PM

To Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org >; City Council
(San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org >;

Cc Joann Ames

Dear Council members -

As the City updates its Historic Preservation Ordinance, I respectfully ask that
Glazenwood’s long-standing treatment in Council-adopted plans and maps not be
changed without clear City Council direction and public consideration. I am not
asking for a new designation or expanded protections, but for continuity and
transparency in how existing policy is carried forward.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Sincerely -

Joann Ames

Jack Bernstein
Glazenwood Residents

1/13/26, 11:43 AM Inbox - Zachary Dahl - Outlook

oa Outlook

RE: Requesting your assistance in clarifying the National Register eligibility of Glazenwood, San
Mateo

From Polanco, Julianne@Parks <Julianne.Polanco@parks.ca.gov>
Date Mon 11/17/2025 11:45 AM

To Andrew Ryan

Cc Brown, Jody L@Parks <Jody.L.Brown@parks.ca.gov>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org>; Frank
Elliott achary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org>; Pries, Shannon@Parks
<Shannon.Pries@ parks.ca.gov>; Crain, Amy@Parks <Amy.Crain@parks.ca.gov>

Mr. Ryan,
Thank you for your patience.

In response to your inquiry below, local governments may conduct historical resources surveys
and determine how best to use the information without the involvement of the State Office of
Historic Preservation (OHP). OHP has no jurisdiction over local government efforts.

Our records show that the Glazenwood Historic District (Glazenwood), City of San Mateo, was
surveyed in 1989 by the City of San Mateo (City). At that time, the City indicated that
Glazenwood appeared to be eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. The survey
that included Glazenwood was provided to OHP, the information entered into OHP’s database,
coded as 3S: Appears to be eligible for NR (National Register) through survey evaluation.
Because a property appears in the BERD does not mean it is listed on or is determined eligible
for either the California Register of Historic Resources (Cal Register) or the National Register of
Historic Places (National Register). To date, OHP has no records that Glazenwood has been
formally determined eligible for or listed in the Cal Register or National Register.

See this weblink for the Built Environment Resources Directory (BERD). One can scroll down
to find San Mateo County, click on it, and the listing of records for the county will appear.
Glazenwood is on or around line 1655. https://ohp.parks.ca.gov/?page_id=30338

To answer your second question, how the City conducts its policies, guidelines, ordinances, and
uses information is not a matter for which OHP has jurisdiction.

Sincerely,

Julianne Polanco

Julianne Polanco

State Historic Preservation Officer
California Office of Historic Preservation
1725 23rd Street, Suite 100
Sacramento, CA 95816

916-445-7000

www.ohp.parks.ca.gov

JulieAnn Murphy

From: Laurie Hietter

Sent: Wednesday, July 30, 2025 1:43 PM

To: Kassandra Gale; Gillian Balk; JulieAnn Murphy

Ce Zachary Dahl; Lisa Vande Voorde; Laurie Watanuki; Tom Lease; Maxine Terner
Subject: [EXT] Feedback and Suggestions from the Recent Historic Preservation Workshop
Attachments: Points for Focus Group.docx; SMHA Important people, places and events in SM.docx

Dear Kassandra, Gillian, and JulieAnn, and Zach

hank you for hosting an insightful and engaging workshop on historic preservation last night. Your
clear explanation of the movement's origins, the rationale for preservation, and the details of
regulations and design review processes was incredibly valuable. The real-world examples o
successful historic preservation in residential neighborhoods were particularly compelling and helped
illustrate the practical benefits for our community. This information is essential for empowering San
Mateo residents to make informed decisions about our shared heritage.
| have a few suggestions to further enhance future workshops. First, regarding the California
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), | recommend clarifying its application to historic preservation
projects. There’s a common perception, included in the LRT website’s FAQs, that renovations in
Mateo most home renovations qualify for Categorical Exemptions under CEQA. For example, in San
Mateo, even major renovations and significant additions in the Glazenwood Historic District have
been processed under these exemptions. Providing specific examples or data on typical timelines
and costs could help dispel misconceptions and reassure homeowners.
The breakout sessions were a creative way to foster discussion, though some logistical challenges
arose. In my group (Group 1), the post-it notes and text boxes were difficult to read due to their small
scale, making it hard to fully participate in the activity. The moderator’s assistance in recording our
deas was greatly appreciated. To ensure broader input, I’ve attached a list of people, places, and
events significant to San Mateo’s history that | couldn’t fully share during the session due to these
constraints.
I'd also like to inquire about the outcomes of the focus group discussions. Will you be sharing a
summary of the stakeholder groups identified and their comments? I’m particularly interested in
nderstanding the range of perspectives expressed. Additionally, I’ve attached a written response to
the topics we discussed, as the focus group time felt too brief to cover everything thoroughly.
| was disheartened to hear some attendees, particularly those opposed to historic preservation
reiterate their objections despite the workshop’s comprehensive presentations and the focus group
discussions. While | respect their perspectives, it is important to recognize that they do not represent
the entirety of San Mateo’s community. I’ve heard from residents who feel intimidated by the vocal
opposition and are hesitant to participate in these discussions. Additionally, some communi
members, including myself, were unable to attend sessions due to travel or scheduling conflicts. We
appreciate that the meetings are available on the City's website.
Finally, I'd like to emphasize that historic preservation and housing goals can coexist. Adaptive reuse!
projects, such as the St. Matthews Hotel and the Belmont building, demonstrate how preservation
can support increased housing while maintaining community character. Highlighting more local

1

850 of 1110


--- page 851 ---
success stories like these could help bridge the gap between differing viewpoints.

hank you again for your excellent presentations and commitment to fostering dialogue. Please let
e know if | can assist in any way, such as connecting you with other community members who
upport preservation efforts. | look forward to hearing about the next steps and the focus group

San Mateo Heritage Allianc
www.smheritage.or

Protecting Our Past for a Better Future

POINTS FOR HISTORIC POLICY UPDATE FOCUS GROUP
WHAT POLICIES ARE WORKING?
1.
10 years ago there was much more City focus on maintaining neighborhood character in 
remodeling. 
2.
Single-family design guidelines were more like standards and encouraged maintaining 
character of nearby architecture. 
3.
The updates to the City’s website are a big improvement. 
4.
The ordinance’s designation of contributors in historic districts is effective in protecting the 
Downtown.
5.
The Downtown storefront maintenance program was effective in improving the downtown
storefronts. This was done through Community Development in the past.   
6.
Duplex Design Guidelines and Small Multi-Family Design Guidelines exist, which should be 
followed to maintain character of nearby architecture.  
7.
The new Mi Rancho Market building on B Street took the art-deco style architecture and signage 
design from the original Blu-White Laundry building. We should do more of that in the 
Downtown.  
SUGGESTED IMPROVEMENTS
1.
Expand ordinance to address the Yoshiko Yamanouchi Historic District and historic districts 
throughout the City. 
2.
Identify a clear process for how properties in an out of a historic district are treated.  
3.
Provide education to the Building Department and Community Development staff to ensure 
appropriate procedures are followed for building and other permits.
4.
Provide Building and CDD staff with a checklist for historic preservation. 
5.
Designate an educated staff person to address historic preservation.  
6.
Identify measures and procedures that will preserve neighborhood architecture if not in a 
historic district. 
7.
Conduct a survey to identify important resources—update the 1989 Historic Building Survey. 
8.
Identify protection measures for properties 100 years old or older. 
9.
Identify what would make a heritage home in San Mateo. 
10. Prohibit horizontal fences in pre-war era neighborhoods. 
11. Develop a sign ordinance for the Downtown Historic District for more consistency and 
compatibility with the architecture. Examples might include: 
a.
Prohibit plastic signs
b.
Use traditional materials and fabrication (that would have been available in the 1920s 
and ‘30s). 
12. Define maintenance to include repainting peeling paint, fading signs, repairing cracks, cleaning 
sidewalks.  
852 of 1110

--- page 853 ---
2 
13. Ensure the updated ordinance recognizes and includes contributors as part of existing and 
future historic districts as historic resources, and that the ordinance applies to them as well as 
individually-eligible properties. 
14. Include provisions for how historic properties in existing and future historic districts are 
maintained, altered, and/or developed. These provisions should apply to contributors as well. 
15. Provide guidelines to ensure new developments adjacent to historic districts be compatible in 
terms of architecture and scale. 
16. The new ADU’s and detached garages with Single Family homes should match the architectural 
style, color, materials of the primary home.  
17. Consider context and require new development projects to blend in better architecturally with 
the older residential neighborhoods and the Historic Downtown. 
18. The Downtown could use a maintenance program to assist some owners with their storefronts.  
WHAT COULD BE MORE EQUITABLE OR EFFECTIVE IN HISTORIC PRESERVATION? 
1. Update the Mills Act process to reflect current rules and don’t charge exorbitant fees. 
2. Publicize the Mills Act program to assist homeowners in older neighborhoods to rehabilitate 
and maintain their homes.  
3. Educate the equity priority communities about the benefits of historic preservation and 
available grant funding. 
4. Assist and encourage homeowners in equity priority neighborhoods to preserve and maintain 
their properties. 
5. Educate and coordinate with the Downtown library’s community liaisons (African-American, 
Iranian American, Japanese American, Hispanic, etc.) 
6. Use qualified individuals instead of the Public Relations Commission for oversight. 
7. Make the process as clear and transparent as possible. 
HOW PRESERVATIONI MIGHT BETTER REFLECT THE FULL HISTORY AND IDENTITY OF 
THE CITY 
1. More plaques and signs to reflect the Japanese-American laundries (Blu-White, etc.), African-
American diner (Noah’s), etc. The Ching Lee Laundry display is a good example—would love 
to see more. 
2. Install a sign to welcome people to the Downtown Historic District, markers on the district 
street signs, etc. 
3. The new Mi Rancho Market building on B Street took the art-deco style architecture and signage 
design from the original Blu-White Laundry building.  We should do more of that in the 
Downtown.  
4. Ensure new buildings in the Downtown reflect the Objective Design Standards and are 
compatible with nearby architecture.  
5. Consider the historic nature of the shopping districts in San Mateo and protect their historic 
nature—25th Avenue, 37th Avenue, 42nd Avenue, etc. 
PRIORTIES FOR PROTECTION 
1. Protect the few remaining buildings built before 1900 (or pre-war) 
2. Historic Downtown 
3. Hillsdale train station building 
853 of 1110

--- page 854 ---
3 
4. Japanese gardens (in Central Park and private homes) 
5. Bank of America building and mural at 3rd and El Camino 
6. Redevelopment of the Masonic Building on Ellsworth to housing 
KEY POINTS TO INCLUDE IN ORDINANCE—INITIAL IDEAS 
1. Compatibility of architecture and with surroundings 
2. Contributors must continue to be defined as historic resources 
3. Ongoing education of City staff and community 
4. Consider requiring compatible signage and materials (e.g., traditional materials rather than 
plastic) for new signs in the Downtown Historic District (see other historic downtown sign 
ordinances) 
5. Maintenance must include maintaining materials of facades (no peeling paint, repair cracks) 
6. Consent should be consistent with other City practices (majority) 
7. Discourage removal of historic elements from buildings 
8. Encourage using high-quality materials (e.g., not architectural foam, foam trim, or foam 
molding that degrades quickly) 
9. Ensure members of the reviewing authority have appropriate backgrounds and include at least 
one architectural historian

Music 
Pacific Recording Studios, also known as Pacific Recorders, located at 1737 S El Camino Real. 
x
Founded: The studio was founded in 1968 by Paul Curcio, co-founder of the band the Mojo
Men.
x
Significant location in the development of the San Francisco sound, hosting recordings by many
notable artists, including:
o
Santana
o
The Grateful Dead
o
The Doobie Brothers
o
Moby Grape
o
Taj Mahal
o
Santana.
The Grateful Dead's Aoxomoxoa LP and Santana's debut album were both recorded here. 
Technological Innovation 
Pacific Recording  was the first 16-track studio in the Bay Area, utilizing an Ampex MM-1000 16-track 
recorder. The chief engineer, Ron Wickersham, also designed and built the mixing console for the 
studio. 
San Mateo County Fairgrounds 
Many famous bands played at the San Mateo County Fairgrounds 
IMPORTANT EVENTS IN SAN MATEO’S HISTORY 
x
Juan Bautista DeAnza Expedition camp on San Mateo Creek in 1776 with Franciscan priests
x
In 1793, Franciscan friars from nearby Mission San Francisco de Asís (also known as Mission
Dolores) established an outpost at San Mateo Creek to support the mission's activities,
including livestock herding and food production. The outpost was called the mission hospice
x
Pony Express station
x
Rail station in first peninsula railroad from San Mateo to San Jose
x
San Mateo becomes a city in 1894
x
Alvinza Hayward estate sold—Hayward Park developed
x
Peninsula Hotel burned down—Glazenwood developed

From: Laura W 
  
Sent: Saturday, October 4, 2025 5:20 PM 
To: Time Capsule <Historicpolicy@ciyofsanmateo.org> 
Cc: 
 Laurie Hietter 
>; Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org> 
Subject: Yoshiko Yamanouchi House Historic District & Imperial Laundry 
 
To the Rincon Consultants. 
 
I would like to submit these photographs to the time capsule.  
  
Photos 1 & 2 - Imperial Laundry - on 1st and B Street  
 
In 1908, Tetsuo Yamanouchi opened the Imperial Laundry at 218 - 1st Avenue San Mateo.  The space was rented 
from the Wisnoms a pioneer San Mateo family.  In addition to the family of five children, the laundry also employed 
as many as seven other people.  The Yamanouchi home was at the corner of 1st Ave and Ellsworth Street.  Tetsuo 
Yamanouchi had been educated and trained as a Zen Buddhist priest in Japan, and he became the spiritual leader 
in 1909.  The living room of the home was the beginning of the San Mateo Buddhist Church.  His wife Yoshiko 
organized the Buddhist Sunday School, the Buddhist Women’s Association, and the Young Buddhist 
Association.  When the small congregation outgrew the living room, an adjacent storefront on 1st Avenue was 
rented for $40 a month by Tetsuo Yamanouchi.  When Tetsuo Yamanouchi died in 1936, his wife Yoshiko took over 
the laundry business and successfully operated it until the family returned to San Mateo at the end of World War 
II.  It was unusual in those times for the wife to take over a business, but Yoshiko was determined the business 
would go on.   
 
In 1940, the business was moved from its original location to a new facility at 46 B Street.   
 
During World War II, the family was incarcerated in Topaz, Utah.  The business was leased by friends during the 
war.  Hasuko and Masashi Max Watanuki, the daughter and son-in-law took over the management of the business 
after the war and the name of the business was changed to Blu-White Laundry because the connotation of the 
word “imperial” may have been perceived as a negative. The name likely came from the appearance of the building 
which was white with blue trim.  The new building was designed by William H Rowe and built by Hugh H. 
MacDonald.  
 
858 of 1110

--- page 859 ---
2
Yoshiko Yamanouchi remained active in the San Mateo Buddhist Temple and in her Buddhist and Civic life.  
  
 
Photo 3 - Yoshiko Yamanouchi House - Historic District - Katsura Walk-way 
 
In December 2023, the Yoshiko Yamanouchi House at 1007 East 5th Avenue San Mateo was listed on the National 
Register of Historic Places at the local level of significance under Criteria A & B in the areas of Ethnic 
Heritage/Asian/Japanese and social History/Woman’s History for its association with a prominent member the 
Issei generation in San Mateo’s Japanese American Community, from early 20th century to today.   
 
The picture below is the Yoshiko Yamanouchi House Historic District which has nine contributors:   The Entrance 
Gate, House, Katsura Building, Katsura Garden, Katsura Walk-way, Storage Room and Pump House, Patio and 
Swimming Pool, West Side Garden. The Japanese style Hill and Pond and Garden was completed in 1958 by 
landscape designer Nagao Sakurai.  The second structure and garden on this site was completed in 1968, by 
architect and designer Kodo Matsubara, scholar of Japanese sukiya-styled architecture.  The Yoshiko Yamanouchi 
House is one of only three properties nationwide spotlighted in the recent National Register publication “Best 
Practices Review.”    
 
An added note:   After the completion of the Japanese Hill and Pond Garden at the Yoshiko Yamanouchi House, 
Nagao Sakurai assisted the Japanese gardeners who built the Japanese Tea Garden in Central Park in San Mateo.   
 
 
Laurie Watanuki 
 
San Mateo, CA

From: Lisa Vande Voorde

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>; Clerk <clerk@cityofsanmateo.org>;

<nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org>; Adam Loraine <aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org>

Subject: 6/2/25 CITY COUNCIL STUDY SESSION: Historic Policy Update Effort & Preservation
Ordinance

Dear Honorable Mayor and City Council Members,

| want to start by saying how pleased | am that the City is going forward with updating the
Historic Preservation Ordinance. It has been a long-time coming, and already positive
strides have been made. The Historic Policy Update Effort on the City website is a great
start in terms of educating the public. The nascent FAQ section already dispels
misinformation about historic resources being spread by Less Red Tape, and the Digital
Time Capsule is a great way to both make the community more aware of the history of San
Mateo, and participate in sharing its historic past through personal photos and memories of
what makes San Mateo so special.

That said, I'd like to provide my input on the questions you are being asked to address at
tonight's Study Session, and hope that my points will be considered as you make your
decision:

Should staff proceed with drafting updates to the Historic Preservation Ordinance to identify
the enabling authority, establish a reviewing body (the Planning Commission), provide
procedures for designation or resources, recognize the economic effects of designation,
describe appeals and enforcement provisions and include a set of concise definitions?

| do NOT agree with establishing the Planning Commission as the review body. The
Planning Commission has a very poor record when it comes to protecting historic resources
in our City. Not only that, several Planning Commissioners have gone on record in public
comment at City Council meetings, in LTEs and comments in the San Mateo Daily Journal,
and on social media platforms including Nextdoor, Facebook, Instagram, and X expressing
their views against historic preservation. At least three of the five members of the current
Planning Commission consider themselves YIMBYs — an organization known to advocate
against historic preservation. Given this track record, this critically important task should go
to another reviewing body, such as a commission or small committee consisting of
members with expertise in historic preservation, planning, and historic consulting. While it
may work in other cities, designating the already biased Planning Commission of OUR city

863 of 1110


--- page 864 ---
is like putting the fox in charge of the henhouse.
 
Should the criteria for a local historic designation conform to the California Register of
Historical Resources (CRHR) requirements, or should additional criteria be included to
acknowledge the city’s unique history and development?
 
I would like to see a combination of BOTH the CRHC requirements, AND additional
criteria.  This would better serve our close to 200 year-old history and our close to 100-
year old homes that contribute so much to the character and feel of our neighborhoods.
 
Should the Historic Preservation Ordinance include a mechanism to designate historic
districts locally? If yes, should owner support be required and at what level?
 
Yes, the ordinance should include a mechanism to designate local historic districts. 
However, I do NOT support owner consent.  It is the JOB and the RESPONSIBILITY of
the CITY to identify, preserve, and protect historic resources.  It is NOT the responsibility of
the PROPERTY OWNER.  Furthermore, cities have long held the power to regulate land
use under zoning laws and preservation ordinances. Requiring homeowner consent could
set a precedent that weakens local authority over planning and land-use decisions.
 
What form of owner consent should be included in the Historic Preservation Ordinance –
affirmative/expressed consent, owner veto, or protest provisions? And, should the owner
consent requirement apply to all local designations or just to historic districts?
 
As stated above, NO form of owner consent should be included in the Historic
Preservation Ordinance.  It is the duty of our City to identify, protect, and preserve our
historic resources. That duty applies to a PROPERTY, not the OWNER of the property. 
Owners change hands, the property and its historic value to the community lasts forever. 
Local designations, historic districts and designation of individual historic structures protect
architecture and cultural history that belong to the broader community, not just individual
homeowners.
 
Thank you for the opportunity to provide public input, and I hope you find it valuable as you
determine the direction you are asked to take on behalf of not just our City, but for our
historic resources that cannot speak for themselves.
 
Sincerely yours,
 
Lisa Vande Voorde
San Mateo Resident

Re: Historic Policy Update Effort Professional Services
Agenda Item No. 10.
Hearing Date: January 21, 2025.

Dear Hon. Members of the City Council:

Our office represents Less Red Tape (“LRT”), a coalition of San Mateo
homeowners and residents who object to the establishment of historic districts without homeowner
consent. In prior correspondence LRT objected to the scope of work contained in the Historic
Policy Update Request for Proposals (“RFP”), and urged the City to first prepare an updated
Historic Preservation Ordinance, and thereafter proceed with the hiring of historic preservation
consultants to assist with preparing historic context statements that are consistent with the scope
of the updated ordinance.

The intent of this request was to allow the City Council to first establish the scope
of an ordinance (i.e., whether consent is required, whether single-family districts are included,
etc.), and then prepare historic context statements that match that scope. LRT still believes this is
the right path and can be accomplished without an outside historical consultant.

If council wishes to move forward on the agenda item and cannot de-couple the
ordinance portion, then LRT urges the City Council to proceed with Option 1 — subject to certain
conditions. The recommended conditions are as follows:

(1) Consent Based Ordinance: Any ordinance prepared or proposed in connection with
this RFP shall require property owner consent prior to the City surveying, designating,
or listing a property as a resource on any local register, survey or list.

(2) Single-Family Residential Exclusion: No Historic Context Statements or surveys
shall be performed, proposed or considered as part of this Historic Policy Update for

A Limited Liability Law Partnership Including Corporations / Los Angeles ® San Francisco ¢ Orange County
72935831v1
865 of 1110


--- page 866 ---
January 20, 2025
Page 2

any potential district that includes single-family residential homes, unless affirmative
consent is first obtained from the homeowners.

(3) Consent Threshold and Process: For potential single family residential districts,
consent will be determined in a similar manner as the City’s Residential Parking Permit
Program: a survey will be mailed to all households in the area, and at least 50% of
households in the proposed program area must respond to the survey. Of those
responding, at least 67% must support the proposed program.

We believe these conditions are both reasonable and narrowly tailored to allow for
the City to accomplish its goal of updating its historic preservation program, while also balancing
the property rights of City residents. To assist the City Council with proceeding in this manner,
we have attached a revised resolution labeled as Option 4. Option 4 simply directs staff to proceed
with the Historic Policy Update subject to these requested terms.

Thank you in advance for your consideration, and we look forward to presenting
our proposal in greater detail at tomorrow’s hearing.

CITY OF SAN MATEO 
RESOLUTION NO. (2025) 
APPROPRIATING FUNDS FOR TASKS AND SUPPORT RELATED TO A CITYWIDE 
HISTORIC POLICY UPDATE 
WHEREAS, on June 17, 2024, the City Council directed staff to prioritize an update of the City's 
historic policies and procedures, including an update of the City's Historic Preservation Ordinance 
and preparation of a Citywide Historic Context Statement; and 
WHEREAS, in August 2024, a Request for Proposals (RFP) for historic policy support services 
from qualified historic professionals was issued; and 
WHEREAS, in October 2024, following a review of proposals and follow-up interviews, staff 
identified the proposal from Rincon Consultants, Inc. as being the best suited to support the City's 
historic policy update effort; and 
WHEREAS, the proposal from Rincon Consultants, Inc. for historic policy support services, 
including community outreach and engagement, is for an amount of $285,843; and 
WHEREAS, a 15% contingency, in the amount of $42,900, is included in the event that additional 
services are required during the course of the project; and 
WHEREAS, the City Council requires that any Historic Preservation Ordinance prepared or 
proposed in connection with these services be subject to certain specified provisions. 
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, 
HEREBY RESOLVES THAT 
1. 
This fund appropriation is not a project of CEQA because it is an organizational or 
administrative activity that will not impact the environment (CEQA Guidelines 
§ 15378(b)(5). 
2. 
The appropriation of $328,743 from the Advance Planning Fund (Fund 225) to the 
Historic Policy Update project is approved.  
3. 
Any Historic Preservation Ordinance prepare in connection with these services or the 
Historic Policy Update project shall include the following: 
(a) The City shall only survey, designate, or list a resource on a local register, survey, 
or list, with property owner consent or upon the application of the property owner.  
(b) No Historic Context Statements or surveys shall be performed or proposed for any 
proposed district composed of primarily Single-Family Residential homes, unless 
affirmative consent is first obtained from the homeowners. 
(c) For potential single family residential districts, consent will be determined in a 
similar manner as the City’s Residential Parking Permit Program: a survey will be 
mailed to all households in the area, and at least 50% of households in the proposed 
district area must respond to the survey. Of those responding, at least 67% must 
support the proposed district.

August 25, 2025

Prasanna W. Rasiah
City Attorney

330 West 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403

Re: |LRT's Requests and Guidance on Historic Preservation Ordinance.
Dear Mr. Rasiah:

I submit this letter on behalf of Less Red Tape (LRT), a coalition of homeowners,
residents, and concerned citizens, to provide comments on the City’s efforts to prepare a new
historic preservation ordinance. As the City Council has specifically directed, any historic
designation enforced by the City of San Mateo should include explicit consent from the property
owner. We want to underscore that this consent requirement must be understood to apply to both
the City’s new historic designation process and the City's processing of applications under the
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). If it is not, project opponents can too easily
interrupt even simple remodeling projects by asserting that the residence is a historic resource
under CEQA, either individually or as part of an alleged district - even if the home was never
actually designated as a historic resource by any agency. In other words, due to the loose standards
provided under CEQA, anything is potentially historic, irrespective of what rules are set forth in
the City's new ordinance. Accordingly, Less Red Tape makes the following requests to assure the
City of San Mateo protects a homeowner's right to consent to historic designations and to not have
"historic preservation" thrust on homeowners by neighborhood advocates:

A. Consent under the City Ordinance for Landmarking or Register Listing.

In agreeing to proceed with this effort to update the City's historic preservation
ordinance, the City Council made it clear that a guiding principle must be property owner consent
for historic designations and districts. Given this guidance, this simple requirement should be
clearly stated in the ordinance to prevent future disputes and ensure that property owners retain
control over historic nominations. For district designations, the new ordinance should require an
affirmative written consent of no less than a super-majority of the title-record property owners
within the district. Other cities across the state have implemented similar requirements, and there
is no reason why San Mateo could not do the same.

B. Closing the CEQA “Backdoor” for Historic Designation.

Irrespective of what is ultimately included in the new City ordinance, CEQA
provides a separate and equally powerful avenue for preservation advocates to argue that a project

A Limited Liability Law Partnership Including Corporations / Los Angeles ® San Francisco ¢ Orange County

73587065v1 868 of 1110


--- page 869 ---
August 25, 2025
Page 2

impacts an alleged historic resource. Even if a particular property has never even been considered
for listing on a historic register, CEQA can still be used to thrust historic preservation on a
homeowner at the last minute. This is not a theoretical idea, late-hit historic claims under CEQA
occur regularly across the state, causing unnecessary delays, increased costs, and needless
litigation risks. In fact, it's what triggered the historic review for 415 Fairfax Avenue, whereby the
Community Development Department required the homeowner to prepare a costly historic
evaluation simply to prove that the home proposed for demolition was not a historic resource.

The City should and can resolve this situation. Without any clear justification, the
City has been treating its Single Family Design Review (SFDDR) as a discretionary action that
requires CEQA review, when it really is not. A quick review of the SMMC Sec. 27.08.032
illustrates that, while the City does have the ability to review and consider projects that trigger
design review, it does not have authority to condition such approvals and is in fact mandated to
approve them if certain narrow findings can be made. Moreover, the scope of the design review
authority does not address environmental impacts, and thus, the review is properly considered
ministerial for the purposes of CEQA notwithstanding the fact that the agency does retain some
discretionary authority. In McCorkle Eastside Neighborhood Group v. City of St. Helena (2018)
31 Cal._App.5th 80, 242 Cal.Rptr.3d 379, for example, the agency's power to conduct an aesthetic
design review did not make a project discretionary because the agency “lack[ed] ... any discretion
to address environmental effects.” (/d. at p. 94.)

In summary, we request that the City’s historic preservation ordinance be drafted
with the following key provisions:

(i) Property owner consent should be explicitly required for all designations
under the ordinance, with a supermajority vote required for districts.

(ii) Clarify that SFDDR is not subject to CEQA, and that no historic evaluations
are required for any property unless it is officially listed in the local register.

By adopting these provisions, the City could ensure that historic preservation
remains a voluntary, consent—driven process while reducing unnecessary delays, costs, and
litigation risks. If there are legal or technical reasons why these provisions cannot be adopted,
could you please clarify why so that Council and the public can fully understand the relevant
considerations? Thank you in advance for your consideration and assistance.

Jeffer Mangels Butler & Mitchell LLP

Alex, 
 
FYI, here are some of my questions for tonight, feel free to share with staff so that they have advanced 
notice. Thanks!! 
 
 
Historic Preservation Ordinance: 
 
1.) For Neighborhoods that want to form a Historic District- why did we not have a Public Meeting to 
determine what consent should look like: ex: a.) 50%+1 vote of voters in the proposed district, b.) at least 
50%+ 1 of the property owners in the proposed district, c.) 66.7 (2/3rds of the voters) in the proposed 
district. 
 
Staff will be conducting community outreach to help guide the final policy decision on this, but given the 
strong opinions on this topic, we wanted to get parameters from the Council on both consent and district 
designation framework first: 
• 
Should the Historic Preservation Ordinance include a mechanism to designate historic districts 
locally? If yes, should owner support be required and at what level?  
• 
What form of owner consent should be included in the Historic Preservation Ordinance – 
affirmative/expressed consent, owner veto, or protest provisions? And, should the owner consent 
requirement apply to all local designations or just to historic districts? 
Based on Council’s direction on these two questions, we will be engaging the community to help inform 
the recommendation that we bring forward in the updated ordinance.  
 
2.) Did Rincon study this request- because I don't see the results/suggestions of what different consent 
formulas could look like, but rather have suggested:  
• Affirmative/expressed consent requires the property owner’s clear agreement to designation.  
• Owner veto would not allow for designation to occur over the property owner’s objection.  
• Protest provisions would allow a city to designate a historic property over an owner’s objection, if a 
supermajority of the reviewing body votes to do so. (A supermajority- 4/5 votes- of the planning 
commission, or the newly formed historic resources commission, OR the City Council? Who is the 
reviewing body?) 
 
Consent within a historic preservation ordinance generally takes one of the three forms outlined in the AR, 
which is why we are seeking input from Council on which form of consent you want to include in the 
updated ordinance.  The second part of the question is related to how Council wants it to be applied – to all 
historic designations or just to historic district designations.  We are also seeking direction from Council on 
how to approach the local historic district designation process (3rd question) and this will help inform how 
we engage with the community and collect input that informs the recommendation that we bring forward in 
the updated ordinance.      
 
2.) What are some of the challenges to having the Historic Ordinance being so out of date? 
 
                As has been exemplified by the community dialogue about this issue over the past two years, there have 
been many challenges with the current state of our Historic Preservation Ordinance, including:  
• 
Lack of clarity about the City’s regulations and requirements, which has lead to public confusion and 
misinformation. 
• 
Lack of a clear process for how to review projects with an eligible historic resource and how to process 
local designation requests. 
875 of 1110

--- page 876 ---
3
• 
Inconsistency with state and federal requirements, which undermines the integrity and public 
perception of our local ordinance.  
 
3.) Mills Act Housing- has this been addressed? When have these homes last been visited? 
 
Here’s the update on the Mills Act contract administration that was shared in the City Council Weekly 
Update on 5/23/25: 
 
Mills Act Contract Administration Update 
There currently are two active Mills Act contracts within the City of San Mateo. The contracts are for the 
historic properties 37 E. Santa Inez Avenue and 70 De Sabla Road.  When these contracts were originally 
executed, they were filed by Staff at the time but not flagged for any follow-up or periodic reviews, which 
resulted in Planning Staff not tracking them over time. Once these Mills Act Agreements were pulled as part 
of community records requests and background research for the Historic Policy Update Effort, they were 
flagged by Staff for needing follow-up to bring them in current compliance.  
Letters sent by certified mail have gone out to both property owners requesting meetings with each to 
discuss contract terms, schedule inspections and verify maintenance activities/ requirements. Based on 
reviews of aerial photography and drive-by inspections, each property appears to be maintained and in 
good upkeep. These conditions will be verified once inspections of each property has been completed.   
It should also be noted that the City is seeing approximately $5,000 annually in reduced property tax 
revenue, not $52,000, as a result of these two Mills Act contracts.   
 
4.) Rather than assign more responsibilities to the Planning Commission, could the City form a Historic 
Resources or Historic Landmarks Commission for San Mateo? 
 
Yes, the City could establish a new commission (i.e. historic commission) to review historic projects and 
designation requests if that’s what the majority of Council wants.  However, given that there will likely not 
be many historic projects/designations that require the reviewing body’s input over time, this commission 
may not hold many meetings and creation of a new commission would require additional staff time to 
manage and support.  Also, the reviewing body could be established as an advisory body, which is how 
most of these bodies in other jurisdictions are established, which would allow for the Council to have the 
final say on designation applications. For these reasons, staff made the recommendation to assign these 
advisory responsibilities to the PC rather than establish a new commission.  We will provide more 
information about this as part of the presentation this evening and proceed however the Council wants us 
to with regard to the reviewing body that’s defined in the updated ordinance. 
 
5.) For this question- what is easier?: Should the criteria for a local historic designation conform to the 
California Register of Historical Resources (CRHR) requirements, or should additional criteria be 
included to acknowledge the city’s unique history and development?  IF additional criteria that are 
unique to the city's history & development- what does that look like? 
 
If Council wants us to further explore local designation criteria, we can definitely fold it into our community 
outreach and staff analysis, and bring back options for consideration in the draft ordinance update. This is 
something that would be informed by community input and the information in the Citywide Historic 
Context Statement, but we wanted to get direction from Council before spending any time on this work.   
 
876 of 1110

--- page 877 ---
4
6.) Historic Signage- there is old signage of Bailey's Carpets on the side of their old building, and on 
Talbot's Toyland. These signs will be lost as the buildings are eventually removed, could we require the 
developers to document these historic signs with pictures so that we don't lose the history if/when the 
building are removed? 
 
                Yes, the updated ordinance could definitely include requirements for how to 
evaluate/document/memorialize historic elements, such as signage, that are being proposed for removal as part 
of a new development. 
 
Transportation Questions: 
 
1.) Where is an overview of the Bicycle Master Plan that shows us the low conflict (bike routes- not 
removing lanes of traffic or parking), but high reward/yield. Alex (you) mentioned about 49% of the 
projects fall into this category, can we prioritize these? 
 
2.) SAFETY is paramount- are we looking at Ped/Bike accident data to evaluate the most urgent needed 
areas & how are we doing with identifying school zones that need more signage & enforcement? 
 
3.) Are we considering the impacts of Measure T's development in transit corridors & concentrated 
population growth against removing lanes of traffic & parking in these zones? 
 
4.) East to West transit, including applying pressure for the resumption of TransBay Express connecting 
Hayward BART to San Mateo CalTrain, and bike/ped overpass Hillsdale Blvd, and development of more 
Safety from Delaware to Bridgepoint (19th Ave corridor.) 
 
5.) Safe Ped Sidewalks and crossing. (Far more Ped accidents than Bike.) What are we doing to be sure 
that sidewalks are maintained and dangerous crossing are made safer? 
 
6.) Street light- LED conversion? Identifying areas that need better lighting? 
 
7.) We seem to have LOST bike & scooter share programs? Why is this? Any opportunity to bring them 
back? 
 
8.) Improved blue placard (Handicapped) parking for downtown, so that people can frequent the pedmall 
restaurants & shops. 
 
9.) More short term 15 min parking &/or food delivery parking in areas of restaurants- not just downtown, 
but ex: 25th Ave, & Bay Meadows 
 
10.) Parking Permits &/or utilization help for North Central- so that they have some relief! 
 
11.) Has the City reviewed/considered a dedicated Bike/Pedway, ex 10' wide (5' N & 5' S) protected bike 
lane that would connect all 3 train stations on the East side of the rail lines? In the space behind Bay 
Meadows businesses, behind the gravel parking lots across from the Event Center (they would give us 
permission), behind Public Storage, then make Pacific Ave 1-way, south to north only, and use the south 
bound lane as the Bike/Pedway. It would have very few East/West crossings, and would resemble the 
behaviors of the Netherlands, Germany & Scandinavia.

Mr. Rob Newsom, Mayor 
Mr. Prasanna Rasiah, City Attorney

San Mateo, California 94403 
 
Subject: Defamation of Heritage Alliance by the City of San Mateo 
 
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Attorney Rasiah: 
 
We, the Board of Directors of the San Mateo Heritage Alliance, are writing to you about the 
serious and defaming allegations made against Heritage Alliance on March 17, 2025, and the 
use of false information as a basis for voting to send a letter to the California State Historic 
Preservation Office (SHPO).  The City Council members repeatedly claimed SMHA reneged on 
a supposed “agreement.” We are asking for a retraction of your false statements, a retraction of 
the SHPO letter, and a public apology. 
 
During the City Council Special Meeting discussion of Agenda Item #5, “Letter to California 
Office of Historic Preservation – Approval”, Council Member Nicole Fernandez requested the 
City Council approve a letter to SHPO requesting an 18-month deferral of the review of the 
Baywood Historic District nomination. What follows are direct quotes and time stamps from the 
video recording.  
 
Council Member Danielle Cwirko-Godycki spoke in support of the letter, stating at 1:38:33, “The 
Heritage Alliance said they would not continue with submission of their application if the City 
moved forward with an expedited local process and updating our historic ordinance. 
Unfortunately, the San Mateo Heritage Alliance has gone back on their word, and submitted 
their application anyway”. That allegation is patently false; we never said this, therefore we 
never went back on our word.   
 
Council Member Loraine continued the false narrative, declaring at 1:46:34, “I am disappointed 
in the Heritage Alliance’s decision to continue this application because the idea of what came 
before Council was with that agreement in mind.”  The Heritage Alliance states again, for the 
record, there was NO such agreement ever made.  
 
Mayor Newsom goes on to say at 1:52:00, “I would have appreciated if the pause was at least 
held until we had another meeting.”  Respectfully, Mayor Newsom, Heritage Alliance slowed the 
nomination for eight months, but did not agree to stop all work or wait until the end of the City 
883 of 1110

--- page 884 ---
Mr. Rob Newsom 
 
 
Mr. Prasanna Rasaiah 
 
 
March 25, 2025 
 
 
Page 2 
 
process. There was never an agreement between the City of San Mateo and the Heritage 
Alliance to enter into any such pause.   
 
It is important to note that Heritage Alliance did choose to delay submitting the revised Baywood 
nomination to SHPO in May 2024 and to not pursue consideration by the State Historic 
Resources Commission in August 2024.  Heritage Alliance independently delayed the submittal 
as a demonstration of good faith for the benefit of our initial discussions for the City to prioritize 
the General Plan requirement to update the historic preservation ordinance and prepare a 
context study in 2024, rather than waiting another five years.   
 
The potential agreement was requested by the City as a way to calm down the rhetoric and 
consternation in the community. The discord is a direct result of the incorrect information LRT 
was spreading and continues to spread in the community. We had been working towards an 
agreement; however, Less Red Tape abruptly pulled out of negotiations, the process towards 
an agreement ground to a halt, and was never resumed. The effort to collaboratively forge an 
agreement between Heritage Alliance, the City, and LRT failed because the City, as well as 
LRT, did not agree to the actions required on their part to make the agreement viable, and a 
true agreement on a pre-defined pause, possible. Heritage Alliance requested that LRT stop 
spreading inaccurate information and cease their divisive campaign against the historic district 
and that the City provide accurate information to counteract LRT’s inaccurate information.  
 
While we voluntarily paused our nomination activity, Heritage Alliance repeatedly told the City 
Manager that our pause was not a long-term pause, we would not stop all work, and we would 
not wait until the end of the City process. It is not clear what the City Manager communicated to 
Less Red Tape and the Council members. It is clear, however, there was no specific 
communication to Council members as to the nature of the pause. Heritage Alliance also 
communicated to each member of the City Council what our pause consisted of on numerous 
occasions.  
 
Heritage Alliance asked City Manager Alex Khojikian (via email the day after the 3/17/25 City 
Council special meeting) to provide evidence of the agreement referenced throughout the 
Council meeting. Mr. Khojikian replied: 
  
”The only agreement I am aware of is the Rincon Agreement for Historic 
Preservation Services that the Council approved on January 21, 2025.”  
 
On that we agree. There was no agreement. Yet, Mr. Khojikian sat silent throughout the Special 
Meeting proceeding, never mentioning that the City did not have such an agreement with the 
Heritage Alliance. As confirmation, please see the attached email from the City Manager to 
Laurie Hietter. 
 
884 of 1110

--- page 885 ---
Mr. Rob Newsom

Mr. Prasanna Rasaiah
March 25, 2025

Page 3

Every Council member on the dais repeated the misinformation about the agreement to pause
the nomination process, giving it more credence when it shouldn’t have had any. This
misinformation was used as the basis to approve a motion for Mayor Newsom to send the letter
to SHPO.

The repeated false statements that Heritage Alliance reneged on an agreement defames the
Heritage Alliance and harms our reputation in the community. Heritage Alliance sees this as a
serious matter that needs to be addressed and rectified immediately. We ask that the City take
these actions:

1. Retract or rescind the letter sent to the California SHPO. Delaying the State’s decision
will only extend the false statements, discord, and animosity.

2. Retract the false statements made at the City Council Special Meeting alleging bad faith
by our organization. The City needs to make a public statement announcing that there
was not “an agreement to pause” the Baywood nomination by Heritage Alliance.

3. Mayor Newsom should issue an apology, both in public and in writing, to the Heritage
Alliance, its Board, and its membership for the false comments made that defame our
organization. The apology should be on behalf of the entire City Council and City staff.

Board President Laurie Hietter is available to you to provide any additional information or
documentation needed.

We look forward to your prompt response and action on this matter.

Sincerely,

Laurie Hietter, for

The San Mateo Heritage Alliance
Laurie Hietter, President

Lisa Vande Voorde, Secretary
Dianne Whitaker, Director

Laurie Watanuki, Director
Ruthmary Cradler, Director

January 16, 2025 
City Council
City of San Mateo 
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo, CA 94403 
SUBJECT: Support for Context Study and Historic Preservation Ordinance Update 
Dear Mayor Newsom and City Council Members:
The San Mateo Heritage Alliance strongly urges the approval of the proposed scope of work to prepare 
a context study and to update our historic preservation ordinance. The current ordinance, dating back 
to 1993, and the outdated 1989 Historic Building Survey are no longer sufficient to meet the demands of 
our growing city while preserving its rich historical legacy. 
The Importance of a Context Study
x
Economic and Efficient: A context study is far more cost-effective than a detailed survey of 
each historic property, providing a broad understanding of our city's historical assets at a 
fraction of the cost. 
x
Framework for Preservation: It will lay down the groundwork necessary for a citywide historic 
resources understanding, informing not just preservation but also future development.
x
Policy Informed by Study: The study will directly influence the updating of our preservation 
policies and ordinance, ensuring they reflect the current state and significance of our historical 
resources.
x
Strategic Planning: By understanding the type, quantity, and location of our historic resources, 
we can make informed decisions that balance growth with preservation. 
The Necessity to Update the Historic Preservation Ordinance
x
Cultural and Economic Benefits: As noted in our current ordinance, preserving our history is 
not just about nostalgia; it's crucial for enhancing our community’s economic, cultural, and 
aesthetic value. 
x
Criteria for Preservation: An updated ordinance will clearly define processes and criteria for 
designating and protecting local historic districts, something missing in our current framework. 
The current ordinance does not recognize or apply to the new Yoshiko Yamanouchi House 
Historic District or the pending Baywood Historic District.
x
Legal Clarity and Protection: It will provide a robust legal framework to recognize, protect, and 
manage historic resources, ensuring they are considered in development reviews. 
886 of 1110

--- page 887 ---
City Council
January 16, 2025
Page 2

The Urgency of Action
¢ Time is of the Essence: Over three decades have passed since significant action was taken on
historic preservation in San Mateo. Delaying further will only increase costs and risks losing
more of our heritage.

¢ Resource Allocation: With budgets potentially tightening, acting now is prudent to leverage
existing funds for maximum impact.

Balancing Historic Preservation with Housing Needs
¢ Compatibility with Modern Needs: Historic properties and districts can accommodate modern
housing solutions like ADUs, expansions, and remodels, proving that preservation does not
hinder but can complement urban development.

¢ Selective Designation: The criteria for historic designation are stringent, ensuring only truly
significant structures or neighborhoods are protected, thus not broadly impeding new housing
developments.

In conclusion, the time to act is now to safeguard San Mateo’s historical narrative while fostering
growth. Approving this scope of work will not only protect our past but also enrich our future. We
urge you to ignore the misinformation and support this initiative for the benefit of San Mateo's cultural
heritage and its residents.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,

Laurie Hietter
President
San Mateo Heritage Alliance

887 of 1110

From: Martin McTaggart

To: Zachary Dahl; Sung H. Kwon
Subject: FW: Historic Ordinance
Date: Monday, June 16, 2025 11:49:14 AM

From: Steve Lesley

Sent: Tuesday, June 10, 2025 8:36 AM

To: City Council (San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>
Subject: Historic Ordinance

City of San Mateo Historic Code

Dear City Council

| read that the City of San Mateo is considering a Residential Historic Ordinance.

As a 35 year resident of San Mateo and as an architect, | ask that you please consider the long
term consequences of historic designations to houses. In my opinion, a blanket historic label
of an area or neighborhood is the wrong approach. Once designated “Historic”, then any
remodeling or additions can become very complicated, expensive, or prohibited. There is
value to modernizing within the already strict zoning codes that can require designing within
the existing style of the house or neighborhood. | have experienced designing a standard 100
year old junkie non-descriptive looking house with other jurisdictions that was located within a
historic neighborhood that became a very expensive and lengthy process. At some point old
homes in disrepair need to be remodeled and updated.

| hope that this ordinance will be very careful in designating a house as “Historic”, and only be
used on an individual basis and not cover an entire neighborhood.

Steven Lesley

San Mateo, CA 94403

888 of 1110

1/13/26, 11:41 AM Historic Policy Effort - Zachary Dahl - Outlook

oa Outlook

Glazenwood historic district designation

From Takako Matsuyama RF essi—isSSSSY

Date Thu 1/1/2026 12:07 PM

To Zachary Dahl <zdahl@cityofsanmateo.org>; Alex Khojikian <akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org >; City Council
(San Mateo) <CityCouncil@cityofsanmateo.org>

Dear members of city council,

My family and | have proudly called Glazenwood home for nearly 40 years.

When we purchased our property, we were unaware that it was located within a designated historic
district.

We only discovered this when we undertook a remodeling project, which required compliance with
historic preservation guidelines and resulting in thousands of dollars in additional expenses due to the
involvement of the historical society.

Although the added costs and requirements were unexpected and burdensome at the time, we have
since come to fully appreciate and value the historic designation.

We are genuinely pleased that our home and neighborhood possess recognized historical significance.
This designation preserves the unique characters of the area, enhances its appeal and contributes
meaningfully to property values for all residents.

We love our neighborhood, take pride in its rich history.

| understand that there are current discussions about potentially removing Glazenwood from historic
district.

| am writing to firmly express our strong opposition to any such change.

One of the key reasons we chose to purchase and remain in this home for 4 decades is the extensive
historic quality of the neighborhood.

Removing the designation would diminish an important aspect that we - and many other long time
residents- have grown to cherish deeply and it would understandably cause significant disappointment
and concern.

Please give serious weight to the views of longtime homeowners like us who strongly support
maintaining the historic status of Glazenwood.

We urge you to preserve this designation in order to protect the special heritage and character of our
community.

Thank you for your consideration and for your efforts in safeguarding our city’s historic neighborhoods.
Sincerely,

Takako Matsuyama

Dear Director Dahl, City Manager Khojikian, and Members of the City Council,

As new owners in Glazenwood, we are writing to express our strong support for maintaining Glazenwood's
historic district designation.

We bought our home here specifically because of the integrity of the neighborhood's 1920s Spanish
Colonial Revival architecture and with the understanding that it was part of a locally designated historic
district that provided protections. The shared commitment to thoughtful design and continuity is central to
Glazenwood's identity and value.

As an architect, | understand that historic designations involve both restrictions and benefits. What's
essential in such matters is that decisions rest with those most directly affected—the property owners and
neighborhood residents themselves—not with outside advocacy groups, regardless of their policy positions.

The June 2024 survey conducted by the San Mateo Heritage Alliance demonstrates overwhelming resident
support: 88% of residents are aware of Glazenwood's historic status, 88% are proud to live in the district,
41% said the designation influenced their decision to buy, and only one respondent expressed a negative
view. This data makes clear where Glazenwood residents stand.

While we understand questions have been raised about the district's formal administrative status, the
consistent voice of actual Glazenwood residents shows strong support for maintaining and strengthening
the protections we value. We encourage the city to clarify and formalize the designation of Glazenwood as
a historic district, including the review and permitting process, as it updates the Historic Preservation
Ordinance as part of the Strive San Mateo General Plan 2040.

We appreciate the city's efforts to provide strong planning processes that protect and support San Mateo's
impressive historic resources while moving the city forward into the future.

Sincerely,

Thomas B Braham, FAIA Gregory M. Zinkl, Ph.D., J.D.
Glazenwood Residents since 2025

building patterns show typical styles of early 20th century which included styles of early 20th century Bungalows of

unit.  Very well maintained.  
 
510 South Grant Street duplex showed the design influence of the Craftsmen movement.  Very well-maintained and 
landscaped. 
 
Local Significance 
 
924 S. Eldorado Street house demonstrates how Classical Revival was interpreted for a modest residential building.   
 
938 S. Eldorado is a well-preserved and well-proportioned 1902 Craftsmen Bungalow.

895 of 1110

--- page 896 ---
Feedback on the Draft Historic Ordinance and Context Statement 
 
1.​ The ordinance is weaker than current conditions and most other nearby peninsula city 
ordinances. Make it at least as strong as the current ordinance, and in line with 
other cities. 
 
2.​ The ordinance and context statement are too long and too incomplete to give 
appropriate feedback right now.  Residents and stakeholders (and perhaps even council 
members!) need more time for a more complete understanding in order to provide 
meaningful comments. The Rincon presentation to the community was less than a week 
ago.  We need to slow this process down, as historic preservation policy should 
be thorough, thoughtful, and comprehensive. 
 
3.​ It is inappropriate for a city to refuse to recognize historic resources that have 
been officially listed on the state register and/or the National Register of Historic 
Places, as this stance undermines long-established democratic preservation processes, 
and long-term preservation of its historic resources. 
 
4.​ As far as homeowner consent, why should San Mateo have more stringent 
consent requirements than State and Federal regulations? Consent should be 
designated as more than 50% (50% plus one), similar to other City requirements. 
 
5.​ Designation criteria needs to be expanded to include all pre-war homes and 
buildings built prior to 1940-45.  The current threshold as drafted for protection of 
these properties is way too high.  Most of these homes are not associated with 
significant historical events or important people, or built by an important architect. Yet, it 
IS these homes that contribute to the unique character of our neighborhoods, and they 
are slowly being eliminated one by one through demolition or significant alteration that 
permanently scar their original design. These homes are special to San Mateo, 
singularly representative of an earlier era, give it a sense of place and history, and 
should be treated as such in the ordinance for proposed modifications to the façade of 
the property or demolition. 
 
6.​ Explain in detail what is included in the Historic Resources Inventory, as right now the 
ordinance does not tell us what is included. The list of city resolutions is not complete 
and does not address all of the resolutions that address historic resources, so more work 
is needed. Also, the Historic Resources Inventory MUST include the resources 
identified in the 1989 City of San Mateo Historic Building Survey Final Report. 
 
7.​ The Context Statement should reference the Baywood Historic Nomination, the 
Glazenwood Historic District (identified in the 2010 and 2024 General Plans), and the 
National and State Register-listed Yoshiko Yamanouchi House Historic District (listed in 
2023). 
 
8.​ The Context Statement is incomplete and missing two sections, including critical 
policy information.

1
From:
Doug D'Anna <d
>
Sent:
Sunday, January 25, 2026 9:24 PM
To:
City Council (San Mateo); Clerk; Zachary Dahl; Alex Khojikian
Subject:
Clarifying the Status of the Glazenwood Historic District in the Draft Historic 
Preservation Ordinance and Historic Context Statement 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY — REQUEST FOR CLARIFICATION 
AND INCLUSION 
Background 
 Glazenwood is identified and described as a Historic District in the City’s 
adopted General Plans, including the 2010 and 2024 General Plans. 
 The City has relied for decades on the 1989 City of San Mateo Historic 
Building Survey Final Report, prepared in cooperation with the State Office of 
Historic Preservation and incorporated into City practice through multiple Council 
actions. 
 Numerous City resolutions—including Mills Act agreements and additions to the 
Historic Building Survey—explicitly rely on the 1989 Survey as the 
foundational historic resources inventory for San Mateo. 
 Despite this history, Glazenwood is not explicitly referenced in the draft 
Historic Preservation Ordinance or the draft Historic Context Statement. 
Issue 
 The draft ordinance and draft context statement do not clearly define what is 
included in the City’s Historic Resources Inventory. 
 The absence of explicit references to Glazenwood creates administrative 
ambiguity, even though the district is already identified in Council-adopted General 
Plans. 
 This omission creates uncertainty regarding how Glazenwood will be 
treated administratively, despite its identification as a historic district in 
council adopted general plans.  
 The draft Context Statement is incomplete, missing required sections and failing 
to reference known historic districts and nominations, including: 
o Baywood Historic Nomination 
o Glazenwood Historic District 
o Yoshiko Yamanouchi House Historic District (State and National Register-
listed) 
Why This Matters 
897 of 1110

--- page 898 ---
2
 General Plans are binding law. Administrative documents and ordinances must 
be consistent with them. 
 Leaving Glazenwood out of the ordinance framework: 
o Creates uncertainty for residents, staff, and decision-makers 
o Invites inconsistent interpretation and enforcement 
o Undermines reliance on long-standing City records and surveys 
o Sets a precedent that adopted General Plan designations can be 
treated inconsistently without explicit Council action.  
 The City has previously recognized that historic resources identified in the 
1989 Survey form the backbone of its preservation framework, including 
eligibility determinations and Mills Act participation. 
Point of Clarification Requested 
We respectfully request Council clarification on the following: 
 That historic districts identified and described in the City’s adopted 
General Plan— including Glazenwood—are included in the City’s Historic 
Resources Inventory, regardless of whether they are individually restated in the 
draft ordinance. 
 That the Historic Resources Inventory includes the resources identified in 
the 1989 City of San Mateo Historic Building Survey Final Report, as relied 
upon in prior Council resolutions and agreements. 
Specific Requests 
We respectfully ask the Council to: 
1. Direct staff to explicitly clarify, in the ordinance findings or staff report, that 
Glazenwood’s General Plan designation remains fully operative. 
2. Require completion and correction of the Historic Context 
Statement before adoption, including explicit references to Glazenwood and other 
recognized historic districts. 
3. Confirm that the Historic Resources Inventory includes the 1989 Survey 
resources, including districts and contributors, NOT just selectively listed 
resolutions. 
Bottom Line 
This is not a request for a new designation or new policy. 
It is a request to affirm existing Council intent, preserve consistency with 
adopted law, and prevent unintended erosion of Glazenwood’s historic district 
status through omission.

Doug D’Anna 
Resident, Glazenwood Historic District

Historical Consent versus Speedbump consent

HistoricalConsent_vs_SpeedBumpConsent.pdf
Hello City Council Members, 
  As you consider drafting the City’s ordinance for Historical Districts and consider the Consent required 
for nomination approval, please consider existing precedent for City actions in other areas.  
I am attaching an example from a recent City decision regarding the installation of a speed bump on a 
city street.  Note that the City demands that an active majority of people affected must actively 
participate in the process as opposed to only examining people who responded to the query.   It is not 
enough to simply note that people did not respond.  NON-RESPONSES SHOULD BE COUNTED AS 
NOMINATION REFUSAL. 
  
The impact of a home being included in a historical district is far for more serious than the addition of 
speed bump in a neighborhood.  It should be AT LEAST as difficult to get approval for a historical district 
to be approved as it is for a speed bump to get approved. 
  
Please see attached flyer from San Mateo’s Public Works Department regarding the decision to disallow 
a speed bump. 
  
Kind regards,
