From:
James Isaacs 
Sent:
Sunday, January 25, 2026 5:24 PM
To:
Adam Loraine; Rob Newsom; Lisa Diaz Nash; Nicole Fernandez; 
danielldcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org; City Council (San Mateo); Alex Khojikian; 
Zachary Dahl; Prasanna Rasiah
Subject:
Historic District Ordinance Discussion
Dear Members of the San Mateo City Council, 
My wife and I have lived in San Mateo since 1989.  We lived in Shoreview, Baywood and currently San 
Mateo Park.  We raised our family here and built our careers here.  We appreciate all of the hard work of 
the planning commission to sensibly arrange growth in our community and your leadership in 
governance of our fine city.  You are not appreciated enough, so thank you! 
The discussion around the Historic District Ordinance is troubling to many.   I live in San Mateo Park, it 
was laid out in the 1880s, and our house was built in 1906.  I am a history buff and I like traditional 
design, but there is very little that is truly 'historic' in San Mateo Park.  Since we moved in 22 years ago 
just about every house in the neighborhood has been re-modeled internally, given a new facade, or 
rebuilt from the ground up in a more 'modern' look.  It would be a constructive deceit to say that this a 
historic district, particularly if the purpose of this is to restrain higher density buildings which current 
regulations out of Sacramento are indicating. 
My son is the one who convinced me that we have to do higher density building even in traditionally single 
family areas, like San Mateo Park where he grew up, "Dad, if I ever expect to afford to buy a house here, 
we're going to have to build more of it, including in San Mateo Park."  He's right.  It has to change.  I used 
to not think this way but I do now. 
Please ensure the final ordinance requires real affirmative consent from a clear supermajority of property 
owners for any new historic district designation (for example, 2/3 or 3/4 “yes” votes), rather than a 
passive or minimal petition threshold. This protects both property rights and community trust, consistent 
with the Council’s earlier position that any district should be based on explicit owner consent rather than 
an opt-out or vague process.  
Thanks for your consideration, and thanks for all you do. 
Best, 
 
James Isaacs

--- page 906 ---
January 25, 2026 
 
City Council, City of San Mateo 
330 W 20th Ave. 
San Mateo, CA 94403 
 
To the honorable San Mateo City Council, 
 
HLC exists to work with communities and their leaders to produce and preserve quality 
affordable homes. To that end, we have opposed efforts to create a historic district in the 
Baywood neighborhood, as such efforts are motivated by a small group of residents who have 
explicitly stated their intent to limit future housing construction near them.  
 
However, we supported direction from San Mateo’s City Council to craft a historic preservation 
policy emphasizing homeowner consent and reasonable standards of qualification. The goal of 
any good historic ordinance is to recognize history is a living process, one that preserves 
features essential in a community while also creating safeguards to ensure the process cannot 
be abused to block neighborhood change, such as much-needed housing.  
 
As written, San Mateo’s draft Historic Preservation Ordinance fails to fulfill the City Council’s 
direction and balance preservation with future housing needs. The ordinance includes unclear 
language that could be interpreted to, in practice, allow virtually any property older than 50 
years old to be included in sweeping historic districts, which would then be subject to 
discretionary approval processes. In cases of major alterations–itself an unclear definition 
–these processes might add tens of thousands of dollars to most modest home modifications 
and render new construction infeasible in large areas of the city.  
 
To ensure San Mateo’s draft Historic Preservation Ordinance reflects the intent of the City 
Council, we recommend the following principles and changes: 
 
1.​ Creating historic districts should be a collaborative, community-driven process, meaning 
residents have a say about whether or not they will be subject to the restrictions of a 
district. We can only support an ordinance that amends Section 27.66.070(a) to 
unambiguously require affirmative, written consent by a super majority of 
residents and property owners within any district before an application can qualify for 
review by the city. As written, Section 27.66.070 has legal ambiguities regarding the 
threshold for consent (see more info in the footnote).1 After the city’s study session last 
1 To submit an application for a historic district, Section 27.66.070(a)(1)(C) requires "Documentation by 
petition establishing at least XX percent of property owners in the proposed historic district as listed in 
San Mateo County’s most recent property ownership assessment roll support the potential designation by 
affixing their signatures to the petition." But the petition does not seem to have any binding impact on the 
 
906 of 1110

--- page 907 ---
week, we hope that Rincon will clarify the ordinance based on clear prior council 
direction and community input.  
2.​ As written, the ordinance allows the planning director to require a Historic Resource 
Evaluation on properties older than 45 years even if they are not a designated historic 
resource or district. This is codifying a uniquely restrictive section of the California 
Environmental Quality Act in local law for no obvious reason, especially considering no 
other state has similar restrictions and CEQA may be amended in the future to exclude 
them. Section 27.66.090 of the ordinance should be removed to ensure that properties 
without a historic designation are not subject to unnecessary costs imposed on 
such properties.  
3.​ The criteria for designating a historic resource or district should be rigorous, affecting 
only properties with a unique and unambiguous relevance to the history of San Mateo. 
Right now, the ordinance as written copies the loose standards used by the state of 
California rather than applying a rigorous definition for qualification--meaning most any 
property older than 50 years old could be declared historic.2 In Appendix A below, we 
recommend rewritten standards for Sections 27.66.040 and 27.66.060 to only 
include properties older than 100 years that have one-of-a-kind architectural 
features or relation to a singular, transformative event in state or local history.  
4.​ Once a property has been designated historic, the owner becomes subject to onerous 
requirements. The majority of exterior alterations or new construction that might be made 
on a Listed Resource of Contributor would require a discretionary Certificate of 
Appropriateness, even if the alteration would otherwise be ministerial. Section 
27.66.100 should be amended to include limitations on the discretionary review 
process, including a ministerial process for new construction that does not otherwise 
affect the structures of a historic resource, permit timelines similar to the Permit 
Streamlining Act (90-day maximum delays between periods of review), and a maximum 
of two public hearings by relevant approval bodies. 
 
In order to ensure the historic preservation process does not conflict with local housing needs, 
the historic districting process should be clear and predictable, with strong safeguards to 
prevent abuse. Historic districts should not be created without clear resident consent, and 
historic resources of any kind–whether districts or individual resources–should meet high 
standards for public benefit and historical relevance.  
2 To understand just how loose and subjective the state criteria for historic resources are: Under the state 
standards, not a single application to create a historic resource or district has been denied by the state 
Historic Resources Commission in at least the past decade. 
actual outcome of the decision to designate a historic district; that decision is based solely on whether or 
not the district meets the the designation criteria and eligibility requirements listed in sections 27.66.060 
and 27.66.050, which are basically copied and pasted from the highly subjective state requirements.  
 
However, the ordinance is ambiguous; Section 27.66.060(a)(1) seems to have clear standards for 
majority approval for “listed resources,” but it reads like it applies exclusively to individual resources, not 
districts. The definitions section clarifies that "Historic Resources" includes districts, but it's not clear that 
Section 27.66.060 actually includes districts given that there is a separate section about districts.  
 
907 of 1110

--- page 908 ---
By implementing the amendments we’ve recommended, San Mateo can create a historic
preservation process that allows for the preservation of essential community history while also
planning for the City’s future.

Thank you for your consideration,

Jeremy Levine
Policy Manager, Housing Leadership Council of San Mateo County

908 of 1110


--- page 909 ---
Appendix A  
 
Purpose: Propose clearer standards for historic districting in San Mateo and California.  
The current standards for historic districting in San Mateo are essential copied and pasted from 
the loose criteria used by the California State Historic Resources Commission, reading as 
followed:  
(a)​Is associated with events that have made a significant contribution to the broad patterns 
of San Mateo’s or California’s history and cultural heritage; or  
(b)​Is associated with the lives of persons or communities important to San Mateo’s or 
California’s past; or  
(c)​ Embodies the distinctive characteristics of a type, period, region, or method of 
construction, or represents the work of an important creative individual, or possesses 
high artistic values; or  
(d)​Has yielded, or may be likely to yield, information important to prehistory or history.  
Furthermore:  
(a)​Listed Resources. To be eligible for consideration as a Listed Resource, the resource 
must be at least fifty (50) years old and meet at least one of the criteria outlined in 
Section 27.66.040. (the prior section) 
(b)​Historic District. To be eligible for consideration as a Historic District, at least XX percent 
of the buildings in the proposed historic district (excluding accessory buildings) must be 
at least fifty (50) years old or otherwise meet the requirement of Section 27.66.040. In 
addition, no more than XX percent of the buildings in the proposed district may be 
non-contributing. Noncontributing buildings may be included as part of a historic district 
only to the extent that they are determined to be essential to the geographic integrity of 
the district. The [reviewing body] shall make determinations identifying non-contributing 
buildings as part of the review process. 
Essentially, any property older than fifty years old would be eligible to be designated a historic 
resource, and the criteria for eligibility are so broad that any property could likely be listed as a 
historic resource.  
 
HLC proposes the following criteria for section 27.66.040 instead:  
A property shall be considered eligible for historic preservation status only if it meets one or 
more of the following criteria. Each designation must be supported by clear, specific, and 
professionally verified evidence that the property is unique, irreplaceable, and of extraordinary 
public historic value. 
 
909 of 1110

--- page 910 ---
1. Site of a Singular Historic Event or Defining Moment 
The property must be the sole or primary remaining location directly tied to a singular, 
transformative event in the City of San Mateo, California, or U.S. history. General associations 
with broad trends or categories (e.g., "postwar development," "mid-century architecture") are 
insufficient unless the property is the only known or last remaining physical representation of 
that trend in the state. 
2. Unique Association with a Historically Transformative Individual 
The property must be the only surviving property closely associated with the peak period of 
activity or residence of a person whose achievements changed the course of local, state, or 
national history. Peripheral associations (e.g., temporary visits or minor ties) are not sufficient. 
The property's significance must be supported by primary sources and academic consensus. 
3. Architectural Rarity and Irreplaceability 
The property must be an exceedingly rare or one-of-a-kind example of a particular architectural 
style, method, or period, or the single best surviving work of an architect whose contribution has 
been recognized as defining or transformative in the architectural history of San Mateo or 
California. Generic examples or mass-produced designs do not qualify. 
To qualify: 
●​ The property must retain a high degree of integrity. 
●​ It must be the only comparable surviving structure in the state. 
4. Irreplaceable Source of Archaeological or Prehistoric Knowledge 
The site must contain wholly unique, undisturbed archaeological evidence that is demonstrably 
unavailable from any other known location in the state. This determination must be based on an 
independent archaeological survey conducted within the past 10 years, adhering to 
state-recognized professional standards. 
Furthermore, we recommend amending Section 27.66.050 to read:  
(c)​ Listed Resources. To be eligible for consideration as a Listed Resource, the resource 
must be at least one hundred (100) years old and meet at least one of the criteria 
outlined in Section 27.66.040. (the prior section) 
(d)​Historic District. To be eligible for consideration as a Historic District, at least 75 percent 
of the buildings in the proposed historic district (excluding accessory buildings) must be 
at least one hundred (100) years old or otherwise meet the requirement of Section 
27.66.040. In addition, no more than XX percent of the buildings in the proposed district 
may be non-contributing. Noncontributing buildings may be included as part of a historic 
district only to the extent that they are determined to be essential to the geographic 
integrity of the district. The Planning Commission shall make determinations identifying 
non-contributing buildings as part of the review process.

January 26, 2026

Historic Policies & Council Direction

Dear City Council Members and City Staff:

San Mateo Forward is a local, pro-housing and pro-change organization made up of San Mateo
residents who believe historic preservation must be precise, limited, and legally defensible. Based
on our review, the current historic policy package is moving the City in the opposite direction.

The Historic Context Statement functions as expansion, not context.

Rather than defining clear historical frameworks, the draft dramatically broadens what may be
treated as historically significant. Neighborhood patterns, subdivision layouts, and social history are
elevated in ways that invite property-by-property eligibility claims without clear limits. When
combined with lowered integrity expectations, this approach allows routine, modified structures to
be treated as historic resources based largely on narrative association. That is not preservation. It is
a recipe for uncertainty and increased costs for homeowners.

This framework invites conflict with state housing law.

California housing statutes explicitly limit the use of discretionary review and historic claims to
obstruct compliant housing. By expanding “potential historical resources” without clear thresholds
or designation safeguards, the City is creating a pathway for project delays that will not survive legal
scrutiny. This exposes San Mateo to housing element enforcement risk and CEQA litigation.

1

914 of 1110


--- page 915 ---
Preservation policy that repeatedly collides with state law is not defensible and ultimately
undermines legitimate historic protections.

The ordinance lacks enforceable guardrails.

The draft ordinance compounds these problems by failing to establish basic due process. Allowing
applicants to collect their own petition signatures for historic districts is not consistent with already
established City consent processes. The City must manage notice and verification directly. Formation
of a historic district should require affirmative consent from at least 80% of affected property
owners.

Historic preservation should protect clearly defined resources, not operate as a citywide
growth-control mechanism. The City should pause adoption, substantially narrow the Historic
Context Statement, finalize ordinance safeguards first, and revamp the outreach program
with a clear focus on the actual policy choices before Council.

The current direction of the historic policy package is unsound and will create lasting policy and legal
problems if left uncorrected.

Sincerely,

Max Mautner
San Mateo Forward volunteer lead

John Lovell
San Mateo Forward volunteer lead

Gary Magill
San Mateo Forward volunteer lead

Yes to People. Yes to Housing.

SAN MATEO sanmateoforward.org

From:
Kolja Schluetter <

Sent:
Sunday, January 25, 2026 12:15 PM
To:
City Council (San Mateo); Clerk; Alex Khojikian; Historic Policy
Cc:
Zachary Dahl; Planning Commission
Subject:
Request for legal safeguards in Historic Context Statement and draft ordinance
Dear San Mateo City Council and Project Team, 
  
As a follow up to my initial letter, I am writing to request that specific legal safeguards be added to both 
the Draft Historic Context Statement and the accompanying historic preservation ordinance before 
either document advances toward adoption. 
  
As currently drafted, the Historic Context Statement functions as more than a neutral background 
document and creates a material risk that non-designated properties could be treated as “potential 
historical resources” for planning and CEQA purposes. Disclaimers alone are not sufficient to address 
this risk unless they are paired with clear, binding limitations in both the Context Statement and the 
ordinance. 
  
To reduce legal exposure, prevent misuse, and provide clarity for homeowners, applicants, and City 
staff, I respectfully request the following changes. Without these changes consent will be significantly 
eroded. 
  
1. Additions to the Historic Context Statement 
The Context Statement should include clear, operative language stating that: 
 
The document is a narrative reference only and does not identify, designate, determine, or imply 
historic eligibility of any property, site, structure, district, or landscape. 
 
The document may not be used, cited, or relied upon to support CEQA determinations or claims 
of historical resource status. 
 
References to architectural styles, neighborhoods, development patterns, social history, or 
cultural themes do not constitute identification of contributors, potential contributors, or eligible 
resources. 
 
In the event of any conflict, the adopted ordinance controls. 
In addition, eligibility-style language should be removed entirely. This includes references to CRHR or 
NRHP criteria, integrity thresholds, or statements suggesting that properties, patterns, or areas may be 
eligible or warrant further study. 
  
2. Additions to the ordinance 
The ordinance should clearly limit what the City will treat as a historical resource for planning and CEQA 
purposes. Specifically, only the following should qualify: 
 
Properties formally designated as local landmarks 
 
Properties within locally designated historic districts 
 
Properties listed on the California Register of Historical Resources 
916 of 1110

--- page 917 ---
2
 
Properties listed on the National Register of Historic Places 
  
The ordinance should further state that: 
 
Non-designated properties shall not be treated as historical resources or potential historical 
resources for CEQA, discretionary review, demolition review, or project conditioning. 
 
The Historic Context Statement may not be used as evidence of historical significance or 
eligibility. 
 
City staff, consultants, boards, or commissions may not independently determine historic 
eligibility outside the formal designation process. 
 
No historic survey, evaluation, or eligibility analysis of a privately owned property may be initiated 
or required without the written consent of the property owner, unless the property is already 
designated or listed. 
 
Compliance with the ordinance and reliance on designated resources constitutes the City’s 
complete consideration of historical resources under CEQA. 
  
3. Importance of sequencing 
These limitations should be adopted concurrently with, or prior to, adoption of the Historic Context 
Statement. Adopting an expansive context document without binding ordinance guardrails creates 
avoidable legal risk and unnecessary uncertainty. 
  
These changes are necessary to ensure historic preservation policy is precise, defensible, and 
consistent with CEQA, state housing law, and basic principles of due process.  
  
Thank you for your consideration. 
  
Best regards, 
 
Kolja Schluetter 
On Jan 20, 2026 at 7:27 PM -0800, Kolja Schluetter <
, wrote: 
 
Dear San Mateo Council Members and Project Team, 
  
I have reviewed the Draft Historic Context Statement (November 2025), the accompanying 
draft ordinance framework, and the outreach conducted to date. My comments are not a 
critique of historic preservation as a goal, but of how this effort is currently structured, 
sequenced, and implemented.  
As drafted, the combined approach creates unnecessary legal risk, policy imbalance, and 
public distrust. 
  
1. Historic Context Statement – scope and downstream risk 
The Historic Context Statement goes well beyond its intended role as a neutral reference 
document. It repeatedly elevates broad development patterns, subdivision layouts, 
neighborhood form, and social history from contextual background to independently 
917 of 1110

--- page 918 ---
3
significant resources. This shifts preservation review away from discrete, evaluable 
resources toward open-ended, neighborhood-scale claims and materially lowers the 
threshold for asserting historical significance. 
  
Although the document states it does not designate resources, its structure and language 
invite pre-designation eligibility arguments. By exhaustively mapping themes and 
associations while emphasizing that CRHR eligibility does not require NRHP-level integrity, 
the document enables almost any property older than roughly 45 years to be argued as 
historically eligible, even when physical integrity or architectural merit is weak. This 
effectively turns context into de facto regulation without legislative safeguards. 
  
The heavy reliance on non-physical attributes such as association, feeling, setting, and 
cultural narratives further increases subjectivity and uncertainty. The extensive discussion 
of cultural and ethnic history, while important, is framed in a way that strongly ties social 
history to physical location. As written, code-compliant redevelopment can be challenged 
on the basis of alleged cultural erasure or displacement even where no designated 
resource exists. This moves preservation review into socio-political adjudication rather 
than objective resource evaluation. 
  
Finally, by broadening what may constitute a “potential historical resource,” the City 
is materially increasing CEQA exposure. Projects that would otherwise qualify for 
ministerial or streamlined review can now be forced into discretionary review or an 
EIR. This risk is foreseeable and should be acknowledged directly. 
  
  
2. Ordinance – missing guardrails and due process 
Several elements of the draft ordinance require substantial correction before adoption. 
  
First, the consent process for historic district applications is fundamentally flawed. Relying 
on applicant-collected petition signatures is not verifiable and is inconsistent with every 
other City process involving neighbor consent. The City, not the applicant, must manage 
notification and consent verification. A supermajority threshold of at least two-thirds of 
affected property owners should be required, with non-responses counted as no 
consent. This is consistent with existing City practice, for example the installation of 
public street speed bumps requires 67% approval of affected homeowners. 
  
Second, if an applicant attempts to bypass the local process by submitting a district 
application directly to the Office of Historic Preservation, the City Manager should be 
required to formally notify OHP that the City does not support the application. This should 
explicitly include the pending Baywood state application. 
  
Third, there should be no new standalone Historic Preservation Commission. Oversight 
should remain within the Planning Department to avoid duplicative bureaucracy and 
maintain accountability within existing structures. 
  
918 of 1110

--- page 919 ---
4
Finally, contributor status must be properly researched and documented. Drive-by or 
superficial assessments are inadequate. If a homeowner disputes contributor 
classification, the applicant must be required to meaningfully revise the application and 
address contrary evidence. 
  
  
3. Outreach – failure to meet Council direction 
The outreach conducted to date does not comply with Council’s direction from last year. 
Rincon and staff were explicitly tasked with collecting input on the ordinance itself. 
Instead, meetings focused largely on general sentiments about San Mateo. 
  
Rincon’s own materials show only 163 community member touchpoints, many of which 
represent repeat participants counted multiple times. With the exception of the mobile 
workshops, attendance was dominated by SMHA and LRT members. This is not a broad or 
representative cross-section of the community. Critically, Council-directed questions, 
such as what consent thresholds are meaningful to residents, were not addressed at all. 
  
In effect, approximately $60,000 was spent to hear from roughly 95 new voices about what 
they like about San Mateo, not about the policy choices at issue. 
  
  
Conclusion 
Taken together, the Historic Context Statement, ordinance draft, and outreach process 
reflect a flawed sequencing and an imbalance between preservation objectives, housing 
obligations, and property rights. The current approach expands regulatory reach, lowers 
evidentiary thresholds, and defers critical policy decisions while exposing the City to 
avoidable legal and political risk. 
I strongly urge the City to pause adoption, revise the Historic Context Statement to clearly 
separate background history from eligibility frameworks, complete and tighten the 
ordinance guardrails, and conduct ordinance-focused outreach that reflects the broader 
community. 
  
Thank you for considering these comments. I would welcome further discussion. 
  
Best regards, 
  
Kolja Schluetter 
919 of 1110

From: Frank Elliott

Subject: Liability Risk & Proposed Language for Non-Consensual Districts 
  
Dear Zach and Consultant Team, 
I asked a question last night that may have been obscured by a subsequent remark. That aside, my 
question remains valid and unanswered. 
My Request to the City: As part of this ordinance process, please add a provision that addresses the gap 
regarding "contributor" classifications for non-locally created districts. Here is the suggested language:
"If the OHP or an outside body approves a nomination that includes classifications 
of  'Contributor' , the City shall presume that classification is incorrect if the owner 
challenges it." 
The Risk to the City: Beyond the impact on homeowners, without this provision, the Staff and City may 
open themselves to potential lawsuits, or at minimum a lot of unnecessary conflict. Currently, there is no 
due process or governmental verification of these classifications. If the City enforces regulations based 
on unverified third-party data, you assume the liability for that data.  
The GREATER Context: In my opinion, the only new residential districts the City will realistically need to 
handle are those processed at the State level, as the local consent threshold will likely prove to be a 
difficult barrier for proponents to overcome. The catalyst for this whole process was to help a situation 
like Baywood, and yet this draft ordinance ignores that situation entirely. I hope Staff and Council will 
spend time addressing non-consensual districts and how we can mitigate the effect on owners. WHY 
ARE WE CREATING AN ELABORATE ORDINANCE FOR DISTRICTS THAT WILL NEVER BE CREATED 
LOCALLY? 
But to focus on this specific request regarding contributor classifications, here are the facts that I am 
currently living: 
1. The State (OHP) Does Not Verify Classifications 
922 of 1110

--- page 923 ---
2
I previously contacted the Office of Historic Preservation (OHP) regarding re-classification for my 
property. Their written response was clear (email attached): 
"The nomination belongs to the applicant. Therefore, any changes must be directed to the 
applicant and preparer, who are copied on this message. Should they make changes to the 
nomination, they would provide the amended nomination to our office for review." 
Furthermore, in a direct Zoom meeting with OHP staff, they confirmed to me that they do not review 
these individual "contributor" classifications in detail. They may do a cursory review, but rely entirely 
on the nominator and their consultant. While Page & Turnbull is a respected firm, they work for the 
applicant, not the State. 
The Reality: The State accepts the list provided by the applicant. They do not have the resources 
(nor is it part of their process) to independently verify if my home—or any home—is correctly 
labeled. 
2. The Nominator Refuses to Correct Errors 
The OHP forwarded my request for changed classification to the nominator. Approximately five months 
later, I heard back from the nominator, Laurie Hietter (San Mateo Heritage Alliance), with a form letter 
stating (letter attached): 
"After careful consideration of all available information, the San Mateo Heritage Alliance 
has decided not to revise property classifications at this time." 
Several others received an identical letter. This letter confirms that determinations were made from the 
"public right-of-way" only, with no indication that the new information I provided (regarding exterior 
changes or permit history) was reviewed by a professional historian. 
3. The Lack of Rigor vs. Permanent Consequence 
I have two direct questions for Rincon: 1) For a district of 444 homes, how much time is realistically 
spent validating each individual property? Based on public information regarding the Baywood 
nomination budget, it appears less than $100 per property is allocated for this vetting. 2) Is it 
appropriate for a drive-by assessment costing $100 or less to result in a permanent "Contributor" label 
that restricts a homeowner’s property rights forever? 
Conclusion 
Specifically, the burden should be on the Nominator (or the City) to prove that the home retains integrity 
using the same strict Secretary of the Interior (DOI) Standards that you intend to impose on 
homeowners. Why should a Nominator be allowed to use a "drive-by" standard to put me on the 
contributor list—ignoring prior alterations that fail the DOI test—while I am held to that 
exacting standard just to get a permit? 
I hope you see the inconsistency in this process and will not put added burdens on homeowners who 
find themselves in districts they did not consent to. The Council has been clear: they want consent for 
districts and do not want to facilitate the bypass of local control.  This one change in the ordinance, will 
substantially reduce the burden of unwanted designations. 
Thank you, 
Frank Elliott

December 3, 2025 
Mr. Frank Elliott 
 
Subject: Request to Reclassify Property in the Proposed Baywood Historic District  
Dear Mr. Elliott: 
Thank you for your recent inquiry regarding the potential reclassification of your property from a 
contributing resource to a non‐contributing resource within the proposed Baywood Historic District. 
We appreciate your engagement with this important process and your stewardship of this historic 
neighborhood. The San Mateo Heritage Alliance retained Page & Turnbull, a qualified architectural 
history firm, to conduct a professional evaluation of the properties in the historic district. The 
evaluation of properties was conducted from the public right‐of‐way and followed the standards and 
guidelines established by the National Register of Historic Places and the California Office of Historic 
Preservation (OHP). The evaluation documented the property’s architectural characteristics and 
analyzed its historic integrity in relation to the seven aspects recognized by the National Register, 
within the context of how it supports the historic district:  
1. Location  
2. Design  
3. Setting  
4. Materials  
5. Workmanship  
6. Feeling  
7. Association 
As the National Register Bulletin states, a property need not retain all seven aspects to contribute to the 
overall significance and integrity of the historic district. After thorough analysis, Page & Turnbull 
concluded that your property retains sufficient historic integrity to be considered a contributing 
resource to the proposed Baywood Historic District.  
Separately, OHP staff have reviewed the draft district nomination and confirmed that the proposed 
Baywood Historic District meets the criteria for listing in the National Register of Historic Places. The 
nomination also received an independent peer review by a second state historian.  
Historic preservation does not mean preserving properties in amber. Historic regulations recognize 
that owners make changes to their properties over time. Not every alteration automatically 
compromises a property’s ability to convey its historic character or its ability to contribute to a historic 
district. While your property may have sustained some previous alterations, at present, the 
professional evaluation finds that your property continues to contribute to the historic and 
architectural significance of the district.  
926 of 1110

--- page 927 ---
December 3, 2025 
Page 2 
 
 
 
2 
After careful consideration of all available information, the San Mateo Heritage Alliance has 
decided not to revise property classifications at this time. The Office of Historic Preservation will 
contact you directly once their formal review of the nomination is complete. Following that review, we 
plan to host community informational meetings to answer questions. 
We are here to be a resource to the community and to educate the public about the importance of 
preserving the rich heritage of the Baywood neighborhood and the City of San Mateo.

--- page 929 ---
January 25, 2026 
 
San Mateo City Council 
Via email: historicpolicy@cityofsanmateo.org  
 
Re: Draft Historic Preservation Ordinance 
Dear Members of the City Council: 
I write as a twenty-year resident of San Mateo in the Beresford Park area, concerned about 
the proposed Historic Preservation Ordinance.  Please consider the following comments 
and proposed revisions to the draft ordinance. 
1. The City should expand its review to address the current Baywood dispute by 
considering relief to residents involuntarily included in historic districts. 
The ordinance may be irrelevant to the current Baywood dispute if Baywood is not likely to 
be designated locally as a historic district.  A supermajority favoring local designation is 
unlikely because, apparently, a majority of Baywood residents oppose the state listing. 
Accordingly, the City should expand its review to consider how to provide relief to 
homeowners involuntarily included in historic districts through state or federal eligibility 
determinations.  Please consider the suggestions below as part of that expanded review. 
2. The City should treat design review as ministerial.  
Even if no districts are designated locally under the proposed ordinance, Baywood is likely 
to become subject to CEQA’s historic impact assessment and mitigation mandates 
because it is likely to be found “eligible” for listing in the current state OHP process.  The 
City can and should avoid unwanted and contentious consideration of historic resource 
impacts to buildings in historic districts for which the owner does not seek historic 
resource protection.  It can do so by treating its Single Family Dwelling Design Review as 
exempt from CEQA. 
The CEQA process, including its historic resource provisions, applies only to discretionary 
decisions. The City has been unnecessarily treating design review as a discretionary 
decision that triggers CEQA review.  The City can and should revise its design review 
provisions to clarify that design review does not trigger CEQA review, and thus does not 
require consideration of historic resource impacts under CEQA.            
The most straightforward way to avoid an unnecessary CEQA process for design review 
would be to adopt objective design standards to facilitate purely ministerial permitting of 
residential projects.  Using objective standards makes it easier to undertake design review 
and makes it clear that there is no CEQA trigger.  Indeed, objective standards are the way 
many jurisdictions are now streamlining permitting to encourage provision of deeply 
needed housing. There is no reason for redundant project-level CEQA review of infill 
929 of 1110

--- page 930 ---
projects after the City has already complied with CEQA to adopt its general plan, update its 
housing element, and adopt its zoning code.  
Even if the City does not adopt objective design standards, it should clarify that its Single 
Family Dwelling Design Review is in intent and practice purely aesthetic.  Under McCorkle 
Eastside Neighborhood Group v. City of St. Helena (2018) 31 Cal.App.5th 80, 94, even a 
discretionary design review limited to aesthetic considerations does not trigger CEQA 
because City lacks discretion to address environmental impacts.  The City should consider 
amendments to the design review provisions for single-family residences to clarify that 
CEQA does not apply to design review, as follows: 
 
Amend SMC Section 27.08.032 to retain only its objective criteria, i.e., the 
determination whether “the structures, site plan, and landscaping are consistent 
with the adopted R1 Single Family Dwelling Design Guidelines” and whether the 
“development meets all applicable standards as adopted by the Planning 
Commission and City Council, conforms with the General Plan, and will correct any 
violations of the zoning ordinance, building code, or other municipal codes that 
exist on the site.”  (SMC Section 27.08.032(d)(1) and (4).)  These are clearly objective 
criteria and a determination of consistency can be processed ministerially by the 
Zoning Administrator.   
 
Amend SMC Section 27.08.032 to eliminate the subjective and standardless 
findings now required that “[t]he development will not be detrimental to the 
harmonious and orderly growth of the City” and “[t]he development will not impair 
the desirability of investment or occupation in the vicinity, and otherwise is in the 
best interests of the public health, safety, or welfare.” (SMC Section 27.08.032(d)(2) 
and (3).)  These vaguely worded findings are not clearly related to design 
considerations and call for ad hoc and unguided discretion that, in the absence of 
objective standards, can be exercised arbitrarily. 
3. The historic preservation ordinance should exempt projects from the 
Certificate of Appropriateness where compliance is infeasible.   
In case historic districts are eventually designated locally under the proposed historic 
ordinance, the ordinance should incorporate an exception to compliance with the 
Secretary of the Interior’s Standards where the compliance is infeasible for economic or 
policy reasons and there are overriding considerations for approval of the project.  CEQA 
provides this off-ramp.  The City’s ordinance should not be more exacting than CEQA. 
CEQA requires an agency to assess the significance of impacts and adopt mitigation or an 
alternative where feasible.  If mitigation or alternatives are infeasible due to “[s]pecific 
economic, legal, social, technological, or other considerations, including provision of 
employment opportunities for highly trained workers,” the agency may approve the project 
despite its unmitigated impacts if it finds overriding circumstances.  (14 CCR 15091(a)(2).)  
The overriding circumstances that permit an agency to approve a project despite 
significant adverse impacts include “specific economic, legal, social, technological, or 
930 of 1110

--- page 931 ---
other benefits, including region-wide or statewide environmental benefits.”  (14 CCR 
15093(a).)  The City might find, for example, that provision of housing and construction jobs 
outweighs some potential impacts to historic resources.  
As drafted, Section 26.66.100(a) and (a)(1) of the ordinance would require that every 
project with a significant impact to a historic resource would have to obtain a Certificate of 
Appropriateness.  The only exception is for the unlikely case of a project undertaken by low 
and very-low income households, i.e., households that most typically rent rather than own 
their homes.  Obtaining a Certificate of Appropriateness mandates mitigation measures by 
meeting the Secretary of interiors Standards.  (Draft Ordinance Section 26.66.100(a) and 
(a)(1).)  Those standards are so exacting that demolition is not allowed unless the structure 
presents a health and safety risk that cannot be mitigated. 
However, the purposes section of the draft ordinance indicates that the legislative intent is 
to consider feasibility, i.e., only to “[a]void demolition of the City’s historic resources, where 
feasible, through preservation, rehabilitation, or other available and appropriate 
alternatives.  (Draft Ordinance Section 26.66.010(e).)    
Accordingly, Section 26.66.100(a) should be revised to add the underlined language as 
follows: “No person shall demolish, construct, move or alter any Listed Resource or 
Contributor within a Historic District listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory 
without first obtaining a Certificate of Appropriateness unless meeting the design 
standards set out in Section 26.66.100(a)(1) is infeasible due to specific economic, legal, 
social, technological, or other considerations, including provision of employment 
opportunities for highly trained workers.”  This is the same language that permits 
exceptions to mitigation of significant impacts under CEQA.  Again, the City should not 
impose a more stringent mitigation obligation than is already required by CEQA. 
4. The ordinance should provide a lesser mitigation burden for contributors to an 
historic district than for individually eligible or listed properties. 
The ordinance’s definition of significant alterations and its conditions for a Certificate of 
Appropriateness treat contributors to historic districts as if they are individually listed or 
eligible historic resources and hold them to the Secretary of the Interior’s Standards for 
individually listed resources.  The ordinance should be revised to recognize that a single 
contributor to a historic district may not be individually eligible for listing and that its 
demolition or alteration might not significantly impact the historic district as a whole.  
Failure to recognize this would be contrary to San Mateo’s current treatment of historic 
districts, which, following CEQA, allows substantial alterations or demolition of 
contributors, as long as the district retains most of its historic qualities, as follows: 
Under CEQA, impacts are usually analyzed to an entire historic district rather than 
to an individual contributor (unless the contributor itself qualifies as a historic 
resource). Therefore, substantial alterations or even demolition of a single district 
contributor that is not individually eligible might not amount to a significant impact 
931 of 1110

--- page 932 ---
ifthe | fistri . ‘its historical bi li
qualities.

(City of San Mateo, Historic Districts Frequently Asked Questions, available
at https://www.cityofsanmateo.org/m/faq?cat=79

Accordingly, some language should be added to both the definition of “significant
alterations” and to the requirements to obtain a Certificate of Appropriateness spelling out
a lesser mitigation standard for a contributor to a historic district than the standard
applicable to an individual resource. This is particularly important if properties are
included in historic districts as contributors without owner consent or without careful
individualized determinations that the property would meet the individual listing criteria.

Thank you for your consideration of these comments.

Sincerely,

John Farrow

Somer Smith

Dear San Mateo City Council (less Lisa Nash) plus Clerk, 
 
I, Kate C O’Young, am an Owner in District 2. It has come to my attention that the new Historic 
Preservation Ordinance Language in the Municipal Code may not protect Owners Adequately. Please 
carefully check the language in these three areas. 
 
CONSENT AT LEAST THAT FOR PARKING PERMITS! 
67% must support. At least 50% must respond to the survey. 
We don't need to reinvent the wheel and spend more money on this than necessary! San Mateo already 
has an existing, known process for gathering owner consent on residential programs...Parking Permits. 
The threshold for historic resources should actually be higher. "Historic" is an additional disclosure at 
sale, the pool of buyers for historic homes is smaller than for homes not historic and historic homes cost 
more to maintain and insure. 
 
NO BYPASS OF CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS 
The OHP in Sacramento doesn't know the buildings of a City better than the locals. And as you know, the 
OHP does no independent due diligence on nominations. As a result, they have rubber stamped 
"historic" buildings up and down the state nominated by NIMBYs and controlling neighbors. 
 
Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP inform 
them immediately of any nominations of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP does not 
approve any historic resources that have not been reviewed and approved first by the City/Council via 
the local process. 
 
NO REFERENCE TO THE CONTEXT STATEMENT IN THE ORDINANCE 
City Council directed Staff to not put Owners at risk of their homes being made “historic” by reference. 
For example, “Spanish Colonial Revival” is an example of a historic home. My understanding is if the 
Ordinance references the Context Statement and the Context Statement includes “Spanish Colonial 
Revival”, then all Spanish Colonial Revival homes in greater San Mateo could now be considered 
“historic”. 
 
I ask that the Historic Preservation Language in the Municipal Code be reviewed and approved by the 
Less Red Tape team with support from land use attorney Daniel Freedman who has been fighting the 
weaponization of historic districts up and down the State. 
 
Thank you for ensuring that private citizens can't encumber their neighbors properties without the

From: Chris Battles Fti(‘i‘é‘SS

Sent: Monday, January 26, 2026 3:56 PM

To: Rob Newsom; Adam Loraine; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki; Nicole Fernandez; Clerk

Ce: Alex Khojikian; Prasanna Rasiah;

Subject: DISTRICT #1: Historic Ordinance - Consent, No Local Bypass, No Ref to Context Stmt

NOTE: This is NOT Al -- | am areal person, which you verify by replying to this email.
Dear San Mateo City Council (minus Lisa Nash) and City Clerk,

I'm a property owner in District 1, and I've been paying close attention to this new Historic Preservation
Ordinance language in the Municipal Code. Bottom line: it still doesn't adequately protect owners like
me from having our properties encumbered without real consent or due process. Please take a hard look
at these three critical areas before anything moves forward.

1. Consent threshold—at least match the parking permit process!

We already have a proven, straightforward way to get owner consent for residential programs in San
Mateo: the parking permit system. It requires 67% support and at least 50% of owners responding to the
survey. That's a solid, known benchmark—why reinvent the wheel and waste more taxpayer money
figuring out something new?

If anything, the bar for slapping a "historic" label on homes should be higher, not lower. Being designated
historic means extra disclosures at sale, a smaller pool of potential buyers, higher maintenance costs,
and often pricier insurance. This isn't just paperwork—‘it's a real hit to property values and flexibility. Stick
close to (or exceed) the parking permit model for owner buy-in.

2. No backdoor bypass of the City for historic resource nominations

The state Office of Historic Preservation (OHP) in Sacramento doesn't know our local buildings anywhere
near as well as we do here. Worse, they do zero independent fact-checking on nominations—they've
rubber-stamped plenty of questionable "historic" designations statewide, often pushed by NIMBY
neighbors or controlling groups trying to block changes.

To shut this down, add clear language to the ordinance requiring the City Manager to demand immediate
notification from OHP of any nomination within San Mateo city limits. And crucially: OHP should not
approve or list any resource here unless it has first gone through—and been approved by—the City's full
local review process and Council.

3. No sneaky reference to the Context Statement in the ordinance itself
Council already directed staff to avoid putting owners at risk of homes being deemed "historic" by

indirect reference or broad strokes. For example, "Spanish Colonial Revival" is a classic style—but if the
ordinance cross-references the Context Statement and that document lists "Spanish Colonial Revival"

938 of 1110


--- page 939 ---
2
as a theme, suddenly every home in that style across greater San Mateo could get swept up as "historic" 
without individual review or consent. That's exactly the kind of trap we're trying to prevent. 
 
Finally, I strongly urge that the final Historic Preservation language in the Municipal Code be run through 
the Less Red Tape team, with direct input from land use attorney Daniel Freedman. He's been battling 
the weaponization of historic designations up and down the state—he knows exactly where these pitfalls 
hide. 
Thanks for stepping up to make sure no private citizen (or small group) can tie up their neighbors' 
properties without full awareness and real owner consent. Property rights matter—let's keep them 
protected. 
Sincerely, Chris Battles District 1 Owner

From:
Andrew Ryan <

Subject:
Council: The draft historic ordinance MUST address Baywood. It's the whole reason 
we're here.
This weekend I heard an alarming meme from several councilmembers: that the new historic ordinance 
was never meant to address Baywood's issues. I want to cut this meme off quickly because it's false, and 
I think Council and Staff are trying to use it as a cop-out to kick the can down the road and avoid making 
hard decisions in 2026. Let's address the arguments I've heard in the "we're not touching Baywood" 
meme: 
 
1. "Council never agreed to make an ordinance just because of Baywood" 
Totally false. Let's recap what happened. In 2024, when tensions were high around Baywood and 
national news stories were appearing covering San Mateo in a strongly negative light, City Manager Alex 
Khojikian asked both the SMHA and LRT for a "truce" while a local ordinance process could be pursued, 
and the fight over Baywood be moved from the State to the City. Assistant City Manager Christine 
Horrisberger was present and can validate this. While LRT agreed to the truce and maintained it, SMHA 
broke the truce and re-submitted their Baywood application to the state in 2024, before the Council even 
met to consider the new ordinance in 2025. 
 
You're doing this whole ordinance because of Baywood. No way this exists otherwise. 
 
2. "Baywood is being made historic under state law, therefore there's nothing the city 
can do for Baywood homeowners" 
This is completely false. In fact *EVERYTHING* that happens to homeowners is EXACTLY under the 
City's control. All the state does is say "boop, you're historic."  Everything else after that is up to the City: 
how permits are granted, how demolitions are handled, how modifications are treated, etc. And the draft 
ordinance just adds MORE red tape, MORE confusion to this process. Even the dreaded CEQA could, in 
theory, be bypassed if we made historic home renovations ministerial, since CEQA only applies to 
discretionary permits. Not saying that's gonna happen, but the point is the City has all kinds of control 
here. 
 
3. "This is a State process, so the City has no say in what happens." 
Again, totally false. The OHP in Sacramento is not completely insensitive to how preservation 
affects local communities. Here's one example of what San Mateo could do: when the City is explicitly 
asked for comment on Baywood as part of the state process, the City could comment "We are formally 
opposed to Baywood being a historic district because our local process was not followed first". That's 
not "interference," that's a city asserting its right to comment to a state agency. The City does this all the 
time on various matters. For example here is an example of an opposition letter written to the state by 
the City of Alameda in 2022 for a historic district which was ultimately denied. 
 
942 of 1110

--- page 943 ---
2
Council, your voters mostly HATE historic and think it's a waste of government time and money and 
recognize it as a pure NIMBY move. I know your voters are emailing you this, both at our urging and just 
on their own. Pro-historic wins you precious few votes in San Mateo, even in conservative areas like 
Baywood. 
 
Please continue to work with residents to understand what they really want, especially in Baywood. 
Unfortunately, Baywood's own representative is unwilling to put aside her own personal biases and 
advocate for what a majority of what Baywood residents want, so Baywood's interests are not being 
adequately represented in this process. This puts more pressure on the rest of you on Council, I'm sorry, 
but that's the way it is. I do not envy the job ahead of you but I'm happy to help you all if possible. 
 
Andrew Ryan 
Baywood Homeowner

I am an Owner in District 1. It has come to my attention that the new Historic Preservation Ordinance 
Language in the Municipal Code may not protect Owners Adequately. Please carefully check the

67% must support. At least 50% must respond to the survey.

has an existing, known process for gathering owner consent on residential programs...Parking Permits. 
The threshold for historic resources should actually be higher. "Historic" is an additional disclosure at 
sale, the pool of buyers for historic homes is smaller than for homes not historic and historic homes cost 
more to maintain and insure. 
 
NO BYPASS OF CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS 
The OHP in Sacramento doesn't know the buildings of a City better than the locals. And as you know, the 
OHP does no independent due diligence on nominations. As a result, they have rubber stamped 
"historic" buildings up and down the state nominated by NIMBYs and controlling neighbors. 
 
Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP inform 
them immediately of any nominations of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP does not 
approve any historic resources that have not been reviewed and approved first by the City/Council via 
the local process. 
 
NO REFERENCE TO THE CONTEXT STATEMENT IN THE ORDINANCE 
City Council directed Staff to not put Owners at risk of their homes being made “historic” by reference. 
For example, “Spanish Colonial Revival” is an example of a historic home. My understanding is that if the 
Ordinance references the Context Statement and the Context Statement includes “Spanish Colonial 
Revival”, then all Spanish Colonial Revival homes in greater San Mateo could now be considered 
“historic”. 
 
I ask that the Historic Preservation Language in the Municipal Code be reviewed and approved by the 
Less Red Tape team with support from land use attorney Daniel Freedman who has been fighting the 
weaponization of historic districts up and down the State.

Historic Policy Effort: San Mateo Historic Ordinance, Item 16 on 1/29/26 Agenda
Hello San Mateo City Council Members, 
I attended the San Mateo Historic Policy Virtual Workshop last week. I appreciate the work Rincon 
has done and the challenges they have had to manage in putting the draft ordinance together. Here is 
some background and critical issues to consider when determining the next steps in developing the 
ordinance: 
1.  A conversation between neighbors about historic properties began in 2023. Almost 
immediately, a small group of people, who would become Less Red Tape (LRT), decided to 
use misinformation and fear mongering to scare the daylights out of Baywood residents as the 
community began the process to become a historic district. Numerous meetings were held to 
discuss the fact-based aspects of becoming a historic district, including several hosted by the 
Baywood Neighborhood Association (BNA). Then the real dirty tricks by LRT began. In an 
attempt to intimidate and cause fear, several neighbors were viciously attacked by LRT 
members with lies and distortions. BNA stopped discussing the historic district as the issue 
was becoming so contentious it distracted from BNA’s focus of being a neighborhood uniting 
force. Our District 1 representative decided to recuse herself in any discussions on a Baywood 
historic district. This left only the Heritage Alliance to address the lies, fear mongering, and 
smears of this group. Heritage Alliance pleaded with the City to be the neutral body to make 
the truth known. The City dragged their feet. Heritage Alliance was the sole body to fight the 
misinformation, but fear is a very hard thing to overcome, especially when it comes to 
something as crucial as one’s home and perhaps largest investment. And sadly, there came a 
time when Heritage Alliance became vilified, but it continues to do everything possible to fight 
the misinformation. 
2. Those devoted to special interest groups that focus on opposing historic districts are now 
attending meetings and repeating the lies of LRT. Rincon has valiantly tried to manage the 
combative atmosphere these groups have caused by answering questions truthfully, and 
patiently re-answering when the original answer isn’t deemed acceptable by those unable to 
accept facts. In addition to attendance by the LRT leaders on Wednesday night, there was 
solid representation of the YIMBY crowd, where they dutifully followed the YIMBY national 
mandate to oppose any historic districts. The main YIMBY talking point is that historic districts 
prevent housing (which they DO NOT). The results of the polls at the meeting reflect the fear 
instilled into residents by LRT, and the bullying by them and YIMBY representatives in skewing 
the polls on the call. 
3. It is a clear mandate of fair and equitable governance that in your decisions during Monday 
night’s meeting, you must consider the following: 
a. A super-majority vote, if adopted, will ensure San Mateo will lose all of its historic 
properties over time. The outside interests have no regard for the beauty our 
architecture bestows on our City. They are interested in building, and only building, and 
they want nothing in their way to stop them. San Mateo will lose its soul. The state and 
national processes for historic designation allow for a community’s approval to become 
951 of 1110

--- page 952 ---
2
a historic district. Let them do their job. Don’t try to duplicate or negate the years of 
expertise they provide. 
b. And some questions: (1) Why should City Council have to make a resolution to include 
state or federally listed properties in the inventory? By default, they should be included 
in that inventory. (2) Where did the idea of a vote come from?  Residents don’t vote 
whether or not to be included on state or federal flood zones, fire hazard zones, or 
wetland and riparian zones. (3) There is a huge omission in the draft ordinance in that 
there is no recognition of state and federally listed properties. Nor is there any provision 
documenting that the City has many eligible properties that they have not addressed for 
over 30 years. Is that really in line with the General Plan goals and policies? 
c. As to the focus groups, it would seem to be a no-brainer to ensure a focus group of 
Glazenwood residents was arranged, but it was not. Glazenwood residents love being 
in a historic district; they have direct experience in maintaining their homes under 
Historic District designation. Why were they left out? Why couldn’t the positive input 
they would have provided been reflected in the discussion? 
d. Further to the Glazenwood historic district designation, why is a City Staff Director 
unilaterally deciding that because a piece of paper cannot be found, Glazenwood is not 
a historic district but only “eligible”? I hope the Council will remind this person that he is 
not deputized to make such a decision. A ton of time was spent asking questions about 
this last Wednesday night and he only dug in deeper with his opinion. 
e. As part of this ordinance, I urge the Council to form an independent body of experts on 
historic architecture, not leave it to City staff or an existing Commission, such as 
Planning, that is well known for their anti-historic stance. This should be in the hands of 
experts, not anti-historic district ideologs.  
Thank you for considering this information before moving forward with the Historic Ordinance. The 
City Council should NOT let the future of historic preservation in the City of San Mateo suffer because 
of the misinformation and deception promoted by LRT and YIMBY lobbyists.

1
From:
Kolja Schluetter 
Sent:
Sunday, January 25, 2026 12:15 PM
To:
City Council (San Mateo); Clerk; Alex Khojikian; Historic Policy
Cc:
Zachary Dahl; Planning Commission
Subject:
Request for legal safeguards in Historic Context Statement and draft ordinance
Dear San Mateo City Council and Project Team, 
  
As a follow up to my initial letter, I am writing to request that specific legal safeguards be added to both 
the Draft Historic Context Statement and the accompanying historic preservation ordinance before 
either document advances toward adoption. 
  
As currently drafted, the Historic Context Statement functions as more than a neutral background 
document and creates a material risk that non-designated properties could be treated as “potential 
historical resources” for planning and CEQA purposes. Disclaimers alone are not sufficient to address 
this risk unless they are paired with clear, binding limitations in both the Context Statement and the 
ordinance. 
  
To reduce legal exposure, prevent misuse, and provide clarity for homeowners, applicants, and City 
staff, I respectfully request the following changes. Without these changes consent will be significantly 
eroded. 
  
1. Additions to the Historic Context Statement 
The Context Statement should include clear, operative language stating that: 
 
The document is a narrative reference only and does not identify, designate, determine, or imply 
historic eligibility of any property, site, structure, district, or landscape. 
 
The document may not be used, cited, or relied upon to support CEQA determinations or claims 
of historical resource status. 
 
References to architectural styles, neighborhoods, development patterns, social history, or 
cultural themes do not constitute identification of contributors, potential contributors, or eligible 
resources. 
 
In the event of any conflict, the adopted ordinance controls. 
In addition, eligibility-style language should be removed entirely. This includes references to CRHR or 
NRHP criteria, integrity thresholds, or statements suggesting that properties, patterns, or areas may be 
eligible or warrant further study. 
  
2. Additions to the ordinance 
The ordinance should clearly limit what the City will treat as a historical resource for planning and CEQA 
purposes. Specifically, only the following should qualify: 
 
Properties formally designated as local landmarks 
 
Properties within locally designated historic districts 
 
Properties listed on the California Register of Historical Resources 
956 of 1110

--- page 957 ---
2
 
Properties listed on the National Register of Historic Places 
  
The ordinance should further state that: 
 
Non-designated properties shall not be treated as historical resources or potential historical 
resources for CEQA, discretionary review, demolition review, or project conditioning. 
 
The Historic Context Statement may not be used as evidence of historical significance or 
eligibility. 
 
City staff, consultants, boards, or commissions may not independently determine historic 
eligibility outside the formal designation process. 
 
No historic survey, evaluation, or eligibility analysis of a privately owned property may be initiated 
or required without the written consent of the property owner, unless the property is already 
designated or listed. 
 
Compliance with the ordinance and reliance on designated resources constitutes the City’s 
complete consideration of historical resources under CEQA. 
  
3. Importance of sequencing 
These limitations should be adopted concurrently with, or prior to, adoption of the Historic Context 
Statement. Adopting an expansive context document without binding ordinance guardrails creates 
avoidable legal risk and unnecessary uncertainty. 
  
These changes are necessary to ensure historic preservation policy is precise, defensible, and 
consistent with CEQA, state housing law, and basic principles of due process.  
  
Thank you for your consideration. 
  
Best regards, 
 
Kolja Schluetter 
On Jan 20, 2026 at 7:27 PM -0800, Kolja Schluetter 
, wrote: 
 
Dear San Mateo Council Members and Project Team, 
  
I have reviewed the Draft Historic Context Statement (November 2025), the accompanying 
draft ordinance framework, and the outreach conducted to date. My comments are not a 
critique of historic preservation as a goal, but of how this effort is currently structured, 
sequenced, and implemented.  
As drafted, the combined approach creates unnecessary legal risk, policy imbalance, and 
public distrust. 
  
1. Historic Context Statement – scope and downstream risk 
The Historic Context Statement goes well beyond its intended role as a neutral reference 
document. It repeatedly elevates broad development patterns, subdivision layouts, 
neighborhood form, and social history from contextual background to independently 
957 of 1110

--- page 958 ---
3
significant resources. This shifts preservation review away from discrete, evaluable 
resources toward open-ended, neighborhood-scale claims and materially lowers the 
threshold for asserting historical significance. 
  
Although the document states it does not designate resources, its structure and language 
invite pre-designation eligibility arguments. By exhaustively mapping themes and 
associations while emphasizing that CRHR eligibility does not require NRHP-level integrity, 
the document enables almost any property older than roughly 45 years to be argued as 
historically eligible, even when physical integrity or architectural merit is weak. This 
effectively turns context into de facto regulation without legislative safeguards. 
  
The heavy reliance on non-physical attributes such as association, feeling, setting, and 
cultural narratives further increases subjectivity and uncertainty. The extensive discussion 
of cultural and ethnic history, while important, is framed in a way that strongly ties social 
history to physical location. As written, code-compliant redevelopment can be challenged 
on the basis of alleged cultural erasure or displacement even where no designated 
resource exists. This moves preservation review into socio-political adjudication rather 
than objective resource evaluation. 
  
Finally, by broadening what may constitute a “potential historical resource,” the City 
is materially increasing CEQA exposure. Projects that would otherwise qualify for 
ministerial or streamlined review can now be forced into discretionary review or an 
EIR. This risk is foreseeable and should be acknowledged directly. 
  
  
2. Ordinance – missing guardrails and due process 
Several elements of the draft ordinance require substantial correction before adoption. 
  
First, the consent process for historic district applications is fundamentally flawed. Relying 
on applicant-collected petition signatures is not verifiable and is inconsistent with every 
other City process involving neighbor consent. The City, not the applicant, must manage 
notification and consent verification. A supermajority threshold of at least two-thirds of 
affected property owners should be required, with non-responses counted as no 
consent. This is consistent with existing City practice, for example the installation of 
public street speed bumps requires 67% approval of affected homeowners. 
  
Second, if an applicant attempts to bypass the local process by submitting a district 
application directly to the Office of Historic Preservation, the City Manager should be 
required to formally notify OHP that the City does not support the application. This should 
explicitly include the pending Baywood state application. 
  
Third, there should be no new standalone Historic Preservation Commission. Oversight 
should remain within the Planning Department to avoid duplicative bureaucracy and 
maintain accountability within existing structures. 
  
958 of 1110

--- page 959 ---
4
Finally, contributor status must be properly researched and documented. Drive-by or 
superficial assessments are inadequate. If a homeowner disputes contributor 
classification, the applicant must be required to meaningfully revise the application and 
address contrary evidence. 
  
  
3. Outreach – failure to meet Council direction 
The outreach conducted to date does not comply with Council’s direction from last year. 
Rincon and staff were explicitly tasked with collecting input on the ordinance itself. 
Instead, meetings focused largely on general sentiments about San Mateo. 
  
Rincon’s own materials show only 163 community member touchpoints, many of which 
represent repeat participants counted multiple times. With the exception of the mobile 
workshops, attendance was dominated by SMHA and LRT members. This is not a broad or 
representative cross-section of the community. Critically, Council-directed questions, 
such as what consent thresholds are meaningful to residents, were not addressed at all. 
  
In effect, approximately $60,000 was spent to hear from roughly 95 new voices about what 
they like about San Mateo, not about the policy choices at issue. 
  
  
Conclusion 
Taken together, the Historic Context Statement, ordinance draft, and outreach process 
reflect a flawed sequencing and an imbalance between preservation objectives, housing 
obligations, and property rights. The current approach expands regulatory reach, lowers 
evidentiary thresholds, and defers critical policy decisions while exposing the City to 
avoidable legal and political risk. 
I strongly urge the City to pause adoption, revise the Historic Context Statement to clearly 
separate background history from eligibility frameworks, complete and tighten the 
ordinance guardrails, and conduct ordinance-focused outreach that reflects the broader 
community. 
  
Thank you for considering these comments. I would welcome further discussion. 
  
Best regards, 
  
Kolja Schluetter 
959 of 1110

CONSENT AT LEAST THAT FOR PARKING PERMITS! 
67% must support. At least 50% must respond to the survey. 
We don't need to reinvent the wheel and spend more money on this than necessary! San Mateo already has 
an existing, known process for gathering owner consent on residential programs...Parking Permits. The 
threshold for historic resources should actually be higher. "Historic" is an additional disclosure at sale, the pool 
of buyers for historic homes is smaller than for homes not historic and historic homes cost more to maintain 
and insure. 
 
NO BYPASS OF CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS 
The OHP in Sacramento doesn't know the buildings of a City better than the locals. And as you know, the OHP 
does no independent due diligence on nominations. As a result, they have rubber stamped "historic" buildings 
up and down the state nominated by NIMBYs and controlling neighbors.  
 
Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP inform them 
immediately of any nominations of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP does not approve any 
historic resources that have not been reviewed and approved first by the City/Council via the local process. 
 
Thank you for ensuring that private citizens can't encumber their neighbors properties without the Owners 
awareness and consent!

Re: Council should have a choice on the Historic Ordinance Language
Dear City Council Members: 
  
I am writing as a fed-up San Mateo resident who is tired of seeing City Hall captured by the private priorities of Lisa Diaz 
Nash and her husband, Mike Nash, at the expense of the rest of us. 
  
The Baywood “historic district” push is not about history.  It is about power, control, and protecting one wealthy enclave 
from change while the city burns time and money on “process theater.”  The fact that the Mayor’s own household is 
deeply entangled in this effort is a glaring conflict of interest, and everyone on this council knows it. 
  
I am sick of watching staff hours, consultant dollars, and political capital poured into a vanity of useless projects that exist 
to give a small circle of insiders more leverage over their neighbors’ property.  Ordinary residents are struggling with real 
issues: crime, homelessness, filthy and unsafe streets, traffic, schools under pressure, and a brutal cost of living.  Instead 
of tackling those important issues, you are pouring fuel on a manufactured “heritage” drama for one of the richest 
neighborhoods in the city. 
  
I attended the City Council meeting last January, where you approved a historic district preservation consulting contract 
with Rincon Consultants for a total not-to-exceed amount of approximately $328,000.  Last Thursday I attended the 
Rincon virtual meeting, and I believe that you’ve already incurred $550K.  My understanding is that we’re already 
running a multi-million deficit, and taxes go up every single year.  This is just more government regulation and more 
waste!  We don’t have revenue problems at the Federal, State, or Local levels.  We have spending problems! 
  
In 2022, the Council voted 4-1 to install the Humboldt / Popular lanes as part of a federal-funded safer project.  By early 
2025 you voted 3-2 to partially remove the lanes and spend up to ~$2 million to restore roughly half the parking, while 
proposing a less-protected bike-boulevard alternative. 
  
Why are you so incompetent? 
    
Let me be crystal clear: 
-          Shut down the Baywood historic district process and any related “heritage” overlays immediately 
-          Stop burning staff time and legal resources to engineer special protections for the Mayor’s neighborhood 
-          Refuse any new commission, design review, or overlay that gives politically connected homeowners a veto 
over by-right projects 
-          Start cutting waste and lowering the tax and fee burden instead of inventing new layers of government to 
micromanage private property 
  
You were not elected to be hall monitors for rooflines and paint colors.  You were elected to keep this city safe, clean, and 
functional, and to respect the basic principle that residents – not a handful of well-connected activists and their spouse – 
decide what happens on their own lots within the law. 
  
I expect you to end this Baywood historic-district nonsense, stop enabling conflicts of interest around the Nash household, 
and get back to doing the core job you were elected to do.  
  
Respectfully, 
  
Peter R. Stone
