Norma Formanek 
 
 
 
January 26, 2026 
 
 
VIA EMAIL TO:   rnewsom@cityofsanmateo.org, aloraine@cityofsanmateo.org, 
dcwirkogodycki@cityofsanmateo.org, ldiaznash@cityofsanmateo.org, 
nfernandez@cityofsanmateo.org, clerk@cityofsanmateo.org 
cc:   akhojikian@cityofsanmateo.org, prasiah@cityofsanmateo.org 
SUBJECT:  Initial Draft Update San Mateo Historic Preservation Ordinance 
Dear Council Members: 
The draft Historic Preservation ordinance is overly broad and contains ambiguities 
that must be resolved before it can be evaluated for Council action.  I will address 
only section 27.66.100, Certificate of Appropriateness, which places broad new 
burdens on homeowners and the City.      
Scope of the Ordinance.  The ordinance would require the owner of a 
“Contributing” home in an Historic District to apply for a Certificate of 
Appropriateness for virtually any “alter[ation]” to 
structural, architectural details or visual characteristics; additions; and 
placement or removal of any significant objects affecting the significant exterior 
visual and/or historical qualities of the property.  (Definitions, Major 
Alteration)   
This requirement applies even if “no other permits or entitlements” are needed.  As a 
result, any “visual” change to my home not requiring a permit today would likely 
trigger the Certificate requirement. 
As drafted, this clause seems also to mean:   
 Alterations that are “structural” and “visual” are covered, whether or not they are 
also historic . 
968 of 1110

--- page 969 ---
 The Ordinance isn’t limited to changes to the home’s exterior or to street 
elevations.  The reference to “exterior” in the above definition appears to apply 
only the “placement or removal” of a significant “object,” not to changes to 
the building itself.  Three limitations to street elevations are narrow:    
demolition;  work requiring a permit on street-facing or side elevations but only 
if the structure is a Historic Resources, not a Contributor; and addition of 
square footage (see 27.66.020(a)(2), (a)(3) and (g).  Addition of a backyard deck, 
for example, would appear to be trigger City review.  
 The Certificate is required for “new” construction on a property that contains a 
Historic Resource or Contributing Property if that construction “could affect 
its visual appearance,” whether or not it does.  This seems to support media 
speculation that this entire effort is an end-run around the State’s recent 
adoption of laws encouraging development of ADUs, in which case litigation 
seems inevitable.  Then again, it may just be an example of early drafting that 
needs tightening up.    
 The “exemption” for repair and maintenance applies only if the work is 
determined (by whom?) not to involve “change in exterior design, material, or 
appearance.   Simply repainting a home would at a minimum require someone 
to “determine” that the paint doesn’t result in an “alteration.”   
 Even so-called “Minor Alterations” that have been “determined to have limited 
potential to affect the defining character and architectural style of a Listed 
Resource or Contributor” require a certificate.   
Is it the City’s intent to place such broad range of new restrictions on home 
ownership?   
Who Decides?  The Ordinance draft doesn’t tell us who decides whether a 
Certificate of Appropriateness is needed or will be granted.   It refers to unnamed 
“reviewing” and “approval” bodies, which the City may not yet have decided.  That 
decision must consider important questions such as:     
 What is the role of the City’s professional staff?  The Zoning Administrator is 
given limited power to review and approve Certificates, but that is subject to 
both public notice and further review by the “reviewing body.”   
969 of 1110

--- page 970 ---
 What subject-matter expertise will required of the officials charged with 
enforcement of this ordinance?   Does the City have sufficient resources for 
timely processing of applications? 
 How will the objectivity of the decision-maker be ensured.  Many cities 
“outsource” historic preservation review.   Does San Mateo intend to do that 
and if so, how?   
 Are there any objective, measurable standards under which a Certificate can be 
denied?   
 What recourse does the homeowner have after denial?   
Crafting ordinances that are clear and workable for both the City and its residents is a 
complex process.  If this Ordinance is to go forward, it needs significant rethinking, 
redrafting and public comment to allow the Council to make a reasoned decision.  I 
urge the Council to take that time and not rush to adoption of an overbroad and 
ambiguous set of regulations. 
Sincerely, 
 
Norma Formanek

I am an Owner in District 5. It has come to my attention that the new Historic Preservation Ordinance 
Language in the Municipal Code may not protect Owners Adequately. Please carefully check the 
language in these three areas.

67% must support. At least 50% must respond to the survey. 
We don't need to reinvent the wheel and spend more money on this than necessary! San Mateo already 
has an existing, known process for gathering owner consent on residential programs...Parking Permits. 
The threshold for historic resources should actually be higher. "Historic" is an additional disclosure at 
sale, the pool of buyers for historic homes is smaller than for homes not historic and historic homes cost 
more to maintain and insure. 
 
NO BYPASS OF CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS 
The OHP in Sacramento doesn't know the buildings of a City better than the locals. And as you know, the 
OHP does no independent due diligence on nominations. As a result, they have rubber stamped 
"historic" buildings up and down the state nominated by NIMBYs and controlling neighbors. 
 
Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP inform 
them immediately of any nominations of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP does not 
approve any historic resources that have not been reviewed and approved first by the City/Council via 
the local process. 
 
NO REFERENCE TO THE CONTEXT STATEMENT IN THE ORDINANCE 
City Council directed Staff to not put Owners at risk of their homes being made “historic” by reference. 
For example, “Spanish Colonial Revival” is an example of a historic home. My understanding is that if the 
Ordinance references the Context Statement and the Context Statement includes “Spanish Colonial 
Revival”, then all Spanish Colonial Revival homes in greater San Mateo could now be considered 
“historic”. 
 
I ask that the Historic Preservation Language in the Municipal Code be reviewed and approved by the 
Less Red Tape team with support from land use attorney Daniel Freedman who has been fighting the 
weaponization of historic districts up and down the State. 
 
Thank you for ensuring that private citizens can't encumber their neighbors properties without the

resources should actually be higher. "Historic" is an addiƟonal disclosure at sale, the pool of buyers for historic homes is 
smaller than for homes not historic and historic homes cost more to maintain and insure. 
 
NO BYPASS OF CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS The OHP in Sacramento doesn't know the buildings of a City 
beƩer than the locals. And as you know, the OHP does no independent due diligence on nominaƟons. As a result, they 
have rubber stamped "historic" buildings up and down the state nominated by NIMBYs and controlling neighbors. 
 
Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP inform them 
immediately of any nominaƟons of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP does not approve any historic 
resources that have not been reviewed and approved ﬁrst by the City/Council via the local process. 
 
NO REFERENCE TO THE CONTEXT STATEMENT IN THE ORDINANCE City Council directed Staﬀ to not put Owners at risk of 
their homes being made “historic” by reference. For example, “Spanish Colonial Revival” is an example of a historic 
home. My understanding is that if the Ordinance references the Context Statement and the Context Statement includes 
“Spanish Colonial Revival”, then all Spanish Colonial Revival homes in greater San Mateo could now be considered 
“historic”.

Hello City Council Members:

| am writing today regarding the Historic Policy work. | attended the Virtual Workshop last week and was
impressed by the work of your consultants. | appreciated the polls that they included.

However, | am very worried that consent of owners in proposed historic districts will not be included in
the final ordinance. | strongly believe that at least 2/3 of the owner's consent to the to historic district
before it can be imposed. | also think that every effort should be made to have at least 50% of the owners
respond. Please ensure that this is included in the final ordinance. This is only fair, since the owners of
the houses will be the ones affected by the designation.

| also believe there should be no bypass of the City for historic resource nominations. Do not rely on the
State Office of Historic Preservation to designate historic resources. The office doesn't do independent
due diligence. (lam very concerned that this will happen to the proposed Baywood district.)

Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP
inform them immediately of any nominations of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP
does not approve any historic resources that have not been reviewed and approved first by the
City/Council via the local process.

Nancy Schneider

here, now is the time to put in strong informed consent protections and make sure historic resource 
nominations don't bypass City Processes / the Council as the OHP does no independent due

67% must support. At least 50% must respond to the survey.

already has an existing, known process for gathering owner consent on residential programs, namely 
Parking Permits. The threshold for historic resources should be higher. "Historic" is an additional 
disclosure at sale, the pool of buyers for historic homes is smaller than for homes not historic and 
historic homes cost more to maintain and insure. 
 
NO BYPASS OF CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS 
The OHP in Sacramento doesn't know the buildings of a City better than the locals. And as you know, 
the OHP does no independent due diligence on nominations. As a result, they have rubber stamped 
"historic" buildings up and down the state nominated by NIMBYs and controlling neighbors.  
 
Please add language to the Historic Ordinance whereby the City Manager requests that the OHP 
inform them immediately of any nominations of resources within their City Limits and that the OHP 
does not approve any historic resources that have not been reviewed and approved first by the 
City/Council via the local process.

Dear San Mateo City Council (less Lisa Diaz Nash) plus Clerk and City Attorney, 
 
I am a homeowner in District 3 writing as an individual. I recently learned that there is an effort to designate homes as "historic" using 
Historic Preservation Language in the Municipal Code that could potentially affect many, if not most, homes across the city. This is a 
giant step backwards when we are trying to build more, not less, housing, to address the acute housing shortage and affordability crisis 
in the region.

homeowners do *not* want their house designated as historic, especially houses barely older than Diet Coke, rollerblades, and 
Cabbage Patch Kids.

1
From:
Joel Scott 
Sent:
Monday, January 26, 2026 1:26 PM
To:
Rob Newsom; Adam Loraine; Danielle Cwirko-Godycki; Nicole Fernandez; Clerk
Cc:
; Alex Khojikian; Prasanna Rasiah
Subject:
D1: Historic Ordinance - Consent, No Local Bypass, No Ref to Context Stmt
NOTE: This is NOT an AI-generated message and I am a real person. You can verify this by replying to this 
email. 
Dear San Mateo City Council and City Clerk, 
I am a homeowner in District 1. It has come to my attention that proposed updates to the Historic 
Preservation Ordinance may not adequately protect property owners. I ask that the draft be carefully 
reviewed and clarified in the three areas below. 
1. CONSENT (AT LEAST THE PARKING PERMIT STANDARD) 
At a minimum: (i) 67% of affected property owners must affirmatively support designation, and (ii) at 
least 50% of affected property owners must respond to the survey/ballot. 
San Mateo already has a known process for obtaining owner consent for residential programs (parking 
permits). A historic designation can create additional permitting complexity and cost, ongoing 
maintenance/insurance issues, and a reduced pool of prospective buyers. The consent standard 
for any historic designation should be at least as protective as existing City programs. 
2. NO BYPASS OF THE CITY FOR HISTORIC RESOURCE NOMINATIONS 
The California Office of Historic Preservation (OHP) is not positioned to evaluate San Mateo properties 
better than local processes. As you know, the OHP have approved "historic" buildings up and down the state with no 
independent due diligence on nominations. This is problematic and raises serious concerns for homeowners impacted by the "historic" 
designation. 
Please add clear language requiring that the City Manager be notified immediately of any OHP 
nomination affecting property within City limits, and that the City will not support (and requests 
OHP not to approve) any nomination that has not first been reviewed and acted on through the 
City’s local process. 
3. NO INCORPORATION OF THE “CONTEXT STATEMENT” BY REFERENCE 
My understanding is that if the Ordinance incorporates the Citywide Historic Context Statement by 
reference, then broad categories included in that Context Statement (e.g., “Spanish Colonial Revival”) 
could be treated as presumptively “historic” in a way that risks sweeping in large numbers of homes 
without clear, individualized review and affirmative owner consent. Please ensure the Ordinance does 
not incorporate the Context Statement by reference in a way that expands designation scope, and 
clarify that the Context Statement is informational/guidance only and does not itself create 
designations. 
982 of 1110

--- page 983 ---
2
I ask that the final Historic Preservation Ordinance language be reviewed with input from the Less 
Red Tape team and land use attorney Daniel Freedman, given their experience with the 
downstream impacts of historic designations on owners and communities. 
Thank you for ensuring that private citizens cannot impose significant encumbrances on their neighbors’ 
properties without owners’ clear awareness and affirmative consent. 
Sincerely, 
Joel Scott 
983 of 1110

--- page 984 ---
P.O. Box 146 San Mateo, CA 94402 
www.smheritage.org 
January 26, 2026 
City Council 
City of San Mateo 
330 W. 20th Street 
San Mateo, CA 94402 
SUBJECT:  Comments on the Draft Historic Context Statement and Draft Historic Preservation 
Ordinance 
Dear Mayor Lorraine and Members of the City Council: 
Thank you for preparing the draft Citywide Historic Context Statement and draft Historic Preservation 
Ordinance as part of implementing the 2040 General Plan. The San Mateo Heritage Alliance appreciates 
the opportunity to provide input and urges the City to ensure these documents maintain—and ideally 
strengthen—San Mateo’s longstanding commitment to historic preservation. 
San Mateo’s preservation framework has developed over decades through City Council resolutions, 
General Plan policies, cooperative agreements with the State Office of Historic Preservation, the 1989 
City of San Mateo Historic Building Survey Final Report (accepted by the State in 1990), and 
subsequent ordinance updates (originally adopted in 1993, expanded citywide in 1995, refined in 2010, 
and aligned with state law in 2022). The 1989 Survey remains the foundational record for identifying 
resources, evaluating districts, and supporting programs like the Mills Act. 
KEY COMMENTS 
The draft documents are incomplete and risk weakening existing protections: 
1. Incomplete Documents and Non-Compliance with State Guidance 
The Historic Context Statement lacks required sections (including critical policy content) and 
fails to reference known historic districts, nominations (e.g., the proposed Baywood Historic 
District, now advancing toward National Register listing), and other elements of San Mateo’s 
preservation history. Meaningful public review requires complete, consistent documents. The 
draft Ordinance omits several elements recommended in the State Office of Historic 
Preservation’s Technical Assistance Bulletin #14, including:  
• 
Identification of enabling authority with qualified experts (e.g., architectural historians 
and architects)  
• 
Establishment of a dedicated Historic Preservation Commission with qualified members  
• 
Appeals procedures  
• 
Severability clause  
984 of 1110

--- page 985 ---
City Council 
January 26, 2026 
Page 2 
 
 
 
2 
• 
Clear policies on what the City considers important to preserve  
• 
Detailed definitions (e.g., major/minor alterations, adverse effect, Historic Resources 
Inventory, character-defining features, important architectural styles, design trends, 
complementary design) 
ACTION: The missing information must be included in the Statement and Ordinance and 
provided for meaningful public review. 
2. Weakened Protections Compared to Current Practices and Neighboring Cities 
The draft is minimalist and reduces safeguards relative to existing regulations (as described on 
the City’s website) and ordinances in comparable cities like Palo Alto, Burlingame, and 
Redwood City. Notable differences include:  
• 
No dedicated, qualified historic commission (all three neighbors have one)  
• 
Stringent owner-consent requirements (supermajority for districts; owner-only 
nominations), unlike neighbors’ more proactive and city-led processes with fewer 
consent hurdles  
• 
Arbitrary percentage thresholds for contributing properties in districts (inconsistent 
with state/federal practices, which define districts by geography and integrity, not fixed 
percentages)  
• 
Discretionary Historic Resource Evaluations (HREs) for buildings over 45 years old 
(weakens current CEQA-informed requirements)  
• 
Reliance on outdated surveys (e.g., 1989 Survey >35 years old; standard validity is 5–10 
years) without mandating updates  
• 
Broad exemptions for safety-related work (buildings can often be repaired without 
exemption)  
• 
Weak enforcement, penalties, maintenance definitions (“good repair” should specify full 
paint coverage, repair of exposed wood, etc.), and incentives (expand beyond Mills Act 
basics, e.g., reduced fees, grants) 
ACTIONS:  
1. Include an architectural historian and architect to be the reviewing body. 
2. Follow state and federal policies using >50% approval for owner consent in historic 
districts. 
3. Remove provision for an arbitrary percentage of properties that must be contributors in 
historic districts.  
985 of 1110

--- page 986 ---
City Council 
January 26, 2026 
Page 3 
 
 
 
3 
3. Failure to Recognize State- and Federally-Listed Resources 
It is profoundly inappropriate—and contrary to shared cultural heritage, democratic processes, 
and community well-being—for the City to disregard or exclude properties listed on the 
California Register of Historical Resources or National Register of Historic Places. These listings 
already reflect rigorous, expert evaluation (including owner consent where applicable) and 
should trigger ordinance protections without requiring redundant local adoption. The 
ordinance must apply to development projects. 
ACTIONS: Revise the ordinance to ensure that State and National Register properties are 
included in the inventory and are subject to the Historic Preservation Ordinance.  
4. Unclear Historic Resources Inventory 
The draft does not clearly define the Inventory or fully incorporate prior Council actions, 
surveys, and resolutions (e.g., omitting key references to the 1989 Survey, 1990 General Plan, 
Mills Act implementations, Glazenwood and Downtown Historic Districts, Yoshiko 
Yamanouchi House Historic District, survey-identified contributors, and proposed Baywood 
Historic District). The 1988 resolution 113 to enter into a Memorandum of Agreement with the 
State Office of Historic Preservation identified the 1989 Survey as the city historic resources 
inventory states “2. The City shall f. compile and [sic] inventory of historic resource that are of 
importance to the community;” This omission risks inconsistent enforcement, erosion of long-
standing records, and loss of public confidence.  
5. Identify Glazenwood as a local historic district. Glazenwood, identified in multiple General 
Plans and Housing Elements as one of two historic districts, must not be downgraded through 
administrative ambiguity. Glazenwood is listed as a historic district in the 1989 Survey, and as 
specified in the 1988 Memorandum of Agreement, is part of the city inventory. 
ACTIONS: Identify Glazenwood as an historic district, similar to the Downtown Historic 
District. 
6. Other Specific Improvements/Actions Needed  
• 
Strengthen purpose/intent to align fully with General Plan policies CD 5.1–5.6 (remove 
or clarify ambiguous terms like “use” of resources; define “where feasible,” “infeasible,” 
and “appropriate alternatives” for avoiding demolition)  
• 
Add local designation criteria highlighting San Mateo’s special features (e.g., pre-1950 
homes, Eichlers, Downtown integrity, “City of Beautiful Homes” legacy)  
• 
Require review by qualified architectural historians for exterior modifications to older 
buildings (not just staff)  
986 of 1110

--- page 987 ---
City Council 
January 26, 2026 
Page 4 
 
 
 
4 
• 
Mandate timelines for all review steps to prevent delays.  
• 
Include community education provisions during designations (with knowledgeable staff 
at meetings to counter misinformation, as seen in Baywood discussions)  
• 
Revise consent to match state/federal norms: allow nominations by anyone (as historic 
value is a public good), but require owner consent for individual listings and majority 
(>50%) support for districts.  
• 
Expand incentives, detail Mills Act processes, define/enforce “good repair,” and 
strengthen penalties/abatement.  
• 
Apply HREs mandatorily for relevant projects; tie Certificate of Appropriateness 
conditions explicitly to Secretary of the Interior’s Standards; exempt interior work with 
unchanged openings. 
Requested Actions 
We respectfully request that the City Council direct staff to implement the above actions including:  
• 
Clarify and confirm that the Historic Resources Inventory includes all resources and districts 
identified through prior Council actions, the 1989 Survey, and state/federal listings (without 
requiring redundant local adoption for the latter).  
• 
Direct staff to complete and correct both the Context Statement and Ordinance, incorporating 
the above refinements, for additional public review before adoption.  
• 
Ensure the final documents are at least as protective as current practices and comparable to 
those in Palo Alto, Burlingame, and Redwood City—robust, equitable, expert-driven, and 
balanced with housing and property rights goals. 
These changes seek continuity with existing Council intent, not new expansions. They would enhance 
transparency, legal defensibility, and effectiveness while honoring San Mateo’s irreplaceable 
architectural and cultural heritage. 
Thank you for your commitment to this important work. We look forward to reviewing the revised 
drafts. 
Sincerely, 
 
Laurie Hietter 
President 
San Mateo Heritage Alliance

• Need for stronger tools, standards, and professional oversight
• Support for inclusive, educational, and adaptive approaches
• Business, Real Estate, and Development Community
• Need for clear, practical guidance, including coherence across plans
• Support for flexible solutions for historic buildings
• Concern about financial burdens to projects

• Is associated with events that have made a 
significant contribution to the broad patterns of San 
Mateo’s or California’s history and cultural heritage;

communities important to San Mateo’s or 
California’s past; or

period, region, or method of construction, or

Effects of Designation - Certificate of Appropriateness 
23
A Certificate of Appropriateness is the approval document issued by the City to 
approve an alteration to a Listed Resource or Contributor within a Historic District 
listed in the San Mateo Historic Resources Inventory.
Not required for non-contributors within a Historic District
Two types of projects subject to COA
Minor Alteration – An alteration that has been determined to have a limited potential to affect 
the defining character and architectural style of a resource.



Replacing windows in-kind

Reroofing

Demolition of a detached garage

the exterior visual and/or historical qualities of the property.

Demolition

Addition of square footage and/or architectural features to street-facing elevations

Historic District Designation Process
24
27.66.070 Procedure for Designating Historic Districts
1.
Application. Application made by property owner/group of property owners within HD 
boundary. Application includes:

Description of significance/applicability of local criteria for designation

Documentation that notification was provided to all potentially affected property owners

Documentation that % of potentially affected property owners support the application
2.
Application Review. Application is reviewed by Planning Division to assess application if 
application is complete and if notification/consent information was included. 
3.
Community Meeting. Applicant holds a community meeting to provide information to 
potentially affected property owners on the status of the application.
4.
Designation Recommendation. The reviewing body reviews the application at a public 
meeting and makes a recommendation to City Council. 
5.
Designation Action. City Council holds a public hearing to consider the application and adopt 
a resolution to approve or deny the application.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  17
Section Name: PUBLIC HEARING
Account Number: 25-3112
File ID: 26-10814
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Alex Khojikian, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Community Development Department
MEETING DATE:
January 26, 2026 
SUBJECT:
Accessory Dwelling Unit Ordinance - Zoning Code Amendments — Ordinance Introduction
RECOMMENDATION:
Introduce an Ordinance to amend San Mateo Municipal Code Chapter 27.19, “Accessory Dwelling Unit and Junior 
Accessory Dwelling Unit – Residential Zones” to address comments from the California Department of Housing and 
Community Development (HCD) to align with current state law, and to address Housing Element programs, and find it 
exempt from CEQA pursuant to Section 21080.17 of the Public Resources Code and Section 15282(h) of the CEQA 
Guidelines. 
BACKGROUND:
Since 2016, the State Legislature has passed several state laws to streamline permitting and promote construction of 
Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs) across California. The City of San Mateo adopted its current local ADU Ordinance on 
March 21, 2022 to implement local regulations for ADUs in alignment with State law, and with City Council directing staff 
to provide annual updates on implementation. Since adoption in 2022, the City has seen significant ADU permitting and 
construction activity, with over 300 permits issued (164 of which have been finalized) over the past three and half years 
(an average of 87 permits issued per year).
As part of the City’s adopted Housing Element (2023-2031), the City prioritized increased ADU production (Policy H1.4) and 
supported it with six implementation actions, including an action focused on amending the City’s ADU Ordinance to align it 
with current State law and to resolve any inconsistencies identified by the California Department of Housing and 
Community Development (HCD). HCD has had statutory authority to review local agencies’ ADU Ordinances since 2016, 
and as of January 1, 2024, also has authority to enforce all State ADU laws. In October 2024, the City received a letter from 
HCD summarizing the agency’s review of the City’s ADU Ordinance (Attachment 3). The HCD letter identified specific 
ordinance provisions requiring updates or clarification. An official City response letter was sent to HCD in November 2024 
(Attachment 4), with a stated goal to update the ADU Ordinance by the end of 2025. 
In January 2025, HCD also published an updated ADU Handbook1  addressing frequently asked questions and summarizing 
recent changes to State ADU law. One notable change is Senate Bill (SB) 1211, effective January 1, 2025, which allows for 
up to eight detached ADUs to be constructed on a lot with an existing multifamily dwelling, provided that the number of 
ADUs does not exceed the number of existing units on the lot. 
On August 26, 2025, a Planning Commission study session was held to introduce ADU Ordinance updates and receive 
Commission feedback. There were a total of four public comments submitted in writing and three public speakers 
provided verbal comments (two of which also submitted their comments in writing). The comments are included as 
1 ADU Handbook link
1020 of 1110

--- page 1021 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Attachment 5.
During this study session, the Planning Commission provided staff with feedback on specific local changes, outside of those 
required for State law compliance, in order to address community needs.  Following the study session, staff conducted 
outreach and hosted a virtual workshop on local ADU regulations and proposed changes on September 25, 2025. The 
workshop included a staff presentation, polling questions to collect information and feedback, and time for attendees to 
ask questions related to the proposed ADU Ordinance update effort. Information related to this policy effort is included on 
the City’s Policy and Zoning Code Update page. 
On December 9, 2025, the Planning Commission held a public hearing to consider the updated ADU Ordinance. There was 
one comment submitted in writing and no public speakers provided verbal comments at the meeting.  The written 
comments provided were from the California Housing Defense Fund (CalHDF) regarding ADU size limits, parking, and the 
ADU Discretionary Review process (Attachment 5). Based on the fact that HCD has reviewed and provided input on the 
draft ADU Ordinance and did not raise concerns with any of the items noted by CalHDF, staff did not identify any changes 
that were legally required based on this comment letter. 
Following the staff presentation and deliberation, the Commission voted to recommend approval of the updated ADU 
Ordinance with one modification proposed by staff and three additional modifications for City Council consideration. 
These recommendations are as follows:
1.
Per staff recommendation, amend Section 27.19.070 to add the following subsection: 27.19.070(b)(2)(C) “Any 
additional ADUs as allowed pursuant to State law”.
2.
ADU Discretionary Review (ADUDR) application appeals in Section 27.19.045 should be heard by the Planning 
Commission, and if appealed again, by City Council. This is in conformance with other appeals procedures in the 
Zoning Code (Title 27), and was recommended by the Planning Commission, rather than for appeals to be heard by 
the City Manager or their designee.
3.
Clarify Section 27.19.045 to state that the ADUDR process may be used to review an ADU proposal that is not 
compliant with any objective standard in the ADU Ordinance, with the exception of FAR maximums. 
4.
Change the standard for location of second story decks or balconies proposed with an ADU in Section 
27.19.060(h)(5) to state that the deck or balcony “shall not face adjacent residential properties.”
DISCUSSION:
Proposed Ordinance Amendments
The proposed ADU Ordinance amendments are included in the Draft Ordinance (Attachment 1). A version with tracked 
changes to show where edits have been proposed to the existing ADU Ordinance text is also included as Attachment 2. 
Some of these edits are more minor/structural to improve organization for clarity, while others would result in a change to 
existing ADU development standards. A complete summary of those changes that result in a new or changed ADU 
development standard is given below. 
Changes Proposed in Response to HCD Comments and State Law Compliance
The changes listed below have been made primarily to address specific comments provided by HCD in their review of the 
City’s ADU Ordinance. These changes include updates to areas of the Ordinance either that are inconsistent with State 
ADU law, or where additional clarification was requested by HCD to demonstrate State law compliance. 
1.
ADU Definition
HCD commented  that the City’s ADU definition only includes reference to single-family ADUs. This is inconsistent 
with State law, as ADUs must be allowed on lots with single-family or multifamily dwellings.  In response, the ADU 
definition has been revised to clarify that ADUs are allowed on lots with either single-family or multifamily 
dwellings.
1021 of 1110

--- page 1022 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
2.
Multifamily Dwelling Structure Definition
The City’s zoning ordinance currently distinguishes between two-family and multifamily dwellings in a manner that 
is not fully consistent with State ADU law. To resolve this, the City added a new definition of “Multifamily Dwelling 
Structure” applicable only to ADU development, consistent with State law, to clarify that a multifamily structure 
consists of two or more attached primary dwelling units.
3.
Allowance for Separate Sale of ADUs per Government Code Section 66341
The City’s existing ordinance prohibits ADUs from being sold separately from the primary residence, with no 
exceptions specified. HCD identified that this is inconsistent with Government Code Section 66341, which requires 
that the City allow for separate sale of ADUs that are built or developed by a qualified nonprofit corporation, with 
additional specific qualifying criteria. Therefore, the update references this statute to allow for separate sales of 
ADUs, only when specified criteria are met.
4.
Proportionality for ADU Impact Fees
The City’s existing ordinance allows ADU impact fees to be set by City Council resolution. HCD noted that the 
ordinance should clearly reflect State law by exempting ADUs under 750 square feet from impact fees and 
requiring proportional fees for larger ADUs. The ordinance has been revised to explicitly incorporate these 
requirements.
5.
Utility Fee Clarification
Separate from impact fees, State law allows for a jurisdiction to charge a connection fee or capacity charge for 
utilities. This charge must be proportionate to the burden of the proposed ADU, based either on square footage or 
number of drainage fixture unit values. Currently, the City’s ADU Ordinance does include a provision to require 
payment of a connection fee or capacity charge for ADUs that are separate from an existing structure. However, 
this connection fee or capacity charge may only be imposed when an ADU is constructed with a new single-family 
dwelling. Therefore, language has been added to clarify that, “An ADU shall not be considered a new residential 
use for the purposes of calculating connection fees or capacity charges for utilities, including water and sewer 
service, except when the ADU is constructed with a new primary residential dwelling unit.”
6.
Removal of Reference to Variance Process
The City’s existing ADU Ordinance includes a statement that, “All other zoning requirements shall be complied 
with unless an authorized variance is approved.” HCD commented that the City should amend the Ordinance to 
“bolster clarity regarding the ADU approval process,” which must provide for ministerial review under State law.  
This reference to the variance process has been removed from the ADU Ordinance. 
7.
Number of ADUs Permitted with Single-Family
HCD has now interpreted that State law requires that single-family lots be allowed to develop up to three types of 
ADUs, as is reflected in Government Code Section 66323. The three required types are:
a.
One Attached or Conversion ADU (from existing or proposed space within a structure); 
b.
One Detached New Construction ADU; and 
c.
One JADU, located within the walls of an existing or proposed Single-Family Dwelling.
HCD’s January 2025 ADU Handbook (p. 19), confirms that all three types must be allowed concurrently when a lot 
has an existing or proposed single-family dwelling, and local agencies may not restrict a property to fewer than 
these three units if each meets the applicable development standards. Current City regulations allow only two 
ADUs (one ADU and one JADU). To comply with State law and HCD’s interpretation, the ADU Ordinance will now 
allow up to three ADUs on a single-family lot. 
8.
Front Setback Clarification
HCD commented that the City may not impose a front setback requirement that would preclude construction of an 
ADU of up to 800 square feet with four-foot side and rear setbacks. Under current local ADU regulations, all new 
construction ADUs are subject to the front setback of the underlying zoning district. Therefore, language has been 
added to clarify that “no front setback is required for a State Exempt ADU.” The City may still impose a front 
1022 of 1110

--- page 1023 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
setback on other new construction ADUs that are not considered state-exempt units. 
“State Exempt ADU” means an accessory dwelling unit that is created pursuant to Government Code Section 
66323. This includes conversion ADUs within an existing single-family residence or accessory structure, as well as a 
new detached ADU of not more than 800 square feet, with four-foot side and rear setbacks.  
9.
Architectural Standards – Not Applicable to State Exempt ADUs
The City’s existing ADU Ordinance includes several architectural standards for ADUs. HCD provided a comment to 
clarify that these architectural standards may not preclude development of “State Exempt ADUs.” This has been 
addressed in the update, which includes definitions of “State Exempt ADUs” and “Nonexempt ADUs”, and clearly 
states that the architectural standards shall only apply to Nonexempt ADUs. 
10. Design Standards – Removal of Subjective Standards 
State ADU law allows for local ordinances to impose only objective standards on accessory dwelling units. In their 
review of the City’s existing ADU Ordinance, HCD noted that some of the design standards such as requiring similar 
exterior materials to the main home, and windows that maximize privacy, are subjective in nature and therefore 
must be either removed or amended to be objective in nature. To address this comment, the subjective standards  
have been removed.
11. JADU Interior Access Requirements
Under current local requirements, all JADUs are required to have an interior access between the JADU and the 
single-family dwelling. State law only requires an interior access when the JADU does not include a separate 
bathroom. City staff met with HCD to discuss these requirements and identified that shared utility controls would 
also create a need for interior access between units. The update amends the code to specify that interior access is 
required between the JADU and single-family dwelling when they share restroom facilities and/or utility controls 
such as a thermostat. 
12. Maximum JADU Size
HCD commented that the City’s current JADU size maximum of 650 square feet exceeded the maximum unit size 
of 500 square feet for a JADU allowed under State law. The update includes a revision to reflect this HCD guidance, 
reducing the JADU maximum unit size from 650 square feet to 500 square feet.  
13. JADU Deed Restriction Requirements
All JADUs are required by State Law to record a deed restriction with the following provisions:
a.
A prohibition on the sale of the junior accessory dwelling unit separate from the sale of the single-family 
residence, including a statement that the deed restriction may be enforced against future purchasers; and
b.
A restriction on the size and attributes of the junior accessory dwelling unit.
The City’s current ordinance includes provisions consistent with the two listed in State law (above), plus two 
additional provisions that are included in JADU deed restrictions: 
c.
The property owner must occupy either the single-family residence or the JADU; and
d.
If the JADU is rented, the unit shall not be rented for a period of less than 30 consecutive calendar days. 
HCD noted that these two additional deed restriction provisions go beyond what is explicitly listed by State law, 
and therefore must be removed from the City’s deed restriction requirements. However, while not explicitly listed 
in a JADU deed restriction, the JADU owner occupancy requirement and prohibition on using JADUs for short-term 
rentals are both still requirements under State law and in the City’s ADU Ordinance.  
Assembly Bill 1154, signed by the Governor on October 10, 2025, effective January 1, 2026,  amended owner-
occupancy requirements to only apply if the JADU has shared bathroom facilities with the existing structure. 
Therefore, this requirement has been updated to clarify that owner-occupancy is not required if the JADU has its 
own bathroom. 
1023 of 1110

--- page 1024 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
14. Addition of Multifamily ADU Development Standards
The current ADU Ordinance does not include specific standards for multifamily ADUs. Rather, there is a reference 
within the local Ordinance to Government Code Section 65852.2(e)(1), which previously included standards for 
multifamily ADU development. However, State Law standards for multifamily ADUs were updated under Senate 
Bill 1211 and renumbered under Senate Bill 477 thereby making the cross-references the local ADU Ordinance 
incorrect.
Most notably, SB 1211 included a change to the number of ADUs permitted on a lot with an existing multifamily 
dwelling. Previously, up to two new detached ADUs were permitted on a lot with an existing or proposed 
multifamily dwelling. However, this has been updated to allow up to eight new detached ADUs on a lot with an 
existing multifamily dwelling, provided that the number of ADUs cannot exceed the number of existing primary 
multifamily dwelling units on the lot. To provide more clarity and reduce the need to cross reference Government 
Code sections the ordinance update includes a new section for Multifamily ADU Development Standards.
Other Changes Proposed to Address Local Needs and Ordinance Functionality 
The below changes have been added, in addition to the changes specifically required by HCD, in order to address issues 
identified by City staff to improve functionality and clarity in implementing the ADU Ordinance. The Planning Commission 
Study Session on August 26, 2025 and community workshop on September 25, 2025 introduced these topic areas for 
discussion and feedback to help inform how to proceed with these updates. Based on the input received, below is a 
summary of the additional proposed changes.
15. Addition of Definitions Section 
The ordinance update includes a new section at the beginning of the chapter with ADU-related definitions, with 
the definitions only applicable to the ADU Ordinance (Chapter 27.19). 
16. Updates to Discretionary Review as Alternative to Ministerial Review
The City’s existing ADU Ordinance provides two paths for ADU review. The first is a ministerial building permit for 
any ADU that meets all of the development standards of the Ordinance. The second option is for an Accessory 
Dwelling Unit Discretionary Review (ADUDR), subject to a Planning Application approved by the Zoning 
Administrator prior to building permit application for any ADU that is not in compliance with the development 
standards of the ADU Ordinance.
At the Planning Commission Study Session, staff provided background on the ADUDR process and sought 
recommendation from the Planning Commission on potentially removing the ADUDR process, paired with more 
permissive objective design standards for ADU height and allowances for second-story decks and balconies. 
The Commission recommended the City keep the ADUDR process in the updated Ordinance as an alternative to 
ministerial review to provide a pathway for consideration of ADU designs that exceed the specified objective 
standards to address certain site-specific design needs. The Commission was also in favor of staff’s 
recommendation to propose more permissive objective standards, expressing support for the idea that these 
changes would result in more ADU projects qualifying for the more streamlined ministerial review process – the 
proposed changes to height, setback, and second story deck standards are discussed in more detail below.  
a.
Clarification of ADUDR Applicability – The ordinance update includes added language to clarify that the 
ADUDR process may be used to review any ADU that is not in full compliance with height, setback, or daylight 
plane provisions of the ADU Ordinance. However, the ADUDR process may not be used to increase the 
maximum allowable floor area of an ADU. 
i.
The Planning Commission recommended updating this language to clarify that the ADUDR may be 
used to review any ADU that is not in full compliance with any objective standard in the ADU 
Ordinance, including but not limited to, height, setback, or daylight plane provisions (excluding 
maximum allowable floor area of an ADU). 
1024 of 1110

--- page 1025 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
b.
ADUDR Requirement for Second-Story Decks and Balconies – Under current ADU Ordinance provisions, any 
second-story deck or balcony proposed with an ADU requires ADUDR. The ordinance update includes new 
objective standards for second-story balconies (discussed further below), which would allow for small interior 
facing second-story decks or balconies to be reviewed ministerially. Any proposed larger second-story decks or 
balconies that do not meet these objective standards will still require an ADUDR under the ordinance update.  
c.
Updated ADUDR Findings – The ordinance update revises the ADUDR findings to improve clarity, objectivity, 
and consistency with State law and City policies. More objective findings have been added to address privacy 
impacts from larger second-story decks, require justification for deviations from height, setback, or daylight 
plane standards due to site-specific hardship, and confirm consistency with the General Plan, aligning the 
ADUDR process with other planning applications.
d.
Appeal Process for ADUDR – The appeal process for the decision of the Zoning Administrator for an ADUDR 
had been streamlined in the ordinance update to provide for appeals to the City Manager or their designee, 
which could be a hearing officer.  However, under the current draft Ordinance, the appeal would go to 
Planning Commission first, and then to City Council if further appealed, consistent with state law.
i.
The Planning Commission recommended not changing the appeals process to the City Manager or 
their designee. Instead the current process of the Planning Commission hearing appeals first, and then 
City Council if further appealed, would remain. 
17. Clarification on Max. ADU Unit Size and FAR Exemption for ADUs
The City’s current regulations do not include any specified maximum ADU size limit. Any ADU floor area exceeding 
800 square feet is regulated by the allowable floor area ratio (FAR) of the underlying zoning. Up to 800 square feet 
of ADU floor area is exempt from the total FAR calculation for a lot. ADUs cannot exceed the size of the primary 
residential unit on the property (either the single-family residence, or any individual unit within a multifamily 
building), with the exception that if the primary residence is less than 800 square feet, an 800 square-foot ADU 
would still be allowed per State law. The existing maximum unit size standards have not been substantially 
changed. 
Since the December 9, 2025 Planning Commission public hearing, several changes to State ADU law have become 
effective, including Senate Bill 543 (SB543), which became effective January 1, 2026. SB543 includes an update to 
specify that requirements for JADU and ADU square footages are based on interior livable space. The proposed 
update includes a definition for “ADU Living Area” that is consistent with how State law defines interior livable 
space. To address this change, a minor edit has been made to the draft Ordinance language to refer to “ADU Living 
Area” in the Maximum Unit Size and Floor Area sections. 
18. Height of Detached ADUs
The City’s current ADU Ordinance sets a maximum height for detached ADUs at 16 feet to top plate and 24 feet to 
roof peak. The ordinance update would increase the plate height requirement by two feet, resulting in a maximum 
height for detached ADUs of 18 feet to top plate and 24 feet to roof peak, which is consistent with state law. The 
increase in height allowance would allow for more standard two-story construction. No change is proposed to the 
height requirements for attached ADUs, which are currently set at 24 feet to plate and 32 feet to peak, consistent 
with the maximum height requirements for single-family residences. 
19. Setback Exception for ADUs in Rear Third of a Lot Meeting Specific Standards
The proposed ordinance update would allow ADUs built in the rear one-third of a parcel to use the same zero-
setback allowances currently permitted for accessory structures. Under existing zoning, accessory structures in this 
area may be built to a zero setback if they meet established height, daylight plane, yard coverage, and floor 
limitations, and these structures may later be converted to ADUs. To streamline ADU production and avoid this 
two-step process, the Planning Commission supported applying the accessory structure setback standards directly 
to new ADUs constructed in the rear one-third of a property.
1025 of 1110

--- page 1026 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
20. Removal of ADU Parking Requirements
The City’s current ADU ordinance requires one off-street parking space per ADU but includes several State-
mandated exemptions, which apply to most properties in San Mateo, such as proximity to transit, location within 
existing structures, or access to car-share services. HCD also noted an additional required exemption when an ADU 
is proposed with a new single- or multifamily dwelling. Because these exemptions already cover nearly all ADU-
eligible parcels in the City, the ordinance update proposes to eliminate all off-street parking requirements for 
ADUs to ensure equitable treatment. This change would not materially alter existing regulations, but the updated 
ordinance would continue to encourage voluntary off-street parking through new supportive provisions, an 
approach that was discussed at the Planning Commission Study Session and generally supported by the 
Commission.
21. Additional Voluntary Parking Standards 
To support additional creative approaches to encourage voluntary off-street parking for ADUs and to help address 
the constraints to on-street parking in many residential areas of the City, staff have included additional provisions 
to incentivize voluntary off-street parking for ADUs and JADUs, as follows:
a.
Voluntarily provided parking associated with ADU or JADU development must be located entirely on the 
same lot, and may be provided in a garage, carport, uncovered, or in tandem orientation. When a garage 
or carport is converted to an ADU or JADU, or demolished in conjunction with construction of an ADU, the 
existing driveway and curb cut are not required to be removed and may be maintained as parking area. 
b.
A maximum of up to two voluntarily provided parking stalls may be provided for each ADU or JADU. 
c.
The maximum driveway width for single-car garages may be up to 20 feet to accommodate additional off-
street parking.
d.
Uncovered parking for an ADU or JADU may be located in the required front, side, and rear yard setback 
areas. If a covered parking structure is proposed to provide voluntary parking below a second-story ADU, 
the parking structure may be located within the footprint of the proposed ADU, provided that it is outside 
of the front yard setback of the underlying zoning district. When a parking structure is proposed within a 
required side or rear setback of the underlying zoning district, the plate height of the parking structure 
shall not exceed nine feet in height.
22. Prohibition of Rooftop Decks or Terraces above an ADU
A standard has been added in the ordinance update to specify that rooftop decks or terraces above any portion of 
an ADU are prohibited. This provision is added to clarify a distinction between rooftop decks and terraces, and 
small second-story decks or balconies. 
23. Allowance of Small Second-story decks or balconies with ministerial review if specific standards are met
Under the City’s current ADU Ordinance, all second story decks and balconies require ADUDR review. At the 
Planning Commission Study Session, the Commission provided staff with feedback on this process, expressing 
support for creating objective standards to allow for some second story decks and balconies to be considered 
ministerially. The Commission expressed the importance of providing standards that would ensure deck and 
balcony designs are sensitive to potential privacy impacts to adjacent neighbors. As a part of the ordinance 
update, staff have proposed that small second story decks or balconies may be considered as part of an ADU 
design for ministerial review, provided that the following standards are met:
a.
Second story decks or balconies shall not be larger than 50 square feet in size.
b.
No portion of a second story deck or balcony shall be located within the setbacks of the underlying zoning, 
regardless of the ADU setbacks.
c.
Second story decks or balconies shall only face the interior of the subject property.
 
i.
The Planning Commission recommended changing this to instead specify that second story decks or 
1026 of 1110

--- page 1027 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
balconies shall not face abutting residential properties. The Commission’s recommendation was based on 
the idea that a deck or balcony facing a street, nonresidential property, park, etc. would not create the 
same privacy concerns as a deck facing into a neighboring home or residential yard area. 
These proposed standards would allow for small second story balconies and decks to be proposed with ADUs 
considered for ministerial review, while addressing concerns about privacy impacts to adjacent properties. This 
would also provide for additional streamlining for decks and balconies that meet these standards, reducing the 
need for ADUDR review. Second story decks or balconies that do not meet these standards could still be 
considered under the ADUDR process. 
NEXT STEPS:
Following introduction, the Ordinance will be brought back for a second reading and adoption at the next available City 
Council meeting, and the Ordinance will go into effect 30 days afterwards. In addition, the City is required to submit the 
approved ordinance to HCD within 60 days of adoption for final review and acceptance by the State. If HCD identifies any 
inconsistencies between the City’s Ordinance and State law, a follow-up code amendment may be necessary. However, 
based on prior discussions with HCD and internal review, Draft Ordinance appears to be fully consistent with State law.
Once the ADU Ordinance is adopted, staff will update all ADU related handouts, educational materials, and website 
information to reflect the updated requirements for development of ADUs and JADUs.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Zoning Code Amendments to implement the Housing Element are funded in the Community Development Department’s
annual operating budget, utilizing the Advanced Planning Fund (Fund 225), and does not impact the City’s General Fund.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
The proposed ordinance is statutorily exempt from the requirements of the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) 
pursuant to Section 21080.17 of the Public Resources Code and Section 15282(h) of the CEQA Guidelines because it is an 
accessory dwelling unit ordinance that is implementing the provisions of the Government Code related to ADUs and 
JADUs. 
NOTICING AND COMMENTS:
All meeting noticing requirements were met. Additionally, staff sent meeting details in the Planning Division eNewsletter, 
citywide eNewsletter, the Housing Element interested parties list and posted to the Planning Division’s What’s Happening 
in Development webpage. Prior to this public hearing, notice was also provided in the Planning Division eNewsletter, the 
citywide eNewsletter, and on City Social Media to advertise the August 26, 2025 Planning Commission Study Session and 
September 25, 2025 Virtual Outreach Webinar. 
Staff has not received any public comments on this item since the Planning Commission public hearing on December 9, 
2025. All public comments received prior to this public hearing at included in Attachment 5.  Any public comments 
received after agenda publication will be posted to this item on the City’s Public Meeting Portal as “Post Packet Public 
Comments.”
